source: graph/concepts/*.json (deep concept layer; spine stays in sidecar.json)

nodes.label: Amino Acids
nodes.summary: Amino acids are the monomeric building blocks of proteins, each with an amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and variable R-group on a central alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Acid-Base Chemistry
nodes.summary: Amino acids are polyprotic, with characteristic pKa values that govern charge as a function of pH.

nodes.label: Buffering near pKa
nodes.summary: An ionizable group buffers most effectively at pH values within about one unit of its pKa.

nodes.label: Henderson-Hasselbalch application
nodes.summary: pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) predicts the protonation state of each ionizable group at a given pH.

nodes.label: Isoelectric point (pI)
nodes.summary: The pI is the pH at which an amino acid carries no net charge, computed as the average of the two pKa values flanking the neutral species.

nodes.label: Net charge at given pH
nodes.summary: Net charge is determined by comparing the solution pH to each group's pKa: groups with pKa above pH are protonated.

nodes.label: Calculating pI
nodes.summary: For amino acids with an ionizable side chain, pI is the average of the two pKa values bracketing the zwitterionic form.

nodes.label: pI of acidic and basic amino acids
nodes.summary: Acidic amino acids have low pI values and basic amino acids have high pI values because the side-chain pKa shifts which two pKas flank neutrality.

nodes.label: Characteristic pKa values
nodes.summary: Typical pKa values are ~2 for the alpha-carboxyl, ~9-10 for the alpha-amino, plus side-chain pKa values for ionizable R-groups.

nodes.label: Polyprotic / stepwise deprotonation
nodes.summary: Amino acids have multiple ionizable groups that gain and lose protons stepwise, from most protonated at low pH to least protonated at high pH.

nodes.label: Titration curve
nodes.summary: An amino acid titration curve shows stepwise deprotonation with buffering plateaus at each pKa and net charge changing with pH.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Classification
nodes.summary: The 20 standard amino acids are grouped by side-chain chemistry: nonpolar, polar uncharged, acidic, and basic.

nodes.label: Acidic (negatively charged) side chains
nodes.summary: Aspartate and glutamate have carboxylic-acid side chains that are deprotonated and negatively charged at physiological pH.

nodes.label: Acidic side-chain pKa values
nodes.summary: Asp (~3.7) and Glu (~4.2) side-chain carboxyls have low pKa values and carry negative charge above those pH values.

nodes.label: Amide-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Asparagine and glutamine carry uncharged amide side chains, the polar derivatives of aspartate and glutamate.

nodes.label: Aromatic side chains
nodes.summary: Phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan contain aromatic rings and absorb UV light near 280 nm.

nodes.label: Basic (positively charged) side chains
nodes.summary: Lysine, arginine, and histidine have basic side chains that are protonated and positively charged at physiological pH (His only partially).

nodes.label: Basic side-chain pKa values
nodes.summary: Lys (~10.5), Arg (~12.5), and His (~6) side chains differ in pKa, setting the pH range over which each carries positive charge.

nodes.label: Essential amino acids
nodes.summary: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesized by humans and must be obtained from the diet.

nodes.label: Hydrophobicity / hydropathy
nodes.summary: A residue's hydropathy index predicts whether it buries in the hydrophobic core or faces aqueous solvent.

nodes.label: Hydroxyl-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Serine, threonine, and tyrosine bear hydroxyl groups that hydrogen-bond and serve as phosphorylation and glycosylation sites.

nodes.label: Nonpolar (hydrophobic) side chains
nodes.summary: Nonpolar amino acids (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Met, Trp) have hydrophobic side chains that cluster in protein interiors.

nodes.label: Polar uncharged side chains
nodes.summary: Polar uncharged amino acids (Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln) have side chains that form hydrogen bonds without a formal charge at pH 7.

nodes.label: Sulfur-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Cysteine and methionine contain sulfur; cysteine can form disulfide bonds and methionine is the start-codon residue.

nodes.label: General Amino Acid Structure
nodes.summary: All standard amino acids share a common backbone differing only in their side chain (R-group).

nodes.label: Alpha-carbon
nodes.summary: The alpha-carbon is the central carbon bonded to the amino group, carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and the R-group.

nodes.label: Amino group
nodes.summary: The amino group (-NH2/-NH3+) is the basic nitrogen-containing functional group attached to the alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: Amphoteric character
nodes.summary: Amino acids are amphoteric: the carboxyl can donate a proton (acid) and the amino group can accept one (base), so they react as both acid and base.

nodes.label: Carboxyl group
nodes.summary: The carboxyl group (-COOH/-COO-) is the acidic functional group attached to the alpha-carbon.

nodes.label: Dipolar nature
nodes.summary: Amino acids carry both positive and negative charges simultaneously, making them dipolar and highly water-soluble.

nodes.label: Side chain (R-group)
nodes.summary: The R-group is the variable side chain that determines each amino acid's chemical properties.

nodes.label: Three- and one-letter abbreviations
nodes.summary: Each amino acid has standard three-letter (e.g., Ala) and one-letter (e.g., A) abbreviations used to write sequences.

nodes.label: The 20 standard amino acids
nodes.summary: Proteins are built from a common set of 20 standard genetically encoded alpha-amino acids, each with a distinct side chain.

nodes.label: Zwitterion
nodes.summary: At physiological pH an amino acid exists as a zwitterion with a protonated amino group and deprotonated carboxyl group, giving net charge near zero.

nodes.label: Special-property residues
nodes.summary: Several amino acids have unique structural roles arising from their distinctive side chains.

nodes.label: Cysteine and disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: Two cysteine thiols can be oxidized to form a covalent disulfide (cystine) bridge that stabilizes tertiary/quaternary structure.

nodes.label: Glycine flexibility
nodes.summary: Glycine's single-hydrogen side chain gives the backbone maximal conformational flexibility and allows tight turns.

nodes.label: Histidine pKa near physiological pH
nodes.summary: Histidine's imidazole side chain has a pKa near 6, letting it act as a proton donor/acceptor in enzyme active sites and buffer near pH 7.

nodes.label: Methionine as the start residue
nodes.summary: Methionine is encoded by the AUG start codon, so it is the first residue incorporated into nearly all eukaryotic polypeptides.

nodes.label: Proline
nodes.summary: Proline's side chain loops back to the backbone nitrogen forming a rigid ring that introduces kinks and disrupts helices/sheets.

nodes.label: Proline is a secondary amine (imino acid)
nodes.summary: Proline's nitrogen is part of its ring, making it a secondary amine (imino acid) that cannot donate a backbone amide hydrogen for hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Ser/Thr/Tyr phosphorylation sites
nodes.summary: The hydroxyl groups of serine, threonine, and tyrosine are phosphorylated by kinases to switch protein activity on or off.

nodes.label: Amino Acid Stereochemistry
nodes.summary: With the exception of glycine, amino acids are chiral and predominantly the L-configuration in proteins.

nodes.label: Absolute configuration (Fischer)
nodes.summary: In a Fischer projection of an L-amino acid the amino group points to the left with the carboxyl at top.

nodes.label: Chirality of amino acids
nodes.summary: The alpha-carbon is a stereocenter (four different groups) in all amino acids except glycine.

nodes.label: D-amino acids (rare biological roles)
nodes.summary: D-amino acids are rare in nature, appearing in bacterial cell walls and some peptide antibiotics, but not in ribosomally synthesized proteins.

nodes.label: D/L vs R/S designation
nodes.summary: L-amino acids correspond to S configuration except cysteine, which is R due to the sulfur in its side chain changing CIP priority.

nodes.label: D/L relative to glyceraldehyde
nodes.summary: The D/L system assigns configuration by relating the amino acid's stereocenter to L- or D-glyceraldehyde as a reference.

nodes.label: Glycine is achiral
nodes.summary: Glycine has two hydrogens on the alpha-carbon, so it is the only standard amino acid that is achiral.

nodes.label: L-amino acids in proteins
nodes.summary: Essentially all amino acids found in proteins are of the L-configuration.

nodes.label: Conjugated and Modified Proteins
nodes.summary: Many proteins are covalently or noncovalently modified with non-amino-acid groups that tune their function.

nodes.label: Conjugated Protein Types
nodes.summary: Conjugated proteins are classified by the chemical identity of their attached prosthetic group.

nodes.label: Apoprotein vs holoprotein
nodes.summary: An apoprotein lacks its prosthetic group and is inactive; the complete, active form with the group bound is the holoprotein.

nodes.label: Glycoproteins
nodes.summary: Glycoproteins carry covalently attached carbohydrate groups important for recognition and stability.

nodes.label: Hemoproteins / chromoproteins
nodes.summary: Hemoproteins (e.g., hemoglobin, cytochromes) carry a heme prosthetic group; chromoproteins carry a colored prosthetic group.

nodes.label: Lipoproteins
nodes.summary: Lipoproteins associate proteins with lipids to transport hydrophobic molecules in the blood.

nodes.label: Metalloproteins
nodes.summary: Metalloproteins contain a bound metal ion essential to their structure or catalytic function.

nodes.label: Nucleoproteins and phosphoproteins
nodes.summary: Nucleoproteins (e.g., histones) bind nucleic acids; phosphoproteins carry covalently bound phosphate groups.

nodes.label: Prosthetic groups
nodes.summary: A prosthetic group is a tightly bound non-amino-acid component required for protein activity, such as heme.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Formation
nodes.summary: Amino acids link via peptide (amide) bonds to form peptides and proteins.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Formation
nodes.summary: A peptide bond forms between the carboxyl of one amino acid and the amino group of the next.

nodes.label: Amide linkage
nodes.summary: The peptide bond is an amide bond joining a carbonyl carbon to a backbone nitrogen.

nodes.label: Polypeptide backbone
nodes.summary: The repeating N-Calpha-C(=O) sequence forms the polypeptide backbone from which side chains project.

nodes.label: Condensation (dehydration) reaction
nodes.summary: Peptide bond formation is a condensation reaction that releases one water molecule.

nodes.label: Directionality of synthesis
nodes.summary: Proteins are synthesized and conventionally read from the N-terminus to the C-terminus.

nodes.label: Peptide bond hydrolysis
nodes.summary: Peptide bonds are cleaved by hydrolysis, adding water across the amide bond, often catalyzed by proteases.

nodes.label: N-terminus and C-terminus
nodes.summary: Polypeptides have a free amino N-terminus (written first) and a free carboxyl C-terminus (written last).

nodes.label: Residue and peptide nomenclature
nodes.summary: Once joined, each amino acid unit is a residue; chains are named dipeptide, oligopeptide, or polypeptide by length.

nodes.label: Peptide Bond Properties
nodes.summary: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character that constrains backbone geometry.

nodes.label: Phi and psi dihedral angles
nodes.summary: Rotation is permitted about the N-Calpha (phi) and Calpha-C (psi) bonds, defining backbone conformation.

nodes.label: Planarity and rigidity
nodes.summary: Partial double-bond character makes the peptide bond planar and rigid, with no free rotation about the C-N bond.

nodes.label: Ramachandran plot
nodes.summary: A Ramachandran plot maps sterically allowed phi/psi combinations, clustering at alpha-helix and beta-sheet regions.

nodes.label: Resonance / partial double-bond character
nodes.summary: Resonance delocalizes electrons between the carbonyl oxygen and the C-N bond, giving the peptide bond partial double-bond character.

nodes.label: Trans configuration preference
nodes.summary: Peptide bonds favor the trans configuration to minimize steric clash, except in some X-proline bonds.

nodes.label: Protein Separation and Analysis
nodes.summary: Proteins are isolated and characterized by techniques exploiting size, charge, and binding properties.

nodes.label: Chromatography
nodes.summary: Chromatography separates proteins by partitioning between a stationary and mobile phase.

nodes.label: Affinity chromatography
nodes.summary: Affinity chromatography isolates a protein by its specific binding to an immobilized ligand.

nodes.label: Ion-exchange chromatography
nodes.summary: Ion-exchange chromatography separates proteins by net charge using a charged stationary phase.

nodes.label: Chromatography principle
nodes.summary: Proteins separate by differential partitioning or interaction between a stationary phase and a flowing mobile phase.

nodes.label: Size-exclusion chromatography
nodes.summary: Size-exclusion (gel-filtration) chromatography separates by size, eluting larger proteins first.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis separates proteins in an electric field based on charge and size.

nodes.label: Isoelectric focusing
nodes.summary: Isoelectric focusing separates proteins along a pH gradient where each migrates to its pI and stops.

nodes.label: Native PAGE
nodes.summary: Native PAGE separates folded proteins by size, shape, and charge without denaturation.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis principle (charge in a field)
nodes.summary: Charged proteins migrate in an electric field toward the oppositely charged electrode, with mobility set by charge, size, and shape.

nodes.label: SDS-PAGE
nodes.summary: SDS-PAGE denatures proteins and coats them with negative charge so they separate by size alone.

nodes.label: 2D gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: 2D gels combine isoelectric focusing then SDS-PAGE to resolve proteins by pI and size.

nodes.label: Quantification and Identification
nodes.summary: Protein concentration and identity are determined by spectroscopic and immunological assays.

nodes.label: Colorimetric assays (Bradford, BCA)
nodes.summary: Bradford and BCA assays produce a color change proportional to protein concentration.

nodes.label: Edman degradation
nodes.summary: Edman degradation sequentially removes and identifies the N-terminal residue to determine sequence.

nodes.label: ELISA
nodes.summary: ELISA quantifies a specific protein using an antibody linked to an enzyme that generates a measurable color signal.

nodes.label: Mass spectrometry
nodes.summary: Mass spectrometry identifies proteins and peptides by their mass-to-charge ratio.

nodes.label: UV absorbance at 280 nm
nodes.summary: Aromatic residues absorb UV near 280 nm, allowing protein concentration estimation via Beer's law.

nodes.label: Western blotting
nodes.summary: Western blots detect a specific protein using antibodies after gel separation and transfer.

nodes.label: X-ray crystallography / structure determination
nodes.summary: X-ray crystallography and NMR determine the atomic-resolution three-dimensional structure of a protein.

nodes.label: Protein Folding and Denaturation
nodes.summary: Proteins fold into a native conformation that can be lost (denatured) and sometimes regained.

nodes.label: Denaturation
nodes.summary: Denaturation disrupts higher-order structure while leaving the primary sequence intact.

nodes.label: Chemical denaturants
nodes.summary: Urea, guanidinium chloride, and detergents disrupt hydrophobic and hydrogen-bonding interactions.

nodes.label: Denaturation definition
nodes.summary: Denaturation is loss of secondary, tertiary, and quaternary structure without breaking peptide bonds.

nodes.label: Heat denaturation
nodes.summary: Heat disrupts noncovalent interactions, unfolding the protein and abolishing function.

nodes.label: Denaturation abolishes function
nodes.summary: Because function depends on 3D shape, denaturation typically destroys biological activity even though the sequence is intact.

nodes.label: pH-induced denaturation
nodes.summary: Extreme pH alters side-chain charges, disrupting ionic interactions and hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Reducing agents break disulfides
nodes.summary: Reducing agents like beta-mercaptoethanol or DTT cleave disulfide bonds, disrupting structure.

nodes.label: Renaturation / refolding
nodes.summary: Some denatured proteins refold spontaneously to the native state when denaturing conditions are removed.

nodes.label: Misfolding and Aggregation
nodes.summary: Improper folding can produce nonfunctional or pathological protein aggregates.

nodes.label: Protein aggregation
nodes.summary: Misfolded proteins can expose hydrophobic patches and aggregate, sometimes forming amyloid fibrils.

nodes.label: Prions
nodes.summary: Prions are misfolded proteins that template misfolding of normal copies, causing disease.

nodes.label: Native State and Folding
nodes.summary: The native state is the functional, lowest-free-energy conformation of a protein.

nodes.label: Chaperone proteins
nodes.summary: Molecular chaperones assist correct folding and prevent aggregation but do not change the final native structure.

nodes.label: Folding funnel / Levinthal's paradox
nodes.summary: Folding follows an energy funnel toward the native state rather than a random search, resolving Levinthal's paradox of too-many conformations.

nodes.label: Native conformation
nodes.summary: The native conformation is the biologically active, thermodynamically favored folded form.

nodes.label: Solvation and water's role
nodes.summary: Folding is driven largely by the aqueous environment, with water excluding nonpolar surfaces and ordering the hydrophobic core.

nodes.label: Anfinsen / thermodynamic hypothesis
nodes.summary: Anfinsen showed that primary sequence alone can specify the native fold, which is the global free-energy minimum.

nodes.label: Non-Enzymatic Protein Function
nodes.summary: Proteins perform diverse non-catalytic roles including structure, transport, motility, signaling, immune defense, and binding.

nodes.label: Cell Adhesion and Clotting Proteins
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate cell-cell adhesion and form structural networks such as blood clots.

nodes.label: Cell adhesion molecules
nodes.summary: Cell adhesion molecules such as cadherins, integrins, and selectins bind cells to each other and to the extracellular matrix.

nodes.label: Fibrinogen / fibrin (clotting)
nodes.summary: Fibrinogen is cleaved to fibrin, which polymerizes into the structural mesh of a blood clot.

nodes.label: Binding and Transport Proteins
nodes.summary: Many proteins function by reversibly binding ligands or transporting molecules.

nodes.label: Binding affinity and reversibility
nodes.summary: Binding is described by an equilibrium dissociation constant; most physiological binding is reversible and tunable.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin oxygen transport
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin reversibly binds oxygen with cooperative, sigmoidal kinetics for efficient transport.

nodes.label: Ligand binding specificity
nodes.summary: Binding proteins recognize specific ligands through complementary shape and noncovalent interactions.

nodes.label: Membrane transport proteins
nodes.summary: Channel and carrier proteins move ions and molecules across membranes.

nodes.label: Myoglobin oxygen storage
nodes.summary: Myoglobin is a single-subunit oxygen-storage protein with hyperbolic, high-affinity binding.

nodes.label: Transport / carrier proteins (e.g., albumin)
nodes.summary: Soluble carrier proteins such as serum albumin bind and shuttle hydrophobic molecules, drugs, and ions through the blood.

nodes.label: Immune and Recognition Function
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate immune defense and cell-cell recognition.

nodes.label: Antibody structure (heavy/light chains)
nodes.summary: Antibodies have two heavy and two light chains held by disulfide bonds, with antigen-binding variable regions and constant regions.

nodes.label: MHC and antigen presentation
nodes.summary: Major histocompatibility complex proteins present peptide antigens on the cell surface for immune recognition.

nodes.label: Motor Proteins
nodes.summary: Motor proteins convert chemical energy into mechanical movement along cytoskeletal tracks.

nodes.label: Actin filaments as tracks
nodes.summary: Actin polymerizes into microfilaments that serve as tracks for myosin and provide cell motility.

nodes.label: ATP hydrolysis powers movement
nodes.summary: Motor proteins couple cycles of ATP binding and hydrolysis to conformational changes that produce directed force.

nodes.label: Kinesin and dynein
nodes.summary: Kinesin and dynein are motor proteins that transport cargo along microtubules in opposite directions.

nodes.label: Myosin
nodes.summary: Myosin is a motor protein that walks along actin filaments using ATP hydrolysis to drive muscle contraction.

nodes.label: Signaling and Recognition Proteins
nodes.summary: Proteins mediate cell signaling, immune recognition, and hormonal control.

nodes.label: Antibodies (immunoglobulins)
nodes.summary: Antibodies are immune proteins that bind antigens with high specificity through variable regions.

nodes.label: G proteins
nodes.summary: G proteins are GTP-binding switch proteins that relay signals from receptors to effectors.

nodes.label: Peptide hormones
nodes.summary: Peptide and protein hormones such as insulin act as extracellular chemical messengers.

nodes.label: Receptor proteins
nodes.summary: Receptor proteins bind signaling molecules and transduce signals across membranes.

nodes.label: Structural Proteins
nodes.summary: Structural proteins provide mechanical support and shape to cells and tissues.

nodes.label: Collagen
nodes.summary: Collagen is a triple-helix structural protein rich in glycine and proline that provides tensile strength to connective tissue.

nodes.label: Elastin
nodes.summary: Elastin gives tissues elasticity, allowing them to stretch and recoil.

nodes.label: Fibrous vs globular proteins
nodes.summary: Fibrous proteins are elongated and insoluble for structure, while globular proteins are compact and soluble for dynamic functions.

nodes.label: Keratin
nodes.summary: Keratin is a fibrous structural protein, rich in disulfide bonds, found in hair, nails, and skin.

nodes.label: Tubulin and actin (cytoskeleton)
nodes.summary: Tubulin (microtubules) and actin (microfilaments) are cytoskeletal structural proteins that also serve as tracks for motor proteins.

nodes.label: Protein Structure (Hierarchy)
nodes.summary: Protein structure is organized into four hierarchical levels: primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary.

nodes.label: Primary Structure
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the linear amino acid sequence linked by peptide bonds.

nodes.label: Stabilized by covalent bonds only
nodes.summary: Primary structure is held together solely by covalent peptide bonds (and disulfides), unlike the noncovalent higher levels.

nodes.label: Effect of sequence changes
nodes.summary: A single amino acid substitution can drastically alter protein structure and function, as in sickle-cell hemoglobin.

nodes.label: Amino acid sequence
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the covalently linked order of amino acids encoded by the gene.

nodes.label: Sequence determines folding
nodes.summary: The amino acid sequence dictates the protein's higher-order structure and ultimately its function.

nodes.label: Quaternary Structure
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure is the assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into a functional complex.

nodes.label: Subunit cooperativity
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure enables cooperative behavior, such as hemoglobin's sigmoidal oxygen-binding curve.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin as quaternary example
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin is a classic quaternary protein with two alpha and two beta subunits, each binding a heme.

nodes.label: Stabilized by the same noncovalent forces
nodes.summary: Subunits are held together by the same hydrophobic, hydrogen-bonding, ionic, and sometimes disulfide interactions that stabilize tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Subunit assembly
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure arises when two or more folded polypeptide subunits assemble into one functional protein.

nodes.label: Secondary Structure
nodes.summary: Secondary structure consists of local backbone conformations stabilized by hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Alpha-helix
nodes.summary: The alpha-helix is a right-handed coil stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds between residues spaced four apart (i to i+4).

nodes.label: Alpha-helix geometry (3.6 residues/turn)
nodes.summary: The alpha-helix has ~3.6 residues per turn with side chains projecting outward and the helix axis running through the coil.

nodes.label: Beta-pleated sheet
nodes.summary: Beta-sheets form from extended strands hydrogen-bonded side by side, in parallel or antiparallel orientation.

nodes.label: Beta-turn / reverse turn
nodes.summary: Beta-turns reverse the polypeptide direction over a few residues, often involving glycine and proline.

nodes.label: Backbone hydrogen bonding
nodes.summary: Secondary structure is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl oxygens and amide hydrogens.

nodes.label: Helix breakers (Pro, Gly)
nodes.summary: Proline kinks and glycine's flexibility disrupt regular alpha-helices and beta-sheets.

nodes.label: Parallel vs antiparallel sheets
nodes.summary: Antiparallel beta-sheets have linear hydrogen bonds and are more stable than the slanted bonds of parallel sheets.

nodes.label: Side chains not involved in stabilization
nodes.summary: Secondary structure depends on backbone hydrogen bonds, not side-chain interactions, which is why it is sequence-independent in principle.

nodes.label: Stabilizing Forces (Summary)
nodes.summary: A defined set of covalent and noncovalent forces stabilizes the higher-order structure of proteins.

nodes.label: Covalent vs noncovalent forces
nodes.summary: Disulfide bonds are covalent, while hydrogen bonds, ionic, hydrophobic, and van der Waals interactions are noncovalent.

nodes.label: Entropy and the hydrophobic effect
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect is entropically driven by releasing ordered water from around nonpolar surfaces.

nodes.label: Which force stabilizes which level
nodes.summary: Primary = peptide bonds; secondary = backbone hydrogen bonds; tertiary/quaternary = side-chain hydrophobic, ionic, hydrogen-bond, and disulfide interactions.

nodes.label: Supersecondary structure / motifs
nodes.summary: Recurring combinations of secondary-structure elements form motifs that recur across many proteins.

nodes.label: Structural motifs
nodes.summary: Motifs such as helix-turn-helix, beta-barrel, and coiled-coil are recurring arrangements of alpha-helices and beta-sheets.

nodes.label: Tertiary Structure
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is the overall three-dimensional shape of a single polypeptide stabilized by side-chain interactions.

nodes.label: Disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: Covalent disulfide bridges between cysteines covalently lock tertiary (and quaternary) structure.

nodes.label: Protein domains
nodes.summary: Domains are independently folding, often functionally distinct modules within a polypeptide.

nodes.label: Side-chain hydrogen bonds
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds between polar side chains help stabilize the folded tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic core / polar surface
nodes.summary: In globular proteins nonpolar residues pack the interior core while polar/charged residues face the aqueous surface.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic interactions
nodes.summary: Burial of nonpolar side chains away from water is the dominant driving force of tertiary folding.

nodes.label: Salt bridges (ionic interactions)
nodes.summary: Electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged side chains (salt bridges) stabilize tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Side-chain (R-group) interactions
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is determined by interactions among side chains, in contrast to the backbone hydrogen bonds of secondary structure.

nodes.label: Three-dimensional fold
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is the global 3D arrangement of all atoms in a single polypeptide chain.

nodes.label: Van der Waals / London forces
nodes.summary: Weak transient van der Waals interactions among tightly packed atoms contribute to fold stability.

nodes.label: Recombinant DNA and Biotechnology
nodes.summary: Laboratory techniques manipulate, amplify, sequence, and analyze nucleic acids and gene expression.

nodes.label: PCR Amplification
nodes.summary: PCR exponentially amplifies a target DNA sequence in vitro.

nodes.label: Exponential amplification (2^n)
nodes.summary: Each PCR cycle doubles the target, so n cycles ideally yield roughly 2^n copies of the desired region.

nodes.label: Polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
nodes.summary: PCR amplifies DNA through repeated cycles of denaturation, primer annealing, and extension.

nodes.label: RT-PCR and qPCR
nodes.summary: RT-PCR amplifies from RNA via reverse transcription, and qPCR quantifies the product in real time.

nodes.label: Taq polymerase and primers
nodes.summary: Heat-stable Taq polymerase extends two flanking primers that define the amplified region.

nodes.label: Sequencing and Expression Analysis
nodes.summary: Sequencing and microarrays read genomes and measure expression genome-wide.

nodes.label: DNA microarray
nodes.summary: DNA microarrays measure expression of thousands of genes simultaneously by hybridization.

nodes.label: Next-generation / high-throughput sequencing
nodes.summary: Next-generation sequencing reads millions of fragments in parallel, enabling whole-genome and transcriptome analysis.

nodes.label: RFLP and DNA fingerprinting
nodes.summary: Restriction fragment length polymorphisms produce individual-specific banding patterns used in fingerprinting.

nodes.label: Sanger (dideoxy) sequencing
nodes.summary: Sanger sequencing uses chain-terminating dideoxynucleotides to read a DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Cloning and Vectors
nodes.summary: DNA fragments are inserted into vectors and propagated in host cells.

nodes.label: cDNA libraries (reverse transcriptase)
nodes.summary: cDNA is made from mRNA by reverse transcriptase and lacks introns, representing expressed genes.

nodes.label: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing
nodes.summary: CRISPR-Cas9 uses a guide RNA to target Cas9 nuclease for precise genome editing.

nodes.label: DNA ligase in cloning
nodes.summary: DNA ligase joins the insert and vector by sealing phosphodiester bonds during recombinant construction.

nodes.label: Expression vectors
nodes.summary: Expression vectors carry a promoter so the host cell transcribes and translates the cloned gene into protein.

nodes.label: Genomic vs cDNA libraries
nodes.summary: A genomic library contains all DNA including introns, whereas a cDNA library contains only expressed sequences.

nodes.label: Gene knockout and knock-in
nodes.summary: Disrupting a gene (knockout) or inserting a defined sequence (knock-in) reveals gene function in model organisms.

nodes.label: Palindromic recognition sequences
nodes.summary: Restriction sites are palindromes that read the same 5'→3' on both strands, allowing symmetric cleavage.

nodes.label: Plasmids and vectors
nodes.summary: Plasmids are circular DNA vectors that carry an insert and a selectable marker into a host.

nodes.label: Restriction endonucleases
nodes.summary: Restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific palindromic recognition sequences, often producing sticky ends.

nodes.label: Selectable markers and screening
nodes.summary: Antibiotic-resistance and reporter genes let researchers select and identify host cells that took up the recombinant vector.

nodes.label: Sticky vs blunt ends
nodes.summary: Sticky ends have complementary single-stranded overhangs for easy ligation; blunt ends are flush.

nodes.label: Transgenic organisms and gene therapy
nodes.summary: Foreign genes can be introduced into organisms to create transgenics or to correct genetic disease.

nodes.label: Separation and Detection
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis and blotting separate and identify specific nucleic acids and proteins.

nodes.label: Mnemonic: SNoW DRoP
nodes.summary: SNoW DRoP maps Southern/Northern/Western blots to DNA/RNA/Protein targets.

nodes.label: Gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments by size as the negatively charged DNA migrates toward the anode.

nodes.label: Northern blot (RNA)
nodes.summary: A Northern blot detects specific RNA sequences to study gene expression.

nodes.label: Labeled probe hybridization
nodes.summary: A labeled single-stranded probe hybridizes to its complementary target to detect a specific sequence on a blot.

nodes.label: Size-dependent migration
nodes.summary: Smaller DNA fragments migrate faster and travel farther through the gel matrix than larger fragments.

nodes.label: Southern blot (DNA)
nodes.summary: A Southern blot detects specific DNA sequences with a labeled probe after electrophoresis.

nodes.label: Western blot (protein)
nodes.summary: A Western blot detects specific proteins using antibodies.

nodes.label: Eukaryotic Chromosome Organization
nodes.summary: Eukaryotic DNA is packaged with proteins into chromatin and chromosomes within the nucleus.

nodes.label: Chromatin States
nodes.summary: Chromatin exists in transcriptionally active and inactive packaging states.

nodes.label: Centromeres
nodes.summary: Centromeres are constricted chromosomal regions where kinetochores assemble for spindle attachment.

nodes.label: Euchromatin
nodes.summary: Euchromatin is loosely packed, transcriptionally active chromatin.

nodes.label: Heterochromatin
nodes.summary: Heterochromatin is densely packed, transcriptionally silent chromatin.

nodes.label: Single-copy vs repetitive DNA
nodes.summary: Genomes contain unique single-copy genes plus large amounts of repetitive DNA such as tandem repeats and transposable elements.

nodes.label: DNA supercoiling
nodes.summary: DNA can be over- or under-wound into supercoils that compact it and influence transcription.

nodes.label: Telomeres
nodes.summary: Telomeres are repetitive non-coding sequences (TTAGGG in humans) that cap and protect chromosome ends.

nodes.label: Histones and Nucleosomes
nodes.summary: Histone proteins package DNA into nucleosomes, the fundamental unit of chromatin.

nodes.label: Histone proteins (H2A, H2B, H3, H4)
nodes.summary: Two each of H2A, H2B, H3, and H4 form the histone octamer core of a nucleosome.

nodes.label: Histone H1 (linker histone)
nodes.summary: Histone H1 binds linker DNA between nucleosomes to compact chromatin into higher-order fibers.

nodes.label: Nucleosome structure
nodes.summary: A nucleosome is ~147 bp of DNA wrapped around a histone octamer, resembling beads on a string.

nodes.label: Levels of DNA packing (chromatin to chromosome)
nodes.summary: DNA is compacted hierarchically from naked DNA to nucleosomes to the 30 nm fiber to looped domains to the condensed metaphase chromosome.

nodes.label: Histone positive charge and DNA binding
nodes.summary: Histones are rich in basic residues lysine and arginine, giving them a positive charge that binds the negative DNA backbone.

nodes.label: Control of Gene Expression
nodes.summary: Gene expression is regulated at transcriptional, post-transcriptional, and epigenetic levels.

nodes.label: Oncogenes and Tumor Suppressors
nodes.summary: Dysregulated genes controlling growth contribute to cancer.

nodes.label: Oncogenes and proto-oncogenes
nodes.summary: Proto-oncogenes promote normal growth and become oncogenes when mutated to drive uncontrolled division.

nodes.label: Tumor suppressor genes (p53)
nodes.summary: Tumor suppressors such as p53 restrain division and trigger repair or apoptosis; loss of function promotes cancer.

nodes.label: Differential gene expression
nodes.summary: All cells share a genome but express different gene subsets, giving rise to distinct cell types and functions.

nodes.label: Levels of regulation overview
nodes.summary: Expression can be controlled at chromatin, transcription, RNA processing, translation, and protein-degradation stages.

nodes.label: Same genome, different expression
nodes.summary: Cell differentiation arises from differential gene expression rather than from differences in DNA content.

nodes.label: Epigenetic Regulation
nodes.summary: Heritable changes in gene activity occur without altering the DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Chromatin remodeling
nodes.summary: Remodeling complexes reposition nucleosomes to expose or hide promoters.

nodes.label: DNA methylation
nodes.summary: Methylation of cytosines, especially at CpG islands, generally silences gene transcription.

nodes.label: Heritability of epigenetic marks
nodes.summary: Epigenetic marks can be copied through cell division and sometimes across generations without changing the DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Histone modification (acetylation/methylation)
nodes.summary: Histone acetylation loosens chromatin to promote transcription, while certain methylations can repress it.

nodes.label: Genomic imprinting
nodes.summary: Genomic imprinting silences one parental allele through inherited epigenetic marks.

nodes.label: X-inactivation (Barr body)
nodes.summary: X-inactivation epigenetically silences one X chromosome in females, forming a Barr body.

nodes.label: Eukaryotic Transcriptional Control
nodes.summary: Eukaryotes use enhancers, transcription factors, and chromatin remodeling to control expression.

nodes.label: Coactivators and Mediator
nodes.summary: Coactivators and the Mediator complex bridge enhancer-bound activators to the basal transcription machinery.

nodes.label: Combinatorial / tissue-specific control
nodes.summary: Combinations of transcription factors produce tissue-specific patterns of gene expression.

nodes.label: DNA-binding motifs (zinc finger, helix-turn-helix, leucine zipper)
nodes.summary: Transcription factors bind DNA through motifs such as zinc fingers, helix-turn-helix, and leucine zippers.

nodes.label: Enhancers and silencers
nodes.summary: Enhancers increase and silencers decrease transcription from a distance via bound regulatory proteins.

nodes.label: Activators and transcription factors
nodes.summary: Activators and transcription factors bind regulatory DNA to recruit or stabilize RNA polymerase II.

nodes.label: Post-Transcriptional and RNA Control
nodes.summary: RNA stability, small RNAs, and translational control fine-tune expression.

nodes.label: miRNA and siRNA (RNA interference)
nodes.summary: Small RNAs like miRNA and siRNA silence genes by degrading mRNA or blocking translation.

nodes.label: mRNA stability and degradation
nodes.summary: mRNA half-life is regulated, with the cap and poly-A tail protecting transcripts from degradation.

nodes.label: Non-coding RNAs
nodes.summary: Non-coding RNAs perform regulatory and structural roles without being translated.

nodes.label: Translational regulation
nodes.summary: Cells regulate how often an mRNA is translated through factors and RNA-binding proteins.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic Operons
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes regulate clustered genes through operons under negative and positive control.

nodes.label: Inducer vs corepressor
nodes.summary: An inducer turns transcription on by inactivating a repressor; a corepressor turns it off by activating a repressor.

nodes.label: Inducible system (lac operon)
nodes.summary: The inducible lac operon is normally off and is switched on by lactose (allolactose) inactivating the repressor.

nodes.label: Operon (promoter, operator, genes)
nodes.summary: An operon is a cluster of coordinately controlled genes with a shared promoter and operator.

nodes.label: Positive control (CAP/cAMP)
nodes.summary: In positive control the CAP-cAMP complex boosts lac transcription when glucose is scarce.

nodes.label: Repressible system (trp operon)
nodes.summary: The repressible trp operon is normally on and is switched off when tryptophan acts as a corepressor.

nodes.label: Repressor and operator
nodes.summary: A repressor protein binds the operator to block RNA polymerase in negative control.

nodes.label: The Genetic Code
nodes.summary: The genetic code maps nucleotide triplets to amino acids during translation.

nodes.label: Codons and Reading Frame
nodes.summary: mRNA is read in nonoverlapping triplet codons within a defined reading frame.

nodes.label: Codon (triplet)
nodes.summary: A codon is a sequence of three mRNA nucleotides specifying one amino acid or a stop signal.

nodes.label: Reading the codon table
nodes.summary: Given an mRNA sequence and a codon table, one can translate codons into the corresponding amino acid sequence.

nodes.label: Reading frame
nodes.summary: The reading frame is the way codons are grouped, set by the start codon and shifted by frameshift mutations.

nodes.label: Start codon (AUG / methionine)
nodes.summary: AUG is the start codon coding for methionine and setting the reading frame.

nodes.label: Stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA)
nodes.summary: UAA, UAG, and UGA are stop codons that terminate translation and code for no amino acid.

nodes.label: 64 codons for 20 amino acids
nodes.summary: Four bases in triplets yield 64 codons encoding 20 amino acids plus stop signals.

nodes.label: Properties of the Code
nodes.summary: The genetic code is degenerate, unambiguous, nearly universal, and commaless.

nodes.label: Commaless and nonoverlapping
nodes.summary: Codons are read continuously without gaps and do not overlap.

nodes.label: Degeneracy (redundancy)
nodes.summary: The code is degenerate because multiple codons can specify the same amino acid.

nodes.label: Degeneracy buffers mutations
nodes.summary: Degeneracy, especially at the wobble position, makes many third-base mutations silent.

nodes.label: Unambiguous (specificity)
nodes.summary: The code is unambiguous because each codon specifies only one amino acid.

nodes.label: Near-universality
nodes.summary: The genetic code is nearly universal across organisms, with rare exceptions in mitochondria.

nodes.label: Wobble hypothesis
nodes.summary: Wobble at the third codon position allows one tRNA anticodon to recognize multiple codons.

nodes.label: Nucleic Acid Structure and Function
nodes.summary: DNA and RNA are polymers of nucleotides that store and transmit genetic information.

nodes.label: DNA vs RNA Comparison
nodes.summary: DNA and RNA differ in sugar, bases, strandedness, and biological role.

nodes.label: Major RNA types (mRNA, tRNA, rRNA)
nodes.summary: mRNA carries the coding message, tRNA delivers amino acids, and rRNA forms the catalytic core of the ribosome.

nodes.label: Relative chemical stability
nodes.summary: DNA is more stable than RNA because the absent 2'-OH prevents self-cleavage of the backbone.

nodes.label: Double-stranded DNA vs single-stranded RNA
nodes.summary: DNA is typically double-stranded whereas RNA is usually single-stranded and can fold into secondary structures.

nodes.label: DNA Double Helix Structure
nodes.summary: DNA is a right-handed antiparallel double helix held together by complementary base pairing.

nodes.label: Antiparallel strand orientation
nodes.summary: The two DNA strands run in opposite 5'→3' directions, one antiparallel to the other.

nodes.label: B-DNA (physiological form)
nodes.summary: B-DNA is the right-handed hydrated form of DNA found under physiological conditions.

nodes.label: Base-stacking interactions
nodes.summary: Hydrophobic van der Waals stacking of adjacent bases contributes substantially to double-helix stability alongside hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Chargaff's rules
nodes.summary: In double-stranded DNA, %A = %T and %G = %C, so purines equal pyrimidines.

nodes.label: Complementary base pairing (A-T, G-C)
nodes.summary: Adenine pairs with thymine via two hydrogen bonds and guanine pairs with cytosine via three hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Denaturation and melting temperature (Tm)
nodes.summary: Heat or extreme pH denatures DNA by breaking hydrogen bonds; the melting temperature is where half the DNA is single-stranded.

nodes.label: G-C content and helix stability
nodes.summary: Higher G-C content increases melting temperature because G-C pairs share three hydrogen bonds versus two for A-T.

nodes.label: UV absorbance and hyperchromic effect (A260)
nodes.summary: Nucleic acids absorb UV light at 260 nm, and absorbance rises (hyperchromic effect) as base stacking is lost upon denaturation.

nodes.label: Major and minor grooves
nodes.summary: The helix has major and minor grooves where proteins read the bases without unwinding the DNA.

nodes.label: Reannealing / hybridization
nodes.summary: Complementary single strands can reanneal (hybridize) when conditions favor base pairing, the basis of many lab techniques.

nodes.label: Watson-Crick double helix model
nodes.summary: Watson and Crick described DNA as two antiparallel strands wound into a right-handed double helix.

nodes.label: Nucleotides and Bases
nodes.summary: Nucleotides are the monomeric building blocks of nucleic acids, each composed of a sugar, phosphate, and nitrogenous base.

nodes.label: Nucleotides as energy carriers
nodes.summary: Nucleotide triphosphates such as ATP and GTP serve as cellular energy currency beyond their role in nucleic acids.

nodes.label: Negatively charged phosphate backbone
nodes.summary: The phosphate groups carry negative charges, making the nucleic acid backbone polyanionic and hydrophilic.

nodes.label: Nucleotide vs nucleoside
nodes.summary: A nucleoside is a base plus sugar; a nucleotide is a nucleoside plus one or more phosphate groups.

nodes.label: Deoxyribose vs ribose
nodes.summary: DNA's sugar deoxyribose lacks the 2'-OH present on RNA's ribose, making DNA more chemically stable.

nodes.label: 5' and 3' directionality
nodes.summary: Nucleic acid strands have a 5' phosphate end and a 3' hydroxyl end, defining directionality read 5'→3'.

nodes.label: dNTPs as polymerization substrates
nodes.summary: Deoxynucleoside triphosphates are the substrates for DNA synthesis; cleavage of pyrophosphate drives chain elongation.

nodes.label: N-glycosidic bond (base to sugar)
nodes.summary: An N-glycosidic bond joins the nitrogenous base to the 1' carbon of the pentose sugar.

nodes.label: Phosphodiester bond
nodes.summary: Phosphodiester bonds link the 3'-OH of one nucleotide to the 5'-phosphate of the next, forming the sugar-phosphate backbone.

nodes.label: Mnemonic: purines = A,G / pyrimidines = C,T,U
nodes.summary: PURe As Gold (PURines = Adenine, Guanine) distinguishes purines from the pyrimidines C, T, U.

nodes.label: Purines (adenine, guanine)
nodes.summary: Purines (adenine and guanine) are double-ring nitrogenous bases.

nodes.label: Pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil)
nodes.summary: Pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil) are single-ring nitrogenous bases.

nodes.label: Sugar carbon numbering (1' to 5')
nodes.summary: The pentose carbons are numbered 1' to 5'; the base attaches at 1', the phosphate at 5', and the free hydroxyl at 3'.

nodes.label: Thymine (DNA) vs uracil (RNA)
nodes.summary: DNA uses thymine while RNA uses uracil, which is thymine lacking the 5-methyl group.

nodes.label: Genetic Recombination
nodes.summary: DNA can be rearranged through recombination, transposition, and exchange between organisms.

nodes.label: Bacterial conjugation
nodes.summary: Conjugation transfers DNA, often a plasmid, between bacteria through a sex pilus.

nodes.label: Gene duplication and divergence
nodes.summary: Unequal crossing over duplicates genes, and the extra copies can diverge to create gene families and new functions.

nodes.label: Homologous recombination
nodes.summary: Homologous recombination exchanges DNA between similar sequences, as in meiotic crossing over.

nodes.label: Transduction (phage-mediated)
nodes.summary: Transduction transfers bacterial DNA from one cell to another via a bacteriophage.

nodes.label: Bacterial transformation
nodes.summary: Transformation is the uptake of naked DNA from the environment by a competent bacterium.

nodes.label: Transposons (jumping genes)
nodes.summary: Transposons are mobile DNA elements that move within or between genomes, sometimes disrupting genes.

nodes.label: DNA Repair and Mutation
nodes.summary: Cells detect and repair DNA damage; uncorrected damage produces mutations.

nodes.label: Mutagens and Carcinogens
nodes.summary: Chemical and physical agents damage DNA and increase mutation rate.

nodes.label: Base analogs and intercalators
nodes.summary: Base analogs mispair during replication and intercalating agents insert between bases to cause indels.

nodes.label: Mutagens as carcinogens (Ames test)
nodes.summary: Many mutagens are carcinogens, and the Ames test uses bacterial reversion to screen for mutagenicity.

nodes.label: Ionizing radiation
nodes.summary: Ionizing radiation causes double-strand breaks and base damage.

nodes.label: UV radiation and thymine dimers
nodes.summary: UV light induces covalent thymine (pyrimidine) dimers that distort the helix.

nodes.label: Types of Mutations
nodes.summary: Mutations are heritable changes in DNA sequence ranging from single bases to large rearrangements.

nodes.label: Frameshift (insertion/deletion)
nodes.summary: Insertions or deletions not in multiples of three shift the reading frame downstream.

nodes.label: Germline vs somatic mutation
nodes.summary: Germline mutations are heritable to offspring, whereas somatic mutations affect only the individual.

nodes.label: In-frame insertion/deletion
nodes.summary: Indels of multiples of three add or remove whole codons without shifting the reading frame.

nodes.label: Loss-of-function vs gain-of-function
nodes.summary: Loss-of-function mutations reduce or abolish a gene product's activity, whereas gain-of-function mutations confer new or enhanced activity.

nodes.label: Missense mutation
nodes.summary: A missense mutation changes one codon to specify a different amino acid.

nodes.label: Nonsense mutation
nodes.summary: A nonsense mutation converts a sense codon into a premature stop codon, truncating the protein.

nodes.label: Point mutation
nodes.summary: A point mutation is a change in a single base pair.

nodes.label: Silent mutation
nodes.summary: A silent mutation changes a codon but encodes the same amino acid due to degeneracy of the code.

nodes.label: Spontaneous vs induced mutation
nodes.summary: Spontaneous mutations arise from errors and chemistry, while induced mutations result from mutagens.

nodes.label: Base-pair substitution
nodes.summary: A substitution replaces one base pair with another and is the underlying change of silent, missense, and nonsense mutations.

nodes.label: Tautomeric shifts and spontaneous mispairing
nodes.summary: Rare tautomeric (and deamination) shifts of bases cause spontaneous mispairing that can become a fixed mutation after replication.

nodes.label: Transition vs transversion
nodes.summary: A transition swaps purine-for-purine or pyrimidine-for-pyrimidine; a transversion swaps a purine for a pyrimidine or vice versa.

nodes.label: Triplet repeat expansion
nodes.summary: Expansion of repeated trinucleotides (e.g., CAG in Huntington disease) causes diseases that worsen across generations.

nodes.label: DNA Repair Systems
nodes.summary: Distinct pathways correct different forms of DNA damage.

nodes.label: Base excision repair
nodes.summary: Base excision repair removes a single damaged base via a glycosylase, creating an AP site that is repaired.

nodes.label: Double-strand break repair (NHEJ, HR)
nodes.summary: Double-strand breaks are repaired by error-prone non-homologous end joining or accurate homologous recombination.

nodes.label: Mismatch repair
nodes.summary: Mismatch repair corrects base-pairing errors that escaped proofreading after replication.

nodes.label: Nucleotide excision repair
nodes.summary: Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky lesions such as thymine dimers by excising a short oligonucleotide.

nodes.label: Xeroderma pigmentosum
nodes.summary: Xeroderma pigmentosum results from defective nucleotide excision repair, causing extreme UV sensitivity and skin cancer.

nodes.label: DNA Replication
nodes.summary: DNA replication semiconservatively copies the genome before cell division.

nodes.label: Replication Enzymes and Proteins
nodes.summary: A coordinated set of enzymes unwinds, primes, synthesizes, and seals DNA.

nodes.label: DNA ligase
nodes.summary: DNA ligase seals the phosphodiester backbone between Okazaki fragments.

nodes.label: DNA polymerase I (primer removal)
nodes.summary: DNA polymerase I removes RNA primers with 5'→3' exonuclease activity and fills the gaps with DNA.

nodes.label: DNA polymerase III (prokaryotic elongation)
nodes.summary: DNA polymerase III is the main replicative enzyme in prokaryotes, extending primers 5'→3'.

nodes.label: Eukaryotic polymerases (alpha, delta, epsilon)
nodes.summary: Eukaryotic DNA polymerases alpha (primer), delta (lagging), and epsilon (leading) carry out replication.

nodes.label: Helicase
nodes.summary: Helicase unwinds the parental double helix at the replication fork.

nodes.label: Primase (RNA primer)
nodes.summary: Primase synthesizes a short RNA primer providing a 3'-OH for DNA polymerase to extend.

nodes.label: Replisome (coordinated complex)
nodes.summary: The replisome is the coordinated assembly of helicase, primase, polymerases, and accessory proteins acting together at the fork.

nodes.label: Sliding clamp / processivity
nodes.summary: The sliding clamp tethers polymerase to DNA to increase processivity.

nodes.label: Single-strand binding proteins (SSBs)
nodes.summary: Single-strand binding proteins coat separated strands to prevent reannealing and degradation.

nodes.label: Topoisomerase / DNA gyrase
nodes.summary: Topoisomerases relieve supercoiling ahead of the fork by cutting and resealing DNA; bacterial gyrase introduces negative supercoils.

nodes.label: Fidelity, Telomerase, and Drugs
nodes.summary: Proofreading, telomere maintenance, and replication-targeting drugs ensure or exploit replication accuracy.

nodes.label: Replication-targeting agents
nodes.summary: Nucleoside analogs and topoisomerase inhibitors block replication and are used as antivirals and chemotherapeutics.

nodes.label: End-replication problem
nodes.summary: Linear chromosome ends shorten each round because the lagging strand cannot be fully primed at the 3' terminus.

nodes.label: Replication fidelity and error rate
nodes.summary: Base selection, proofreading, and mismatch repair together give DNA replication an extremely low error rate.

nodes.label: Proofreading (3'→5' exonuclease)
nodes.summary: DNA polymerase proofreads with 3'→5' exonuclease activity to remove misincorporated nucleotides.

nodes.label: Telomerase
nodes.summary: Telomerase is a reverse transcriptase carrying its own RNA template that extends telomeres in germ and stem cells.

nodes.label: Telomerase, aging, and cancer
nodes.summary: Telomere shortening limits somatic divisions, while reactivated telomerase contributes to cancer cell immortality.

nodes.label: Mechanism and Mode
nodes.summary: Replication is semiconservative, bidirectional, and template-directed.

nodes.label: Synthesis occurs 5'→3'
nodes.summary: DNA polymerases add nucleotides only to the free 3'-OH, so new strands grow 5'→3'.

nodes.label: Bidirectional replication / replication bubble
nodes.summary: Replication proceeds in both directions from an origin, forming a replication bubble with two forks.

nodes.label: Lagging strand and Okazaki fragments
nodes.summary: The lagging strand is synthesized discontinuously as Okazaki fragments away from the fork.

nodes.label: Leading strand
nodes.summary: The leading strand is synthesized continuously toward the replication fork.

nodes.label: Meselson-Stahl experiment
nodes.summary: Density-gradient experiments with 15N/14N proved DNA replication is semiconservative.

nodes.label: Origin of replication
nodes.summary: Replication initiates at specific origins; eukaryotes have many origins per chromosome, prokaryotes one.

nodes.label: Replication fork
nodes.summary: The replication fork is the Y-shaped region where the parental duplex unwinds and synthesis occurs.

nodes.label: Semiconservative replication
nodes.summary: Each daughter duplex retains one parental strand and one newly synthesized strand (Meselson-Stahl).

nodes.label: Semidiscontinuous synthesis
nodes.summary: Because both strands grow 5'→3', one is continuous and one discontinuous, making replication semidiscontinuous.

nodes.label: Template-directed synthesis
nodes.summary: Each parental strand serves as a template, and base-pairing rules dictate the sequence of the new complementary strand.

nodes.label: Transcription
nodes.summary: Transcription synthesizes RNA from a DNA template by RNA polymerase.

nodes.label: Central Dogma
nodes.summary: Genetic information flows DNA → RNA → protein, with reverse transcription as an exception.

nodes.label: DNA → RNA → protein
nodes.summary: The central dogma states genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to protein.

nodes.label: Reverse transcription exception
nodes.summary: Retroviruses reverse the flow by using reverse transcriptase to make DNA from an RNA template.

nodes.label: Transcription Machinery
nodes.summary: RNA polymerase and DNA promoter elements direct RNA synthesis.

nodes.label: RNA Pol I, II, III
nodes.summary: RNA Pol I makes rRNA, Pol II makes mRNA (and some snRNA), and Pol III makes tRNA and 5S rRNA.

nodes.label: No primer required
nodes.summary: Unlike DNA polymerase, RNA polymerase can initiate synthesis de novo without a primer.

nodes.label: Polycistronic vs monocistronic mRNA
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic operons make polycistronic mRNA encoding several proteins, while eukaryotic mRNA is typically monocistronic.

nodes.label: Promoter and TATA box
nodes.summary: Promoters such as the TATA box are upstream sequences where transcription machinery assembles.

nodes.label: RNA polymerase
nodes.summary: RNA polymerase synthesizes RNA 5'→3' from a DNA template without needing a primer.

nodes.label: Sigma factor (prokaryotic promoter recognition)
nodes.summary: In prokaryotes the sigma subunit directs core RNA polymerase to recognize promoter consensus sequences (e.g., -10 and -35 boxes).

nodes.label: Template vs coding strand
nodes.summary: RNA is copied from the template (antisense) strand and matches the coding (sense) strand except U replaces T.

nodes.label: General transcription factors
nodes.summary: General transcription factors bind the promoter and recruit RNA polymerase II in eukaryotes.

nodes.label: Upstream / downstream and +1 start site
nodes.summary: Position is numbered relative to the +1 transcription start site, with promoter elements upstream (negative numbers) of the gene.

nodes.label: Eukaryotic RNA Processing
nodes.summary: The primary transcript (hnRNA) is processed into mature mRNA before export.

nodes.label: 5' 7-methylguanosine cap
nodes.summary: A 7-methylguanosine cap is added to the 5' end to protect mRNA and aid ribosome binding.

nodes.label: Alternative splicing
nodes.summary: Alternative splicing joins different exon combinations so one gene can encode multiple protein isoforms.

nodes.label: hnRNA / pre-mRNA primary transcript
nodes.summary: The initial unprocessed transcript is heterogeneous nuclear RNA (pre-mRNA) requiring modification.

nodes.label: Introns and exons
nodes.summary: Introns are non-coding intervening sequences removed from pre-mRNA, while exons are retained and expressed.

nodes.label: Nuclear export of mature mRNA
nodes.summary: Only fully processed mRNA is exported from the nucleus through nuclear pores for translation in the cytoplasm.

nodes.label: 3' poly-A tail
nodes.summary: A poly-A tail of adenines is added to the 3' end to stabilize mRNA and assist export and translation.

nodes.label: Self-splicing introns (ribozymes)
nodes.summary: Some introns are catalytic RNA (ribozymes) that splice themselves without protein enzymes.

nodes.label: snRNPs and lariat formation
nodes.summary: snRNPs recognize splice sites and excise introns as a lariat structure.

nodes.label: Splicing and the spliceosome
nodes.summary: The spliceosome, built from snRNPs, excises introns and ligates exons.

nodes.label: 5' and 3' untranslated regions (UTRs)
nodes.summary: Mature mRNA has noncoding 5' and 3' UTRs flanking the coding sequence that regulate translation and stability.

nodes.label: Stages of Transcription
nodes.summary: Transcription proceeds through initiation, elongation, and termination.

nodes.label: Coupled transcription-translation (prokaryotes)
nodes.summary: Lacking a nucleus, prokaryotes can translate an mRNA while it is still being transcribed.

nodes.label: Elongation
nodes.summary: During elongation RNA polymerase adds ribonucleotides complementary to the template, growing the transcript 5'→3'.

nodes.label: Initiation
nodes.summary: Initiation begins when RNA polymerase binds the promoter and unwinds DNA to start synthesis.

nodes.label: Termination
nodes.summary: Termination ends transcription via rho-dependent or rho-independent (hairpin) mechanisms in prokaryotes.

nodes.label: Transcription bubble
nodes.summary: RNA polymerase locally unwinds DNA into a transcription bubble, transcribing the exposed template before the DNA rewinds behind it.

nodes.label: Translation
nodes.summary: Translation synthesizes a polypeptide from an mRNA template on the ribosome.

nodes.label: Translation Components
nodes.summary: Ribosomes, tRNA, and mRNA cooperate to build proteins.

nodes.label: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase (tRNA charging)
nodes.summary: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases charge each tRNA with its correct amino acid using ATP.

nodes.label: Anticodon
nodes.summary: The anticodon is the three-base tRNA loop that pairs antiparallel and complementary to the mRNA codon.

nodes.label: Ribosome (large and small subunits)
nodes.summary: The ribosome is a two-subunit rRNA-protein complex that catalyzes peptide bond formation.

nodes.label: A, P, and E sites
nodes.summary: The ribosome has an A site (incoming aminoacyl-tRNA), P site (peptidyl-tRNA), and E site (exit).

nodes.label: 70S prokaryotic vs 80S eukaryotic ribosomes
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic ribosomes are 70S (50S + 30S) while eukaryotic ribosomes are 80S (60S + 40S).

nodes.label: Ribosomal RNA (rRNA)
nodes.summary: rRNA forms the structural and catalytic core of the ribosome and is itself a ribozyme.

nodes.label: Transfer RNA (tRNA)
nodes.summary: tRNA is an adapter that carries a specific amino acid and base-pairs its anticodon to an mRNA codon.

nodes.label: tRNA cloverleaf structure
nodes.summary: tRNA folds into a cloverleaf with an anticodon loop and a 3' CCA acceptor end that carries the amino acid.

nodes.label: Post-Translational Modification
nodes.summary: Newly made proteins are folded, modified, targeted, and sometimes cleaved.

nodes.label: Proteolytic cleavage
nodes.summary: Proteolytic cleavage activates proteins by removing segments, as in converting proinsulin to insulin.

nodes.label: Folding and chaperones
nodes.summary: Chaperone proteins assist nascent polypeptides in folding to their native conformation.

nodes.label: Glycosylation
nodes.summary: Glycosylation adds carbohydrate groups to proteins, often in the ER and Golgi.

nodes.label: Phosphorylation
nodes.summary: Phosphorylation adds phosphate groups to regulate protein activity.

nodes.label: Signal sequence and protein targeting
nodes.summary: An N-terminal signal sequence directs ribosomes to the rough ER for secretory and membrane proteins.

nodes.label: Ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation
nodes.summary: Ubiquitin tags proteins for degradation by the proteasome.

nodes.label: Stages of Translation
nodes.summary: Translation proceeds through initiation, elongation, and termination.

nodes.label: Antibiotics targeting translation
nodes.summary: Many antibiotics selectively inhibit the 70S prokaryotic ribosome, exploiting its difference from the 80S eukaryotic ribosome.

nodes.label: Elongation cycle
nodes.summary: Elongation repeatedly binds aminoacyl-tRNA at the A site, forms a peptide bond, and translocates.

nodes.label: Elongation factors (EF-Tu, EF-G) and GTP
nodes.summary: GTP-dependent elongation factors deliver aminoacyl-tRNA to the A site and drive translocation along the mRNA.

nodes.label: Energy cost of translation (GTP)
nodes.summary: Translation consumes GTP during initiation, elongation, and termination plus ATP during tRNA charging.

nodes.label: Initiation
nodes.summary: Initiation assembles the small subunit, initiator tRNA, and start codon, then recruits the large subunit.

nodes.label: Initiator tRNA (fMet vs Met)
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes initiate with N-formylmethionine while eukaryotes initiate with methionine.

nodes.label: N-terminus to C-terminus synthesis
nodes.summary: Polypeptides are synthesized from the N-terminus to the C-terminus.

nodes.label: Peptidyl transferase / peptide bond
nodes.summary: Peptidyl transferase, an rRNA ribozyme, catalyzes peptide bond formation between adjacent amino acids.

nodes.label: Polyribosome (polysome)
nodes.summary: Multiple ribosomes can translate a single mRNA simultaneously as a polysome.

nodes.label: Shine-Dalgarno / Kozak sequences
nodes.summary: The prokaryotic Shine-Dalgarno sequence and eukaryotic Kozak sequence position the ribosome at the start codon.

nodes.label: Termination and release factors
nodes.summary: Release factors recognize stop codons and trigger release of the completed polypeptide.

nodes.label: Translocation
nodes.summary: Translocation moves the ribosome one codon along the mRNA, shifting tRNAs from A to P to E sites.

nodes.label: Viral Genetics and Reverse Transcription
nodes.summary: Viruses replicate genetic information using host machinery and special enzymes.

nodes.label: Viral genome types
nodes.summary: Viral genomes can be DNA or RNA, single- or double-stranded, and positive- or negative-sense.

nodes.label: Lytic vs lysogenic cycles
nodes.summary: The lytic cycle destroys the host immediately, while the lysogenic cycle integrates viral DNA as a provirus/prophage.

nodes.label: Retroviruses
nodes.summary: Retroviruses carry RNA genomes and use reverse transcriptase to integrate DNA into the host genome.

nodes.label: Reverse transcriptase
nodes.summary: Reverse transcriptase synthesizes DNA from an RNA template, reversing the central dogma.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Basis & Mutations
nodes.summary: The chromosome theory of inheritance and chromosomal/genetic mutations underlie transmission and variation.

nodes.label: Aneuploidy & Nondisjunction
nodes.summary: Errors in chromosome separation produce abnormal chromosome numbers that disrupt inheritance.

nodes.label: Autosomal Aneuploidy Syndromes
nodes.summary: Trisomy 21 (Down), trisomy 18 (Edwards), and trisomy 13 (Patau) are viable autosomal trisomies.

nodes.label: Euploidy vs Aneuploidy
nodes.summary: Euploidy is a complete chromosome-set multiple; aneuploidy is the gain or loss of individual chromosomes.

nodes.label: Monosomy & Trisomy
nodes.summary: Aneuploidy yields monosomy (2n-1) or trisomy (2n+1), as in trisomy 21 (Down syndrome).

nodes.label: Nondisjunction
nodes.summary: Failure of chromosomes/chromatids to separate in meiosis produces gametes with abnormal chromosome numbers.

nodes.label: Polyploidy
nodes.summary: Polyploidy is having more than two complete chromosome sets, common in plants and lethal in most animals.

nodes.label: Sex-Chromosome Aneuploidies
nodes.summary: Turner syndrome (XO monosomy) and Klinefelter syndrome (XXY trisomy) result from sex-chromosome nondisjunction.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance
nodes.summary: Genes reside on chromosomes whose meiotic behavior explains Mendel's laws.

nodes.label: Karyotype
nodes.summary: A karyotype is the organized display of an individual's full chromosome set used to detect abnormalities.

nodes.label: Chromosome Theory Statement
nodes.summary: Sutton and Boveri proposed that Mendelian factors are carried on chromosomes, linking genes to chromosome behavior.

nodes.label: Gene Mutations as a Variation Source
nodes.summary: Heritable DNA sequence changes are the ultimate source of new alleles and genetic variation.

nodes.label: Frameshift (Insertion/Deletion)
nodes.summary: Insertions or deletions not in multiples of three shift the reading frame downstream.

nodes.label: Germline vs Somatic Mutation
nodes.summary: Only germline mutations are heritable; somatic mutations affect the individual but are not passed on.

nodes.label: Mutagens
nodes.summary: Chemical and radiation mutagens increase mutation rate and thus heritable variation.

nodes.label: Mutation as the Origin of New Alleles
nodes.summary: Mutation is the ultimate source of all new alleles and thus all heritable genetic variation in a population.

nodes.label: Point Mutation
nodes.summary: A point mutation changes a single nucleotide and may be silent, missense, or nonsense.

nodes.label: Spontaneous vs Induced Mutation
nodes.summary: Spontaneous mutations arise from replication errors/tautomeric shifts; induced mutations are caused by mutagens.

nodes.label: Silent, Missense & Nonsense Mutations
nodes.summary: A substitution is silent (no amino-acid change), missense (different amino acid), or nonsense (premature stop codon).

nodes.label: Transitions vs Transversions
nodes.summary: A transition swaps a purine for a purine (or pyrimidine for pyrimidine); a transversion swaps a purine for a pyrimidine.

nodes.label: Chromosomal Structural Aberrations
nodes.summary: Breakage and rejoining of chromosomes cause deletions, duplications, inversions, and translocations.

nodes.label: Deletion
nodes.summary: A deletion removes a chromosomal segment, losing the genes it contained.

nodes.label: Duplication
nodes.summary: A duplication repeats a chromosomal segment, increasing gene copy number.

nodes.label: Inversion
nodes.summary: An inversion reverses the orientation of a chromosomal segment.

nodes.label: Robertsonian Translocation
nodes.summary: Fusion of two acrocentric chromosomes at the centromere can cause familial Down syndrome.

nodes.label: Translocation
nodes.summary: A translocation moves a chromosomal segment to a nonhomologous chromosome.

nodes.label: Extranuclear & Epigenetic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Some inheritance bypasses nuclear Mendelian rules via organelle DNA, imprinting, and chromatin modification.

nodes.label: Cytoplasmic (Mitochondrial) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Organelle DNA is inherited from the cytoplasm, typically maternally, outside Mendelian segregation.

nodes.label: Heteroplasmy
nodes.summary: Cells may carry a mixture of normal and mutant mitochondrial DNA, so disease severity depends on the mutant proportion.

nodes.label: Maternal (Mitochondrial) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Mitochondrial DNA is inherited almost exclusively from the mother via the egg cytoplasm.

nodes.label: Organelle (Mitochondrial/Chloroplast) DNA
nodes.summary: Mitochondria and chloroplasts carry their own circular DNA passed through the cytoplasm.

nodes.label: Epigenetic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Heritable changes in gene expression without DNA sequence change, via methylation and chromatin marks.

nodes.label: Epigenetics Defined
nodes.summary: Epigenetics is heritable change in gene expression that does not alter the underlying DNA sequence.

nodes.label: DNA Methylation
nodes.summary: Heritable methylation of cytosines silences genes without altering the underlying DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Histone Modification
nodes.summary: Heritable acetylation/methylation of histones alters chromatin accessibility and gene expression.

nodes.label: Genomic Imprinting
nodes.summary: Some genes are expressed only from the maternal or only the paternal allele due to parent-of-origin marks.

nodes.label: Non-Mendelian & Complex Inheritance
nodes.summary: Many traits deviate from simple dominance, including codominance, linkage, sex linkage, and polygenic inheritance.

nodes.label: Variations on Dominance
nodes.summary: Incomplete dominance, codominance, and multiple alleles modify simple dominant/recessive expression.

nodes.label: ABO Blood Group System
nodes.summary: The ABO system combines multiple alleles (IA, IB, i) with codominance of IA/IB and recessive i.

nodes.label: Codominance
nodes.summary: Both alleles are fully and simultaneously expressed in the heterozygote, as in AB blood type.

nodes.label: Incomplete Dominance
nodes.summary: Heterozygotes show an intermediate (blended) phenotype, as in pink snapdragons.

nodes.label: Lethal Alleles
nodes.summary: Alleles lethal when homozygous distort expected ratios (e.g., a 3:1 becomes 2:1 among survivors).

nodes.label: Multiple Alleles
nodes.summary: A gene may have more than two alleles in a population, though an individual carries only two.

nodes.label: Penetrance & Expressivity
nodes.summary: Penetrance is the fraction of genotypes showing the phenotype; expressivity is the degree of expression.

nodes.label: Pleiotropy
nodes.summary: A single gene affects multiple phenotypic traits, as in sickle cell disease.

nodes.label: Rh Blood Group
nodes.summary: The Rh (D) antigen is inherited as a simple dominant; Rh+ x Rh- crosses underlie hemolytic disease of the newborn.

nodes.label: Environmental Influence on Phenotype
nodes.summary: The environment can modify gene expression and phenotype (gene-environment interaction).

nodes.label: Gene Interactions
nodes.summary: Epistasis and polygenic inheritance involve multiple genes shaping a phenotype.

nodes.label: Bombay Phenotype (Recessive Epistasis)
nodes.summary: Homozygous recessive at the H locus prevents ABO antigen display, masking the ABO genotype as type O.

nodes.label: Continuous (Quantitative) Variation
nodes.summary: Polygenic and environmental effects produce a bell-shaped distribution of phenotypes like height.

nodes.label: Epistasis
nodes.summary: One gene masks or modifies the phenotypic expression of another gene at a different locus.

nodes.label: Modified Dihybrid Ratios
nodes.summary: Epistasis converts the 9:3:3:1 dihybrid ratio into variants such as 9:3:4, 12:3:1, or 9:7.

nodes.label: Polygenic Inheritance
nodes.summary: Many genes contribute additively to a single quantitative trait, producing continuous variation.

nodes.label: Genetic Linkage & Mapping
nodes.summary: Genes near each other on a chromosome are inherited together, deviating from independent assortment.

nodes.label: 50% Recombination Ceiling
nodes.summary: Genes far apart or on different chromosomes show a maximum ~50 percent recombination, appearing unlinked.

nodes.label: Building Linkage Maps
nodes.summary: Recombination frequencies between gene pairs are summed to order genes and estimate distances on a chromosome.

nodes.label: Linked Genes
nodes.summary: Genes on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together and violate independent assortment.

nodes.label: Map Units (Centimorgans)
nodes.summary: 1 map unit (centimorgan) equals a 1 percent recombination frequency, used to build genetic maps.

nodes.label: Parental vs Recombinant Types
nodes.summary: Linked-gene crosses yield abundant parental-type offspring and fewer recombinant types produced by crossing over.

nodes.label: Recombination Frequency
nodes.summary: Recombination frequency = recombinant offspring / total offspring, increasing with distance between genes.

nodes.label: Sex Determination & Sex Linkage
nodes.summary: Sex chromosomes determine sex and carry sex-linked genes with characteristic inheritance patterns.

nodes.label: Autosomes vs Sex Chromosomes
nodes.summary: 22 autosome pairs carry non-sex genes while the 23rd pair (sex chromosomes) determines sex.

nodes.label: Criss-Cross Inheritance
nodes.summary: X-linked recessive traits pass from affected fathers to carrier daughters to affected grandsons.

nodes.label: Mosaicism (Calico Cats)
nodes.summary: Random X-inactivation in heterozygous females yields patches of differing gene expression, as in calico cat coat color.

nodes.label: X-Inactivation (Barr Body)
nodes.summary: Dosage compensation randomly inactivates one X in females, forming a Barr body and causing mosaicism.

nodes.label: X-Linked Inheritance
nodes.summary: X-linked recessive traits appear more often in males who are hemizygous for the X chromosome.

nodes.label: X-Linked Recessive Disease Examples
nodes.summary: Hemophilia, red-green color blindness, and Duchenne muscular dystrophy are classic X-linked recessive conditions.

nodes.label: XX/XY Sex Determination
nodes.summary: In humans, XX is female and XY is male, with the SRY gene on Y triggering male development.

nodes.label: Y-Linked (Holandric) Inheritance
nodes.summary: Y-linked genes pass strictly from father to all sons.

nodes.label: Sex-Linked vs Sex-Influenced/Limited
nodes.summary: Traits can be sex-linked (on sex chromosomes) or sex-influenced/sex-limited (autosomal but sex-dependent expression).

nodes.label: Sex-Influenced Traits
nodes.summary: Autosomal traits expressed differently in the two sexes due to hormones, as in pattern baldness.

nodes.label: Sex-Limited Traits
nodes.summary: Autosomal genes expressed in only one sex, such as milk production in females.

nodes.label: Meiosis & Gametogenesis
nodes.summary: Meiosis halves chromosome number from diploid to haploid, producing genetically unique gametes via two divisions.

nodes.label: Gametogenesis
nodes.summary: Gametogenesis is the production of gametes via meiosis, differing in males (spermatogenesis) and females (oogenesis).

nodes.label: Fertilization Restores Diploidy
nodes.summary: Fusion of two haploid gametes at fertilization restores the diploid (2n) chromosome number.

nodes.label: Meiotic Arrest in Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Primary oocytes arrest in prophase I until ovulation; secondary oocytes arrest in metaphase II until fertilization.

nodes.label: Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Oogenesis produces one functional egg plus polar bodies, with arrests at prophase I and metaphase II.

nodes.label: Polar Bodies & Unequal Cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Unequal cytoplasmic division in oogenesis yields one large ovum and small nonfunctional polar bodies.

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis
nodes.summary: Spermatogenesis produces four functional haploid sperm per primary spermatocyte, continuously from puberty.

nodes.label: Meiosis I Stages
nodes.summary: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes through prophase I, metaphase I, anaphase I, and telophase I.

nodes.label: Anaphase I & Disjunction
nodes.summary: Homologous chromosomes separate (disjunction) to opposite poles while sister chromatids stay together.

nodes.label: Metaphase I
nodes.summary: Tetrads align at the metaphase plate, with homologs attached to spindle fibers from opposite poles.

nodes.label: Prophase I
nodes.summary: In prophase I homologs synapse to form tetrads and crossing over occurs at chiasmata.

nodes.label: Synapsis & Synaptonemal Complex
nodes.summary: Synapsis is the pairing of homologs, held together by the protein synaptonemal complex.
