nodes.label: Telophase I & Cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Telophase I yields two haploid cells, each with chromosomes still composed of two sister chromatids.

nodes.label: Meiosis II Stages
nodes.summary: Meiosis II is a mitosis-like division that separates sister chromatids without prior DNA replication.

nodes.label: Anaphase II
nodes.summary: Sister chromatids separate to opposite poles in each cell.

nodes.label: Interkinesis (No S-phase)
nodes.summary: Interkinesis is the brief interval between meiosis I and II during which no DNA replication occurs.

nodes.label: Metaphase II
nodes.summary: Individual chromosomes align singly at the metaphase plate, like in mitotic metaphase.

nodes.label: Prophase II
nodes.summary: Chromosomes recondense and a new spindle forms in each haploid cell; no crossing over occurs.

nodes.label: Telophase II
nodes.summary: Four genetically distinct haploid daughter cells are produced.

nodes.label: Meiosis Overview & Comparison to Mitosis
nodes.summary: Meiosis is a reductional division (2n to n) with one round of DNA replication followed by two divisions.

nodes.label: Tetrad (Bivalent)
nodes.summary: A tetrad is a paired set of two homologous chromosomes (four chromatids) formed during synapsis in prophase I.

nodes.label: Centromere & Kinetochore in Meiosis
nodes.summary: In meiosis I, sister kinetochores attach to the same pole (co-orientation) so homologs separate; in meiosis II they attach to opposite poles like mitosis.

nodes.label: Diploid (2n) vs Haploid (n)
nodes.summary: Diploid cells carry two sets of chromosomes (2n); haploid gametes carry one set (n); humans are 2n=46, n=23.

nodes.label: Meiosis vs Mitosis Genetic Outcome
nodes.summary: Mitosis yields two identical diploid cells; meiosis yields four genetically diverse haploid cells.

nodes.label: Four Haploid Daughter Cells
nodes.summary: Meiosis produces four genetically distinct haploid cells from one diploid parent.

nodes.label: Homologous Chromosomes
nodes.summary: Homologs are paired chromosomes of the same length/centromere position carrying the same genes at the same loci, one maternal and one paternal.

nodes.label: Single S-phase, Two Divisions
nodes.summary: DNA replicates once before meiosis, then two consecutive divisions yield four haploid cells.

nodes.label: Reductional vs Equational Division
nodes.summary: Meiosis I is reductional (separates homologs, 2n to n); Meiosis II is equational (separates sister chromatids).

nodes.label: Purpose of Meiosis
nodes.summary: Meiosis produces haploid gametes for sexual reproduction and generates genetic variation, maintaining constant chromosome number across generations.

nodes.label: Sister Chromatids
nodes.summary: Sister chromatids are identical copies of one chromosome joined at the centromere after replication.

nodes.label: Mendelian Genetics
nodes.summary: Mendel's laws describe how single genes with dominant and recessive alleles are transmitted across generations.

nodes.label: Genetic Crosses & Punnett Squares
nodes.summary: Punnett squares predict genotypic and phenotypic ratios of offspring from parental crosses.

nodes.label: Backcross
nodes.summary: A backcross mates an offspring with one of its parents (or a genotype identical to a parent).

nodes.label: Dihybrid Cross & 9:3:3:1 Ratio
nodes.summary: A dihybrid cross of two heterozygotes (AaBb x AaBb) yields a 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio.

nodes.label: Monohybrid Cross & 3:1 Ratio
nodes.summary: A heterozygous monohybrid cross (Aa x Aa) yields a 3:1 dominant-to-recessive phenotypic ratio and 1:2:1 genotype ratio.

nodes.label: P, F1, F2 Generations
nodes.summary: The parental (P), first filial (F1), and second filial (F2) generations track inheritance across crosses.

nodes.label: Punnett Square Method
nodes.summary: A Punnett square arrays parental gamete genotypes to enumerate all possible offspring genotype combinations and their probabilities.

nodes.label: Reciprocal Cross
nodes.summary: Reciprocal crosses swap the sexes of the parental phenotypes to detect sex-linked or cytoplasmic inheritance.

nodes.label: Test Cross
nodes.summary: Crossing an unknown dominant phenotype with a homozygous recessive reveals the unknown genotype.

nodes.label: Mendel's Experimental Approach
nodes.summary: Mendel's pea-plant crosses established particulate (not blending) inheritance and the gene concept.

nodes.label: Particulate vs Blending Inheritance
nodes.summary: Traits are passed as discrete heritable units (genes), not blended, so recessive traits can reappear in later generations.

nodes.label: Mendel's Pea-Plant Crosses
nodes.summary: Mendel used controlled crosses of true-breeding pea plants with discrete traits to infer the rules of inheritance.

nodes.label: True-Breeding (Pure Line)
nodes.summary: A true-breeding line is homozygous for a trait and produces offspring identical to the parent when self-crossed.

nodes.label: Mendel's Laws
nodes.summary: Mendel derived the laws of segregation and independent assortment plus the concept of dominance.

nodes.label: Law (Concept) of Dominance
nodes.summary: In a heterozygote, the dominant allele masks the recessive allele in the phenotype.

nodes.label: Law of Independent Assortment
nodes.summary: Alleles of different genes assort independently into gametes when genes are on different chromosomes.

nodes.label: Law of Segregation
nodes.summary: Each individual carries two alleles per gene that separate during gamete formation so each gamete gets one.

nodes.label: Probability in Genetics
nodes.summary: The rules of multiplication and addition compute combined inheritance probabilities.

nodes.label: Probability Method for Multi-Gene Crosses
nodes.summary: Solve complex multi-gene crosses by treating each gene independently and multiplying single-gene probabilities instead of a large Punnett square.

nodes.label: Product Rule (AND)
nodes.summary: The probability of independent events both occurring is the product of their individual probabilities.

nodes.label: Sum Rule (OR)
nodes.summary: The probability of either of two mutually exclusive events is the sum of their probabilities.

nodes.label: Core Genetic Terminology
nodes.summary: Genes, alleles, loci, genotype and phenotype define the language of inheritance.

nodes.label: Allele
nodes.summary: An allele is an alternative version of a gene at a given locus.

nodes.label: Complete Dominance
nodes.summary: In complete dominance the heterozygote phenotype is identical to the homozygous dominant phenotype.

nodes.label: Dominant Allele
nodes.summary: A dominant allele is fully expressed in the phenotype when present in at least one copy.

nodes.label: Gene
nodes.summary: A gene is a unit of heredity occupying a specific locus and encoding a trait.

nodes.label: Genotype
nodes.summary: Genotype is the genetic (allelic) makeup of an organism at a locus.

nodes.label: Hemizygous
nodes.summary: Hemizygous describes having only one allele for a gene, as for X-linked genes in males.

nodes.label: Heterozygous
nodes.summary: Heterozygous means having two different alleles at a locus.

nodes.label: Homozygous
nodes.summary: Homozygous means having two identical alleles at a locus.

nodes.label: Locus
nodes.summary: A locus is the fixed physical position of a gene on a chromosome.

nodes.label: Phenotype
nodes.summary: Phenotype is the observable trait resulting from genotype and environment.

nodes.label: Recessive Allele
nodes.summary: A recessive allele is expressed phenotypically only when homozygous.

nodes.label: Wild-Type vs Mutant
nodes.summary: Wild type is the most common naturally occurring allele/phenotype; mutant alleles arise by mutation.

nodes.label: Pedigree Analysis
nodes.summary: Pedigrees trace inheritance through family trees to deduce trait inheritance patterns and carrier status.

nodes.label: Pedigree Symbols & Conventions
nodes.summary: Standardized symbols depict sex, affected status, mating, and generations in a family tree.

nodes.label: Carrier
nodes.summary: A carrier is a heterozygote who is phenotypically unaffected but can transmit a recessive allele.

nodes.label: Proband & Generation Numbering
nodes.summary: The proband is the index individual through whom a pedigree is ascertained; generations are numbered with Roman numerals.

nodes.label: Pedigree Symbols
nodes.summary: Squares represent males, circles females, shaded shapes affected individuals, and lines matings/offspring.

nodes.label: Identifying Inheritance Patterns
nodes.summary: Pedigree clues distinguish autosomal vs X-linked and dominant vs recessive inheritance.

nodes.label: Autosomal Dominant Pattern
nodes.summary: Trait appears every generation, affects both sexes, and each affected child has an affected parent.

nodes.label: Autosomal Recessive Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected children often have unaffected carrier parents; trait can skip generations and affects sexes equally.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial Pedigree Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected mothers transmit to all children; affected fathers transmit to none.

nodes.label: Male-to-Male Transmission Rule
nodes.summary: Father-to-son transmission rules out X-linked inheritance because sons inherit the father's Y, not his X.

nodes.label: Computing Risk from a Pedigree
nodes.summary: Carrier probabilities and offspring risk are computed by combining genotype inferences with the product and sum rules.

nodes.label: X-Linked Dominant Pattern
nodes.summary: Affected fathers transmit to all daughters but no sons; affects both sexes with female bias.

nodes.label: X-Linked Recessive Pattern
nodes.summary: More affected males than females, no male-to-male transmission, often through carrier mothers.

nodes.label: Population Genetics & Hardy-Weinberg
nodes.summary: Population genetics models allele and genotype frequencies and how they change to drive evolution.

nodes.label: Forces That Change Allele Frequencies
nodes.summary: Mutation, gene flow, genetic drift, nonrandom mating, and selection disrupt Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

nodes.label: Bottleneck Effect
nodes.summary: A drastic population reduction randomly shrinks genetic diversity.

nodes.label: Fitness
nodes.summary: Fitness is the relative reproductive success of a genotype, determining which alleles increase in frequency.

nodes.label: Founder Effect
nodes.summary: A new population started by few individuals carries only a nonrandom subset of the original gene pool.

nodes.label: Gene Flow (Migration)
nodes.summary: Movement of alleles between populations via migration alters allele frequencies and increases diversity.

nodes.label: Genetic Drift
nodes.summary: Random change in allele frequencies, strongest in small populations.

nodes.label: Microevolution & Allele-Frequency Change
nodes.summary: Microevolution is a change in allele frequencies within a population over generations, the basis of evolution.

nodes.label: Mutation as Evolutionary Force
nodes.summary: Mutation introduces new alleles, slowly changing allele frequencies and being the ultimate source of variation.

nodes.label: Natural Selection
nodes.summary: Differential reproductive success based on fitness changes allele frequencies adaptively.

nodes.label: Nonrandom Mating & Inbreeding
nodes.summary: Assortative mating and inbreeding shift genotype frequencies, increasing homozygosity.

nodes.label: Directional, Stabilizing & Disruptive Selection
nodes.summary: Directional selection favors one extreme, stabilizing favors the mean, and disruptive favors both extremes.

nodes.label: Gene Pool & Allele Frequencies
nodes.summary: A gene pool is all alleles in a population; their frequencies define its genetic structure.

nodes.label: Allele & Genotype Frequencies
nodes.summary: Allele frequency is the proportion of a given allele in the gene pool, summing to 1.

nodes.label: Gene Pool
nodes.summary: The gene pool is the total collection of alleles for all genes in a population.

nodes.label: Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
nodes.summary: In an idealized non-evolving population, allele and genotype frequencies stay constant across generations.

nodes.label: Allele Frequency Equation (p + q = 1)
nodes.summary: For two alleles, p + q = 1 where p and q are the dominant and recessive allele frequencies.

nodes.label: Hardy-Weinberg Assumptions
nodes.summary: Equilibrium requires no mutation, no migration, no selection, random mating, and a large population.

nodes.label: Solving H-W Problems
nodes.summary: Given the recessive phenotype frequency (q^2), solve for q, then p, then carrier frequency (2pq).

nodes.label: Genotype Equation (p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1)
nodes.summary: Genotype frequencies follow p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1 for AA, Aa, and aa respectively.

nodes.label: Interpreting the H-W Terms
nodes.summary: p^2 is homozygous dominant frequency, 2pq heterozygous (carrier) frequency, and q^2 homozygous recessive frequency.

nodes.label: Sources of Genetic Variation in Meiosis
nodes.summary: Independent assortment, crossing over, and random fertilization generate enormous genetic diversity.

nodes.label: Crossing Over (Recombination)
nodes.summary: Crossing over exchanges segments between homologs in prophase I, producing recombinant chromosomes.

nodes.label: Chiasmata
nodes.summary: Chiasmata are the visible cross points where homologous nonsister chromatids exchange genetic material.

nodes.label: Crossing Over Expands Diversity Beyond Assortment
nodes.summary: Crossing over creates novel allele combinations on a single chromosome, multiplying diversity beyond the 2^n from independent assortment.

nodes.label: Nonsister Chromatid Exchange
nodes.summary: Genetic exchange occurs between nonsister chromatids of homologous chromosomes, not between sisters.

nodes.label: Recombinant Chromatids
nodes.summary: Crossing over creates chromatids with new combinations of maternal and paternal alleles.

nodes.label: Independent Assortment
nodes.summary: Random orientation of homolog pairs at metaphase I shuffles maternal and paternal chromosomes independently.

nodes.label: 2^n Gamete Combinations
nodes.summary: Independent assortment alone yields 2^n possible gamete chromosome combinations (2^23 in humans).

nodes.label: Random Metaphase I Orientation
nodes.summary: Each tetrad orients independently at metaphase I, so each pole receives a random mix of maternal and paternal homologs.

nodes.label: Random Fertilization
nodes.summary: Random union of any sperm with any egg multiplies the diversity already produced by meiosis.

nodes.label: Zygote Combinations
nodes.summary: Random fertilization combined with independent assortment yields (2^n)^2 chromosome combinations per zygote, vastly more with crossing over.

nodes.label: Bioenergetics & Thermodynamics
nodes.summary: The thermodynamic principles governing energy flow, free energy, and coupled reactions in living systems.

nodes.label: Energy coupling & high-energy compounds
nodes.summary: How unfavorable reactions are driven by coupling to favorable ones, especially ATP hydrolysis.

nodes.label: Adenylate kinase (2 ADP ⇌ ATP + AMP)
nodes.summary: Adenylate kinase interconverts adenine nucleotides, generating AMP as a sensitive signal of low energy charge.

nodes.label: ATP–ADP cycle
nodes.summary: ATP is continuously regenerated from ADP using energy from catabolism, then hydrolyzed to power anabolism.

nodes.label: ATP hydrolysis as energy currency
nodes.summary: ATP → ADP + Pi is highly exergonic (ΔG°' ≈ -7.3 kcal/mol) and powers cellular work.

nodes.label: ATP as intermediate-energy carrier
nodes.summary: ATP sits in the middle of the phosphoryl-transfer-potential scale, letting it accept phosphate from high-potential donors and donate it to low-potential acceptors.

nodes.label: ATP structure
nodes.summary: ATP consists of adenine, ribose, and three phosphate groups linked by two phosphoanhydride bonds.

nodes.label: ATP → AMP + PPi hydrolysis
nodes.summary: Cleaving ATP to AMP + pyrophosphate, then hydrolyzing PPi, makes biosynthetic steps essentially irreversible by costing two high-energy bonds.

nodes.label: Phosphocreatine (creatine phosphate)
nodes.summary: Phosphocreatine is a high-energy reservoir in muscle that rapidly regenerates ATP via creatine kinase.

nodes.label: High-energy phosphate bonds
nodes.summary: Phosphoanhydride bonds release large free energy on hydrolysis due to resonance stabilization, charge repulsion, and solvation.

nodes.label: Nucleoside diphosphokinase (interconversion)
nodes.summary: Nucleoside diphosphokinase transfers phosphate between nucleotides (e.g., GTP + ADP ⇌ GDP + ATP), equilibrating the high-energy phosphate pool.

nodes.label: Other nucleotide triphosphates (GTP, UTP, CTP)
nodes.summary: GTP, UTP, and CTP serve as energy currencies in specific pathways (e.g., GTP in the citric acid cycle and protein synthesis).

nodes.label: Phosphoryl group transfer potential
nodes.summary: Compounds like PEP and 1,3-BPG have higher phosphoryl transfer potential than ATP, enabling substrate-level phosphorylation.

nodes.label: Reaction coupling
nodes.summary: An endergonic reaction is driven forward by coupling to an exergonic one so the net ΔG is negative.

nodes.label: Biological oxidation–reduction
nodes.summary: Electron transfer reactions and the carriers that shuttle reducing equivalents in metabolism.

nodes.label: Free energy from redox (ΔG°' = -nFΔE°')
nodes.summary: ΔG°' = -nFΔE°' relates the free energy of a redox reaction to its electromotive force.

nodes.label: Soluble vs membrane-bound carriers
nodes.summary: NAD+, FAD, and NADP+ are soluble/mobile carriers while ubiquinone and cytochromes operate within membranes.

nodes.label: FAD/FADH2
nodes.summary: FAD accepts two electrons and two protons to form FADH2, a carrier tightly bound to flavoenzymes.

nodes.label: Half-reactions and reduction potential
nodes.summary: Each redox reaction splits into two half-reactions, each with a standard reduction potential (E°').

nodes.label: NAD+/NADH
nodes.summary: NAD+ accepts two electrons and one proton to form NADH, a major carrier for catabolic oxidation.

nodes.label: NADP+/NADPH
nodes.summary: NADPH is the primary reducing agent for biosynthesis (anabolism) and antioxidant defense, distinct from NADH.

nodes.label: Oxidation and reduction in metabolism
nodes.summary: Oxidation is loss of electrons (often as H), reduction is gain; catabolism oxidizes fuels to release energy.

nodes.label: Electrons flow toward higher reduction potential
nodes.summary: A more positive E°' means greater electron affinity, so electrons flow spontaneously from low to high reduction potential, releasing free energy.

nodes.label: Carriers derived from vitamins (niacin, riboflavin)
nodes.summary: NAD+/NADP+ derive from niacin (B3) and FAD/FMN from riboflavin (B2), linking diet to redox capacity.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic principles
nodes.summary: Free energy, enthalpy, entropy, equilibrium, and spontaneity as applied to biochemical reactions.

nodes.label: Actual ΔG vs reactant/product concentrations
nodes.summary: ΔG = ΔG°' + RT ln Q; actual ΔG depends on real cellular concentrations, not just ΔG°'.

nodes.label: Change in free energy (ΔG = ΔH - TΔS)
nodes.summary: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS determines whether a reaction is spontaneous at constant T and P.

nodes.label: Relationship of ΔG°' to Keq
nodes.summary: ΔG°' = -RT ln Keq links the standard free energy change to the equilibrium constant.

nodes.label: Endergonic vs exergonic reactions
nodes.summary: Exergonic reactions release free energy (ΔG<0); endergonic reactions require free energy input (ΔG>0).

nodes.label: Chemical equilibrium and ΔG
nodes.summary: At equilibrium ΔG = 0 and the net rates of forward and reverse reactions are equal.

nodes.label: First law of thermodynamics
nodes.summary: Energy is conserved; the total energy of the universe is constant and can only be transformed.

nodes.label: Gibbs free energy (G)
nodes.summary: G = H - TS; the energy available to do useful work at constant temperature and pressure.

nodes.label: Near-equilibrium vs metabolically irreversible reactions
nodes.summary: Most pathway steps are near-equilibrium and readily reversible, while a few large-negative-ΔG steps are effectively irreversible and serve as control points.

nodes.label: Living cells as open systems at steady state
nodes.summary: Cells maintain local order by exchanging matter and energy with the environment and operating far from equilibrium at steady state.

nodes.label: Second law of thermodynamics
nodes.summary: The entropy of the universe always increases for any spontaneous process.

nodes.label: Spontaneity and the sign of ΔG
nodes.summary: ΔG < 0 (exergonic) is spontaneous, ΔG > 0 (endergonic) is non-spontaneous, ΔG = 0 is at equilibrium.

nodes.label: Standard free energy change (ΔG°')
nodes.summary: ΔG°' is the standard free energy change under biochemical standard conditions (pH 7, 1 M, 25°C).

nodes.label: ΔG as a state function (additivity)
nodes.summary: Free energy is a state function, so the ΔG of sequential reactions sums (Hess's law), the basis of energy coupling.

nodes.label: Thermodynamics vs kinetics
nodes.summary: ΔG predicts whether a reaction can occur, not how fast; kinetics (activation energy) governs rate.

nodes.label: Citric Acid (Krebs/TCA) Cycle
nodes.summary: The mitochondrial cycle that fully oxidizes acetyl-CoA to CO2, generating NADH, FADH2, and GTP.

nodes.label: Anaplerotic & cataplerotic reactions
nodes.summary: Reactions that replenish or draw off cycle intermediates for biosynthesis.

nodes.label: Intermediates as biosynthetic precursors
nodes.summary: Citrate exports for fatty acid synthesis, α-KG and OAA form amino acids, and succinyl-CoA forms heme.

nodes.label: Catalytic (not consumed) role of intermediates
nodes.summary: Cycle intermediates act catalytically and are regenerated, so their levels must be maintained by anaplerosis when drawn off for biosynthesis.

nodes.label: Pyruvate carboxylase (anaplerosis)
nodes.summary: Pyruvate carboxylase replenishes oxaloacetate, an anaplerotic reaction also key to gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Cycle overview
nodes.summary: Location, per-turn yield, and the central hub role of the citric acid cycle.

nodes.label: Carbon bookkeeping (CO2 not from acetyl carbons)
nodes.summary: The two CO2 released in one turn come from oxaloacetate carbons, not the entering acetyl carbons, which leave in later turns.

nodes.label: Central metabolic hub
nodes.summary: The cycle integrates carbohydrate, fat, and protein catabolism and supplies biosynthetic precursors.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial matrix location
nodes.summary: The citric acid cycle operates in the mitochondrial matrix (except succinate dehydrogenase in the inner membrane).

nodes.label: Indirect oxygen dependence of the CAC
nodes.summary: The cycle needs no O2 directly but halts when NADH/FADH2 accumulate because the ETC (which requires O2) cannot regenerate NAD+/FAD.

nodes.label: Per-glucose CAC yield
nodes.summary: Two acetyl-CoA per glucose mean two turns, doubling the per-turn yield.

nodes.label: Per-turn product yield
nodes.summary: Each acetyl-CoA yields 3 NADH, 1 FADH2, 1 GTP, and 2 CO2 per turn of the cycle.

nodes.label: Regulation of the CAC
nodes.summary: Control of the cycle by energy charge, NADH, and key product inhibitors.

nodes.label: NADH/ATP inhibition, ADP/Ca2+ activation
nodes.summary: The cycle is inhibited by high NADH and ATP and activated by ADP and calcium (signaling energy demand/contraction).

nodes.label: Three regulated enzymes
nodes.summary: Citrate synthase, isocitrate dehydrogenase, and α-KG dehydrogenase are the main regulatory points.

nodes.label: Control by NAD+/oxaloacetate availability
nodes.summary: Flux depends on substrate supply: the cycle slows when NAD+ or oxaloacetate is scarce (e.g., OAA drained into gluconeogenesis).

nodes.label: Steps and enzymes
nodes.summary: The eight enzymatic reactions and the key decarboxylation and energy-capture events.

nodes.label: Aconitase
nodes.summary: Aconitase isomerizes citrate to isocitrate via cis-aconitate.

nodes.label: α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase
nodes.summary: α-Ketoglutarate dehydrogenase decarboxylates α-KG to succinyl-CoA, producing NADH and CO2.

nodes.label: α-KG dehydrogenase shares PDH's coenzymes
nodes.summary: α-KG dehydrogenase uses the same five coenzymes as PDH (TPP, lipoate, CoA, FAD, NAD+), so thiamine deficiency hits both.

nodes.label: Citrate synthase
nodes.summary: Citrate synthase condenses acetyl-CoA with oxaloacetate to form citrate (the committed entry step).

nodes.label: Two oxidative decarboxylations
nodes.summary: Isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-KG dehydrogenase release the two CO2 molecules and most of the NADH.

nodes.label: Fumarase
nodes.summary: Fumarase hydrates fumarate to malate.

nodes.label: Isocitrate dehydrogenase
nodes.summary: Isocitrate dehydrogenase, the rate-limiting enzyme, oxidatively decarboxylates isocitrate to α-ketoglutarate, producing NADH and CO2.

nodes.label: Malate dehydrogenase
nodes.summary: Malate dehydrogenase oxidizes malate to oxaloacetate, producing NADH and regenerating the cycle.

nodes.label: Succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II)
nodes.summary: Succinate dehydrogenase oxidizes succinate to fumarate, producing FADH2 and serving as ETC Complex II.

nodes.label: Succinyl-CoA synthetase
nodes.summary: Succinyl-CoA synthetase produces succinate and GTP (substrate-level phosphorylation) from succinyl-CoA.

nodes.label: Gluconeogenesis
nodes.summary: The synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors, largely reversing glycolysis with bypass enzymes.

nodes.label: Bypass (irreversible) enzymes
nodes.summary: The four enzymes that circumvent the three irreversible glycolytic steps.

nodes.label: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase
nodes.summary: Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, the rate-limiting gluconeogenic enzyme, hydrolyzes F1,6BP to F6P.

nodes.label: Glucose-6-phosphatase
nodes.summary: Glucose-6-phosphatase (in liver/kidney ER) hydrolyzes G6P to free glucose for export to blood.

nodes.label: Oxaloacetate shuttling via malate
nodes.summary: Oxaloacetate cannot cross the inner membrane, so it is exported as malate (or aspartate) and regenerated in the cytosol.

nodes.label: PEP carboxykinase (PEPCK)
nodes.summary: PEPCK converts oxaloacetate to phosphoenolpyruvate using GTP.

nodes.label: Pyruvate carboxylase
nodes.summary: Pyruvate carboxylase (biotin-dependent, mitochondrial) converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate.

nodes.label: Acetyl-CoA activates pyruvate carboxylase
nodes.summary: Acetyl-CoA from β-oxidation allosterically activates pyruvate carboxylase, signaling fasting and driving gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Gluconeogenesis overview
nodes.summary: Location, precursors, and the energetic cost of making glucose.

nodes.label: Energetic cost of gluconeogenesis
nodes.summary: Making one glucose from two pyruvate consumes 6 high-energy phosphates (4 ATP + 2 GTP) plus 2 NADH.

nodes.label: Liver (and kidney) location
nodes.summary: Gluconeogenesis occurs mainly in the liver (and renal cortex) during fasting to maintain blood glucose.

nodes.label: Even-chain fatty acids cannot make glucose
nodes.summary: Acetyl-CoA cannot be converted to pyruvate, so even-chain fatty acids cannot serve as glucose precursors.

nodes.label: Not simply reversed glycolysis
nodes.summary: Three irreversible glycolytic steps must be bypassed by separate enzymes, so gluconeogenesis is not a simple reversal.

nodes.label: Precursors (lactate, glycerol, amino acids)
nodes.summary: Glucose is synthesized from lactate, glycerol, and glucogenic amino acids—not from acetyl-CoA/fatty acids.

nodes.label: Regulation & reciprocal control
nodes.summary: How gluconeogenesis is coordinated reciprocally with glycolysis.

nodes.label: Cori cycle
nodes.summary: The Cori cycle recycles muscle lactate to liver glucose, shifting the ATP burden to the liver.

nodes.label: F2,6BP reciprocal regulation
nodes.summary: F2,6BP activates PFK-1 and inhibits FBPase-1, reciprocally favoring glycolysis over gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Glucose–alanine cycle
nodes.summary: The glucose–alanine cycle transports muscle amino nitrogen to the liver as alanine for glucose synthesis.

nodes.label: Hormonal control (glucagon/insulin)
nodes.summary: Glucagon (fasting) stimulates gluconeogenesis while insulin (fed) suppresses it.

nodes.label: Glycogen Metabolism
nodes.summary: The synthesis and breakdown of glycogen, the body's storage polysaccharide of glucose.

nodes.label: Glycogen storage diseases
nodes.summary: Inherited enzyme defects that disrupt glycogen synthesis or breakdown.

nodes.label: McArdle disease (muscle phosphorylase)
nodes.summary: Type V deficiency of muscle glycogen phosphorylase causes exercise intolerance and muscle cramps.

nodes.label: Pompe disease (lysosomal acid α-glucosidase)
nodes.summary: Type II deficiency of lysosomal acid α-glucosidase causes glycogen accumulation with cardiomyopathy.

nodes.label: Von Gierke disease (glucose-6-phosphatase)
nodes.summary: Type I deficiency of glucose-6-phosphatase causes severe fasting hypoglycemia and hepatomegaly.

nodes.label: Glycogenesis (synthesis)
nodes.summary: The pathway building glycogen from glucose using UDP-glucose.

nodes.label: Branching enzyme
nodes.summary: Branching enzyme creates α-1,6 branch points to increase solubility and add chain ends.

nodes.label: Glycogen synthase
nodes.summary: Glycogen synthase, the rate-limiting enzyme of glycogenesis, forms α-1,4 linkages.

nodes.label: Glycogenin primer
nodes.summary: Glycogenin autoglucosylates to form the protein primer on which glycogen synthase extends a new chain.

nodes.label: UDP-glucose activated donor
nodes.summary: UDP-glucose is the activated glucose donor for glycogen synthesis.

nodes.label: Glycogenolysis (breakdown)
nodes.summary: The pathway releasing glucose-1-phosphate from glycogen.

nodes.label: Debranching enzyme
nodes.summary: Debranching enzyme removes α-1,6 branch points so phosphorylase can continue degradation.

nodes.label: Glucose-1-phosphate to glucose-6-phosphate
nodes.summary: Phosphoglucomutase converts glucose-1-phosphate to glucose-6-phosphate for metabolism.

nodes.label: Glycogen phosphorylase
nodes.summary: Glycogen phosphorylase, the rate-limiting breakdown enzyme, cleaves α-1,4 bonds to release glucose-1-phosphate.

nodes.label: Liver releases glucose; muscle cannot
nodes.summary: Only liver (and kidney) has glucose-6-phosphatase, so muscle glycogen serves the muscle and cannot raise blood glucose.

nodes.label: Phosphorolysis (not hydrolysis)
nodes.summary: Glycogen phosphorylase uses inorganic phosphate to cleave the glycosidic bond, releasing glucose-1-phosphate without spending ATP.

nodes.label: Regulation of glycogen metabolism
nodes.summary: Hormonal and allosteric reciprocal control of synthesis and breakdown.

nodes.label: Allosteric control (AMP, Ca2+, glucose)
nodes.summary: AMP and Ca2+ activate phosphorylase in muscle while glucose inhibits liver phosphorylase.

nodes.label: cAMP/PKA signaling cascade
nodes.summary: Hormone binding activates adenylyl cyclase → cAMP → PKA → phosphorylase kinase amplifying the signal.

nodes.label: Hormonal control (glucagon/epinephrine/insulin)
nodes.summary: Glucagon and epinephrine trigger glycogenolysis via cAMP/PKA while insulin promotes glycogenesis.

nodes.label: Reciprocal phosphorylation control
nodes.summary: Phosphorylation activates glycogen phosphorylase and inactivates glycogen synthase simultaneously.

nodes.label: Glycogen structure & storage
nodes.summary: The branched structure of glycogen and its main storage sites.

nodes.label: Branched glucose polymer
nodes.summary: Glycogen is a branched polymer of glucose with α-1,4 linkages and α-1,6 branch points.

nodes.label: Functional benefit of branching
nodes.summary: Branching increases solubility and multiplies non-reducing ends so glucose can be added or released rapidly.

nodes.label: Liver vs muscle glycogen
nodes.summary: Liver glycogen maintains blood glucose while muscle glycogen fuels the muscle itself.

nodes.label: Glycolysis
nodes.summary: The cytosolic ten-step pathway converting glucose to two pyruvate, yielding net ATP and NADH.

nodes.label: Fates of pyruvate
nodes.summary: The aerobic and anaerobic destinations of pyruvate after glycolysis.

nodes.label: Aerobic: conversion to acetyl-CoA
nodes.summary: Under aerobic conditions pyruvate enters mitochondria and is decarboxylated to acetyl-CoA.

nodes.label: Carboxylation to oxaloacetate
nodes.summary: Pyruvate carboxylase converts pyruvate to oxaloacetate, an anaplerotic/gluconeogenic fate.

nodes.label: Alcoholic (ethanol) fermentation
nodes.summary: In yeast, pyruvate is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde then reduced to ethanol, regenerating NAD+.

nodes.label: Fermentation gives no net ATP beyond glycolysis
nodes.summary: Fermentation adds no ATP itself; its sole purpose is to recycle NAD+ so the 2 net ATP of glycolysis can keep being made.

nodes.label: Anaerobic: lactate fermentation
nodes.summary: Under anaerobic conditions lactate dehydrogenase reduces pyruvate to lactate, regenerating NAD+.

nodes.label: NAD+ regeneration in fermentation
nodes.summary: Fermentation regenerates NAD+ so glycolysis can continue when the electron transport chain is unavailable.

nodes.label: Tissues dependent on glycolysis (RBCs)
nodes.summary: Red blood cells lack mitochondria and rely entirely on glycolysis (to lactate) for ATP.

nodes.label: Conversion to alanine (transamination)
nodes.summary: Pyruvate can be transaminated to alanine, linking glycolysis to amino acid metabolism.

nodes.label: Other sugars feeding glycolysis
nodes.summary: Entry points for fructose, galactose, and glycogen-derived glucose into the glycolytic pathway.

nodes.label: Fructose metabolism
nodes.summary: Fructose enters glycolysis via fructokinase and aldolase B (liver) bypassing PFK-1 regulation.

nodes.label: Galactose metabolism
nodes.summary: Galactose is converted via the Leloir pathway (galactokinase, GALT) to glucose-6-phosphate.

nodes.label: Lactose and disaccharide entry
nodes.summary: Dietary disaccharides are hydrolyzed to monosaccharides that feed into glycolysis.

nodes.label: Glycolysis overview
nodes.summary: Location, net products, and the energy-investment/energy-payoff structure of glycolysis.

nodes.label: Glucose uptake via GLUT transporters
nodes.summary: Glucose enters cells through GLUT transporters (e.g., insulin-responsive GLUT4 in muscle/fat, GLUT2 in liver/β-cells).

nodes.label: Cytosolic location & anaerobic capability
nodes.summary: Glycolysis occurs in the cytosol and proceeds without oxygen, making it usable anaerobically.

nodes.label: Net reaction equation of glycolysis
nodes.summary: Glucose + 2 NAD+ + 2 ADP + 2 Pi → 2 pyruvate + 2 NADH + 2 H+ + 2 ATP + 2 H2O.

nodes.label: Net ATP and NADH yield
nodes.summary: Glycolysis nets 2 ATP, 2 NADH, and 2 pyruvate per glucose (4 ATP made, 2 invested).

nodes.label: Energy investment vs payoff phases
nodes.summary: The first five steps invest 2 ATP; the last five steps generate 4 ATP and 2 NADH.

nodes.label: Regulation of glycolysis
nodes.summary: Allosteric and hormonal control of the rate-limiting glycolytic enzymes.

nodes.label: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate (F2,6BP)
nodes.summary: F2,6BP is the most potent activator of PFK-1, linking insulin/glucagon signaling to glycolytic rate.

nodes.label: Hexokinase product inhibition
nodes.summary: Hexokinase is inhibited by its product glucose-6-phosphate, whereas glucokinase is not.

nodes.label: Pasteur effect
nodes.summary: Oxygen suppresses glycolytic rate (the Pasteur effect) because aerobic ATP production lowers ADP/AMP and raises ATP/citrate, slowing PFK-1.

nodes.label: PFK-1 allosteric control
nodes.summary: PFK-1 is inhibited by ATP and citrate and activated by AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate.

nodes.label: PFK-2 / fructose bisphosphatase-2 bifunctional enzyme
nodes.summary: The bifunctional PFK-2/FBPase-2 enzyme makes/degrades F2,6BP and is controlled by phosphorylation.

nodes.label: Pyruvate kinase regulation
nodes.summary: Pyruvate kinase is activated by F1,6BP (feedforward) and inhibited by ATP, alanine, and phosphorylation.

nodes.label: Warburg effect (aerobic glycolysis)
nodes.summary: Many tumors rely on glycolysis to lactate even with oxygen present, a metabolic hallmark of cancer cells.

nodes.label: Steps and key enzymes
nodes.summary: The individual reactions, irreversible regulatory steps, and substrate-level phosphorylation events.

nodes.label: Aldolase
nodes.summary: Aldolase cleaves fructose-1,6-bisphosphate into DHAP and glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate.

nodes.label: 2,3-BPG shunt (Rapoport–Luebering)
nodes.summary: In red blood cells some 1,3-BPG is diverted to 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate, which lowers hemoglobin's oxygen affinity at the cost of one ATP.

nodes.label: Enolase
nodes.summary: Enolase dehydrates 2-phosphoglycerate to phosphoenolpyruvate (PEP), a high-energy compound.

nodes.label: Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase
nodes.summary: GAPDH oxidizes G3P and adds Pi to form 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate while reducing NAD+ to NADH.

nodes.label: Hexokinase / glucokinase
nodes.summary: Hexokinase phosphorylates glucose to glucose-6-phosphate using ATP; glucokinase is the liver isozyme with high Km.

nodes.label: Three irreversible regulatory steps
nodes.summary: Hexokinase, PFK-1, and pyruvate kinase catalyze the three irreversible, regulated steps of glycolysis.

nodes.label: Phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1)
nodes.summary: PFK-1 is the committed, rate-limiting step, converting F6P to fructose-1,6-bisphosphate using ATP.

nodes.label: Phosphoglucose isomerase
nodes.summary: Phosphoglucose isomerase converts glucose-6-phosphate to fructose-6-phosphate.

nodes.label: Phosphoglycerate kinase
nodes.summary: Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers phosphate from 1,3-BPG to ADP (substrate-level phosphorylation), forming 3-phosphoglycerate.

nodes.label: Phosphoglycerate mutase
nodes.summary: Phosphoglycerate mutase converts 3-phosphoglycerate to 2-phosphoglycerate.

nodes.label: Phosphorylation traps glucose in the cell
nodes.summary: Charged glucose-6-phosphate cannot cross the membrane, so the first step commits and traps glucose intracellularly.

nodes.label: Pyruvate kinase
nodes.summary: Pyruvate kinase transfers phosphate from PEP to ADP (substrate-level phosphorylation), yielding pyruvate and ATP.

nodes.label: Substrate-level phosphorylation
nodes.summary: ATP is formed by direct phosphoryl transfer from a substrate, independent of the electron transport chain.

nodes.label: Triose phosphate isomerase
nodes.summary: Triose phosphate isomerase interconverts DHAP and G3P so both flow into the payoff phase.

nodes.label: Metabolic Integration & Hormonal Regulation
nodes.summary: How fuel metabolism is coordinated across tissues and physiologic states by hormones.

nodes.label: Basal metabolic rate & whole-body energy
nodes.summary: Whole-body energy expenditure and how fuel choice is measured.

nodes.label: Basal metabolic rate (BMR)
nodes.summary: BMR is the energy expended at rest to maintain basic physiologic functions.

nodes.label: Caloric content of fuels
nodes.summary: Carbohydrate and protein yield ~4 kcal/g while fat yields ~9 kcal/g.

nodes.label: Calorimetry (direct vs indirect)
nodes.summary: Energy expenditure is measured directly as heat output or indirectly from oxygen consumption and CO2 production.

nodes.label: Respiratory quotient (RQ)
nodes.summary: The respiratory quotient (CO2 produced/O2 consumed) indicates which fuel is being oxidized.

nodes.label: Hormonal control of fuel metabolism
nodes.summary: The principal hormones that direct fuel storage and mobilization.

nodes.label: Cortisol
nodes.summary: Cortisol promotes longer-term gluconeogenesis and protein/fat mobilization under chronic stress.

nodes.label: Epinephrine (catecholamines)
nodes.summary: Epinephrine triggers rapid fuel mobilization (glycogenolysis, lipolysis) during stress or exercise.

nodes.label: Glucagon
nodes.summary: Glucagon (fasting) mobilizes fuels via glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, and lipolysis.

nodes.label: Insulin
nodes.summary: Insulin (fed state) promotes glucose uptake and anabolic storage of glycogen, fat, and protein.

nodes.label: Insulin:glucagon ratio sets metabolic direction
nodes.summary: The ratio of insulin to glucagon, not either alone, determines whether tissues store or mobilize fuel.

nodes.label: Fed vs fasting states
nodes.summary: The metabolic shifts between absorptive, postabsorptive, and starvation states.

nodes.label: Blood glucose homeostasis
nodes.summary: Insulin lowers and glucagon/epinephrine/cortisol raise blood glucose to hold it within a narrow range.

nodes.label: Fuel use during exercise
nodes.summary: Short intense exercise relies on phosphocreatine and anaerobic glycolysis; sustained exercise shifts to fatty acid oxidation.

nodes.label: Fasting (postabsorptive) state
nodes.summary: During fasting glucagon drives glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis to maintain blood glucose.

nodes.label: Fed (absorptive) state
nodes.summary: In the fed state insulin drives glucose uptake, glycogenesis, and lipogenesis for storage.

nodes.label: Prolonged starvation
nodes.summary: Prolonged starvation shifts the body to fatty acid and ketone fuel, sparing protein and glucose.

nodes.label: Tissue-specific metabolism
nodes.summary: The specialized fuel preferences and metabolic roles of major organs.

nodes.label: Adipose tissue storage
nodes.summary: Adipose tissue stores triacylglycerols and releases free fatty acids and glycerol during fasting.

nodes.label: Brain fuel preferences
nodes.summary: The brain relies on glucose but adapts to ketone bodies during prolonged starvation.

nodes.label: Liver as metabolic hub
nodes.summary: The liver buffers blood glucose via glycogen, gluconeogenesis, and ketogenesis and processes nutrients first.

nodes.label: Muscle fuel use
nodes.summary: Muscle uses glucose, fatty acids, and its own glycogen, and exports lactate/alanine to the liver.

nodes.label: Lipid (Fatty Acid) Metabolism
nodes.summary: The mobilization, oxidation, synthesis, and storage of fats as a major fuel reserve.

nodes.label: β-Oxidation
nodes.summary: The mitochondrial pathway degrading fatty acids two carbons at a time to acetyl-CoA.

nodes.label: Fatty acid activation (acyl-CoA synthetase)
nodes.summary: Fatty acids are activated to fatty acyl-CoA in the cytosol at the cost of 2 ATP equivalents.

nodes.label: Carnitine shuttle
nodes.summary: The carnitine shuttle (CPT-I/CPT-II) transports long-chain fatty acyl groups into the mitochondrial matrix.

nodes.label: CPT-I inhibition by malonyl-CoA
nodes.summary: Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I, preventing simultaneous fatty acid synthesis and oxidation.

nodes.label: High ATP yield of fatty acid oxidation
nodes.summary: Fatty acid oxidation yields far more ATP per carbon than glucose (e.g., palmitate ~106 net ATP).

nodes.label: Four repeating steps of β-oxidation
nodes.summary: Each cycle is oxidation (FADH2), hydration, oxidation (NADH), and thiolysis, releasing one acetyl-CoA.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial matrix location
nodes.summary: β-Oxidation occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, physically separated from cytosolic fatty acid synthesis.

nodes.label: Odd-chain fatty acids (propionyl-CoA)
nodes.summary: Odd-chain fatty acids yield propionyl-CoA, which is converted to succinyl-CoA and can be glucogenic.

nodes.label: Per-cycle yield (FADH2, NADH, acetyl-CoA)
nodes.summary: Each β-oxidation cycle produces 1 FADH2, 1 NADH, and 1 acetyl-CoA.

nodes.label: Peroxisomal oxidation of very-long-chain fatty acids
nodes.summary: Very-long-chain fatty acids are first shortened by peroxisomal β-oxidation, which generates heat/H2O2 rather than capturing energy in the first step.

nodes.label: Fats more reduced/energy-dense than carbohydrate
nodes.summary: Fatty acid carbons are more reduced than carbohydrate carbons, so their oxidation releases more energy per gram (~9 vs ~4 kcal/g).

nodes.label: Unsaturated fatty acid oxidation
nodes.summary: Oxidation of unsaturated fatty acids requires additional isomerase and reductase enzymes.

nodes.label: Ketone body metabolism
nodes.summary: Synthesis and use of ketone bodies as an alternative fuel during fasting.

nodes.label: Ketones as brain fuel in starvation
nodes.summary: In prolonged starvation ketone bodies become a major brain fuel, sparing glucose and muscle protein.

nodes.label: Ketoacidosis (excess ketone bodies)
nodes.summary: Overproduction of acidic ketone bodies in uncontrolled diabetes or starvation lowers blood pH (ketoacidosis).

nodes.label: Ketogenesis
nodes.summary: During prolonged fasting the liver converts excess acetyl-CoA into ketone bodies (acetoacetate, β-hydroxybutyrate, acetone).

nodes.label: Ketolysis (ketone utilization)
nodes.summary: Peripheral tissues and the brain convert ketone bodies back to acetyl-CoA for energy during fasting.

nodes.label: Liver cannot use ketone bodies
nodes.summary: The liver lacks thiophorase (SCOT) and so produces but cannot use ketone bodies.

nodes.label: Lipid mobilization & transport
nodes.summary: Release of fatty acids from storage and their delivery to tissues.

nodes.label: Free fatty acid transport on albumin
nodes.summary: Free fatty acids travel in the blood bound to serum albumin.

nodes.label: Glycerol enters glycolysis/gluconeogenesis
nodes.summary: Glycerol from lipolysis is phosphorylated and converted to DHAP for glycolysis or gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Lipolysis (hormone-sensitive lipase)
nodes.summary: Hormone-sensitive lipase hydrolyzes stored triacylglycerols to free fatty acids and glycerol during fasting.

nodes.label: Lipoprotein transport (chylomicrons, VLDL)
nodes.summary: Dietary and hepatic triacylglycerols are carried in chylomicrons and VLDL and released to tissues by lipoprotein lipase.

nodes.label: Fatty acid & triacylglycerol synthesis
nodes.summary: The anabolic cytosolic pathway building fatty acids and fats in the fed state.

nodes.label: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC)
nodes.summary: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase, the committed/rate-limiting step, makes malonyl-CoA using biotin and ATP.

nodes.label: Citrate shuttle for acetyl-CoA export
nodes.summary: Citrate carries acetyl-CoA from the mitochondria to the cytosol for fatty acid synthesis.

nodes.label: Fatty acid synthase
nodes.summary: Fatty acid synthase elongates the chain two carbons per cycle using malonyl-CoA and NADPH, producing palmitate.

nodes.label: Cytosolic location of fatty acid synthesis
nodes.summary: Fatty acid synthesis occurs in the cytosol, compartmentally separated from mitochondrial β-oxidation.

nodes.label: NADPH requirement for synthesis
nodes.summary: Fatty acid synthesis requires NADPH supplied by the pentose phosphate pathway and malic enzyme.

nodes.label: Reciprocal regulation with β-oxidation
nodes.summary: Malonyl-CoA and citrate coordinate synthesis (fed) versus oxidation (fasting) to prevent futile cycling.

nodes.label: Triacylglycerol synthesis & storage
nodes.summary: Fatty acids are esterified to glycerol to form triacylglycerols stored in adipose tissue.

nodes.label: Overview of Metabolism
nodes.summary: The organizing concepts of catabolism, anabolism, pathways, and metabolic regulation.

nodes.label: Catabolism vs anabolism
nodes.summary: The two complementary halves of metabolism: degradative energy-releasing and synthetic energy-consuming.

nodes.label: Amphibolic pathways
nodes.summary: Amphibolic pathways (e.g., the citric acid cycle) serve both catabolic and anabolic roles.

nodes.label: Anabolism
nodes.summary: Anabolism builds complex molecules from simpler precursors, consuming ATP and NADPH.

nodes.label: Catabolism
nodes.summary: Catabolism degrades complex molecules into simpler ones, releasing energy as ATP and reducing equivalents.

nodes.label: Acetyl-CoA as the convergence point
nodes.summary: Carbohydrate, fat, and protein catabolism converge on acetyl-CoA, the common two-carbon entry into the citric acid cycle.

nodes.label: Three stages of catabolism
nodes.summary: Catabolism proceeds in stages: macromolecule breakdown, conversion to acetyl-CoA, and oxidation in the CAC/ETC.

nodes.label: Regulation of metabolic pathways
nodes.summary: The control mechanisms that match metabolic flux to cellular energy needs.

nodes.label: Allosteric regulation
nodes.summary: Effectors bind regulatory sites to shift enzyme activity, often signaling energy charge (ATP/AMP).

nodes.label: AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK)
nodes.summary: AMPK senses high AMP/ATP, switching on catabolic ATP-generating pathways and switching off anabolic ATP-consuming ones.

nodes.label: Committed (rate-limiting) step
nodes.summary: Pathways are regulated at the first committed, irreversible, rate-limiting enzyme.

nodes.label: Compartmentalization of pathways
nodes.summary: Opposing pathways are separated into different compartments (e.g., glycolysis in cytosol, β-oxidation in mitochondria) to prevent futile cycling.

nodes.label: Covalent modification (phosphorylation)
nodes.summary: Reversible phosphorylation by kinases/phosphatases switches key metabolic enzymes on or off.

nodes.label: Energy charge / ATP:AMP ratio
nodes.summary: High ATP signals abundant energy (slows catabolism); high AMP/ADP signals energy need (activates catabolism).

nodes.label: Control of enzyme amount (induction/repression)
nodes.summary: Hormones and nutrients alter transcription/degradation to change enzyme levels over hours to days, a slower control than allostery or phosphorylation.

nodes.label: Feedback inhibition
nodes.summary: An end product allosterically inhibits an early enzyme to prevent overproduction.

nodes.label: Feedforward activation
nodes.summary: An upstream intermediate activates a downstream enzyme to anticipate increased flux (e.g., F1,6BP activating pyruvate kinase).

nodes.label: Futile (substrate) cycles
nodes.summary: Opposing reactions running simultaneously waste ATP as heat; reciprocal control normally prevents them but they can be exploited for thermogenesis.

nodes.label: Hormonal control (insulin/glucagon/epinephrine)
nodes.summary: Insulin promotes storage (fed state) while glucagon and epinephrine mobilize fuels (fasting/stress).

nodes.label: Reciprocal regulation
nodes.summary: Opposing pathways (e.g., glycolysis and gluconeogenesis) are coordinately controlled so one is off when the other is on.

nodes.label: Oxidative Phosphorylation
nodes.summary: The mitochondrial process coupling electron transport to ATP synthesis via the proton-motive force.

nodes.label: Chemiosmosis & ATP synthase
nodes.summary: The proton-motive force and the enzyme that uses it to synthesize ATP.

nodes.label: ATP synthase (Complex V)
nodes.summary: ATP synthase lets protons flow back into the matrix, using the energy to phosphorylate ADP to ATP.

nodes.label: Chemiosmotic theory (Mitchell)
nodes.summary: The chemiosmotic hypothesis states the proton-motive force, not a chemical intermediate, drives ATP synthesis.

nodes.label: Coupling of electron transport to ATP synthesis
nodes.summary: Electron transport and phosphorylation are obligately coupled through the gradient: blocking one stops the other unless an uncoupler intervenes.

nodes.label: F0 and F1 subunits
nodes.summary: The membrane F0 channel conducts protons and the matrix F1 head catalyzes ATP synthesis by rotation.

nodes.label: Two components of the proton-motive force
nodes.summary: The proton-motive force combines a chemical pH gradient and an electrical membrane potential across the inner membrane.

nodes.label: Proton gradient (proton-motive force)
nodes.summary: Proton pumping into the intermembrane space creates an electrochemical gradient storing free energy.

nodes.label: Respiratory (acceptor) control by ADP
nodes.summary: ETC rate is governed by ADP availability: without ADP the gradient builds up and electron flow slows.

nodes.label: Rotary catalysis (binding-change mechanism)
nodes.summary: Proton flow spins the rotor, driving conformational changes in F1 that bind ADP+Pi and release ATP.

nodes.label: Electron transport chain
nodes.summary: The series of inner-membrane complexes that pass electrons from NADH/FADH2 to oxygen.

nodes.label: Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase)
nodes.summary: Complex I oxidizes NADH, passes electrons to ubiquinone, and pumps protons across the membrane.

nodes.label: Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase)
nodes.summary: Complex II feeds electrons from FADH2 to ubiquinone but does not pump protons.

nodes.label: Complex III (cytochrome bc1)
nodes.summary: Complex III transfers electrons from ubiquinol to cytochrome c and pumps protons.

nodes.label: Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase)
nodes.summary: Complex IV transfers electrons to O2, forming water and pumping protons (the final electron acceptor step).

nodes.label: Cytochrome c
nodes.summary: Cytochrome c is a mobile peripheral protein carrying electrons from Complex III to Complex IV.

nodes.label: Cytochromes and iron–sulfur centers
nodes.summary: Electron-carrying prosthetic groups (heme iron in cytochromes, Fe-S clusters, copper centers) cycle between oxidation states to pass single electrons.

nodes.label: Electron flow down the redox gradient
nodes.summary: Electrons flow from carriers of lower to higher reduction potential, releasing energy used to pump protons.

nodes.label: Inner mitochondrial membrane location
nodes.summary: The ETC is embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane, whose cristae increase surface area.

nodes.label: Oxygen as the final electron acceptor
nodes.summary: Molecular oxygen accepts electrons at Complex IV to form water, making the ETC oxygen-dependent.

nodes.label: Reactive oxygen species from electron leak
nodes.summary: Partial reduction of O2 escaping the chain forms superoxide and other reactive oxygen species, requiring antioxidant defenses.

nodes.label: Ubiquinone (coenzyme Q)
nodes.summary: Ubiquinone is a mobile lipid-soluble carrier shuttling electrons from Complexes I and II to Complex III.

nodes.label: Inhibitors & uncouplers
nodes.summary: Agents that block electron transport or dissipate the proton gradient.

nodes.label: ATP synthase inhibitors (oligomycin)
nodes.summary: Oligomycin blocks the F0 proton channel, stopping ATP synthesis and secondarily backing up the ETC.

nodes.label: Brown adipose tissue thermogenesis
nodes.summary: Brown fat uses uncoupling protein 1 (thermogenin) to generate heat instead of ATP.

nodes.label: ETC inhibitors (cyanide, rotenone, antimycin)
nodes.summary: Inhibitors block specific complexes (e.g., cyanide and CO at Complex IV) halting electron flow and ATP synthesis.

nodes.label: Effect of ETC blockade (gradient collapse)
nodes.summary: Blocking any complex stops upstream electron flow, collapses proton pumping, and halts ATP synthesis, forcing cells onto glycolysis.

nodes.label: Uncouplers (2,4-DNP, thermogenin)
nodes.summary: Uncouplers dissipate the proton gradient so electron transport continues but produces heat instead of ATP.

nodes.label: ATP yield & shuttles
nodes.summary: The stoichiometry of ATP production and how cytosolic NADH enters the mitochondria.

nodes.label: ATP yield per NADH and FADH2
nodes.summary: Each NADH yields ~2.5 ATP and each FADH2 ~1.5 ATP (classically 3 and 2).

nodes.label: Glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle
nodes.summary: The glycerol-3-phosphate shuttle delivers cytosolic NADH electrons to FADH2 (~1.5 ATP).

nodes.label: Malate–aspartate shuttle
nodes.summary: The malate–aspartate shuttle delivers cytosolic NADH electrons to mitochondrial NADH (~2.5 ATP).

nodes.label: P/O ratio
nodes.summary: The P/O ratio is the ATP made per pair of electrons (per oxygen atom reduced); ~2.5 for NADH and ~1.5 for FADH2.

nodes.label: Total ATP yield per glucose
nodes.summary: Complete aerobic oxidation of glucose yields ~30–32 ATP (classically ~36–38).

nodes.label: ATP/ADP translocase and Pi carrier
nodes.summary: Adenine nucleotide translocase exchanges matrix ATP for cytosolic ADP, and the phosphate carrier imports Pi, both driven by the proton gradient.

nodes.label: Pyruvate Dehydrogenase Complex
nodes.summary: The mitochondrial complex that oxidatively decarboxylates pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, bridging glycolysis and the CAC.

nodes.label: PDH reaction
nodes.summary: The irreversible link reaction converting pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, CO2, and NADH.

nodes.label: Arsenic poisoning (lipoic acid)
nodes.summary: Arsenite binds lipoic acid's thiol groups, inhibiting PDH and other α-ketoacid dehydrogenases.

nodes.label: Five coenzymes of PDH
nodes.summary: PDH requires thiamine pyrophosphate, lipoic acid, CoA, FAD, and NAD+ (TLC For Nancy).

nodes.label: Irreversibility of the link reaction
nodes.summary: The PDH reaction is irreversible, committing carbohydrate carbon away from glucose synthesis.

nodes.label: Overall PDH reaction
nodes.summary: PDH converts pyruvate + CoA + NAD+ to acetyl-CoA + CO2 + NADH in the mitochondrial matrix.

nodes.label: Thiamine deficiency (beriberi/Wernicke)
nodes.summary: Thiamine deficiency impairs PDH (and α-KG dehydrogenase), causing lactate buildup and neurologic disease, classically in alcoholism.

nodes.label: Pyruvate transport into the mitochondrion
nodes.summary: Cytosolic pyruvate is imported across the inner mitochondrial membrane before oxidation by PDH.

nodes.label: Regulation of PDH
nodes.summary: Allosteric and covalent control of the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex.

nodes.label: Fatty-acid acetyl-CoA shuts down PDH
nodes.summary: During fasting, β-oxidation raises acetyl-CoA and NADH, inhibiting PDH so pyruvate is spared for gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: PDH kinase/phosphatase control
nodes.summary: PDH is inactivated by PDH kinase (phosphorylation) and reactivated by PDH phosphatase (dephosphorylation).

nodes.label: Product inhibition (acetyl-CoA, NADH)
nodes.summary: PDH is inhibited by its products acetyl-CoA and NADH, signaling sufficient energy.

nodes.label: Pentose Phosphate Pathway
nodes.summary: The cytosolic shunt producing NADPH and ribose-5-phosphate from glucose-6-phosphate.

nodes.label: NADPH, glutathione & G6PD deficiency
nodes.summary: The antioxidant role of PPP-derived NADPH and the consequence of its failure.

nodes.label: G6PD deficiency and hemolytic anemia
nodes.summary: G6PD deficiency limits NADPH in RBCs, causing oxidative hemolysis on exposure to oxidant drugs, fava beans, or infection.

nodes.label: NADPH reduces glutathione (antioxidant defense)
nodes.summary: NADPH keeps glutathione reduced via glutathione reductase, neutralizing reactive oxygen species.

nodes.label: PPP overview and purpose
nodes.summary: The biosynthetic roles of the pentose phosphate pathway and its two phases.

nodes.label: Cytosolic location, no ATP yield
nodes.summary: The PPP runs in the cytosol and neither consumes nor produces ATP directly.

nodes.label: NADPH production
nodes.summary: The PPP generates NADPH for reductive biosynthesis and antioxidant defense (glutathione reduction).

nodes.label: Ribose-5-phosphate production
nodes.summary: The PPP produces ribose-5-phosphate for nucleotide and nucleic acid synthesis.

nodes.label: Oxidative and non-oxidative phases
nodes.summary: The irreversible NADPH-generating phase and the reversible sugar-interconversion phase.

nodes.label: Metabolic flexibility of the PPP
nodes.summary: The PPP can adjust output to favor NADPH, ribose-5-phosphate, or glycolytic intermediates as the cell needs.

nodes.label: G6PD as rate-limiting enzyme
nodes.summary: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase is the rate-limiting enzyme, regulated by the NADP+/NADPH ratio.

nodes.label: Non-oxidative phase (transketolase/transaldolase)
nodes.summary: The reversible non-oxidative phase uses transketolase and transaldolase to interconvert sugars and link to glycolysis.

nodes.label: Oxidative phase (G6PD)
nodes.summary: The irreversible oxidative phase, committed by glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase, yields 2 NADPH and ribulose-5-phosphate.

nodes.label: Transketolase requires thiamine (TPP)
nodes.summary: Transketolase uses thiamine pyrophosphate, so its activity is a clinical marker of thiamine status.

nodes.label: Protein & Amino Acid Metabolism
nodes.summary: The catabolism of amino acids for energy and the disposal of their nitrogen.

nodes.label: Carbon skeleton fates
nodes.summary: How deaminated amino acid carbon skeletons enter central metabolism.

nodes.label: Entry into the citric acid cycle
nodes.summary: Amino acid carbon skeletons enter metabolism as pyruvate, acetyl-CoA, or one of several CAC intermediates.

nodes.label: Glucogenic amino acids
nodes.summary: Glucogenic amino acids yield pyruvate or CAC intermediates that can form glucose.

nodes.label: Ketogenic amino acids
nodes.summary: Ketogenic amino acids (leucine, lysine) yield acetyl-CoA or acetoacetate and cannot form glucose.

nodes.label: Protein as a fuel of last resort
nodes.summary: Body protein is catabolized for energy only when carbohydrate and fat stores are insufficient, sparing functional tissue.

nodes.label: Nitrogen removal
nodes.summary: Transamination and deamination that strip the amino group from amino acids.

nodes.label: Ammonia toxicity & transport
nodes.summary: Free ammonia is neurotoxic and is transported safely to the liver as glutamine and alanine.

nodes.label: Oxidative deamination (glutamate dehydrogenase)
nodes.summary: Glutamate dehydrogenase releases the amino group of glutamate as free ammonia.

nodes.label: PLP (vitamin B6) cofactor for aminotransferases
nodes.summary: Pyridoxal phosphate, derived from vitamin B6, is the essential cofactor for transamination reactions.

nodes.label: Transamination
nodes.summary: Aminotransferases (using PLP) transfer an amino group to α-ketoglutarate, forming glutamate.

nodes.label: Urea cycle
nodes.summary: The hepatic urea cycle converts toxic ammonia into urea for safe excretion.

nodes.label: Cell Adhesion and Recognition
nodes.summary: Cells adhere to one another and to the matrix through specialized adhesion molecules enabling tissue organization.

nodes.label: Cell Adhesion Molecules (CAMs)
nodes.summary: Cell adhesion molecules mediate cell-cell and cell-matrix binding.

nodes.label: Cadherins
nodes.summary: Cadherins are calcium-dependent CAMs mediating homophilic cell-cell adhesion in adherens junctions and desmosomes.

nodes.label: Immunoglobulin Superfamily CAMs
nodes.summary: Ig-superfamily CAMs mediate calcium-independent cell-cell adhesion in immune and nervous tissue.

nodes.label: Integrins
nodes.summary: Integrins bind extracellular matrix components and transmit signals between the matrix and cytoskeleton.

nodes.label: Selectins
nodes.summary: Selectins are carbohydrate-binding CAMs mediating transient leukocyte rolling adhesion on endothelium.

nodes.label: Cell Recognition
nodes.summary: Surface carbohydrates and markers let cells identify self and one another.

nodes.label: Contact Inhibition
nodes.summary: Normal cells stop dividing and moving on contact with neighbors; loss of contact inhibition is a hallmark of cancer.

nodes.label: Glycocalyx in Recognition
nodes.summary: The carbohydrate-rich glycocalyx serves as a molecular fingerprint for cell-cell recognition.

nodes.label: Cell-Surface Markers
nodes.summary: Surface glycoprotein markers (e.g., MHC) distinguish self from non-self for the immune system.

nodes.label: Cell Communication and Signaling
nodes.summary: Cells coordinate behavior through extracellular signaling molecules acting on receptors and intracellular cascades.

nodes.label: Signaling and Cell Fate
nodes.summary: Intercellular signals direct differentiation, division, and programmed death within multicellular tissues.

nodes.label: Apoptosis (Programmed Cell Death)
nodes.summary: Apoptosis is a regulated suicide program triggered by intrinsic or extrinsic signals to remove cells without inflammation.

nodes.label: Signal-Driven Differentiation
nodes.summary: External signals and cell-cell contacts direct stem and progenitor cells to differentiate into specialized cell types.

nodes.label: Modes of Signaling
nodes.summary: Signaling is classified by the distance and route a signal travels.

nodes.label: Autocrine Signaling
nodes.summary: Autocrine signaling acts on the same cell that secreted the signaling molecule.

nodes.label: Direct (Juxtacrine) Signaling
nodes.summary: Juxtacrine signaling passes molecules directly between adjacent cells, often via gap junctions or surface contact.

nodes.label: Endocrine Signaling
nodes.summary: Endocrine signaling releases hormones into the bloodstream to act on distant target cells.

nodes.label: Paracrine Signaling
nodes.summary: Paracrine signaling releases local mediators that act on nearby neighboring cells.

nodes.label: Synaptic (Neuronal) Signaling
nodes.summary: Synaptic signaling releases neurotransmitters across a synapse to a tightly localized target cell.

nodes.label: Receptor Types
nodes.summary: Receptors are classified as surface receptors for hydrophilic ligands or intracellular receptors for lipophilic ligands.

nodes.label: Intracellular (Nuclear) Receptors
nodes.summary: Lipophilic signals such as steroid hormones bind cytoplasmic/nuclear receptors that act as transcription factors.

nodes.label: Ligand-Gated Ion Channel Receptors
nodes.summary: Ligand-gated (ionotropic) receptors open an ion channel directly upon ligand binding, producing fast electrical responses.

nodes.label: Ligand Solubility and Receptor Location
nodes.summary: Hydrophilic ligands bind surface receptors, whereas lipophilic ligands (steroids) cross the membrane to bind intracellular receptors.

nodes.label: Receptor Tyrosine Kinases
nodes.summary: RTKs dimerize and autophosphorylate on tyrosines upon ligand binding, initiating cascades like MAPK that control growth.

nodes.label: Signal Transduction
nodes.summary: Receptor activation converts an extracellular signal into intracellular responses.

nodes.label: Signal Cascade Amplification
nodes.summary: Each step of a kinase/enzyme cascade activates many downstream molecules, amplifying a small signal into a large response.

nodes.label: G-Protein-Coupled Receptors
nodes.summary: GPCRs activate G proteins that modulate enzymes producing second messengers in response to ligand binding.

nodes.label: Second Messengers
nodes.summary: Second messengers like cAMP, IP3, DAG, and Ca2+ amplify and relay receptor signals inside the cell.

nodes.label: Signal Termination
nodes.summary: Signals are turned off by phosphatases, GTP hydrolysis, second-messenger degradation, and receptor desensitization.

nodes.label: Connective Tissue
nodes.summary: Connective tissue supports, binds, and protects, consisting of cells dispersed in an extracellular matrix.

nodes.label: Resident Connective-Tissue Cells
nodes.summary: Beyond fibroblasts, connective tissue houses immune and storage cells dispersed in the matrix.

nodes.label: Adipocytes
nodes.summary: Adipocytes are connective-tissue cells specialized to store lipid as a triglyceride droplet.

nodes.label: Macrophages
nodes.summary: Macrophages are phagocytic immune cells resident in connective tissue that engulf debris and pathogens.

nodes.label: Mast Cells
nodes.summary: Mast cells in connective tissue release histamine and heparin, mediating inflammation and allergic responses.

nodes.label: Extracellular Matrix
nodes.summary: Connective tissue is defined by abundant extracellular matrix of fibers and ground substance.

nodes.label: Matrix Adhesion Proteins (Fibronectin, Laminin)
nodes.summary: Fibronectin and laminin are extracellular matrix glycoproteins that link cells (via integrins) to collagen and the basement membrane.

nodes.label: Collagen
nodes.summary: Collagen is the most abundant protein in the body, providing high tensile strength to the matrix.

nodes.label: Elastin and Elastic Fibers
nodes.summary: Elastin gives connective tissue elasticity, allowing recoil in skin, lungs, and arteries.

nodes.label: Fibroblasts
nodes.summary: Fibroblasts are the principal connective-tissue cells that secrete collagen and other matrix components.

nodes.label: Ground Substance
nodes.summary: Ground substance is the gel-like component of glycosaminoglycans and proteoglycans filling the space between cells and fibers.

nodes.label: Reticular Fibers
nodes.summary: Reticular fibers are fine collagen (type III) networks that form the supportive scaffold of organs such as lymph nodes and bone marrow.

nodes.label: Connective Tissue Types
nodes.summary: Connective tissue includes loose, dense, and specialized forms such as bone, cartilage, blood, and adipose.

nodes.label: Adipose Tissue
nodes.summary: Adipose tissue stores energy as fat, insulates, and cushions organs.

nodes.label: Blood as Connective Tissue
nodes.summary: Blood is a fluid connective tissue of cells suspended in plasma (liquid matrix).

nodes.label: Bone (Osseous Tissue)
nodes.summary: Bone is mineralized connective tissue providing structural support and a calcium reservoir.

nodes.label: Bone Cells (Osteoblasts, Osteocytes, Osteoclasts)
nodes.summary: Osteoblasts deposit bone matrix, osteocytes maintain it, and osteoclasts resorb it, jointly remodeling bone.

nodes.label: Cartilage
nodes.summary: Cartilage is avascular connective tissue of chondrocytes in a flexible matrix cushioning joints.

nodes.label: Dense Connective Tissue
nodes.summary: Dense connective tissue is fiber-rich, forming tendons and ligaments for strong attachment.

nodes.label: Loose Connective Tissue
nodes.summary: Loose (areolar) connective tissue loosely packs and supports organs with sparse fibers.

nodes.label: Cytoskeleton
nodes.summary: The cytoskeleton is a protein filament network providing shape, structural support, intracellular transport, and motility.

nodes.label: Cilia and Flagella
nodes.summary: Cilia and flagella are microtubule-based motile appendages built on the 9+2 axoneme.

nodes.label: 9+2 Axoneme Structure
nodes.summary: Motile cilia and flagella contain a 9+2 axoneme: nine outer microtubule doublets surrounding two central singlets.

nodes.label: Cilia vs Flagella
nodes.summary: Cilia are short and numerous moving fluid over a surface, while flagella are long and few propelling the cell.

nodes.label: Dynein Arms and Beating
nodes.summary: Dynein arms cause adjacent doublets to slide, bending the axoneme to produce ciliary and flagellar beating.

nodes.label: Eukaryotic vs Prokaryotic Flagella
nodes.summary: Eukaryotic flagella beat using microtubule sliding, whereas prokaryotic flagella are flagellin filaments rotated by a proton-driven motor.

nodes.label: Intermediate Filaments
nodes.summary: Intermediate filaments provide tensile mechanical strength and anchor organelles.

nodes.label: Intermediate Filament Functions
nodes.summary: Intermediate filaments bear mechanical stress, maintain cell integrity, and form the nuclear lamina.

nodes.label: Intermediate Filament Structure
nodes.summary: Intermediate filaments are ~10 nm rope-like fibers of diverse proteins (keratin, vimentin, lamins) resisting tension.

nodes.label: Microfilaments (Actin)
nodes.summary: Microfilaments are thin actin polymers driving shape change, contraction, and cytokinesis.

nodes.label: Actin Filament Structure
nodes.summary: Microfilaments are ~7 nm helical polymers of globular actin (G-actin) forming filamentous actin (F-actin).

nodes.label: Cytokinesis Contractile Ring
nodes.summary: An actin-myosin contractile ring pinches the cell in two during cytokinesis.

nodes.label: Microfilament Functions
nodes.summary: Microfilaments drive muscle contraction, cytokinesis cleavage furrow, amoeboid movement, and cell-shape support.

nodes.label: Actin-Myosin Interaction
nodes.summary: Myosin is a motor protein that walks along actin filaments to generate contraction and intracellular movement.

nodes.label: Actin Polymerization Dynamics
nodes.summary: Actin filaments are polar and grow/shrink by ATP-actin addition at the plus (barbed) end, enabling treadmilling and rapid remodeling.

nodes.label: Microtubules
nodes.summary: Microtubules are hollow tubulin tubes that form the mitotic spindle, tracks for transport, and cilia/flagella.

nodes.label: Basal Bodies
nodes.summary: Basal bodies are centriole-derived structures that anchor and template cilia and flagella.

nodes.label: Centrioles and Centrosome
nodes.summary: Centrioles in a 9+0 triplet arrangement organize the centrosome (MTOC) and spindle poles during division.

nodes.label: Dynamic Instability
nodes.summary: Microtubules rapidly switch between growth and catastrophic shrinkage (dynamic instability) governed by GTP-tubulin hydrolysis.

nodes.label: Microtubule Functions
nodes.summary: Microtubules form the mitotic spindle, provide tracks for organelle transport, and build cilia and flagella.

nodes.label: Kinesin and Dynein Motors
nodes.summary: Kinesin moves cargo toward the plus end and dynein toward the minus end along microtubules using ATP.

nodes.label: Microtubule Structure (Tubulin)
nodes.summary: Microtubules are ~25 nm hollow tubes of alpha- and beta-tubulin dimers with plus and minus ends.

nodes.label: Epithelial Tissue
nodes.summary: Epithelial tissue covers surfaces and lines cavities, forming continuous polarized sheets with a basement membrane.

nodes.label: General Epithelial Features
nodes.summary: Epithelia are avascular, polarized cell sheets attached to a basement membrane.

nodes.label: Avascularity and Diffusion
nodes.summary: Epithelia lack blood vessels and obtain nutrients by diffusion from underlying connective tissue.

nodes.label: Basement Membrane
nodes.summary: The basement membrane is an extracellular protein layer anchoring epithelium to underlying connective tissue.

nodes.label: Epithelial Functions
nodes.summary: Epithelia function in protection, absorption, secretion, filtration, and sensory reception across body surfaces.

nodes.label: Apical-Basal Polarity
nodes.summary: Epithelial cells have a distinct apical (lumen-facing) and basolateral surface maintained by tight junctions.

nodes.label: Epithelial Layering
nodes.summary: Epithelia are classified by the number of cell layers.

nodes.label: Pseudostratified Epithelium
nodes.summary: Pseudostratified epithelium appears layered but all cells touch the basement membrane, as in the airway.

nodes.label: Simple Epithelium
nodes.summary: Simple epithelium is a single cell layer suited to absorption, secretion, and diffusion.

nodes.label: Stratified Epithelium
nodes.summary: Stratified epithelium has multiple cell layers providing protection, as in skin.

nodes.label: Transitional Epithelium
nodes.summary: Transitional epithelium (urothelium) stretches and recoils to accommodate volume changes in the bladder and ureters.

nodes.label: Epithelial Cell Shapes
nodes.summary: Epithelial cells are classified by shape: squamous, cuboidal, or columnar.

nodes.label: Columnar Epithelium
nodes.summary: Columnar cells are tall and column-shaped, lining the digestive tract for secretion and absorption.

nodes.label: Cuboidal Epithelium
nodes.summary: Cuboidal cells are cube-shaped and specialized for secretion and absorption as in glands and kidney tubules.

nodes.label: Squamous Epithelium
nodes.summary: Squamous cells are flat and thin, specialized for diffusion and filtration as in alveoli and capillaries.

nodes.label: Specialized Epithelia
nodes.summary: Some epithelia have specialized structure or function (glandular, parenchyma).

nodes.label: Glandular Epithelium
nodes.summary: Glandular epithelium forms endocrine glands (ductless, release into blood) and exocrine glands (release through ducts).

nodes.label: Parenchyma vs Stroma
nodes.summary: Parenchyma is the functional epithelial tissue of an organ, distinct from the supportive connective stroma.

nodes.label: Epithelial Regeneration
nodes.summary: Epithelia have high turnover and regenerative capacity, continually replaced from a basal stem-cell population.

nodes.label: Intercellular Junctions
nodes.summary: Specialized junctions connect adjacent cells for adhesion, sealing, and communication.

nodes.label: Adherens Junctions
nodes.summary: Adherens junctions link cells via cadherins connected to the actin cytoskeleton.

nodes.label: Desmosomes
nodes.summary: Desmosomes are anchoring junctions that rivet cells together against mechanical stress.

nodes.label: Hemidesmosomes
nodes.summary: Hemidesmosomes anchor the basal cell surface to the basement membrane via integrins.

nodes.label: Desmosome Structure and Function
nodes.summary: Desmosomes anchor cells via cadherins linked to intermediate filaments, providing mechanical strength.

nodes.label: Gap Junctions
nodes.summary: Gap junctions form channels that directly connect the cytoplasm of adjacent cells.

nodes.label: Electrical and Metabolic Coupling
nodes.summary: Gap junctions electrically couple cells, enabling synchronized contraction in cardiac and smooth muscle.

nodes.label: Gap Junction Structure and Function
nodes.summary: Gap junctions are connexon channels that allow ions and small molecules to pass directly between adjacent cytoplasms.

nodes.label: Plasmodesmata (Plant Analog)
nodes.summary: Plasmodesmata are cytoplasmic channels through plant cell walls that connect adjacent plant cells, functionally analogous to gap junctions.

nodes.label: Tight Junctions
nodes.summary: Tight junctions seal neighboring cells to prevent paracellular leakage.

nodes.label: Epithelial Barrier Role
nodes.summary: Tight junctions create selectively permeable epithelial barriers as in the gut and blood-brain barrier.

nodes.label: Tight Junction Structure and Function
nodes.summary: Tight (occluding) junctions fuse adjacent membranes via claudins/occludins to block paracellular passage and maintain polarity.

nodes.label: Membrane Potential
nodes.summary: Ion gradients and selective permeability create an electrical potential across the plasma membrane.

nodes.label: Excitable Membranes
nodes.summary: Some cells use changes in membrane potential as signals across the membrane.

nodes.label: Depolarization and Hyperpolarization
nodes.summary: Opening ion channels can depolarize (less negative) or hyperpolarize (more negative) the membrane, changing excitability.

nodes.label: Membrane Potential as Intracellular Signal
nodes.summary: Voltage changes serve as rapid signals coordinating responses in excitable cells such as neurons and muscle.

nodes.label: Resting Membrane Potential
nodes.summary: At rest the cell interior is negative relative to the exterior, typically about -70 mV.

nodes.label: Resting Membrane Potential
nodes.summary: The resting membrane potential is the steady voltage across an unstimulated membrane, around -70 mV inside-negative.

nodes.label: Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz Equation
nodes.summary: The Goldman equation computes membrane potential accounting for permeabilities of multiple ions simultaneously.

nodes.label: Resting Ion Distribution
nodes.summary: At rest K+ is high inside and Na+/Cl-/Ca2+ are high outside, a distribution set by the Na+/K+ pump and selective leak permeability.

nodes.label: Potassium Leak Channels
nodes.summary: High resting permeability to K+ through leak channels sets the membrane potential near the K+ equilibrium potential.

nodes.label: Nernst Equation
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential for a single ion from its concentration ratio across the membrane.

nodes.label: Pump Contribution to Potential
