nodes.summary: The electrogenic Na+/K+ pump maintains the ion gradients that underlie the resting potential.

nodes.label: Membrane Transport
nodes.summary: Solutes cross the membrane by passive diffusion or by protein-mediated and energy-dependent transport mechanisms.

nodes.label: Active Transport
nodes.summary: Active transport moves solutes against their gradient using energy from ATP or another gradient.

nodes.label: Antiport (Exchange)
nodes.summary: An antiporter moves two solutes in opposite directions across the membrane.

nodes.label: Calcium ATPase Pumps
nodes.summary: Ca2+ ATPases (plasma membrane PMCA and sarcoplasmic SERCA) pump calcium out of the cytosol to keep cytosolic Ca2+ low.

nodes.label: Electrochemical Gradient
nodes.summary: The electrochemical gradient combines chemical concentration and electrical charge gradients driving ion movement.

nodes.label: Primary Active Transport
nodes.summary: Primary active transport hydrolyzes ATP directly to pump solutes against their electrochemical gradient.

nodes.label: Proton Pumps (H+ ATPase)
nodes.summary: Proton pumps move H+ across membranes against gradient (e.g., gastric H+/K+ ATPase, lysosomal V-ATPase), driving acidification and gradients.

nodes.label: Secondary Active Transport
nodes.summary: Secondary active transport uses an existing ion gradient (often Na+) to co-transport another solute against its gradient.

nodes.label: Sodium-Potassium Pump (Na+/K+ ATPase)
nodes.summary: The Na+/K+ ATPase exports 3 Na+ and imports 2 K+ per ATP, maintaining the resting membrane potential.

nodes.label: Symport (Co-transport)
nodes.summary: A symporter moves two solutes in the same direction across the membrane.

nodes.label: Bulk Transport (Endo/Exocytosis)
nodes.summary: Cells move large particles and fluid in or out by vesicle-mediated bulk transport.

nodes.label: Endocytosis
nodes.summary: Endocytosis brings material into the cell by inward membrane invagination forming a vesicle.

nodes.label: Exocytosis
nodes.summary: Exocytosis fuses vesicles with the plasma membrane to secrete contents and add membrane.

nodes.label: Phagocytosis
nodes.summary: Phagocytosis (cell eating) engulfs large solid particles or whole cells into a phagosome.

nodes.label: Pinocytosis
nodes.summary: Pinocytosis (cell drinking) nonspecifically takes in extracellular fluid and dissolved solutes.

nodes.label: Receptor-Mediated Endocytosis
nodes.summary: Specific ligands bind surface receptors that cluster in clathrin-coated pits for selective uptake.

nodes.label: Osmosis and Tonicity
nodes.summary: Water moves across a semipermeable membrane toward the higher solute concentration.

nodes.label: Osmosis
nodes.summary: Osmosis is the passive diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane toward higher solute concentration.

nodes.label: Hypertonic Solution
nodes.summary: A hypertonic external solution draws water out of the cell, causing shrinkage (crenation).

nodes.label: Hypotonic Solution
nodes.summary: A hypotonic external solution drives water into the cell, causing swelling and possible lysis.

nodes.label: Isotonic Solution
nodes.summary: An isotonic solution has equal solute concentration so there is no net water movement.

nodes.label: Osmolarity and Osmoles
nodes.summary: Osmolarity counts total dissolved solute particles per liter (accounting for dissociation), determining the osmotic driving force.

nodes.label: Osmoregulation
nodes.summary: Cells and organisms regulate internal solute and water balance (e.g., contractile vacuoles, cell walls, kidney) to survive osmotic stress.

nodes.label: Osmotic Pressure
nodes.summary: Osmotic pressure is the pressure needed to stop net water flow, given by Pi = iMRT.

nodes.label: Passive Transport
nodes.summary: Passive transport moves solutes down their electrochemical gradient without ATP.

nodes.label: Aquaporins
nodes.summary: Aquaporins are channel proteins that greatly accelerate water movement across the membrane.

nodes.label: Carrier Proteins
nodes.summary: Carrier proteins bind a solute and change conformation to shuttle it across, with saturable kinetics.

nodes.label: Channel Proteins
nodes.summary: Channels form hydrophilic pores that allow specific ions or water to pass rapidly down their gradient.

nodes.label: Facilitated Diffusion
nodes.summary: Polar or charged solutes move down their gradient through channel or carrier proteins without ATP.

nodes.label: Gated Channels
nodes.summary: Gated channels open in response to voltage, ligand binding, or mechanical stress.

nodes.label: Transporter Saturation Kinetics
nodes.summary: Carrier-mediated transport saturates at high solute concentration (a Vmax/Km profile), unlike simple diffusion which rises linearly.

nodes.label: Simple Diffusion
nodes.summary: Small nonpolar molecules and gases diffuse directly across the bilayer down their concentration gradient.

nodes.label: Muscle Tissue
nodes.summary: Muscle is a contractile tissue type built from actin-myosin assemblies, organized as skeletal, cardiac, or smooth muscle.

nodes.label: Cardiac Muscle
nodes.summary: Cardiac muscle is striated, branched, and involuntary, with intercalated discs and gap junctions coupling cells for synchronized contraction.

nodes.label: Muscle Tissue Overview
nodes.summary: Muscle tissue is specialized for contraction, generating force and movement via the sliding of actin and myosin filaments.

nodes.label: Skeletal Muscle
nodes.summary: Skeletal muscle is striated, multinucleate, and under voluntary control, moving the skeleton.

nodes.label: Smooth Muscle
nodes.summary: Smooth muscle is non-striated, uninucleate, and involuntary, lining hollow organs and blood vessels.

nodes.label: Nervous Tissue
nodes.summary: Nervous tissue is the excitable signaling tissue type composed of neurons and supporting glial cells.

nodes.label: Glial (Neuroglial) Cells
nodes.summary: Glia support, insulate (myelinate), and nourish neurons; they include astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, Schwann cells, and microglia.

nodes.label: Neurons
nodes.summary: Neurons are excitable cells that generate and transmit action potentials via dendrites, soma, and axon.

nodes.label: Nervous Tissue Overview
nodes.summary: Nervous tissue conducts electrical signals and integrates information, forming the brain, spinal cord, and nerves.

nodes.label: Levels of Biological Organization
nodes.summary: Multicellular organisms are organized hierarchically from cells through tissues, organs, and systems.

nodes.label: Emergent Properties and Specialization
nodes.summary: Higher organizational levels exhibit emergent properties from cell specialization and division of labor not present in single cells.

nodes.label: Organizational Hierarchy
nodes.summary: Cells assemble into tissues, organs, and organ systems with emergent function.

nodes.label: Organ Level
nodes.summary: An organ is a structure of multiple tissue types cooperating for a specialized function.

nodes.label: Organ System Level
nodes.summary: An organ system is a group of organs working together toward a major physiological role.

nodes.label: Tissue Level
nodes.summary: A tissue is a group of similar cells working together to perform a common function.

nodes.label: Stem Cells and Differentiation
nodes.summary: Stem cells are unspecialized cells that self-renew and differentiate (totipotent/pluripotent/multipotent) to build and maintain tissues.

nodes.label: Four Primary Tissue Types
nodes.summary: The body has four primary tissue types: epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous.

nodes.label: Plasma Membrane Structure
nodes.summary: The plasma membrane is a selectively permeable fluid mosaic of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins, cholesterol, and carbohydrates.

nodes.label: Membrane Composition
nodes.summary: The membrane is built from amphipathic phospholipids, cholesterol, proteins, and surface carbohydrates.

nodes.label: Amphipathic Phospholipid Structure
nodes.summary: Each phospholipid has a polar phosphate-containing head group and two nonpolar fatty-acid tails, making it amphipathic.

nodes.label: Cholesterol as Fluidity Buffer
nodes.summary: Cholesterol stabilizes fluidity, increasing it at low temperature and decreasing it at high temperature, and reduces small-molecule permeability.

nodes.label: Glycolipids
nodes.summary: Glycolipids are membrane lipids with carbohydrate chains on the extracellular face that contribute to cell recognition and the glycocalyx.

nodes.label: Glycoproteins and Glycocalyx
nodes.summary: Glycoproteins display carbohydrate chains forming the glycocalyx, mediating cell recognition, adhesion, and protection.

nodes.label: Phosphoglycerides (Glycerophospholipids)
nodes.summary: Phosphoglycerides such as phosphatidylcholine and phosphatidylserine are glycerol-backbone phospholipids that are the dominant structural lipids of most membranes.

nodes.label: Phospholipid Bilayer
nodes.summary: Amphipathic phospholipids self-assemble into a bilayer with hydrophilic heads facing aqueous environments and hydrophobic tails facing inward.

nodes.label: Fatty-Acid Saturation and Fluidity
nodes.summary: Unsaturated (cis-double-bond) tails kink and increase fluidity, while saturated straight tails pack tightly and decrease fluidity.

nodes.label: Bilayer Self-Assembly and the Hydrophobic Effect
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect drives amphipathic lipids to spontaneously self-assemble into bilayers, micelles, or liposomes to minimize tail-water contact and maximize entropy of water.

nodes.label: Sphingolipids
nodes.summary: Sphingolipids are sphingosine-backbone membrane lipids concentrated in lipid rafts and the outer leaflet.

nodes.label: Temperature and Membrane Fluidity
nodes.summary: Higher temperature increases fluidity and lower temperature causes gel-phase ordering; cells adjust lipid composition (homeoviscous adaptation) to maintain fluidity.

nodes.label: Waxes
nodes.summary: Waxes are esters of long-chain fatty acids and alcohols that form protective, waterproof coatings.

nodes.label: Membrane Dynamics and Remodeling
nodes.summary: Membranes are dynamic: they fuse, bud, and recycle to exchange material and remodel cell shape.

nodes.label: Membrane Budding and Vesicle Formation
nodes.summary: Localized membrane invagination and pinching off forms vesicles during endocytosis and intracellular trafficking.

nodes.label: Membrane Fusion
nodes.summary: Vesicle and membrane fusion (e.g., SNARE-mediated) merges two bilayers, underlying exocytosis and vesicle trafficking.

nodes.label: Membrane Recycling and Exchange
nodes.summary: Endocytosis and exocytosis continuously exchange and recycle membrane material, balancing surface area and exchanging matter with the environment.

nodes.label: Fluid Mosaic Model
nodes.summary: The Singer-Nicolson model describes the membrane as a 2D fluid in which lipids and proteins diffuse laterally.

nodes.label: Membrane Asymmetry
nodes.summary: The two leaflets differ in lipid and protein composition; carbohydrates face only the extracellular side.

nodes.label: Transverse (Flip-Flop) Movement
nodes.summary: Spontaneous flip-flop between leaflets is energetically unfavorable and slow; flippases use ATP to catalyze it.

nodes.label: Lateral Diffusion
nodes.summary: Lipids and many proteins diffuse rapidly within their own leaflet (lateral movement) but rarely flip-flop between leaflets.

nodes.label: Lipid Rafts
nodes.summary: Lipid rafts are cholesterol- and sphingolipid-rich ordered microdomains that organize signaling proteins.

nodes.label: Singer-Nicolson Fluid Mosaic Model
nodes.summary: The membrane is a mosaic of proteins floating in or on a fluid phospholipid bilayer that allows lateral movement.

nodes.label: Selective Permeability
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic core lets small nonpolar and gas molecules pass freely while blocking ions and large polar molecules.

nodes.label: Membrane Proteins
nodes.summary: Membrane proteins are classified by association with the bilayer and perform transport, signaling, enzymatic, and structural roles.

nodes.label: Membrane-Bound Enzymes
nodes.summary: Some membrane proteins are enzymes (e.g., adenylyl cyclase) that catalyze reactions at the membrane surface in signaling and metabolism.

nodes.label: Membrane Protein Functions
nodes.summary: Membrane proteins act as transporters, receptors, enzymes, anchors, cell-recognition markers, and intercellular junctions.

nodes.label: Integral (Intrinsic) Membrane Proteins
nodes.summary: Integral proteins are embedded within the hydrophobic core and require detergents to be extracted.

nodes.label: Lipid-Anchored Proteins
nodes.summary: Lipid-anchored proteins are covalently attached to a membrane lipid (e.g., GPI anchor) rather than spanning the bilayer.

nodes.label: Peripheral (Extrinsic) Membrane Proteins
nodes.summary: Peripheral proteins associate loosely with the membrane surface or other proteins and are removed by mild treatments.

nodes.label: Cell-Surface Receptors
nodes.summary: Receptor proteins bind extracellular ligands to trigger intracellular signaling, including G-protein-coupled and receptor tyrosine kinases.

nodes.label: Transmembrane Proteins
nodes.summary: Transmembrane proteins span the entire bilayer, often via hydrophobic alpha-helical or beta-barrel segments.

nodes.label: Apical Membrane Specializations
nodes.summary: Cells extend membrane surface for absorption or movement via microvilli and motile appendages.

nodes.label: Microvilli and Brush Border
nodes.summary: Microvilli are actin-cored membrane projections that increase apical surface area for absorption, forming the intestinal brush border.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic cell structure
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic cells have a defined set of envelope and cytoplasmic structures distinct from eukaryotes.

nodes.label: Cell envelope (wall & membrane)
nodes.summary: The cell envelope includes the plasma membrane and the peptidoglycan cell wall.

nodes.label: Lysozyme target
nodes.summary: Lysozyme hydrolyzes the glycosidic bond between NAG and NAM, weakening the cell wall.

nodes.label: Membrane invaginations (respiratory/photosynthetic)
nodes.summary: Lacking organelles, prokaryotes localize the ETC, oxidative phosphorylation, and photosynthesis to the plasma membrane and its infoldings.

nodes.label: NAG and NAM backbone
nodes.summary: Peptidoglycan consists of alternating N-acetylglucosamine (NAG) and N-acetylmuramic acid (NAM) cross-linked by amino acid chains.

nodes.label: Cell wall & osmotic protection
nodes.summary: The rigid cell wall prevents osmotic lysis in hypotonic environments; removing it yields fragile protoplasts/spheroplasts.

nodes.label: Penicillin & cell-wall synthesis
nodes.summary: Beta-lactam antibiotics like penicillin inhibit the transpeptidase enzymes that cross-link peptidoglycan, killing growing bacteria but sparing wall-less host cells.

nodes.label: Peptidoglycan cell wall
nodes.summary: The bacterial cell wall is built of peptidoglycan, a polymer of NAG and NAM sugars cross-linked by peptide bridges.

nodes.label: Plasma membrane
nodes.summary: The prokaryotic plasma membrane is a phospholipid bilayer that controls transport and hosts the electron transport chain.

nodes.label: External structures
nodes.summary: Surface appendages and coatings mediate motility, attachment, and protection.

nodes.label: Biofilms
nodes.summary: Bacteria embedded in a secreted glycocalyx/extracellular matrix form biofilms that resist antibiotics and immune attack.

nodes.label: Capsule / glycocalyx
nodes.summary: A polysaccharide capsule promotes adhesion and protects against phagocytosis and desiccation.

nodes.label: Chemotaxis
nodes.summary: Bacteria perform chemotaxis by alternating runs and tumbles via flagellar rotation toward attractants.

nodes.label: Flagellum
nodes.summary: The bacterial flagellum is a proton-driven rotary structure of filament, hook, and basal body used for motility.

nodes.label: Flagellar arrangements
nodes.summary: Flagella are arranged as monotrichous, lophotrichous, amphitrichous, or peritrichous patterns.

nodes.label: Flagellar structure (filament/hook/basal body)
nodes.summary: The flagellum is built of a filament (flagellin protein), a hook, and a basal body anchored in the membrane that acts as the rotary motor.

nodes.label: Pili and fimbriae
nodes.summary: Fimbriae aid adhesion, while the sex pilus mediates attachment for conjugation.

nodes.label: Gram staining & wall architecture
nodes.summary: Gram staining distinguishes bacteria by cell wall thickness and outer membrane.

nodes.label: Acid-fast wall (mycolic acid)
nodes.summary: Mycobacteria have a waxy mycolic-acid-rich wall that resists Gram staining and requires an acid-fast stain instead.

nodes.label: Endotoxin vs exotoxin
nodes.summary: Endotoxins are heat-stable LPS components released on lysis; exotoxins are secreted proteins, often highly toxic and made by gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria.

nodes.label: Gram-negative (thin wall + outer membrane)
nodes.summary: Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer plus an outer membrane and stain pink/red.

nodes.label: Gram-positive (thick wall)
nodes.summary: Gram-positive bacteria have a thick peptidoglycan layer with teichoic acids and stain purple/violet.

nodes.label: LPS / endotoxin
nodes.summary: The gram-negative outer membrane contains lipopolysaccharide (LPS) whose lipid A acts as an endotoxin.

nodes.label: Periplasmic space
nodes.summary: Gram-negative bacteria have a periplasmic space between the inner and outer membranes.

nodes.label: Gram stain procedure
nodes.summary: Gram staining uses crystal violet, iodine mordant, alcohol decolorization, and safranin counterstain.

nodes.label: Teichoic acids
nodes.summary: Teichoic and lipoteichoic acids are anionic polymers in the gram-positive wall that aid structure, ion regulation, and host adhesion.

nodes.label: Internal structures
nodes.summary: The cytoplasm contains the genome, ribosomes, and inclusion structures.

nodes.label: Endospores
nodes.summary: Some gram-positive bacteria form dormant, highly resistant endospores under stress.

nodes.label: No nucleus-bound cytoskeleton / inclusions
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes store nutrients in cytoplasmic inclusion granules and possess simple cytoskeletal homologs but lack the complex eukaryotic cytoskeleton.

nodes.label: Nucleoid & circular chromosome
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic DNA is a single circular chromosome located in the membrane-free nucleoid region.

nodes.label: Plasmids
nodes.summary: Plasmids are small circular extrachromosomal DNA molecules that replicate independently and often carry accessory genes.

nodes.label: 70S ribosomes
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic 70S ribosomes are built from 30S and 50S subunits and are targets of many antibiotics.

nodes.label: Sporulation & germination
nodes.summary: Sporulation packages the chromosome into a dehydrated, heat- and chemical-resistant endospore that germinates back into a vegetative cell when conditions improve.

nodes.label: DNA supercoiling & no histones
nodes.summary: The bacterial chromosome is compacted by supercoiling (DNA gyrase) and nucleoid-associated proteins rather than true eukaryotic histones.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic genetics & genetic exchange
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes generate variation through mutation and horizontal gene transfer rather than meiosis.

nodes.label: Gene expression & regulation
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes organize genes into operons and regulate transcription in response to the environment.

nodes.label: Catabolite (glucose) repression
nodes.summary: High glucose lowers cAMP so CAP cannot activate the lac operon, ensuring glucose is used preferentially over lactose (diauxic growth).

nodes.label: Coupled transcription-translation
nodes.summary: Because prokaryotes lack a nucleus, translation begins on mRNA while transcription is still occurring.

nodes.label: Inducible vs repressible operons
nodes.summary: Inducible operons are normally off and switched on by an inducer; repressible operons are normally on and switched off by a corepressor.

nodes.label: lac operon (inducible)
nodes.summary: The inducible lac operon is turned on by allolactose binding the repressor, allowing lactose metabolism.

nodes.label: No mRNA processing / short-lived mRNA
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic mRNA is not capped, spliced, or polyadenylated and is rapidly degraded, enabling fast regulatory responses.

nodes.label: Operon concept
nodes.summary: An operon is a cluster of co-transcribed genes controlled by a single promoter and operator.

nodes.label: Operon components (promoter/operator/regulator)
nodes.summary: An operon comprises a promoter, an operator (repressor-binding site), structural genes, and a separate regulator gene encoding the repressor.

nodes.label: Polycistronic mRNA
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic operons produce polycistronic mRNA encoding several proteins from one transcript.

nodes.label: Positive control / CAP-cAMP
nodes.summary: CAP bound to cAMP enhances lac transcription, mediating catabolite repression by glucose.

nodes.label: trp operon (repressible)
nodes.summary: The repressible trp operon is switched off when tryptophan acts as a corepressor activating the repressor.

nodes.label: Horizontal (lateral) gene transfer
nodes.summary: Bacteria exchange genetic material laterally by three principal mechanisms.

nodes.label: Conjugation
nodes.summary: In conjugation DNA is transferred directly between cells through a sex pilus / conjugation bridge.

nodes.label: F (fertility) factor & F+/F- cells
nodes.summary: The F plasmid encodes the sex pilus; F+ donors transfer it to F- recipients during conjugation.

nodes.label: F' (F-prime) cells
nodes.summary: An F' plasmid forms when an integrated F factor excises imprecisely, carrying adjacent chromosomal genes that it then transfers during conjugation.

nodes.label: Hfr cells & integrated F factor
nodes.summary: Hfr cells have the F factor integrated into the chromosome and can transfer chromosomal genes during conjugation.

nodes.label: R plasmids & antibiotic resistance
nodes.summary: R plasmids carry antibiotic resistance genes that can spread by conjugation.

nodes.label: Homologous recombination of transferred DNA
nodes.summary: Linear DNA acquired by transformation, transduction, or Hfr conjugation must recombine into the recipient chromosome to be stably inherited.

nodes.label: Transduction
nodes.summary: In transduction a bacteriophage carries bacterial DNA from one cell to another.

nodes.label: Generalized vs specialized transduction
nodes.summary: Generalized transduction packages random host DNA during lytic infection; specialized transduction transfers genes adjacent to an excised prophage.

nodes.label: Transformation
nodes.summary: In transformation a competent bacterium takes up naked DNA from its environment and incorporates it.

nodes.label: Mutation & variation
nodes.summary: Mutations are a primary source of new genetic variation in clonal populations.

nodes.label: Rapid bacterial evolution & resistance
nodes.summary: Short generation times and large populations let beneficial mutations (e.g., antibiotic resistance) spread quickly through selection.

nodes.label: Mutation as variation source
nodes.summary: Random mutations during replication generate heritable variation that natural selection can act on.

nodes.label: Transposons
nodes.summary: Transposons are mobile genetic elements that can move within and between DNA molecules.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic growth & reproduction
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes reproduce asexually by binary fission and follow a characteristic population growth curve.

nodes.label: Binary fission
nodes.summary: Binary fission is the asexual division mechanism of prokaryotes.

nodes.label: Asexual, clonal reproduction
nodes.summary: Binary fission produces clonal offspring, so genetic variation requires mutation or horizontal gene transfer.

nodes.label: Binary fission mechanism
nodes.summary: In binary fission the circular chromosome replicates and the cell divides into two genetically identical daughter cells.

nodes.label: No mitosis or spindle
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic division lacks mitotic spindles and the cell cycle phases of eukaryotic mitosis.

nodes.label: Origin replication & chromosome segregation
nodes.summary: Replication begins at a single origin (oriC), and the two copies attach to the membrane and separate as the cell elongates and forms a septum.

nodes.label: Bacterial growth curve
nodes.summary: A batch culture progresses through four characteristic growth phases.

nodes.label: Death (decline) phase
nodes.summary: In death phase the death rate exceeds the division rate and population declines.

nodes.label: Lag phase
nodes.summary: During lag phase cells adapt to the environment and synthesize enzymes without dividing.

nodes.label: Log (exponential) phase
nodes.summary: During exponential phase cells divide at a constant maximal rate, giving exponential population growth.

nodes.label: Stationary phase
nodes.summary: In stationary phase nutrient depletion and waste cause growth and death rates to balance.

nodes.label: Exponential growth quantitation
nodes.summary: Population in exponential phase doubles each generation, modeled as N = N0 * 2^(t/g) where g is generation time.

nodes.label: Quorum sensing
nodes.summary: Bacteria sense population density via secreted autoinducers and coordinate group behaviors (biofilms, virulence) once a threshold is reached.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic physiology & metabolism
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes are classified by energy/carbon source and by their relationship to oxygen.

nodes.label: Nitrogen fixation
nodes.summary: Some prokaryotes fix atmospheric N2 into ammonia using nitrogenase.

nodes.label: Nutritional classification
nodes.summary: Organisms are grouped by carbon and energy source.

nodes.label: Autotrophs vs heterotrophs
nodes.summary: Autotrophs fix CO2 as their carbon source while heterotrophs consume organic carbon.

nodes.label: Chemotrophs vs phototrophs
nodes.summary: Chemotrophs obtain energy from chemical compounds while phototrophs use light.

nodes.label: Four nutritional categories
nodes.summary: Combining energy and carbon source yields chemoautotrophs, chemoheterotrophs, photoautotrophs, and photoheterotrophs.

nodes.label: Saprophytes, parasites & symbionts
nodes.summary: Heterotrophic prokaryotes may live as saprophytes (decomposers), parasites (harming a host), or symbionts/mutualists (e.g., gut and nitrogen-fixing bacteria).

nodes.label: Oxygen requirements
nodes.summary: Bacteria differ in their need for and tolerance of oxygen.

nodes.label: Aerotolerant anaerobes
nodes.summary: Aerotolerant anaerobes do not use oxygen but can survive in its presence.

nodes.label: Facultative anaerobes
nodes.summary: Facultative anaerobes use oxygen when present but can switch to anaerobic metabolism when it is absent.

nodes.label: Microaerophiles
nodes.summary: Microaerophiles require oxygen but only at concentrations lower than atmospheric.

nodes.label: Obligate aerobes
nodes.summary: Obligate aerobes require oxygen as the terminal electron acceptor for aerobic respiration.

nodes.label: Obligate anaerobes
nodes.summary: Obligate anaerobes cannot use oxygen and are often killed by it, relying on fermentation or anaerobic respiration.

nodes.label: Oxygen toxicity & protective enzymes
nodes.summary: Reactive oxygen species harm cells; aerobes and aerotolerant anaerobes survive O2 because they possess superoxide dismutase and catalase, which obligate anaerobes lack.

nodes.label: Energy generation
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes generate ATP across the plasma membrane via respiration or fermentation.

nodes.label: Anaerobic respiration
nodes.summary: Anaerobic respiration uses non-oxygen terminal electron acceptors such as nitrate or sulfate.

nodes.label: Fermentation
nodes.summary: Fermentation regenerates NAD+ without an electron transport chain, yielding products such as lactate or ethanol.

nodes.label: Membrane-localized ETC
nodes.summary: In prokaryotes the electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation occur in the plasma membrane.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic cell classification & domains
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes are unicellular organisms lacking a membrane-bound nucleus, divided into the domains Bacteria and Archaea.

nodes.label: Domains: Bacteria vs Archaea
nodes.summary: The two prokaryotic domains differ in membrane lipids, cell wall chemistry, and rRNA sequence.

nodes.label: Archaea-eukaryote shared traits
nodes.summary: Archaea share several molecular features with eukaryotes (e.g., similar RNA polymerases and histone-like proteins), making them evolutionarily closer to eukaryotes than to bacteria.

nodes.label: Archaeal distinguishing features
nodes.summary: Archaea have ether-linked branched membrane lipids and lack peptidoglycan, and many are extremophiles.

nodes.label: Extremophiles (archaea)
nodes.summary: Many archaea are thermophiles, halophiles, or methanogens adapted to extreme environments.

nodes.label: Three-domain system
nodes.summary: Life is divided into Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukarya based largely on 16S/18S rRNA sequence comparison.

nodes.label: Bacterial morphology
nodes.summary: Bacteria are classified by shape and arrangement.

nodes.label: Cellular arrangements (prefixes)
nodes.summary: Bacterial arrangements after division are named by prefix: diplo- (pairs), strepto- (chains), staphylo- (clusters), tetrad, and sarcina.

nodes.label: Bacilli (rods)
nodes.summary: Bacilli are rod-shaped bacteria.

nodes.label: Cocci (spheres)
nodes.summary: Cocci are spherical bacteria, occurring as diplococci, streptococci (chains), or staphylococci (clusters).

nodes.label: Spirilla / spirochetes (spirals)
nodes.summary: Spirilla and spirochetes are spiral-shaped bacteria.

nodes.label: Prokaryote vs eukaryote size & organization
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes are smaller and structurally simpler than eukaryotes.

nodes.label: Absence of membrane-bound organelles
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes lack a true nucleus and membrane-bound organelles, housing DNA in a nucleoid region.

nodes.label: Smaller cell and ribosome size
nodes.summary: Prokaryotic cells (~1-10 um) are smaller than eukaryotic cells and have 70S ribosomes rather than 80S.

nodes.label: Unicellular organization
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes are single-celled organisms, though some grow in colonies, biofilms, or multicellular-like aggregates without true tissue differentiation.

nodes.label: Subviral particles
nodes.summary: Prions and viroids are infectious agents simpler than viruses.

nodes.label: Prions and viroids
nodes.summary: Subviral agents lack the full virion structure.

nodes.label: Prions
nodes.summary: Prions are infectious misfolded proteins that template the misfolding of normal proteins and contain no nucleic acid.

nodes.label: Prion propagation mechanism
nodes.summary: A prion converts normal alpha-helical PrP into the beta-sheet-rich misfolded form, which aggregates and causes neurodegenerative disease without replicating genetic material.

nodes.label: Viroids
nodes.summary: Viroids are small circular RNA molecules without a capsid that infect plants.

nodes.label: Viral life cycles
nodes.summary: Viruses replicate through ordered attachment, entry, replication, assembly, and release, with lytic and lysogenic alternatives in phages.

nodes.label: Animal virus considerations
nodes.summary: Animal viruses share the general cycle but differ in entry, latency, and effects on the host cell.

nodes.label: Antigenic drift & shift
nodes.summary: RNA viruses like influenza evade immunity by gradual mutation (antigenic drift) or reassortment of segmented genomes (antigenic shift).

nodes.label: Cytopathic effects
nodes.summary: Viral infection can damage host cells, causing lysis, inclusion bodies, or fusion into multinucleate syncytia.

nodes.label: Acute vs persistent/chronic infection
nodes.summary: Animal viruses can cause rapid acute infections or persistent/chronic infections with continuous low-level production or latency.

nodes.label: General replication steps
nodes.summary: All viral cycles share core stages of infection.

nodes.label: Assembly (maturation)
nodes.summary: New genomes and capsid proteins self-assemble into progeny virions.

nodes.label: Attachment / adsorption
nodes.summary: Viruses attach to specific host-cell surface receptors, determining host range and tissue tropism.

nodes.label: Budding vs lysis & envelope acquisition
nodes.summary: Enveloped viruses bud from the host membrane (often non-lethal, persistent shedding), while naked viruses typically accumulate and lyse the cell.

nodes.label: Penetration / entry
nodes.summary: Viruses enter the host by genome injection, endocytosis, or membrane fusion.

nodes.label: Release (lysis or budding)
nodes.summary: Progeny virions exit by lysing the cell or by budding, the latter acquiring an envelope.

nodes.label: Genome replication & protein synthesis
nodes.summary: The virus hijacks host machinery to replicate its genome and synthesize viral proteins.

nodes.label: Uncoating
nodes.summary: Uncoating releases the viral genome from its capsid inside the host cell.

nodes.label: Viral latency
nodes.summary: Some animal viruses establish a latent infection, persisting silently before reactivating.

nodes.label: Bacteriophage cycles
nodes.summary: Bacteriophages follow lytic or lysogenic pathways.

nodes.label: Lysogenic cycle
nodes.summary: In the lysogenic cycle phage DNA integrates as a prophage and is replicated passively until induced (temperate phage).

nodes.label: Lysogenic conversion
nodes.summary: A prophage can express genes that alter the host phenotype, including toxin genes that make some bacteria pathogenic (e.g., diphtheria, cholera).

nodes.label: Lytic cycle
nodes.summary: In the lytic cycle the phage immediately replicates and lyses the host, releasing progeny phages (virulent phage).

nodes.label: Prophage & induction
nodes.summary: A prophage is integrated phage DNA that can be induced by stress to enter the lytic cycle.

nodes.label: Viral structure & classification
nodes.summary: Viruses are acellular infectious particles consisting of nucleic acid in a protein coat that require a host to replicate.

nodes.label: Viral genomes
nodes.summary: Viral genomes vary in nucleic acid type, strandedness, and polarity.

nodes.label: DNA vs RNA genomes
nodes.summary: Viral genomes may be DNA or RNA, single- or double-stranded, and linear or circular.

nodes.label: Negative-sense RNA
nodes.summary: Negative-sense (-) RNA must first be transcribed into +RNA by a virus-packaged RNA polymerase before translation.

nodes.label: Positive-sense RNA
nodes.summary: Positive-sense (+) RNA can be directly translated by host ribosomes upon entry.

nodes.label: Provirus / integrase
nodes.summary: Integrase inserts retroviral cDNA into the host chromosome to form a provirus.

nodes.label: Retroviruses & reverse transcriptase
nodes.summary: Retroviruses use reverse transcriptase to convert their +RNA genome into DNA that integrates into the host genome.

nodes.label: RNA-dependent RNA polymerase
nodes.summary: RNA viruses must carry or encode an RNA-dependent RNA polymerase because host cells lack the enzyme to replicate RNA from an RNA template.

nodes.label: Small genome / encodes few proteins
nodes.summary: Viral genomes are tiny and encode only essential proteins (capsid, polymerases, regulators), relying on host machinery for everything else.

nodes.label: Are viruses alive?
nodes.summary: Viruses straddle the definition of life because they have genetic material yet lack metabolism and independent reproduction.

nodes.label: Viral structure
nodes.summary: A virion is composed of a genome, capsid, and sometimes an envelope.

nodes.label: Acellular / obligate intracellular nature
nodes.summary: Viruses are acellular obligate intracellular parasites that cannot reproduce or generate energy on their own.

nodes.label: Bacteriophage structure
nodes.summary: A bacteriophage has a head capsid, tail sheath, and tail fibers used to infect bacteria.

nodes.label: Capsid
nodes.summary: The capsid is the protein coat, made of capsomere subunits, that encloses and protects the viral genome.

nodes.label: Capsid symmetry (helical/icosahedral)
nodes.summary: Capsids self-assemble from capsomeres into helical, icosahedral, or complex symmetries, allowing a small genome to encode a large coat.

nodes.label: Viral envelope
nodes.summary: Enveloped viruses acquire a host-derived lipid membrane that makes them more vulnerable to drying and detergents.

nodes.label: Envelope glycoprotein spikes
nodes.summary: Envelope-embedded glycoprotein spikes mediate host receptor binding and entry and are key antigenic targets.

nodes.label: Host range & tropism (specificity)
nodes.summary: A virus infects only cells bearing the receptor its surface proteins recognize, defining a narrow host range and tissue tropism.

nodes.label: Naked (nonenveloped) viruses
nodes.summary: Naked viruses lack an envelope and are generally more environmentally stable than enveloped viruses.

nodes.label: Virion size
nodes.summary: Virions are much smaller than cells and are not retained by standard bacterial filters.

nodes.label: Loss of cell-cycle control: cancer
nodes.summary: Cancer arises from mutations that disrupt cell-cycle control, producing uncontrolled, immortal, invasive cell proliferation.

nodes.label: Cell-cycle dysregulation
nodes.summary: Mutations in regulatory genes free cells from cycle control.

nodes.label: Uncontrolled proliferation
nodes.summary: Cancer cells ignore checkpoints, density-dependent inhibition, and growth-signal requirements, dividing without restraint.

nodes.label: Oncogenes and proto-oncogenes
nodes.summary: Proto-oncogenes promote normal division; gain-of-function mutations convert them to oncogenes that drive excessive proliferation.

nodes.label: Ras and signaling oncogenes
nodes.summary: A constitutively active Ras GTPase or hyperactive growth-factor receptor signals division continuously even without external growth factors.

nodes.label: Tumor suppressor genes
nodes.summary: Tumor suppressor genes (e.g., p53, Rb) restrain division; loss-of-function mutations remove the brakes on the cycle.

nodes.label: Multistep / two-hit progression
nodes.summary: Cancer typically requires accumulation of multiple mutations, including inactivation of both alleles of a tumor suppressor (the two-hit hypothesis).

nodes.label: Properties of cancer cells
nodes.summary: Cancer cells differ from normal cells in growth and behavior.

nodes.label: Anchorage independence
nodes.summary: Cancer cells can divide without attaching to a surface, unlike normal anchorage-dependent cells.

nodes.label: Angiogenesis
nodes.summary: Tumors induce new blood-vessel growth (angiogenesis) to supply oxygen and nutrients needed for continued growth.

nodes.label: Benign vs malignant tumors
nodes.summary: Benign tumors stay localized and encapsulated; malignant tumors invade surrounding tissue and can spread.

nodes.label: Loss of density-dependent inhibition
nodes.summary: Normal cells stop dividing when crowded (contact inhibition); cancer cells continue, piling into masses.

nodes.label: Evasion of apoptosis
nodes.summary: Cancer cells acquire mutations that disable apoptotic pathways, allowing damaged cells to survive and divide.

nodes.label: Growth-factor independence
nodes.summary: Cancer cells divide without external growth-factor signals, often by self-stimulation or constitutively active signaling.

nodes.label: Replicative immortality
nodes.summary: Cancer cells evade replicative senescence, often by reactivating telomerase to maintain telomeres.

nodes.label: Metastasis
nodes.summary: Malignant cells detach and travel via blood or lymph to seed secondary tumors at distant sites.

nodes.label: The eukaryotic cell cycle
nodes.summary: The cell cycle is the ordered sequence of events (G1, S, G2, M, plus G0) by which a cell grows, replicates its DNA, and divides.

nodes.label: Cell cycle checkpoints
nodes.summary: Checkpoints verify conditions are met before allowing the cycle to advance.

nodes.label: DNA-damage response and repair pause
nodes.summary: Detected DNA damage halts the cycle to permit repair, preventing replication or segregation of damaged DNA.

nodes.label: E2F transcription factors
nodes.summary: E2F transcription factors, released when Rb is phosphorylated, activate genes required for S-phase entry and DNA replication.

nodes.label: G1/S checkpoint
nodes.summary: The G1 checkpoint verifies cell size, nutrients, growth signals, and DNA integrity before committing to DNA replication.

nodes.label: G2/M checkpoint
nodes.summary: The G2 checkpoint confirms DNA has been fully and correctly replicated before allowing entry into mitosis.

nodes.label: M (spindle assembly) checkpoint
nodes.summary: The spindle-assembly checkpoint at metaphase verifies every kinetochore is attached to spindle fibers before anaphase begins.

nodes.label: p53 tumor suppressor
nodes.summary: p53 senses DNA damage and arrests the cycle (mainly at G1) for repair or triggers apoptosis if damage is irreparable.

nodes.label: Retinoblastoma (Rb) protein
nodes.summary: Rb restrains the G1/S transition by sequestering E2F transcription factors until it is phosphorylated by cyclin-CDK.

nodes.label: Cell cycle control system
nodes.summary: Progression is driven by cyclin/CDK complexes and policed by checkpoints.

nodes.label: Anchorage dependence (normal cells)
nodes.summary: Normal cells must attach to a substrate or extracellular matrix to divide; without anchorage they arrest the cycle.

nodes.label: CDK inhibitors (CKIs)
nodes.summary: CDK inhibitor proteins (e.g., p21, p27) bind and block cyclin-CDK complexes to halt progression, often downstream of p53.

nodes.label: Cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs)
nodes.summary: CDKs are kinases that are inactive alone and become active when bound by their specific cyclin, then phosphorylate target proteins to advance the cycle.

nodes.label: Cyclin-CDK complex
nodes.summary: A cyclin binds its CDK to form an active complex (e.g., a maturation/mitosis-promoting factor) that triggers a specific phase transition.

nodes.label: Cyclin degradation and oscillation
nodes.summary: Targeted ubiquitin-mediated degradation of cyclins inactivates CDKs and resets the cycle, producing the cyclical rise and fall that times transitions.

nodes.label: Cyclins
nodes.summary: Cyclins are regulatory proteins whose concentration rises and falls cyclically to drive transitions between cell-cycle phases.

nodes.label: Density-dependent inhibition (normal cells)
nodes.summary: Normal cells stop dividing when they crowd a surface (contact inhibition), arresting in G1 until space and signals return.

nodes.label: G1/S transition control
nodes.summary: G1 cyclin-CDK activity drives the cell past the restriction point and into S phase by promoting DNA-replication machinery.

nodes.label: Growth factors and external signals
nodes.summary: External growth factors (e.g., PDGF) bind receptors to trigger signaling that drives a quiescent cell past the G1 restriction point into the cycle.

nodes.label: Maturation/M-phase-promoting factor (MPF)
nodes.summary: MPF is the cyclin B-CDK1 complex that triggers entry into mitosis and is destroyed by cyclin degradation to allow exit from M phase.

nodes.label: Restriction (R) point
nodes.summary: The restriction point late in G1 is the commitment point after which a cell will complete the cycle independent of external growth signals.

nodes.label: Phases of the cell cycle
nodes.summary: Interphase (G1, S, G2) prepares the cell and the mitotic (M) phase divides it.

nodes.label: G0 phase (quiescence)
nodes.summary: G0 is a non-dividing resting/quiescent state that cells like mature neurons and muscle may enter, exiting the active cycle from G1.

nodes.label: G1 phase (first gap / growth)
nodes.summary: In G1 the cell grows, synthesizes proteins and organelles, and has one copy (2N) of each chromosome as a single chromatid.

nodes.label: G2 phase (second gap)
nodes.summary: In G2 the cell continues to grow, duplicates organelles and centrosomes, and synthesizes proteins (e.g., tubulin) needed for mitosis.

nodes.label: Interphase
nodes.summary: Interphase comprises G1, S, and G2 and is the period of growth and DNA replication between divisions, occupying ~90% of the cycle.

nodes.label: M phase (mitotic phase)
nodes.summary: M phase is the division phase comprising mitosis (nuclear division) and cytokinesis (cytoplasmic division), the shortest part of the cycle.

nodes.label: Ploidy and DNA content through the cycle
nodes.summary: Chromosome number (ploidy) stays 2N through interphase while DNA content goes 2C (G1) to 4C (after S) and back to 2C after division.

nodes.label: S phase (DNA synthesis)
nodes.summary: During S phase DNA is replicated so each chromosome becomes two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere; the cell stays diploid (2N) but its DNA content doubles from 2C to 4C.

nodes.label: Telomeres, telomerase, and replicative limit
nodes.summary: Telomere shortening limits the number of divisions a normal somatic cell can undergo.

nodes.label: Telomere shortening and the Hayflick limit
nodes.summary: Because DNA polymerase cannot fully replicate chromosome ends, telomeres shorten each division until cells reach replicative senescence (the Hayflick limit).

nodes.label: Telomerase
nodes.summary: Telomerase is a reverse-transcriptase enzyme that extends telomeres, active in germ cells, stem cells, and most cancers but largely silent in somatic cells.

nodes.label: Telomeres
nodes.summary: Telomeres are repetitive non-coding DNA caps at chromosome ends that protect coding sequence and shorten with each round of replication.

nodes.label: Programmed cell death (apoptosis)
nodes.summary: Apoptosis is regulated, programmed cell death essential for development and homeostasis, distinct from necrosis.

nodes.label: Apoptosis mechanism
nodes.summary: Apoptosis dismantles the cell in an orderly, non-inflammatory way.

nodes.label: Apoptotic bodies and phagocytosis
nodes.summary: The dying cell packages itself into membrane-bound apoptotic bodies cleared by phagocytes without inflammation.

nodes.label: Bcl-2 family regulators
nodes.summary: Pro- and anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 family proteins balance to control mitochondrial release of cytochrome c and the decision to die.

nodes.label: Caspases
nodes.summary: Caspases are proteases that execute apoptosis by cleaving cellular substrates in a regulated cascade.

nodes.label: Apoptosis definition and features
nodes.summary: Apoptosis is genetically programmed cell suicide marked by cell shrinkage, nuclear/DNA fragmentation, and membrane blebbing.

nodes.label: Role in development and homeostasis
nodes.summary: Apoptosis sculpts structures (e.g., interdigital webbing) and removes damaged or excess cells to maintain tissue balance.

nodes.label: Extrinsic (death-receptor) pathway
nodes.summary: External death-ligands binding cell-surface death receptors (e.g., Fas) trigger caspase activation from outside the cell.

nodes.label: Intrinsic (mitochondrial) pathway
nodes.summary: Internal stress (DNA damage) permeabilizes mitochondria, releasing cytochrome c to form the apoptosome that activates caspases.

nodes.label: Necrosis (contrast)
nodes.summary: Necrosis is uncontrolled, injury-driven death contrasting with apoptosis.

nodes.label: Necrosis vs apoptosis
nodes.summary: Necrosis is unregulated death from injury causing cell swelling, lysis, and inflammation, unlike orderly non-inflammatory apoptosis.

nodes.label: Cell differentiation and developmental fate
nodes.summary: Differentiation is the process by which an unspecialized cell acquires a specialized structure and function through differential gene expression.

nodes.label: Molecular basis of differentiation
nodes.summary: Differentiation arises from selective gene expression, not loss of DNA.

nodes.label: Determination
nodes.summary: Determination is the irreversible commitment of a cell to a particular fate that precedes overt differentiation.

nodes.label: Differential gene expression
nodes.summary: All somatic cells share the same genome; differentiation results from expressing different subsets of genes.

nodes.label: Epigenetic regulation of fate
nodes.summary: Stable, heritable changes such as DNA methylation and histone modification lock in cell-type-specific gene expression without altering the DNA sequence.

nodes.label: Genomic equivalence and totipotency evidence
nodes.summary: Cloning (e.g., nuclear transplantation) shows differentiated nuclei retain a complete genome capable of directing a whole organism.

nodes.label: Cell potency hierarchy
nodes.summary: Cells range from totipotent to unipotent in their developmental potential.

nodes.label: Multipotent
nodes.summary: Multipotent cells (e.g., hematopoietic stem cells) can differentiate into a limited range of related cell types.

nodes.label: Pluripotent
nodes.summary: Pluripotent cells (e.g., embryonic stem cells) can become any of the three germ-layer cell types but not extraembryonic tissue.

nodes.label: Decline of potency with development
nodes.summary: As development proceeds cells progressively lose potency, narrowing from totipotent to pluripotent to multipotent to fully differentiated states.

nodes.label: Totipotent
nodes.summary: Totipotent cells (e.g., the zygote and early blastomeres) can give rise to any cell type plus extraembryonic tissues.

nodes.label: Unipotent
nodes.summary: Unipotent cells can produce only one cell type but retain self-renewal capacity.

nodes.label: Signals directing differentiation
nodes.summary: Intrinsic determinants and extrinsic signals guide cell fate.

nodes.label: Apoptosis in patterning
nodes.summary: Programmed cell death shapes developing structures (e.g., removing webbing between digits) as part of normal morphogenesis.

nodes.label: Cytoplasmic determinants
nodes.summary: Maternal cytoplasmic determinants unevenly distributed in the egg influence the fate of daughter cells after division.

nodes.label: Induction
nodes.summary: Induction is the process by which one group of cells influences the developmental fate of neighboring cells via signaling molecules.

nodes.label: Master regulatory and homeotic genes
nodes.summary: Master regulatory genes (including homeotic/Hox genes) encode transcription factors that direct body-segment identity and tissue patterning.

nodes.label: Morphogens and concentration gradients
nodes.summary: Morphogens are signaling molecules whose concentration gradient assigns different fates to cells at different positions.

nodes.label: Tissue-specific transcription factors
nodes.summary: Transcription factors activate cell-type-specific gene sets to drive and maintain the differentiated state.

nodes.label: Gametogenesis and the reproductive cycle
nodes.summary: Gametogenesis is the production of mature gametes via meiosis, differing in timing and product number between sexes.

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis vs oogenesis
nodes.summary: The two gametogenic pathways differ in number, size, and timing of products.

nodes.label: Key differences (sperm vs egg formation)
nodes.summary: Spermatogenesis is continuous and yields four equal sperm; oogenesis is cyclic, arrested, and yields one large ovum plus polar bodies.

nodes.label: Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Oogenesis produces one large ovum and polar bodies from one oogonium.

nodes.label: Meiotic arrest in oogenesis
nodes.summary: Primary oocytes arrest in prophase I until ovulation and complete meiosis II only upon fertilization.

nodes.label: Polar bodies
nodes.summary: Polar bodies are small non-functional cells discarded during oogenesis to conserve cytoplasm in the ovum.

nodes.label: Oogenesis process and asymmetry
nodes.summary: Oogenesis yields one functional ovum (retaining most cytoplasm) plus polar bodies, with unequal cytoplasmic division.

nodes.label: Oogenic cell stages
nodes.summary: Oogonium to primary oocyte (meiosis I) to secondary oocyte plus first polar body (meiosis II completed at fertilization) to ovum.

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis
nodes.summary: Spermatogenesis produces four functional sperm from one spermatogonium.

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis process and yield
nodes.summary: A diploid spermatogonium yields four equally sized haploid spermatids that mature into sperm, occurring continuously after puberty in the seminiferous tubules.

nodes.label: Spermiogenesis (sperm maturation)
nodes.summary: Round spermatids mature into motile spermatozoa by shedding cytoplasm and forming a flagellum and acrosome.

nodes.label: Spermatogenic cell stages
nodes.summary: Spermatogonium to primary spermatocyte (meiosis I) to secondary spermatocyte (meiosis II) to spermatid to spermatozoon.

nodes.label: Meiosis and gametogenesis
nodes.summary: Meiosis is two successive divisions that produce four haploid, genetically diverse gametes from one diploid germ cell.

nodes.label: Mitosis vs meiosis comparison
nodes.summary: The two division types differ in number of divisions, products, and pairing behavior.

nodes.label: Key differences (mitosis vs meiosis)
nodes.summary: Mitosis = 1 division, 2 identical diploid cells, no synapsis; meiosis = 2 divisions, 4 diverse haploid cells, with synapsis and crossing over.

nodes.label: Metaphase alignment difference
nodes.summary: In mitosis and meiosis II chromosomes line up single-file, whereas in metaphase I homologs line up as paired tetrads.

nodes.label: Errors in meiosis
nodes.summary: Nondisjunction produces aneuploid gametes.

nodes.label: Aneuploidy and nondisjunction outcomes
nodes.summary: Aneuploidy (e.g., trisomy 21) results when nondisjunction yields a zygote with an extra or missing chromosome.

nodes.label: Nondisjunction
nodes.summary: Nondisjunction is failure of homologs (meiosis I) or sister chromatids (meiosis II) to separate, producing gametes with abnormal chromosome number.

nodes.label: Meiosis I
nodes.summary: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes and is where genetic recombination occurs.

nodes.label: Anaphase I
nodes.summary: In anaphase I homologous chromosomes separate to opposite poles while sister chromatids stay joined.

nodes.label: Chiasma
nodes.summary: A chiasma is the visible X-shaped point of crossing over where non-sister chromatids exchange DNA.

nodes.label: Crossing over
nodes.summary: Crossing over is the reciprocal exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of homologs, creating recombinant chromosomes.

nodes.label: Independent assortment
nodes.summary: Random orientation of each tetrad at metaphase I assorts maternal and paternal homologs independently, yielding 2^n combinations.

nodes.label: Metaphase I
nodes.summary: In metaphase I tetrads (paired homologs) align at the metaphase plate, with homologs facing opposite poles.

nodes.label: Prophase I
nodes.summary: In prophase I homologs pair (synapsis) forming tetrads/bivalents and undergo crossing over at chiasmata.

nodes.label: Synapsis and the synaptonemal complex
nodes.summary: Synapsis is the pairing of homologous chromosomes held together by the protein synaptonemal complex.

nodes.label: Telophase I and cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Telophase I yields two haploid cells, each chromosome still consisting of two sister chromatids.

nodes.label: Tetrad / bivalent
nodes.summary: A tetrad (bivalent) is a paired set of two homologous chromosomes comprising four chromatids.

nodes.label: Meiosis II
nodes.summary: Meiosis II separates sister chromatids in each haploid cell without further DNA replication.

nodes.label: Anaphase II
nodes.summary: In anaphase II centromeres split and sister chromatids separate to opposite poles, as in mitosis.

nodes.label: Four haploid products
nodes.summary: Meiosis II completes the process, producing four haploid cells each with unreplicated single-chromatid chromosomes.

nodes.label: Metaphase II
nodes.summary: In metaphase II individual chromosomes (sister-chromatid pairs) align single-file at the metaphase plate.

nodes.label: No S phase between divisions
nodes.summary: There is no DNA replication between meiosis I and II, so meiosis II begins with already-replicated haploid cells.

nodes.label: Prophase II
nodes.summary: In prophase II chromosomes recondense and a new spindle forms in each haploid cell, with no pairing or crossing over.

nodes.label: Telophase II and cytokinesis
nodes.summary: In telophase II nuclear envelopes reform and cytokinesis splits each cell, finishing with four haploid daughter cells.

nodes.label: Overview of meiosis
nodes.summary: One round of DNA replication followed by two divisions halves the chromosome number.

nodes.label: Definition and outcome
nodes.summary: Meiosis produces four haploid (1N) gametes that are genetically distinct from each other and the parent.

nodes.label: Diploid and haploid
nodes.summary: Diploid (2N) cells carry two sets of chromosomes; haploid (1N) gametes carry one, restored to 2N by fertilization.

nodes.label: Homologous chromosomes
nodes.summary: Homologous chromosomes are a maternal-paternal pair with the same genes at the same loci but possibly different alleles.

nodes.label: Reductional vs equational division
nodes.summary: Meiosis I is reductional (2N to 1N, separating homologs) and meiosis II is equational (separating sister chromatids, like mitosis).

nodes.label: One replication, two divisions
nodes.summary: A single S-phase DNA replication is followed by two consecutive divisions (meiosis I and II), which is why chromosome number is halved.

nodes.label: Sources of genetic variation
nodes.summary: Meiosis and fertilization generate genetic diversity.

nodes.label: Independent assortment as a variation source
nodes.summary: Independent assortment of homologs produces 2^n possible gamete chromosome combinations (2^23 in humans).

nodes.label: Random fertilization
nodes.summary: Random union of any sperm with any egg multiplies diversity beyond that generated within meiosis.

nodes.label: Recombination as a variation source
nodes.summary: Crossing over shuffles alleles between homologs, generating new combinations not present in either parent chromosome.

nodes.label: Mitosis (somatic cell division)
nodes.summary: Mitosis divides one diploid nucleus into two genetically identical diploid daughter nuclei for growth and repair.

nodes.label: Overview and purpose of mitosis
nodes.summary: Mitosis produces two identical diploid cells from one parent cell.

nodes.label: Definition and outcome
nodes.summary: Mitosis is nuclear division yielding two daughter nuclei genetically identical to the parent and to each other.

nodes.label: Karyokinesis vs cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Mitosis proper (karyokinesis) is division of the nucleus; cytokinesis is the separate division of the cytoplasm that completes cell division.

nodes.label: Biological purpose
nodes.summary: Mitosis enables growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction by producing clones of somatic cells.

nodes.label: Somatic vs germ cells
nodes.summary: Mitosis occurs in somatic (body) cells, whereas meiosis is restricted to germ cells that produce gametes.

nodes.label: Phases of mitosis
nodes.summary: Prophase, prometaphase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase progress nuclear division.

nodes.label: Anaphase
nodes.summary: In anaphase the centromeres split and sister chromatids are pulled to opposite poles, becoming individual chromosomes.

nodes.label: Mechanism of chromosome movement
nodes.summary: Kinetochore microtubules shorten to pull chromatids poleward (anaphase A) while polar microtubules elongate to push poles apart (anaphase B).

nodes.label: Cleavage furrow vs cell plate
nodes.summary: Animal cells pinch apart with a cleavage furrow while plant cells build a new cell plate from vesicles down the middle.

nodes.label: Cytokinesis
nodes.summary: Cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm via an actin-myosin contractile ring (cleavage furrow) in animal cells, yielding two daughter cells.

nodes.label: Metaphase
nodes.summary: In metaphase chromosomes align single-file along the metaphase plate (cell equator) with sister chromatids facing opposite poles.

nodes.label: Prometaphase
nodes.summary: In prometaphase the nuclear envelope breaks down and kinetochore microtubules attach to chromosomes.

nodes.label: Prophase
nodes.summary: In prophase chromatin condenses into visible chromosomes, the spindle begins forming, and centrosomes migrate to opposite poles.

nodes.label: Telophase
nodes.summary: In telophase chromosomes decondense, nuclear envelopes reform around each set, and the spindle disassembles.

nodes.label: Structures of mitotic division
nodes.summary: Centrosomes, spindle fibers, kinetochores, and chromatids organize chromosome segregation.

nodes.label: Astral microtubules / asters
nodes.summary: Astral microtubules radiate from centrosomes to the cell cortex to position the spindle within the cell.

nodes.label: Centromere
nodes.summary: The centromere is the constricted chromosomal region holding sister chromatids together and bearing the kinetochore.

nodes.label: Centrosome and centrioles
nodes.summary: The centrosome (containing a pair of centrioles in animal cells) is the microtubule-organizing center that nucleates the spindle.

nodes.label: Chromatin condensation
nodes.summary: Loosely packed interphase chromatin condenses into compact, visible chromosomes at the start of mitosis to allow clean segregation.

nodes.label: Chromosome and sister chromatids
nodes.summary: A replicated chromosome consists of two identical sister chromatids joined at the centromere.

nodes.label: Cohesin and sister-chromatid cohesion
nodes.summary: Cohesin protein complexes hold sister chromatids together until separase cleaves them at the metaphase-to-anaphase transition.

nodes.label: Kinetochore
nodes.summary: The kinetochore is a protein complex assembled on the centromere where kinetochore microtubules attach.

nodes.label: Mitotic spindle apparatus
nodes.summary: The spindle is the array of microtubules (kinetochore, polar/overlap, and astral fibers) that segregates chromosomes.

nodes.label: Prokaryotic cell division (binary fission)
nodes.summary: Prokaryotes divide by binary fission, a simpler asexual process without mitosis or a true spindle.

nodes.label: Binary fission
nodes.summary: Binary fission replicates a single circular chromosome and partitions it into two identical cells.

nodes.label: Binary fission process
nodes.summary: The circular chromosome replicates from a single origin, copies move to opposite ends, and the cell pinches in two via a septum (FtsZ ring).

nodes.label: Binary fission vs mitosis
nodes.summary: Binary fission lacks a nucleus, spindle apparatus, and distinct phases; mitosis is the more complex eukaryotic equivalent with chromosome condensation and a spindle.

nodes.label: Cell specialization and tissue organization
nodes.summary: Specialized cells organize into the four primary tissue types and coordinate through intercellular communication.

nodes.label: Cell-cell communication and junctions
nodes.summary: Specialized cells coordinate via signaling and junctions.

nodes.label: Adherens junctions
nodes.summary: Adherens junctions link cells via cadherins connected to the actin cytoskeleton, forming an adhesion belt below tight junctions.

nodes.label: Desmosomes
nodes.summary: Desmosomes anchor adjacent cells together for mechanical strength via intermediate filaments.

nodes.label: Gap junctions
nodes.summary: Gap junctions are channels allowing direct passage of ions and small molecules between adjacent cells for coordination.

nodes.label: Plasmodesmata (plant cells)
nodes.summary: Plasmodesmata are cytoplasmic channels through plant cell walls connecting adjacent cells, analogous to gap junctions.

nodes.label: Modes of intercellular signaling
nodes.summary: Cells communicate via autocrine, paracrine, endocrine, and direct contact (juxtacrine) signaling.

nodes.label: Tight junctions
nodes.summary: Tight junctions seal adjacent cells to prevent paracellular leakage and maintain epithelial polarity.

nodes.label: Extracellular matrix and basement membrane
nodes.summary: The ECM provides structural and signaling context for specialized cells.

nodes.label: Extracellular matrix (ECM)
nodes.summary: The ECM is a network of secreted proteins (collagen, elastin) and proteoglycans that supports cells and conveys signals affecting growth and differentiation.

nodes.label: Integrins and cell-matrix attachment
nodes.summary: Integrins are transmembrane receptors anchoring cells to the ECM and transmitting signals that regulate survival, division, and migration.

nodes.label: Germ layers and their derivatives
nodes.summary: Three embryonic germ layers give rise to all adult tissues.

nodes.label: Ectoderm
nodes.summary: Ectoderm forms the epidermis, nervous system, and sensory structures.

nodes.label: Endoderm
nodes.summary: Endoderm forms the epithelial lining of the digestive and respiratory tracts and associated organs (liver, pancreas).

nodes.label: Mesoderm
nodes.summary: Mesoderm forms muscle, bone, blood, connective tissue, and the urogenital and circulatory systems.

nodes.label: Four primary tissue types
nodes.summary: Cells specialize into epithelial, connective, muscle, and nervous tissue.

nodes.label: Connective tissue
nodes.summary: Connective tissue supports and binds other tissues using cells embedded in extracellular matrix.

nodes.label: Epithelial tissue
nodes.summary: Epithelial tissue covers surfaces and lines cavities, providing protection, absorption, and secretion.

nodes.label: Muscle tissue
nodes.summary: Muscle tissue (skeletal, cardiac, smooth) is specialized for contraction and movement.

nodes.label: Nervous tissue
nodes.summary: Nervous tissue is specialized for generating and transmitting electrical signals.

nodes.label: Stem cells and regeneration
nodes.summary: Stem cells are undifferentiated cells defined by self-renewal and the capacity to differentiate into specialized cell types.

nodes.label: Defining properties of stem cells
nodes.summary: Self-renewal and potency define stem cells.

nodes.label: Asymmetric division
nodes.summary: A stem cell can divide asymmetrically to yield one stem cell and one differentiating daughter, balancing renewal and supply.

nodes.label: Stem cell niche
nodes.summary: The niche is the specialized local microenvironment whose signals maintain stem cells in an undifferentiated, self-renewing state.

nodes.label: Potency of stem cells
nodes.summary: A stem cell's defining second property is potency: the capacity to give rise to one or more specialized cell types.

nodes.label: Self-renewal
nodes.summary: Stem cells can divide indefinitely while maintaining an undifferentiated population.

nodes.label: Regeneration and stem-cell applications
nodes.summary: Stem cells underlie tissue renewal, regeneration, and therapeutic potential.

nodes.label: Therapeutic and regenerative uses
nodes.summary: Stem cells (e.g., bone-marrow transplants) and reprogramming approaches are used to replace damaged tissue and model disease.

nodes.label: Tissue renewal and repair
nodes.summary: Resident stem cells continually replace lost cells (e.g., skin, gut epithelium, blood) and drive wound repair.

nodes.label: Types and sources of stem cells
nodes.summary: Stem cells differ by source and potency.

nodes.label: Adult (somatic) stem cells
nodes.summary: Adult stem cells reside in tissues (e.g., bone marrow) and are typically multipotent, replenishing specific lineages.

nodes.label: Embryonic stem cells
nodes.summary: Embryonic stem cells derive from the inner cell mass of the blastocyst and are pluripotent.

nodes.label: Hematopoietic stem cells
nodes.summary: Hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow are multipotent and give rise to all blood cell lineages.

nodes.label: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs)
nodes.summary: iPSCs are differentiated cells reprogrammed to a pluripotent state by forced expression of key transcription factors.

nodes.label: Central Nervous System
nodes.summary: The CNS comprises the brain and spinal cord and integrates and coordinates all neural activity.

nodes.label: Brain Regions
nodes.summary: The brain divides into forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, each with specialized functions.

nodes.label: Amygdala
nodes.summary: The amygdala processes fear, aggression, and the emotional salience of stimuli.

nodes.label: Basal ganglia
nodes.summary: The basal ganglia regulate the initiation and smoothing of voluntary movement.

nodes.label: Brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla)
nodes.summary: The brainstem controls vital autonomic functions such as breathing, heart rate, and reflexes.

nodes.label: Cerebellum
nodes.summary: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning.

nodes.label: Cerebral cortex
nodes.summary: The cerebral cortex governs higher functions including perception, cognition, and voluntary movement.

nodes.label: Corpus callosum
nodes.summary: The corpus callosum is the white-matter tract connecting and coordinating the two cerebral hemispheres.

nodes.label: Forebrain divisions
nodes.summary: The forebrain (prosencephalon) includes the telencephalon (cerebrum) and diencephalon (thalamus, hypothalamus).

nodes.label: Hindbrain (rhombencephalon)
nodes.summary: The hindbrain includes the cerebellum, pons, and medulla controlling balance and vital functions.

nodes.label: Hippocampus
nodes.summary: The hippocampus is essential for forming and consolidating new long-term memories.

nodes.label: Hypothalamus
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus maintains homeostasis and links the nervous system to the endocrine system via the pituitary.

nodes.label: Limbic system
nodes.summary: The limbic system, including the amygdala and hippocampus, governs emotion and memory.

nodes.label: Cortical lobes (frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital)
nodes.summary: The four cortical lobes specialize in executive, somatosensory, auditory/memory, and visual functions respectively.

nodes.label: Medulla oblongata
nodes.summary: The medulla controls involuntary functions including heart rate, breathing, and blood pressure.

nodes.label: Midbrain (mesencephalon)
nodes.summary: The midbrain relays visual and auditory reflexes and contains dopaminergic motor nuclei.

nodes.label: Pons
nodes.summary: The pons relays signals between cortex and cerebellum and helps regulate breathing.

nodes.label: Reticular formation
nodes.summary: The reticular formation regulates arousal, sleep-wake cycles, and consciousness.

nodes.label: Thalamus
nodes.summary: The thalamus is the relay station that routes sensory information (except smell) to the cortex.

nodes.label: Spinal Cord and Reflexes
nodes.summary: The spinal cord relays signals and mediates rapid reflex arcs.

nodes.label: Cord gray/white arrangement
nodes.summary: In the spinal cord gray matter is central (butterfly shape) and white matter is peripheral, opposite the brain.

nodes.label: Monosynaptic reflex (stretch/knee-jerk)
nodes.summary: The monosynaptic stretch reflex involves one synapse between a sensory and a motor neuron.

nodes.label: Polysynaptic reflex (withdrawal)
nodes.summary: Polysynaptic reflexes such as withdrawal include one or more interneurons between sensory and motor neurons.

nodes.label: Reflex arc
nodes.summary: A reflex arc links a sensory receptor to an effector through the spinal cord with minimal brain involvement.

nodes.label: Dorsal and ventral roots
nodes.summary: Sensory fibers enter via the dorsal root and motor fibers exit via the ventral root.

nodes.label: Ascending and descending tracts
nodes.summary: Ascending tracts carry sensory signals up and descending tracts carry motor commands down the cord.

nodes.label: CNS Organization
nodes.summary: The CNS is organized into gray and white matter and protected by meninges and CSF.

nodes.label: Blood-brain barrier
nodes.summary: Tight junctions of brain capillary endothelium selectively restrict substances entering the CNS.

nodes.label: Cerebrospinal fluid
nodes.summary: CSF cushions the CNS, supplies nutrients, and removes waste from the ventricles and subarachnoid space.

nodes.label: Embryonic brain divisions (3 then 5 vesicles)
nodes.summary: The neural tube forms forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, which subdivide into five secondary vesicles.

nodes.label: Gray matter vs white matter
nodes.summary: Gray matter contains cell bodies while white matter contains myelinated axon tracts.

nodes.label: Meninges
nodes.summary: The dura, arachnoid, and pia mater protect the brain and spinal cord.

nodes.label: Ventricles
nodes.summary: The brain's ventricular system holds CSF produced by the choroid plexus and ependymal cells.

nodes.label: Coordination and Homeostasis
nodes.summary: The nervous and endocrine systems integrate to maintain homeostasis across organ systems.

nodes.label: Nervous vs Endocrine Control
nodes.summary: Nervous and endocrine systems differ in speed, duration, and breadth of their signals.

nodes.label: Neuroendocrine integration
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus and adrenal medulla illustrate direct coupling of neural and endocrine signaling.

nodes.label: Speed vs duration tradeoff
nodes.summary: Neural signals are fast and brief while hormonal signals are slower but longer-lasting and widespread.

nodes.label: Homeostatic Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Feedback loops keep internal variables within set ranges.

nodes.label: Blood glucose regulation
nodes.summary: Insulin and glucagon act antagonistically to keep blood glucose within a narrow range.

nodes.label: Calcium homeostasis
nodes.summary: PTH and calcitonin act antagonistically to keep blood calcium within a tight range.

nodes.label: HPA axis
nodes.summary: The hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis releases CRH, ACTH, and cortisol to mediate the slower stress response.

nodes.label: Osmoregulation and blood pressure
nodes.summary: ADH and aldosterone regulate water and electrolyte balance to control blood pressure and osmolarity.

nodes.label: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
nodes.summary: Low blood pressure triggers renin release, generating angiotensin II and aldosterone to restore volume and pressure.

nodes.label: Set point and feedback loops
nodes.summary: Homeostasis maintains a regulated variable near a set point using sensors, integrators, and effectors.

nodes.label: Integrated stress response
nodes.summary: Stress activates both sympathetic output and the HPA axis to mobilize energy and prepare the body.

nodes.label: Thermoregulation
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus regulates body temperature via shivering, sweating, and vasomotor responses.

nodes.label: Endocrine System
nodes.summary: The endocrine system uses hormones secreted into the blood to regulate distant target organs.

nodes.label: Peripheral Endocrine Glands
nodes.summary: Peripheral glands secrete hormones regulating metabolism, calcium, stress, and reproduction.

nodes.label: Adrenal androgens
nodes.summary: The adrenal cortex secretes weak androgens (e.g., DHEA) that contribute to secondary sex characteristics.

nodes.label: Adrenal cortex (cortisol, aldosterone, sex steroids)
nodes.summary: The adrenal cortex secretes glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and androgens.

nodes.label: Adrenal medulla (epinephrine/norepinephrine)
nodes.summary: The adrenal medulla releases epinephrine and norepinephrine to amplify the fight-or-flight response.

nodes.label: Aldosterone
nodes.summary: Aldosterone promotes renal Na+ reabsorption and K+ secretion, raising blood pressure.

nodes.label: Calcitonin
nodes.summary: Calcitonin from the thyroid lowers blood calcium by inhibiting osteoclasts.

nodes.label: Cortisol
nodes.summary: Cortisol is a stress glucocorticoid that raises blood glucose and suppresses immune function.

nodes.label: GI hormones (gastrin, secretin, CCK)
nodes.summary: The digestive tract secretes gastrin, secretin, and cholecystokinin to regulate digestion.

nodes.label: Glucagon (alpha cells)
nodes.summary: Glucagon from pancreatic alpha cells raises blood glucose via glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis.

nodes.label: Gonadal hormones
nodes.summary: The testes secrete testosterone and the ovaries secrete estrogen and progesterone.

nodes.label: Atrial natriuretic peptide
nodes.summary: ANP from the heart promotes sodium and water excretion to lower blood pressure.

nodes.label: Insulin (beta cells)
nodes.summary: Insulin from pancreatic beta cells lowers blood glucose by promoting uptake and storage.

nodes.label: Kidney hormones (EPO, renin)
nodes.summary: The kidney secretes erythropoietin to stimulate red-cell production and renin to initiate the RAAS pathway.

nodes.label: Pancreas as endocrine and exocrine gland
nodes.summary: The pancreas has endocrine islets of Langerhans and exocrine acini that secrete digestive enzymes.

nodes.label: Pineal gland (melatonin)
nodes.summary: The pineal gland secretes melatonin to regulate circadian and sleep-wake rhythms.

nodes.label: Parathyroid hormone (PTH)
nodes.summary: PTH raises blood calcium by activating osteoclasts, increasing renal reabsorption, and activating vitamin D.

nodes.label: Somatostatin (delta cells)
nodes.summary: Somatostatin from pancreatic delta cells inhibits insulin and glucagon secretion.

nodes.label: Thymus (thymosin)
nodes.summary: The thymus secretes thymosin that supports T-lymphocyte maturation.

nodes.label: Thyroid hormone (T3/T4)
nodes.summary: Thyroid hormone raises basal metabolic rate and is required for normal development.

nodes.label: Vitamin D (calcitriol)
nodes.summary: Active vitamin D raises blood calcium by increasing intestinal calcium absorption.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic-Pituitary Axis
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus and pituitary form the master control center coordinating endocrine output.

nodes.label: ACTH
nodes.summary: ACTH stimulates the adrenal cortex to release cortisol.

nodes.label: ADH (vasopressin)
nodes.summary: ADH increases water reabsorption in the kidney collecting ducts to conserve water.

nodes.label: Anterior pituitary hormones
nodes.summary: The anterior pituitary secretes FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, prolactin, and growth hormone (FLAT PEG).

nodes.label: Growth hormone
nodes.summary: Growth hormone promotes growth and metabolism, acting partly via IGF-1 from the liver.

nodes.label: Gonadotropins (FSH and LH)
nodes.summary: FSH and LH from the anterior pituitary regulate gametogenesis and gonadal sex-hormone secretion.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus secretes releasing and inhibiting hormones that control the anterior pituitary.

nodes.label: Oxytocin
nodes.summary: Oxytocin stimulates uterine contraction during labor and milk ejection during nursing.

nodes.label: Hypophyseal portal system
nodes.summary: A portal blood system carries hypothalamic hormones directly to the anterior pituitary.

nodes.label: Posterior pituitary hormones
nodes.summary: The posterior pituitary stores and releases hypothalamus-made ADH and oxytocin.

nodes.label: Posterior pituitary is neural tissue
nodes.summary: The posterior pituitary is a downgrowth of neural tissue that stores hormones made by hypothalamic neurons.

nodes.label: Prolactin
nodes.summary: Prolactin from the anterior pituitary stimulates milk production and is tonically inhibited by dopamine.

nodes.label: Tropic vs direct hormones
