nodes.summary: A correct inference must be traceable to specific passage support; if it cannot be tied to the text, it is wrong.

nodes.label: Text Structure & Organization
nodes.summary: Understanding how a passage is organized and how its parts fit together.

nodes.label: Relationships Between Ideas
nodes.summary: Mapping how the ideas within a passage connect logically.

nodes.label: Tracing the Flow of the Argument
nodes.summary: Following how the argument moves from premise to premise to conclusion across paragraphs is foundational to structure questions.

nodes.label: Hierarchy and Subordination of Ideas
nodes.summary: Ideas sit at different levels—governing claims, sub-claims, and supporting detail—and mapping this hierarchy clarifies how the parts depend on one another.

nodes.label: Logical Connections Among Ideas
nodes.summary: Ideas relate as support, contrast, cause, elaboration, or qualification, and these links are cued by structure and transitions.

nodes.label: Transitions Between Paragraphs
nodes.summary: The connective move from one paragraph to the next (continuation, contrast, escalation, pivot) defines how the argument's blocks relate.

nodes.label: Identifying the Pivot / Turning Point
nodes.summary: Many passages turn at a contrast cue ('but', 'yet', 'however') where the author shifts to the position they actually endorse—locating this pivot is high-value.

nodes.label: Recognizing Signposting Language
nodes.summary: Signpost phrases ('first', 'on the other hand', 'as a result', 'in sum') flag the structural role of upcoming text.

nodes.label: Organizational Patterns
nodes.summary: Recognizing the rhetorical pattern by which the author arranges ideas.

nodes.label: Cause-and-Effect Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may trace causes and their consequences, and recognizing this chain aids comprehension of the argument.

nodes.label: Chronological / Sequential Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may organize material in time order or as a sequence of stages, especially in historical or narrative texts.

nodes.label: Claim-then-Support Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may state a thesis and then march through supporting evidence, or build evidence toward a delayed thesis.

nodes.label: Compare-and-Contrast Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may be organized by juxtaposing two or more views, things, or periods to highlight similarities and differences.

nodes.label: Definition / Classification Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may proceed by defining a concept and then dividing it into categories or types, organizing ideas taxonomically.

nodes.label: Descriptive / Expository Mode
nodes.summary: A passage may simply describe or explain a subject without arguing, and recognizing an expository (non-argumentative) mode reframes the reader's expectations.

nodes.label: Problem-and-Solution Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may set out a problem and then evaluate or propose solutions, framing the author's stance.

nodes.label: Thesis-Antithesis (Concession) Structure
nodes.summary: A passage may present an opposing view, then rebut it, so the reader must track which view the author endorses.

nodes.label: Passage Mapping & Big Picture
nodes.summary: Building a mental outline of the passage as a whole.

nodes.label: Locating the Relevant Text for a Question
nodes.summary: A good passage map lets the reader jump back to the exact lines a question targets rather than rereading the whole passage.

nodes.label: Building a Mental Outline
nodes.summary: An efficient reader notes the role of each paragraph to form a lightweight map that speeds later question lookups.

nodes.label: Predicting the Passage's Direction
nodes.summary: Structural cues let a reader anticipate where the argument is heading, aiding comprehension and pacing.

nodes.label: Function of a Specific Sentence
nodes.summary: Questions may ask why a particular sentence or detail was included—its rhetorical job within the paragraph.

nodes.label: Applying and Extrapolating Ideas to New Contexts
nodes.summary: This RB sub-skill takes the passage's ideas and uses them to interpret, predict, or judge situations the passage never discussed.

nodes.label: Analogical reasoning
nodes.summary: RB frequently tests the ability to recognize, complete, or evaluate analogies built on the passage's relationships.

nodes.label: Evaluating the author's own analogy
nodes.summary: When the passage itself uses an analogy, RB can ask which new case it best fits or where the analogy breaks down, testing comprehension of its limits.

nodes.label: Detecting a flawed or weak analogy
nodes.summary: An analogy fails when the cases differ in a respect that is relevant to the point being made.

nodes.label: Choosing the most analogous situation
nodes.summary: "Most analogous" questions reward the choice whose internal relationship mirrors the passage's relationship most exactly.

nodes.label: Matching the relationship in an analogy
nodes.summary: A correct analogy preserves the relationship between elements, not the identity of the elements themselves.

nodes.label: Generalizing from passage specifics
nodes.summary: Extrapolation can mean drawing the broader rule that the passage's particular instances point toward.

nodes.label: Respecting the boundary conditions of a claim
nodes.summary: A sound extrapolation honors the conditions under which the author's claim holds and does not apply it where those conditions fail.

nodes.label: Avoiding overgeneralization
nodes.summary: A trap answer stretches a limited passage point into a sweeping universal claim the evidence cannot bear.

nodes.label: Scope-shift trap (broadening or narrowing)
nodes.summary: A distractor quietly widens or narrows the population, domain, or claim of the passage, producing a statement the author's evidence does not actually license.

nodes.label: Moving from specific instances to a general rule
nodes.summary: A valid generalization captures exactly the pattern the passage's examples share without claiming more than they support.

nodes.label: Predicting author response or outcome
nodes.summary: Some RB questions ask what the author would say, do, or expect in a hypothetical the passage did not cover.

nodes.label: Judging what the author would agree or disagree with
nodes.summary: The credited statement is the one most consistent with the author's stance; the wrong ones clash with or are silent to that stance.

nodes.label: Predicting the author's reaction to a rival view
nodes.summary: A common stem presents an outside thinker's claim and asks how the author would react, requiring projection of the author's stance onto an opposing position.

nodes.label: Predicting how the author would respond
nodes.summary: The answer is inferred from the author's demonstrated values and reasoning, projecting them onto the new situation.

nodes.label: Reasoning through hypotheticals
nodes.summary: RB hypotheticals ("suppose that...") must be answered by running the passage's logic forward into the imagined case.

nodes.label: Predicting the likely consequence of a view
nodes.summary: Extrapolation can require forecasting the plausible result if the author's principle were carried out or accepted.

nodes.label: Projecting the author's value judgment
nodes.summary: Where the passage reveals what the author prizes or condemns, an RB question may ask the author to evaluate a new case as good/bad, justified/unjustified.

nodes.label: Transferring a principle to a new domain
nodes.summary: The core move is recognizing the abstract principle in the passage and mapping it onto an unrelated concrete situation.

nodes.label: Mapping an abstract principle to a concrete case
nodes.summary: Application questions ask which concrete scenario best instantiates the general principle the author articulated.

nodes.label: Cross-domain mapping
nodes.summary: Application often moves a humanities idea into a wholly different field (science, business, everyday life), keeping only the relationship intact.

nodes.label: Applying an idea to a practical or policy case
nodes.summary: Some application questions ask which real-world practice, decision, or policy the author's principle would endorse or condemn, testing transfer from theory to action.

nodes.label: Recognizing structural rather than surface similarity
nodes.summary: A correct application matches the underlying logical structure of the passage idea, not merely the surface topic or vocabulary.

nodes.label: Surface-similarity trap
nodes.summary: A wrong choice may reuse the passage's topic words while violating its actual principle, exploiting superficial matching.

nodes.label: Selecting the best example of an idea
nodes.summary: A frequent RB task gives several scenarios and asks which one the author's idea best fits, or which least fits.

nodes.label: Identifying the best illustrative example
nodes.summary: The credited example fully satisfies every defining feature of the passage concept, not just some of them.

nodes.label: Building a criteria checklist for the concept
nodes.summary: Listing the necessary features of the passage's concept lets you test each candidate scenario systematically.

nodes.label: EXCEPT / LEAST-fit example questions
nodes.summary: When asked which scenario the principle does NOT fit, the answer is the single choice that fails the defining criteria the others meet.

nodes.label: Discriminating partial from full fit
nodes.summary: Several scenarios may satisfy some criteria; the credited one satisfies all, so the task is rejecting choices that fit only partially.

nodes.label: Synthesizing across passage ideas
nodes.summary: Some RB items require combining two or more of the passage's ideas, or the passage with the stimulus, to reach a conclusion neither states alone.

nodes.label: Combining two passage ideas into a new conclusion
nodes.summary: The credited answer follows only when two separately stated passage points are joined, a move the surface-level distractors miss.

nodes.label: Integrating the passage with new stimulus material
nodes.summary: Many RB stems pair the passage with a fresh fact or scenario; the answer must respect both the passage logic and the added material together.

nodes.label: Argument-Analysis Substrate for RB
nodes.summary: RB reasoning rests on the ability to dissect arguments into claims, evidence, assumptions, and inferences.

nodes.label: Reconstructing argument structure
nodes.summary: Before extending or testing an argument, the reader must separate its conclusion from its supporting premises.

nodes.label: Distinguishing claim from evidence
nodes.summary: The conclusion is what the author wants you to accept; the evidence is what is offered to make you accept it.

nodes.label: Identifying unstated assumptions
nodes.summary: An assumption is an unstated belief the argument needs to be true for its conclusion to follow from its premises.

nodes.label: Necessary versus sufficient assumptions
nodes.summary: A necessary assumption must hold for the argument to work; a sufficient assumption would by itself guarantee the conclusion.

nodes.label: The premise-to-conclusion link
nodes.summary: Strengthen/weaken reasoning targets the inferential link between premises and conclusion, not just the truth of either.

nodes.label: Causal reasoning in extension
nodes.summary: Many RB stimuli hinge on whether a causal claim survives new information or transfers to a new case.

nodes.label: New information that introduces a confound
nodes.summary: A weakener can supply a third factor that explains both variables, breaking the author's causal claim.

nodes.label: Correlation versus causation
nodes.summary: A causal conclusion drawn only from correlation is vulnerable to alternative cause, reverse causation, or coincidence.

nodes.label: Reverse causation as a weakener
nodes.summary: New information showing the supposed effect actually drives the supposed cause undercuts a causal claim by flipping its direction.

nodes.label: Drawing valid inferences for extension
nodes.summary: RB requires inferences that follow logically from passage-plus-stimulus rather than leaps beyond what is supported.

nodes.label: Deductive certainty versus probable extension
nodes.summary: RB inferences range from must-be-true deductions to most-likely extrapolations, and the stem signals which level is required.

nodes.label: What counts as a supported inference
nodes.summary: A valid inference must be true if the passage and stimulus are true, requiring no unstated extra leap.

nodes.label: Recognizing an unsupported leap
nodes.summary: A wrong choice may sound reasonable yet require an assumption the passage and stimulus never grant.

nodes.label: Recognizing reasoning flaws
nodes.summary: Spotting a flaw in the author's reasoning sets up extensions about what would fix or expose it.

nodes.label: Ad hominem and straw man
nodes.summary: Attacking the person, or a distorted version of an opposing view, are flaws that can affect how an argument should be applied or judged.

nodes.label: Appeals to authority, emotion, and popularity
nodes.summary: Resting a conclusion on an authority's say-so, an emotional pull, or widespread belief rather than evidence is a flaw RB items can target.

nodes.label: Circular reasoning
nodes.summary: An argument whose conclusion is smuggled into its premises offers no real support and cannot be extended soundly.

nodes.label: False dichotomy
nodes.summary: Treating two options as the only possibilities when others exist is a flaw new information can expose.

nodes.label: Hasty generalization
nodes.summary: Drawing a broad conclusion from too few or unrepresentative cases is a reasoning flaw an RB question may exploit.

nodes.label: Slippery slope
nodes.summary: Claiming one step inevitably leads to an extreme outcome without justifying each link is a flaw an extension or counterexample can expose.

nodes.label: Foundations of Reasoning Beyond the Text
nodes.summary: Reasoning Beyond the Text questions ask the test-taker to use the passage's ideas in situations the author did not explicitly address, by either applying them to new contexts or combining them with new information.

nodes.label: Evaluating RB answer choices
nodes.summary: RB answer choices are vetted against the passage's logic, scope, and direction rather than against memorized content.

nodes.label: Watching degree and qualifier language
nodes.summary: Absolute words (always, never, must) usually overreach, while measured qualifiers (may, some, often) better match the cautious scope of most passages.

nodes.label: Checking the direction of the answer
nodes.summary: A choice that points opposite to the author's stance or to the question's demand (e.g., weakens when asked to strengthen) is wrong even if it is true.

nodes.label: The distortion / subtle-twist distractor
nodes.summary: A choice that swaps a key term, shifts the relationship, or changes the agent of an action mirrors the passage closely while reversing or warping its meaning.

nodes.label: Common RB distractor patterns
nodes.summary: RB traps recur as out-of-scope, opposite-direction, half-right/half-wrong, too-extreme, and true-but-irrelevant choices.

nodes.label: The half-right / half-wrong distractor
nodes.summary: A choice can open with an accurate restatement of the passage and then attach a clause that distorts, overstates, or reverses it, so the whole choice must be read.

nodes.label: The out-of-scope distractor
nodes.summary: The most common RB trap introduces a consideration the passage never raises and the stimulus does not license, making the choice irrelevant however plausible.

nodes.label: Filtering for relevance to the question task
nodes.summary: A true or appealing statement is still wrong if it does not address the specific RB task the question stem sets.

nodes.label: The too-extreme distractor
nodes.summary: A choice can match the passage's direction but push it to an absolute or radical degree the author never claimed, failing the degree test.

nodes.label: The true-but-irrelevant distractor
nodes.summary: A statement can be factually or even passage-consistently true yet fail to perform the specific RB task the stem demands, so truth alone never earns the credit.

nodes.label: The fidelity principle
nodes.summary: However far an RB question travels from the passage, the credited answer must remain loyal to the author's reasoning, values, and conclusions.

nodes.label: Tracking the author's voice and attitude
nodes.summary: Holding the author's tone and value commitments in mind keeps an extension or new-information judgment aligned with how the author actually thinks.

nodes.label: No outside subject-matter knowledge required
nodes.summary: CARS answers are derivable from the passage plus the stimulus given; prior expertise in the topic is never needed and can mislead.

nodes.label: Honoring the spirit, not just the letter
nodes.summary: A faithful extension captures what the author meant and would endorse, even where the exact words don't appear, while resisting choices that quote the passage but betray its intent.

nodes.label: Staying faithful to authorial logic
nodes.summary: The right answer is the one the author would most plausibly accept given the reasoning actually presented, not the one the test-taker personally prefers.

nodes.label: Grasping the passage before reasoning beyond it
nodes.summary: Every RB move depends on first having a secure grip on the author's main idea, purpose, tone, and argument.

nodes.label: Isolating the argument's core claim and support
nodes.summary: Knowing the precise claim and the evidence offered for it tells you what a new application would have to preserve or what new information would strengthen or weaken.

nodes.label: Author's purpose constrains the extension
nodes.summary: What the author is trying to accomplish (persuade, critique, describe, reconcile) bounds which new applications and new information are relevant.

nodes.label: Main idea as the anchor for extension
nodes.summary: A correct RB answer must remain consistent with the passage's central thesis, so the main idea is the test against which any extension is checked.

nodes.label: Extracting the transferable principle
nodes.summary: RB hinges on distilling the abstract, generalizable principle behind a passage's specific example so it can be carried to a different domain.

nodes.label: Awareness of the passage's scope and limits
nodes.summary: Recognizing how far the author's claim is meant to reach prevents over-extending an idea into territory the author would not endorse.

nodes.label: Reading tone and attitude as constraints
nodes.summary: The author's tone (skeptical, admiring, ironic, cautious) signals which extensions and judgments are faithful and which clash with how the author feels about the subject.

nodes.label: Identifying the author's underlying values
nodes.summary: Many RB predictions and judgments turn on the value commitments implicit in the passage, which become the basis for projecting the author into new situations.

nodes.label: Definition of the RB skill
nodes.summary: RB is the third and most demanding CARS skill, requiring the reader to take the passage beyond its own boundaries while staying faithful to its logic.

nodes.label: Reasoning beyond the text, defined
nodes.summary: RB requires extending or testing passage ideas against material that lies outside the passage itself, rather than merely locating or restating what was stated.

nodes.label: Proportion of RB on the exam
nodes.summary: Reasoning Beyond the Text accounts for roughly 40% of CARS questions, the largest of the three skill categories.

nodes.label: How the three CARS skills relate
nodes.summary: FOC, RW, and RB form a ladder: comprehend the text, reason within it, then reason beyond it; RB presupposes the first two and is the only skill that imports material from outside the passage.

nodes.label: The two RB sub-skills
nodes.summary: AAMC divides RB into Applying/Extrapolating the passage's ideas to new contexts and Incorporating new information that may support, challenge, or modify the passage.

nodes.label: RB versus Foundations of Comprehension
nodes.summary: Unlike FOC, which is answered from information stated or directly implied in the passage, RB introduces material or scenarios the passage never mentions.

nodes.label: RB versus Reasoning Within the Text
nodes.summary: RW evaluates and integrates ideas internal to the passage, whereas RB brings the passage into contact with external contexts or new facts.

nodes.label: Incorporating Information into the Passage
nodes.summary: This RB sub-skill introduces new facts, claims, or scenarios and asks how they interact with the passage's argument.

nodes.label: Targeting the right part of the argument
nodes.summary: New information acts on a specific premise, assumption, or conclusion, so locating that target is essential.

nodes.label: Introducing an alternative explanation
nodes.summary: A common weakener offers a rival cause or interpretation that accounts for the same evidence the author used.

nodes.label: Acting on an unstated assumption
nodes.summary: New information often strengthens or weakens by confirming or denying an assumption that bridges the author's premise to conclusion.

nodes.label: Attacking or supporting a stated premise
nodes.summary: New information can act directly on a premise the author asserts, raising or lowering its credibility and thereby the conclusion's support.

nodes.label: Using a counterexample
nodes.summary: A single well-chosen counterexample can weaken a universal claim by showing a case the claim cannot accommodate.

nodes.label: Identifying the conclusion under attack or support
nodes.summary: Pinpointing the exact conclusion the question concerns prevents crediting information that affects a different claim.

nodes.label: Judging consistency with the passage
nodes.summary: Some questions ask whether a new statement is consistent with, contradicts, or is independent of the author's position.

nodes.label: New claim consistent with the passage
nodes.summary: A statement is consistent when it can be true at the same time as the author's claims without conflict.

nodes.label: New claim that contradicts the passage
nodes.summary: A statement contradicts the passage when it cannot be true together with a claim the author endorses.

nodes.label: Recognizing a logically independent claim
nodes.summary: A new statement may be neither supported nor contradicted by the passage because the two address different matters, making it logically independent.

nodes.label: Reconciling apparent conflicts
nodes.summary: Some questions test whether a new fact only seems to conflict but can be reconciled with the passage on closer reading.

nodes.label: Assessing the effect of new information
nodes.summary: The central task is to judge whether new information strengthens, weakens, or leaves unchanged the author's claim.

nodes.label: Weighing degree of impact
nodes.summary: Evaluating new information requires gauging how much, not merely whether, it moves the strength of the argument.

nodes.label: "Most" strengthen / weaken questions
nodes.summary: When several choices affect the argument, the credited answer is the one with the largest impact on the central claim.

nodes.label: Information that has no effect
nodes.summary: Information is irrelevant when it neither supports nor challenges the specific claim, often because it is out of scope.

nodes.label: Information that strengthens the argument
nodes.summary: New evidence strengthens when it supplies additional support for, or removes an objection to, the author's central claim.

nodes.label: Information that weakens the argument
nodes.summary: New evidence weakens when it contradicts a key premise, undermines the link between premise and conclusion, or offers a competing explanation.

nodes.label: Weaken versus disprove (degree confusion)
nodes.summary: A strengthen/weaken task asks for a shift in the argument's plausibility, not proof or refutation; choices that claim to definitively prove or disprove usually overreach.

nodes.label: Incorporating data, studies, and findings
nodes.summary: Some RB stimuli present hypothetical research results and ask how they bear on a humanities argument.

nodes.label: Findings that would support the claim
nodes.summary: A described result supports the passage when its outcome is what the author's view predicts.

nodes.label: Findings that would undermine the claim
nodes.summary: A result undermines the passage when its outcome is the opposite of what the author's view would lead one to expect.

nodes.label: Judging the relevance of offered evidence
nodes.summary: Evidence must actually speak to the author's claim; data about a different population or variable may be irrelevant.

nodes.label: Sample representativeness and generalizability
nodes.summary: A finding's force on a general claim depends on whether its sample or setting is representative; an unrepresentative study supports or weakens only weakly.

nodes.label: Modifying or revising the author's view
nodes.summary: New information can force a question about how the author's position would have to change in response.

nodes.label: Preferring the minimal required change
nodes.summary: When asked how a view must change, the credited answer makes the smallest revision the new fact compels, not the most dramatic overhaul among the choices.

nodes.label: Whether the thesis survives the new information
nodes.summary: RB can ask whether new information merely qualifies the thesis or fatally undermines it, requiring a judgment of degree.

nodes.label: How new evidence would modify the thesis
nodes.summary: The answer states the minimal, logically required adjustment to the author's claim given the new fact.

nodes.label: RB Question Recognition and Strategy
nodes.summary: Recognizing the RB task from the stem and matching it to the right approach is itself a tested skill.

nodes.label: A disciplined RB answering process
nodes.summary: A repeatable process keeps the test-taker anchored to the passage while reasoning outward.

nodes.label: Suppressing personal opinion and outside facts
nodes.summary: Reasoning beyond the text must still proceed from the author's logic, never from the reader's own views or knowledge.

nodes.label: Choosing the best, not the perfect, answer
nodes.summary: The credited RB answer is the most defensible of the four, even if it is imperfect, so choices are compared against each other.

nodes.label: Process of elimination by RB criteria
nodes.summary: Eliminating choices that are out of scope, wrong-direction, or too extreme isolates the choice faithful to the passage.

nodes.label: Parsing exactly what the stem asks
nodes.summary: Before reasoning, the test-taker must pin down the precise RB task and direction (apply vs predict, strengthen vs weaken) the stem sets, since the whole approach follows from it.

nodes.label: Prephrasing the answer
nodes.summary: Forming an expected answer from the passage before reading choices guards against being lured by attractive distractors.

nodes.label: Returning to the relevant passage point
nodes.summary: Even though RB reaches outside the text, the test-taker must re-ground the reasoning in the specific passage idea at issue.

nodes.label: Recognizing RB question stems
nodes.summary: Characteristic phrasings signal which RB move (apply, predict, strengthen, weaken, analogy) the question demands.

nodes.label: Analogy stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "most analogous to" or "corresponds to the relationship between" flag an analogical-reasoning task.

nodes.label: Application stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "which of the following is the best example" or "the author would most likely apply this to" signal an application task.

nodes.label: Handling EXCEPT, NOT, and LEAST stems
nodes.summary: Negative stems invert the task, so the credited answer is the odd one out; misreading the negation is a frequent careless error.

nodes.label: Hypothetical and "suppose" stems
nodes.summary: A stem that introduces a new scenario ("suppose that...", "if it were true that...") flags an extrapolation task.

nodes.label: Prediction / author-response stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "the author would most likely agree that" or "would predict that" flag a task of projecting the author into a new case.

nodes.label: Strengthen/weaken stem cues
nodes.summary: Phrases like "which finding would most undermine" or "would best support the author" signal a new-information task.

nodes.label: Managing time and difficulty on RB items
nodes.summary: Because RB items are dense, pacing and triage decisions affect overall CARS performance.

nodes.label: Calibrating confidence under uncertainty
nodes.summary: Committing to the best-supported choice and moving on prevents over-investing in a single ambiguous RB item.

nodes.label: Triaging hard RB questions
nodes.summary: Flagging and returning to the most abstract RB items protects time for questions that are faster to secure.

nodes.label: Argument analysis within the text
nodes.summary: A central within-text skill is breaking the passage's reasoning into its component claims, evidence, and assumptions as the author presents them.

nodes.label: Assumptions within the argument
nodes.summary: The unstated beliefs the author's reasoning depends on.

nodes.label: What an assumption is
nodes.summary: An assumption is an unstated premise the argument needs in order for its conclusion to follow from its stated reasons.

nodes.label: Finding the gap between premise and conclusion
nodes.summary: The assumption is the bridge spanning the logical gap from the stated evidence to the conclusion.

nodes.label: Identifying a necessary assumption in the text
nodes.summary: A necessary assumption is one without which the author's stated reasoning would collapse.

nodes.label: Surfacing implicit premises the author relies on
nodes.summary: Beyond the single bridging assumption, an argument can rest on several taken-for-granted beliefs the reader must make explicit.

nodes.label: Components of an argument
nodes.summary: The structural parts that make up any reasoned case in the passage.

nodes.label: Identifying the conclusion or main claim
nodes.summary: The conclusion is the point the author is trying to get the reader to accept; everything else supports it.

nodes.label: Recognizing concessions and qualifications
nodes.summary: A concession admits a point to the other side while still maintaining the author's overall position.

nodes.label: Identifying counterarguments the author raises
nodes.summary: Authors often present an opposing view in order to rebut, qualify, or concede part of it.

nodes.label: Classifying the type of evidence used
nodes.summary: Authors support claims with examples, statistics, expert testimony, analogy, or anecdote, and the type bears on the argument's strength.

nodes.label: Premise and conclusion indicator words
nodes.summary: Words like 'because' and 'since' flag premises, while 'therefore' and 'thus' flag conclusions.

nodes.label: Distinguishing main from intermediate conclusions
nodes.summary: A sub-conclusion is both supported by some premises and used to support the final conclusion.

nodes.label: Identifying premises and evidence
nodes.summary: Premises are the reasons and evidence offered in support of the conclusion.

nodes.label: Identifying the author's rebuttal or response
nodes.summary: After raising a counterview the author typically answers it, and recognizing the rebuttal separates the author's position from the view rejected.

nodes.label: Evaluating reasoning within the text
nodes.summary: Judging how well the author's stated reasons support the conclusion, using only the passage's own logic.

nodes.label: Recognizing common reasoning fallacies
nodes.summary: Patterns like hasty generalization, false dichotomy, circular reasoning, and post hoc reasoning can undermine an author's case.

nodes.label: Distinguishing correlation from causation in the argument
nodes.summary: An author may treat two co-occurring facts as causally linked when the text supports only correlation.

nodes.label: Checking internal consistency of claims
nodes.summary: A strong reader notices when two passage claims contradict or sit in tension with each other.

nodes.label: Spotting a flaw in the author's reasoning
nodes.summary: Some questions ask the reader to recognize where the author's own logic is weak or unsupported.

nodes.label: Detecting overgeneralization in the text
nodes.summary: An author overgeneralizes when a broad conclusion is drawn from too little or too narrow evidence.

nodes.label: Judging whether evidence is relevant to the claim
nodes.summary: Evidence can be true yet fail to bear on the specific conclusion the author draws from it.

nodes.label: Judging how well evidence supports a claim
nodes.summary: Within-text evaluation asks whether the author's own evidence adequately backs the claim being made.

nodes.label: Spotting unsupported assertions
nodes.summary: Some passage claims are simply asserted with no evidence offered, a distinct weakness from a flaw in stated reasoning.

nodes.label: Author voice, tone, and attitude
nodes.summary: Recognizing the author's stance, voice, and emotional coloring as expressed within the text.

nodes.label: Author purpose and rhetorical intent
nodes.summary: Why the author wrote the passage and what each rhetorical move is meant to accomplish.

nodes.label: Inferring the intended audience
nodes.summary: Vocabulary, assumptions, and framing reveal whom the author is writing for and how that shapes the text.

nodes.label: Why a specific example or quotation is used
nodes.summary: An example, anecdote, or quotation is included to illustrate, support, complicate, or counter a claim, and the question asks which.

nodes.label: Purpose of a specific sentence or example
nodes.summary: Local rhetorical questions ask why the author included a particular detail, quote, or example.

nodes.label: Identifying the overall purpose
nodes.summary: The overall purpose is the author's primary goal: to inform, persuade, critique, describe, or entertain.

nodes.label: Recognizing rhetorical devices and their effect
nodes.summary: Devices like analogy, rhetorical question, repetition, and appeal serve identifiable persuasive or illustrative functions.

nodes.label: Author position and bias
nodes.summary: Determining where the author stands and how their viewpoint shapes the text.

nodes.label: Tracking whose view is being expressed
nodes.summary: Passages cite multiple voices, so the reader must attribute each claim to the author, a critic, or a third party.

nodes.label: Recognizing author bias and slant
nodes.summary: Selective emphasis, loaded language, and one-sided framing reveal the author's bias within the text.

nodes.label: Gauging the author's degree of certainty
nodes.summary: Modal language ('may,' 'must,' 'perhaps') shows how strongly the author commits to a claim.

nodes.label: Telling a neutral author from a committed one
nodes.summary: Some passages report views even-handedly without the author taking sides, and over-reading a position into a neutral author is a common error.

nodes.label: Separating author opinion from reported fact
nodes.summary: Distinguishing the author's own view from facts or others' views they merely report is essential to within-text reasoning.

nodes.label: Identifying the author's position
nodes.summary: The author's position is their stance on the central issue, which the reader infers from their claims and emphasis.

nodes.label: Tone and mood
nodes.summary: The emotional quality and attitude conveyed by the author's language.

nodes.label: Tone defined
nodes.summary: Tone is the author's attitude toward the subject as revealed through word choice, phrasing, and emphasis.

nodes.label: Reading tone from diction and connotation
nodes.summary: Charged, evaluative, or hedged word choices are the primary evidence for the author's tone.

nodes.label: Recognizing ambivalent or mixed tone
nodes.summary: An author can hold a qualified or two-sided attitude (admiring yet wary), and the correct tone answer must capture that complexity.

nodes.label: Gauging the intensity and moderation of tone
nodes.summary: Hedging words ('somewhat,' 'arguably') temper tone, so the correct answer often avoids extreme tone labels.

nodes.label: Distinguishing tone words
nodes.summary: Tone can be approving, critical, skeptical, ambivalent, nostalgic, or neutral, and answers often hinge on fine distinctions among them.

nodes.label: Detecting a shift in tone
nodes.summary: An author's tone can change across the passage, signaling a change in attitude toward the subject.

nodes.label: Comprehension foundations
nodes.summary: Reasoning Within the Text rests on accurately understanding what the passage literally and figuratively says before any inference is drawn.

nodes.label: Active reading process
nodes.summary: Strategic reading behaviors that build an accurate mental model of the passage on the first read.

nodes.label: Anticipating where the argument is going
nodes.summary: Skilled readers predict the next move (a contrast, a qualification, a conclusion) from signposts and rhetorical setup.

nodes.label: Chunking the passage into idea units
nodes.summary: Breaking a passage into paragraph-level idea units lets the reader track the argument's development rather than drowning in details.

nodes.label: Building a running mental map of the passage
nodes.summary: Holding the passage's skeleton (thesis, main moves, where each idea lives) in working memory speeds accurate return to the text.

nodes.label: Monitoring comprehension and rereading
nodes.summary: Recognizing a lapse in understanding and rereading the relevant span prevents errors from compounding across the passage.

nodes.label: Reading transition words as signposts
nodes.summary: Transition words ('however,' 'therefore,' 'although,' 'for example') flag logical relationships and shifts that structure the author's reasoning.

nodes.label: Figurative and connotative meaning
nodes.summary: Understanding non-literal language, where meaning is carried by connotation, imagery, and rhetorical figures.

nodes.label: Connotation vs. denotation
nodes.summary: Denotation is a word's literal definition while connotation is its emotional or evaluative coloring, and CARS often hinges on connotation.

nodes.label: Idiom and rhetorical-figure recognition
nodes.summary: Idioms and rhetorical figures (hyperbole, understatement, rhetorical questions) mean something other than their literal wording.

nodes.label: Interpreting imagery and symbolism
nodes.summary: Sensory imagery and symbols carry thematic meaning beyond their literal description, which the reader decodes from context.

nodes.label: Detecting irony and sarcasm
nodes.summary: Irony conveys a meaning opposite to or different from the literal words, signaled by context, exaggeration, or contrast with known author views.

nodes.label: Interpreting metaphor and analogy in text
nodes.summary: A metaphor or analogy maps one idea onto another, and comprehending it means recovering the point of comparison the author intends.

nodes.label: Literal comprehension
nodes.summary: Grasping the explicit, surface-level content the author states directly in the text.

nodes.label: Tracking author-supplied definitions and key terms
nodes.summary: Authors often stipulate a special meaning for a term; the reader must use that in-passage definition rather than the everyday sense.

nodes.label: Parsing dense or complex syntax
nodes.summary: Long sentences with embedded clauses, qualifications, and double negatives must be decoded to recover the simple proposition underneath.

nodes.label: Handling negation and double negatives
nodes.summary: Negated and doubly-negated statements ('not unlike,' 'no one denies') must be unpacked into their positive equivalent to be understood.

nodes.label: Paraphrasing a sentence or claim
nodes.summary: Restating a passage claim in different words while preserving its exact meaning is the core test of literal comprehension.

nodes.label: Reading qualifiers and limiting words precisely
nodes.summary: Words like 'some,' 'most,' 'all,' 'only,' 'rarely,' and 'never' carry exact logical force, and missing them changes the meaning of a claim.

nodes.label: Tracking pronoun and phrase referents
nodes.summary: Accurate comprehension requires identifying what pronouns and demonstratives ('it,' 'this,' 'they,' 'such') refer back to in the text.

nodes.label: Identifying explicitly stated information
nodes.summary: Some questions are answered by locating information the author states outright, with the correct choice paraphrasing the text rather than going beyond it.

nodes.label: Word meaning in context
nodes.summary: The intended meaning of a word or phrase is determined by its surrounding context, which can override its common dictionary definition.

nodes.label: Inference within the text
nodes.summary: Drawing conclusions that the author implies but does not state, staying tightly tethered to what the text supports.

nodes.label: Types of within-text inference
nodes.summary: The distinct kinds of unstated conclusions a reader can validly draw from the passage.

nodes.label: Inferring a belief the author takes for granted
nodes.summary: What the author treats as obvious or unproblematic reveals an underlying belief the reader can infer.

nodes.label: Drawing a direct implication
nodes.summary: A valid implication is something that must be true given what the author states, even though it is not stated.

nodes.label: Inferring implied or suggested meaning
nodes.summary: Authors imply meaning through word choice, emphasis, and what they leave unsaid, which the reader reconstructs.

nodes.label: Inferring an unstated attitude or evaluation
nodes.summary: From word choice and emphasis the reader infers how the author feels about a subject even when no attitude is stated outright.

nodes.label: Inferring what the author would agree with
nodes.summary: Based on stated views, the reader infers which additional statements the author would most likely endorse.

nodes.label: Inferring an implied relationship between ideas
nodes.summary: The reader infers how two passage ideas relate (cause, contrast, support) when the link is left implicit.

nodes.label: Inference validity and limits
nodes.summary: Keeping inferences supported by the text and rejecting those that go too far.

nodes.label: Rejecting could-be-true answers on must-be-true questions
nodes.summary: A choice that the passage merely permits but does not require is wrong when the question demands what must follow.

nodes.label: Choosing the smallest justified inferential leap
nodes.summary: The best inference answer goes only as far as the text licenses, not the most dramatic interpretation.

nodes.label: Applying the must-be-true standard
nodes.summary: A valid within-text inference must be true if the passage is true, distinguishing it from a merely plausible or possible reading.

nodes.label: Avoiding outside knowledge in inferences
nodes.summary: Within-text reasoning forbids importing personal or specialized knowledge not contained in the passage.

nodes.label: Requiring textual support for inferences
nodes.summary: A valid CARS inference is anchored in the passage; the correct answer can be defended by pointing to specific text.

nodes.label: Rejecting unwarranted inferences
nodes.summary: An inference is wrong if it requires information or assumptions the passage does not provide.

nodes.label: Main idea and thesis
nodes.summary: Identifying the central claim and purpose that organizes the entire passage is a foundational within-text skill.

nodes.label: Paragraph and sentence function
nodes.summary: Understanding the role each paragraph or sentence plays in advancing the central idea.

nodes.label: Distinguishing supporting detail from main point
nodes.summary: Examples, statistics, and anecdotes support a point but are not themselves the point being argued.

nodes.label: Function of a paragraph in the passage
nodes.summary: Each paragraph serves a role (introduce, support, qualify, counter, exemplify, conclude) relative to the main idea.

nodes.label: Function of a single sentence in context
nodes.summary: An individual sentence can introduce, illustrate, qualify, transition, or conclude, and its role is read from its position and relation to neighbors.

nodes.label: Identifying topic sentences
nodes.summary: A topic sentence states the controlling idea of its paragraph, often near the start, anchoring the surrounding detail.

nodes.label: Summary and gist
nodes.summary: Condensing the passage's content into its essential points.

nodes.label: Selecting the best passage summary
nodes.summary: The best summary captures the major claim and its key support while omitting minor details and distortions.

nodes.label: Distinguishing an accurate gist from a distorted one
nodes.summary: A wrong summary subtly overstates, understates, or mis-emphasizes the passage even while sounding plausible.

nodes.label: Choosing an apt title for the passage
nodes.summary: An appropriate title reflects the central idea and scope of the whole passage, not a single paragraph.

nodes.label: Central thesis identification
nodes.summary: Locating the single controlling claim the passage as a whole supports.

nodes.label: Main idea vs. topic
nodes.summary: The topic is what the passage is about, while the main idea is the specific claim the author makes about that topic.

nodes.label: Inferring an unstated thesis
nodes.summary: When no single sentence states the thesis, the main idea must be synthesized from the cumulative direction of the paragraphs.

nodes.label: Matching answer scope to the thesis
nodes.summary: A correct main-idea answer covers the whole passage without being too broad or too narrow relative to the actual thesis.

nodes.label: Locating where the thesis is stated
nodes.summary: A thesis may appear in the introduction, be withheld until the conclusion, or be split across the passage, and knowing common placements speeds its capture.

nodes.label: Within-text question strategy
nodes.summary: Test-taking reasoning skills for answering CARS questions that draw only on the passage itself.

nodes.label: Answer-choice evaluation
nodes.summary: Systematic reasoning to select the best within-text answer and eliminate distractors.

nodes.label: Choosing the best answer, not a perfect one
nodes.summary: CARS asks for the most defensible choice among four, which may be imperfect but is better supported than the rest.

nodes.label: Comparing the two best remaining choices
nodes.summary: When two choices survive elimination, the decision turns on a precise textual difference between them.

nodes.label: Returning to the text to verify
nodes.summary: The correct within-text answer can be confirmed against specific passage content rather than memory.

nodes.label: Parsing exactly what the question stem asks
nodes.summary: Identifying the question type and its precise demand before evaluating choices prevents answering the wrong question.

nodes.label: Pre-phrasing an answer before reading choices
nodes.summary: Forming an expected answer from the passage before viewing options reduces susceptibility to attractive wrong choices.

nodes.label: Process of elimination
nodes.summary: Eliminating clearly unsupported choices narrows the field to the best-supported answer.

nodes.label: Recognizing distractor patterns
nodes.summary: The recurring traps in wrong within-text answer choices.

nodes.label: Distorted-detail choices
nodes.summary: A distractor twists a real passage detail by changing its degree, scope, or relationship.

nodes.label: Faulty-attribution choices
nodes.summary: A distractor states a real view from the passage but assigns it to the wrong voice (author vs. a cited source).

nodes.label: Partially-correct (half-right) choices
nodes.summary: A choice that is accurate in one clause but wrong in another must be eliminated entirely.

nodes.label: Opposite or reversed answer choices
nodes.summary: A trap choice states the reverse of the passage, attractive because it uses familiar passage words.

nodes.label: Out-of-scope answer choices
nodes.summary: Choices that introduce ideas beyond the passage are wrong even if true in the real world.

nodes.label: Outside-knowledge trap choices
nodes.summary: A choice is true in the real world and tempting, but is wrong because the passage itself never supports it.

nodes.label: Right-idea-wrong-question choices
nodes.summary: A choice may correctly state a passage point that belongs to a different question than the one asked.

nodes.label: Too-extreme answer choices
nodes.summary: Choices using absolute language ('always,' 'never,' 'only') often overstate what the moderate passage actually supports.

nodes.label: Too-narrow or partial-scope choices
nodes.summary: A choice that captures only one paragraph or detail is wrong on a question that asks about the whole passage.

nodes.label: True-but-does-not-answer choices
nodes.summary: A choice can be supported by the passage yet still fail to answer the specific question asked.

nodes.label: Within-text question archetypes
nodes.summary: The recurring families of questions that test reasoning within the text.

nodes.label: Whose-view-is-it (attribution) questions
nodes.summary: These ask the reader to assign a stated claim to the author or to a specific cited voice in the passage.

nodes.label: Author-would-agree questions
nodes.summary: These ask which statement the author would most likely accept based on their stated views.

nodes.label: Detail and explicit-information questions
nodes.summary: These ask what the passage states about a specific point and are answered by locating the relevant text.

nodes.label: Function and purpose-of-detail questions
nodes.summary: These ask why the author included a particular sentence, example, or paragraph.

nodes.label: Inference and implication questions
nodes.summary: These ask what follows from or is suggested by the passage without being explicitly stated.

nodes.label: Main idea and primary purpose questions
nodes.summary: These ask for the central claim or overall goal of the passage and demand a whole-passage answer.

nodes.label: Relationship-between-ideas questions
nodes.summary: These ask how two passage ideas, claims, or viewpoints relate to each other.

nodes.label: Passage-structure and organization questions
nodes.summary: These ask how the passage is organized or how its parts relate.

nodes.label: Tone and attitude questions
nodes.summary: These ask for the author's emotional stance toward the subject as revealed in the text.

nodes.label: Word-in-context questions
nodes.summary: These ask what a word or phrase means as the author uses it in the passage.

nodes.label: Special question formats
nodes.summary: Within-text questions with structural twists that change how reasoning is applied.

nodes.label: EXCEPT, NOT, and LEAST questions
nodes.summary: These reverse the task so the correct answer is the one the passage does not support.

nodes.label: 'Primarily' and 'best' qualifier questions
nodes.summary: Qualifiers like 'primarily' demand the choice that captures the central rather than a peripheral point.

nodes.label: Roman-numeral (I/II/III) questions
nodes.summary: Each numbered statement is evaluated independently against the passage before combining valid ones.

nodes.label: Pacing and time management
nodes.summary: Managing the fixed time budget so within-text reasoning can be applied to every question.

nodes.label: Flagging hard questions and moving on
nodes.summary: Marking a time-consuming question and returning later protects easier points from being lost to the clock.

nodes.label: Never leaving a question blank
nodes.summary: Because there is no penalty for wrong answers, every question should be answered even if a best guess is required.

nodes.label: Pacing per passage and per question
nodes.summary: CARS allots roughly ten minutes per passage, so the reader budgets reading and questions to finish the section.

nodes.label: Relationships among ideas within the text
nodes.summary: Understanding how the passage's ideas connect to and constrain one another is a core within-text reasoning skill.

nodes.label: Consistency and implication among claims
nodes.summary: Reasoning about what the passage's claims jointly entail or contradict.

nodes.label: Spotting contradictory or incompatible claims
nodes.summary: A reader notices when accepting one passage claim would force rejecting another.

nodes.label: Determining what claims jointly entail
nodes.summary: Combining two passage statements can entail a third conclusion that neither states alone.

nodes.label: Recognizing how the author resolves an apparent tension
nodes.summary: When two passage claims seem to clash, the author often reconciles them, and the reader tracks how the tension is dissolved.

nodes.label: Judging which idea best fits the passage's logic
nodes.summary: Some questions ask which statement is most consistent with the reasoning the author has already laid out.

nodes.label: Logical relationships between claims
nodes.summary: The reasoned connections (support, contrast, cause, condition) linking the passage's statements.

nodes.label: Recognizing an analogical relationship between ideas
nodes.summary: The author may treat two ideas as parallel so that what is true of one is presented as true of the other.

nodes.label: Recognizing causal relationships in the text
nodes.summary: A causal relation holds when the author claims one thing brings about another, distinct from mere correlation or sequence.

nodes.label: Recognizing conditional relationships
nodes.summary: A conditional claim ('if X then Y') makes one statement depend on another, and its converse is not implied.

nodes.label: Recognizing contrast and opposition
nodes.summary: Two ideas stand in contrast when the author sets them against each other to highlight a difference.

nodes.label: Recognizing part-whole and category relations
nodes.summary: Some questions test whether an idea is a subtype, instance, or component of another idea in the passage.

nodes.label: Recognizing support relationships
nodes.summary: One statement supports another when it gives a reason to accept it.

nodes.label: Multiple viewpoints within the text
nodes.summary: Managing passages that present and relate several competing perspectives.

nodes.label: Mapping points of agreement and disagreement
nodes.summary: Within-text questions ask where two cited views converge and where they diverge.

nodes.label: Relating the author's view to cited views
nodes.summary: The reader determines whether the author endorses, rejects, or qualifies each view they present.

nodes.label: Differentiating competing viewpoints
nodes.summary: The reader must keep distinct views separate and not blend the author's view with those they cite.

nodes.label: Accurately representing a cited view on its own terms
nodes.summary: A correct answer states a cited position as the passage frames it, without contaminating it with the author's rebuttal.

nodes.label: Passage structure and organization
nodes.summary: Reasoning within the text requires recognizing how the author organizes and sequences ideas to build meaning.

nodes.label: Logical flow and development
nodes.summary: How ideas connect and build on one another across the passage.

nodes.label: Recognizing continuation vs. contrast
nodes.summary: Distinguishing whether a new sentence extends the prior idea or opposes it is essential to tracking the argument.

nodes.label: Tracing how an idea develops
nodes.summary: Following an idea as it is introduced, refined, qualified, or overturned across paragraphs reveals the author's reasoning.

nodes.label: Integrating widely separated parts of the passage
nodes.summary: Many within-text questions require connecting a claim in one paragraph to a related statement several paragraphs away.

nodes.label: Reading the role of the opening and conclusion
nodes.summary: The first and last paragraphs often frame the thesis, restate the stakes, or resolve the argument, anchoring the whole passage.

nodes.label: Why information appears where it does
nodes.summary: The position of a claim (setup, support, concession, or conclusion) reflects its function in the author's reasoning.

nodes.label: Detecting a shift or turn in the argument
nodes.summary: Words like 'but,' 'yet,' and 'however' signal a pivot where the author changes direction or introduces a counterpoint.

nodes.label: Organizational patterns
nodes.summary: The common rhetorical structures authors use to arrange their material.

nodes.label: Cause-and-effect structure
nodes.summary: The passage is organized around the claim that one thing produces or results from another.

nodes.label: Chronological or narrative structure
nodes.summary: Ideas are arranged in temporal sequence, often to trace a development, history, or process.

nodes.label: Claim-and-support structure
nodes.summary: The author states a thesis and then arranges evidence, reasons, and examples to support it.

nodes.label: Compare-and-contrast structure
nodes.summary: The author develops the passage by setting two or more ideas, works, or views against each other to highlight similarities and differences.

nodes.label: Definition-and-classification structure
nodes.summary: The passage develops a concept by defining it and sorting instances into categories or types.

nodes.label: General-to-specific and specific-to-general structure
nodes.summary: The author may move from a broad principle down to particular cases, or build from particulars up to a generalization.

nodes.label: Problem-and-solution structure
nodes.summary: The author frames a problem and then advances or evaluates one or more proposed solutions.

nodes.label: Thesis-antithesis-synthesis structure
nodes.summary: The author presents a view, raises an opposing view, and reconciles them into a synthesized position.

nodes.label: Mechanics in living systems
nodes.summary: Translational mechanics applies to locomotion, muscle/skeletal levers, and bodily forces.

nodes.label: Locomotion and ground forces
nodes.summary: Walking and running rely on friction and Newton's third law against the ground.

nodes.label: Friction for traction
nodes.summary: Static friction between foot and ground provides the traction needed to walk or run without slipping.

nodes.label: Ground reaction force
nodes.summary: The ground pushes back on a foot (Newton's third law), propelling locomotion forward.

nodes.label: Impulse in landing and impact reduction
nodes.summary: Bending the knees on landing lengthens stopping time, lowering the force the body experiences for a given Δp.

nodes.label: Musculoskeletal forces
nodes.summary: Muscles, tendons, and joints generate and transmit forces as biological levers.

nodes.label: Joint torque and balance
nodes.summary: Muscle force times its lever arm produces the torque that balances loads and moves limbs.

nodes.label: Body levers trade force for speed/range
nodes.summary: Third-class body levers favor speed and range of motion at the cost of requiring large muscle forces (MA < 1).

nodes.label: Muscle force as tension
nodes.summary: Skeletal muscle exerts tension transmitted through tendons to move bones about joints.

nodes.label: Stability and center of gravity in the body
nodes.summary: Standing balance keeps the body's center of gravity over the base of support to avoid tipping torque.

nodes.label: Uniform circular motion and centripetal force
nodes.summary: Objects moving in circles at constant speed require a centripetal force directed toward the center.

nodes.label: Centripetal acceleration and force
nodes.summary: Circular motion involves inward (centripetal) acceleration produced by a net inward force.

nodes.label: Centripetal acceleration (a = v²/r)
nodes.summary: Centripetal acceleration ac = v²/r points toward the circle's center even at constant speed.

nodes.label: Banked curves and vertical circles
nodes.summary: Banked turns use the normal-force component for centripetal force, and vertical circles vary tension/normal with position.

nodes.label: Centrifugal force (fictitious)
nodes.summary: Centrifugal force is a fictitious outward effect seen in a rotating frame, not a real interaction force.

nodes.label: Velocity tangent, acceleration radial
nodes.summary: In uniform circular motion velocity is tangent to the path while acceleration points radially inward.

nodes.label: Centripetal force (F = mv²/r)
nodes.summary: The net inward (centripetal) force Fc = mv²/r is required to keep an object in circular motion.

nodes.label: Sources of centripetal force
nodes.summary: Centripetal force can be supplied by tension, gravity, friction, or the normal force, not a new force type.

nodes.label: Period, frequency, and angular speed
nodes.summary: Circular motion is characterized by period, frequency, tangential speed, and angular velocity.

nodes.label: Angular velocity (ω = 2πf, v = ωr)
nodes.summary: Angular velocity ω = 2πf = 2π/T relates to tangential speed by v = ωr.

nodes.label: Period and frequency (T = 1/f)
nodes.summary: Period T is the time per revolution and frequency f = 1/T is revolutions per second.

nodes.label: Tangential speed (v = 2πr/T)
nodes.summary: Speed around the circle is v = 2πr/T, the circumference divided by the period.

nodes.label: Equilibrium, torque, and statics
nodes.summary: Equilibrium requires zero net force and zero net torque, the basis of biomechanical statics (levers).

nodes.label: Levers and biomechanics
nodes.summary: The body's joints act as levers classified by the arrangement of fulcrum, load, and effort.

nodes.label: First-class lever
nodes.summary: In a first-class lever the fulcrum lies between the effort and the load (e.g., the atlanto-occipital joint of the head).

nodes.label: Mechanical advantage
nodes.summary: Mechanical advantage is the ratio of load to effort (or effort arm to load arm) a machine provides.

nodes.label: Second-class lever
nodes.summary: In a second-class lever the load lies between the fulcrum and effort (e.g., standing on the balls of the feet).

nodes.label: Third-class lever
nodes.summary: In a third-class lever the effort lies between fulcrum and load, common in the body (e.g., biceps flexing the forearm).

nodes.label: Rotational equilibrium
nodes.summary: Rotational equilibrium requires the net torque about any axis to be zero.

nodes.label: Complete equilibrium (ΣF = 0 and Στ = 0)
nodes.summary: A rigid body is in full equilibrium only when both net force and net torque are zero.

nodes.label: Choosing a pivot to simplify torque analysis
nodes.summary: Because Στ = 0 about any axis, choosing the pivot at an unknown force eliminates it from the torque equation.

nodes.label: Second condition (Στ = 0)
nodes.summary: For rotational equilibrium the sum of clockwise torques equals the sum of counterclockwise torques.

nodes.label: Stable, unstable, and neutral equilibrium
nodes.summary: Equilibrium is stable, unstable, or neutral depending on whether a small displacement raises, lowers, or leaves the center of gravity unchanged.

nodes.label: Torque (moment of force)
nodes.summary: Torque is the rotational effect of a force about a pivot.

nodes.label: Force couple
nodes.summary: A couple is two equal, opposite, non-collinear forces that produce net torque but zero net force.

nodes.label: Torque (τ = rF sinθ)
nodes.summary: Torque τ = rF sinθ measures the rotational effect of a force applied at distance r from the pivot.

nodes.label: Torque direction (CW vs CCW)
nodes.summary: Torque has a rotational sense, conventionally counterclockwise positive and clockwise negative.

nodes.label: Lever arm (moment arm)
nodes.summary: The lever arm is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line of action of the force.

nodes.label: Units of torque (N·m)
nodes.summary: Torque is measured in newton-meters (N·m), dimensionally like energy but a distinct vector quantity.

nodes.label: Translational equilibrium
nodes.summary: Translational equilibrium means the net force on a body is zero.

nodes.label: Equilibrant force
nodes.summary: The equilibrant is the single force equal and opposite to the resultant that puts a body in equilibrium.

nodes.label: First condition (ΣF = 0)
nodes.summary: For translational equilibrium the vector sum of all forces equals zero (ΣFx = 0 and ΣFy = 0).

nodes.label: Static vs dynamic equilibrium
nodes.summary: Static equilibrium is rest with zero net force; dynamic equilibrium is constant velocity with zero net force.

nodes.label: Forces and Newton's laws (dynamics)
nodes.summary: Forces are pushes/pulls that change motion according to Newton's three laws of motion.

nodes.label: Apparent weight and accelerating frames
nodes.summary: In an accelerating frame the normal force (apparent weight) differs from true weight.

nodes.label: Apparent weight in an elevator
nodes.summary: In an accelerating elevator apparent weight N = m(g ± a), exceeding mg when accelerating up and less when down.

nodes.label: Weightlessness (apparent) in free fall
nodes.summary: In free fall the normal force is zero, producing apparent weightlessness even though gravity still acts.

nodes.label: Newtonian gravitation
nodes.summary: Masses attract via the universal law of gravitation, which defines g near Earth.

nodes.label: Gravitational constant G
nodes.summary: G ≈ 6.67×10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² is the universal gravitational constant.

nodes.label: Center of gravity
nodes.summary: The center of gravity is the point where total weight acts, coinciding with center of mass in uniform gravity.

nodes.label: Center of mass
nodes.summary: The center of mass is the mass-weighted average position where gravity effectively acts and translational motion is tracked.

nodes.label: Surface gravity g = GM/R²
nodes.summary: The local gravitational acceleration equals GM/R², linking weight to a planet's mass and radius.

nodes.label: Variation of g with altitude and location
nodes.summary: Local g decreases with altitude (greater r) and varies slightly with latitude and planetary body.

nodes.label: Inverse-square distance dependence
nodes.summary: Gravitational force falls off as 1/r², so doubling separation quarters the force.

nodes.label: Law of universal gravitation (F = Gm₁m₂/r²)
nodes.summary: Every two masses attract with force F = Gm₁m₂/r², an inverse-square law along the line joining them.

nodes.label: Newton's three laws
nodes.summary: Newton's laws relate net force to inertia, acceleration, and interaction pairs.

nodes.label: Identifying action–reaction pairs
nodes.summary: Action–reaction pairs act on different objects and therefore never cancel on a single body.

nodes.label: Free-body diagrams
nodes.summary: A free-body diagram isolates one object and shows all external forces as vectors for ΣF analysis.

nodes.label: First law (inertia)
nodes.summary: An object at rest stays at rest and one in motion stays in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force.

nodes.label: Inertia and mass
nodes.summary: Inertia is an object's resistance to changes in motion and is quantified by its mass.

nodes.label: Mass vs weight
nodes.summary: Mass is an intrinsic scalar measure of inertia while weight is the gravitational force on that mass and varies with g.

nodes.label: Net force and force balance
nodes.summary: The net (resultant) force is the vector sum of all forces and determines acceleration.

nodes.label: Second law (F = ma)
nodes.summary: Net force equals mass times acceleration (ΣF = ma), with acceleration in the direction of net force.

nodes.label: Third law (action–reaction)
nodes.summary: For every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force on the other body.

nodes.label: Types of forces
nodes.summary: Mechanics problems involve gravity, normal, tension, friction, applied, and spring forces.

nodes.label: Applied / contact push force
nodes.summary: An applied force is any external push or pull exerted directly on an object.

nodes.label: Contact vs field (action-at-a-distance) forces
nodes.summary: Contact forces require touching surfaces while field forces (gravity, electromagnetic) act at a distance.

nodes.label: Drag / air resistance force
nodes.summary: Drag is a velocity-dependent resistive force from a fluid that opposes an object's motion through it.

nodes.label: Fundamental forces of nature
nodes.summary: The four fundamental forces are gravitational, electromagnetic, strong nuclear, and weak nuclear.

nodes.label: Weight (W = mg)
nodes.summary: Weight is the gravitational force on a mass, W = mg, directed toward Earth's center.

nodes.label: Hooke's law (F = −kx)
nodes.summary: A spring's restoring force is proportional to and opposite the displacement, F = −kx, where k is the spring constant.

nodes.label: Normal force
nodes.summary: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface that prevents interpenetration.

nodes.label: Spring (restoring) force
nodes.summary: A spring exerts a restoring force opposing its displacement (treated via Hooke's law).

nodes.label: Tension
nodes.summary: Tension is the pulling force transmitted along a rope, cable, or muscle/tendon.

nodes.label: Friction
nodes.summary: Friction is a contact force opposing relative motion or its tendency between surfaces.

nodes.label: Properties of friction
nodes.summary: Friction depends on normal force and surfaces but not on apparent contact area or speed.

nodes.label: Angle of repose (tanθ = μs)
nodes.summary: An object on an incline begins to slide when tanθ exceeds μs, so the critical angle satisfies tanθ = μs.

nodes.label: Independence from contact area
nodes.summary: To the MCAT model, friction does not depend on the apparent surface contact area.

nodes.label: Dependence on normal force
nodes.summary: Friction is proportional to the normal force pressing the surfaces together.

nodes.label: Direction opposes relative motion
nodes.summary: Friction always acts parallel to the surface, opposing the direction of (impending) relative sliding.

nodes.label: Static and kinetic friction
nodes.summary: Friction is static before sliding and kinetic during sliding, each with its own coefficient.

nodes.label: Coefficient of friction (μ)
nodes.summary: The dimensionless coefficient μ depends on the two surfaces and relates friction to normal force.

nodes.label: Kinetic friction (fk = μkN)
nodes.summary: Kinetic friction fk = μkN opposes motion at constant magnitude while surfaces slide.

nodes.label: μs > μk relationship
nodes.summary: The coefficient of static friction is generally greater than the kinetic coefficient for the same surfaces.

nodes.label: Rolling friction
nodes.summary: Rolling friction resists a rolling object and is generally much smaller than sliding kinetic friction.

nodes.label: Static friction (fs ≤ μsN)
nodes.summary: Static friction adjusts up to a maximum fs,max = μsN to prevent the start of sliding.

nodes.label: Inclined planes and connected systems
nodes.summary: Applied dynamics problems combine forces on inclines, pulleys, and connected masses.

nodes.label: Connected masses and tension
nodes.summary: Masses linked by ropes share a common acceleration analyzed with Newton's second law.

nodes.label: Atwood machine
nodes.summary: An Atwood machine has two masses over a pulley with a = g(m₁−m₂)/(m₁+m₂).

nodes.label: Common acceleration of linked masses
nodes.summary: Connected masses move with one acceleration found by applying ΣF = ma to the whole system.

nodes.label: Tension as an internal force
nodes.summary: Rope tension is found by isolating one mass after solving the whole system for acceleration.

nodes.label: Forces on an inclined plane
nodes.summary: Gravity on an incline resolves into components parallel and perpendicular to the surface.

nodes.label: Acceleration down a frictionless incline (a = g sinθ)
nodes.summary: A block slides down a frictionless incline with acceleration a = g sinθ, independent of mass.

nodes.label: Gravity components (mg sinθ, mg cosθ)
nodes.summary: On an incline the parallel weight component is mg sinθ and the perpendicular component is mg cosθ.

nodes.label: Normal force on an incline (N = mg cosθ)
nodes.summary: On a frictionless incline the normal force equals mg cosθ, less than the full weight.

nodes.label: Incline with friction (a = g(sinθ − μcosθ))
nodes.summary: With kinetic friction the down-incline acceleration is a = g(sinθ − μk cosθ).

nodes.label: Kinematics (translational motion)
nodes.summary: Kinematics describes translational motion through displacement, velocity, and acceleration without reference to its causes.

nodes.label: Position, displacement, velocity, acceleration
nodes.summary: Motion is characterized by position and its first and second time derivatives.

nodes.label: Acceleration
nodes.summary: Acceleration a = Δv/Δt is the rate of change of velocity, a vector with units m/s².

nodes.label: Average vs instantaneous velocity
nodes.summary: Average velocity is Δx/Δt over an interval while instantaneous velocity is the limit as Δt→0 (slope of x-t graph).

nodes.label: Average vs instantaneous acceleration
nodes.summary: Average acceleration is Δv/Δt over an interval while instantaneous acceleration is its limit as Δt→0.

nodes.label: Acceleration vs deceleration (sign conventions)
nodes.summary: Speed increases when a and v point the same way and decreases when they oppose, regardless of sign.

nodes.label: Distance vs displacement
nodes.summary: Distance is the total path length (scalar) while displacement is the straight-line change in position (vector).

nodes.label: Speed vs velocity
nodes.summary: Speed is distance/time (scalar) while velocity is displacement/time (vector).

nodes.label: Free fall and projectile motion
nodes.summary: Objects under gravity alone undergo free fall, and launched objects follow parabolic projectile paths.

nodes.label: Effect of air resistance on falling bodies
nodes.summary: Air resistance opposes motion and, when present, makes heavier or denser objects fall faster than light ones.

nodes.label: Free fall (motion under gravity only)
nodes.summary: Free fall is motion influenced only by gravity, with constant downward acceleration g and zero horizontal acceleration.

nodes.label: Acceleration due to gravity (g ≈ 9.8 m/s²)
nodes.summary: Near Earth's surface all objects fall with acceleration g ≈ 9.8 m/s² downward, independent of mass.

nodes.label: Horizontal projectile component (constant vx)
nodes.summary: Horizontal velocity vx = v₀cosθ stays constant because no horizontal force acts (ignoring air resistance).

nodes.label: Launch angle for maximum range (45°)
nodes.summary: On level ground (no air resistance) horizontal range is maximized at a 45° launch angle, and complementary angles share a range.

nodes.label: Parabolic trajectory shape
nodes.summary: Combining constant horizontal velocity with uniform vertical acceleration gives a parabolic path.

nodes.label: Independence of horizontal and vertical motion
nodes.summary: In projectile motion horizontal velocity is constant while vertical motion is governed independently by gravity.

nodes.label: Time of flight, max height, and range
nodes.summary: Flight time, peak height, and horizontal range are found by combining the independent vertical and horizontal equations.

nodes.label: Trajectory symmetry and apex
nodes.summary: At the apex vy = 0, and a level-ground trajectory is symmetric with equal ascent and descent times.

nodes.label: Terminal velocity
nodes.summary: A falling body reaches terminal (constant) velocity when drag balances weight so net force and acceleration are zero.

nodes.label: Vertical projectile component (vy with g)
nodes.summary: Vertical velocity vy = v₀sinθ − gt and the object follows free-fall kinematics in the vertical direction.

nodes.label: Motion graphs
nodes.summary: Position-, velocity-, and acceleration-time graphs encode motion through slopes and areas.

nodes.label: Acceleration–time graph (area = Δv)
nodes.summary: The area under an acceleration–time graph gives the change in velocity.

nodes.label: Position–time graph (slope = velocity)
nodes.summary: The slope of a position–time graph gives instantaneous velocity.

nodes.label: Slope vs area interpretation of motion graphs
nodes.summary: Slopes give rates (derivatives) and areas give accumulations (integrals), linking the three motion graphs.

nodes.label: Velocity–time graph (slope = a, area = Δx)
nodes.summary: On a velocity–time graph the slope is acceleration and the area under the curve is displacement.

nodes.label: Reference frames and relative velocity
nodes.summary: Velocity is measured relative to a reference frame and transforms by vector addition between frames.

nodes.label: Inertial reference frames
nodes.summary: Motion is described relative to a frame; an inertial frame is non-accelerating, where Newton's laws hold.

nodes.label: Relative velocity (vector addition of velocities)
nodes.summary: The velocity of A relative to C equals v(A/B) + v(B/C), e.g. swimmer-in-current or boat-crossing problems.

nodes.label: Uniformly accelerated motion (kinematic equations)
nodes.summary: Constant-acceleration motion is solved with the four kinematic (SUVAT) equations.

nodes.label: Validity (constant acceleration only)
nodes.summary: The kinematic equations apply only when acceleration is constant over the interval analyzed.

nodes.label: v = v₀ + at
nodes.summary: Final velocity equals initial velocity plus acceleration times time for constant acceleration.

nodes.label: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx
nodes.summary: Final velocity squared equals initial velocity squared plus 2aΔx, eliminating time.

nodes.label: x = ½(v₀ + v)t
nodes.summary: Displacement equals the average of initial and final velocity times time.

nodes.label: x = v₀t + ½at²
nodes.summary: Displacement equals initial-velocity time term plus one-half acceleration times time squared.

nodes.label: Momentum, impulse, and collisions
nodes.summary: Momentum is conserved in isolated systems, and impulse changes momentum during collisions.

nodes.label: Collisions
nodes.summary: Collisions are classified by whether kinetic energy is conserved.

nodes.label: One- vs two-dimensional collisions
nodes.summary: In two dimensions momentum is conserved independently along each perpendicular axis.

nodes.label: Elastic collisions
nodes.summary: In an elastic collision both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved.

nodes.label: Inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: In an inelastic collision momentum is conserved but kinetic energy is not.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy lost in inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: Inelastic collisions convert some kinetic energy to heat, sound, or deformation; the most is lost when objects stick.

nodes.label: Perfectly inelastic collisions
nodes.summary: In a perfectly inelastic collision the objects stick together and move with a common final velocity.

nodes.label: Conservation of momentum
nodes.summary: Total momentum of an isolated system is conserved through interactions.

nodes.label: Isolated system condition
nodes.summary: Momentum conservation holds only when external forces are negligible during the interaction.

nodes.label: Conservation of linear momentum
