nodes.summary: With no net external force, total system momentum before equals total momentum after (Σpi = Σpf).

nodes.label: Recoil and explosions
nodes.summary: From rest, conservation of momentum makes fragments recoil with equal and opposite momenta (e.g. gun recoil).

nodes.label: Linear momentum and impulse
nodes.summary: Momentum is mass times velocity, and impulse is the force–time product that changes it.

nodes.label: Momentum (p = mv)
nodes.summary: Linear momentum p = mv is a vector pointing in the direction of velocity.

nodes.label: Extending contact time to reduce force
nodes.summary: For a fixed Δp, increasing collision time decreases the force (basis of airbags and follow-through).

nodes.label: Impulse (J = FΔt = Δp)
nodes.summary: Impulse J = FΔt equals the change in momentum Δp of an object.

nodes.label: Impulse as area under F–t graph
nodes.summary: For a varying force, impulse equals the area under the force–time curve.

nodes.label: Impulse–momentum theorem
nodes.summary: The impulse delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (FΔt = mΔv).

nodes.label: Units, dimensions, and vectors
nodes.summary: Physics of motion is built on consistent SI units, dimensional analysis, and vector/scalar quantities.

nodes.label: Scalars vs vectors
nodes.summary: Quantities are scalars (magnitude only) or vectors (magnitude and direction).

nodes.label: Vector components (Ax = A cosθ, Ay = A sinθ)
nodes.summary: A vector resolves into perpendicular components Ax = A cosθ and Ay = A sinθ relative to an axis.

nodes.label: Cross (vector) product
nodes.summary: The cross product A×B = AB sinθ yields a vector perpendicular to both and underlies torque.

nodes.label: Dot (scalar) product
nodes.summary: The dot product A·B = AB cosθ yields a scalar and underlies the definition of work.

nodes.label: Magnitude and direction from components
nodes.summary: Magnitude is A = √(Ax² + Ay²) and direction is θ = arctan(Ay/Ax).

nodes.label: Right-hand rule for cross products
nodes.summary: The right-hand rule gives the direction of a cross product (and thus torque or angular vectors).

nodes.label: Scalar quantities
nodes.summary: Scalars such as distance, speed, mass, time, and energy have magnitude but no direction.

nodes.label: Scalar multiplication of a vector
nodes.summary: Multiplying a vector by a scalar scales its magnitude and, for a negative scalar, reverses its direction.

nodes.label: Unit vectors (î, ĵ, k̂)
nodes.summary: Unit vectors î, ĵ, k̂ have magnitude one and define the x, y, z directions for component notation.

nodes.label: Vector quantities
nodes.summary: Vectors such as displacement, velocity, acceleration, force, and momentum have both magnitude and direction.

nodes.label: Vector addition (tip-to-tail / parallelogram)
nodes.summary: Vectors add tip-to-tail or by the parallelogram rule, and the resultant is their geometric sum.

nodes.label: Vector subtraction
nodes.summary: Subtracting a vector means adding its negative (reversed direction).

nodes.label: Units and dimensional analysis
nodes.summary: Physical quantities are expressed in SI base and derived units and checked by dimensional analysis.

nodes.label: Derived units
nodes.summary: Derived units combine base units, e.g. newton (kg·m/s²), joule (kg·m²/s²), and watt (J/s).

nodes.label: Dimensional analysis
nodes.summary: Equations must be dimensionally consistent, and units can be tracked to convert and validate results.

nodes.label: Metric prefixes
nodes.summary: Prefixes such as kilo (10³), centi (10⁻²), milli (10⁻³), micro (10⁻⁶), and nano (10⁻⁹) scale SI units.

nodes.label: SI base units
nodes.summary: The mechanical SI base units are the meter (length), kilogram (mass), and second (time).

nodes.label: Unit conversion (factor-label method)
nodes.summary: Quantities are converted between units by multiplying by ratios equal to one (e.g. km/h to m/s).

nodes.label: Work, energy, and power
nodes.summary: Work transfers energy, energy is the capacity to do work, and power is the rate of doing work.

nodes.label: Conservation of mechanical energy
nodes.summary: Total mechanical energy is conserved when only conservative forces act.

nodes.label: Conservation of mechanical energy
nodes.summary: With only conservative forces, KE + PE remains constant so KEi + PEi = KEf + PEf.

nodes.label: Conservative vs nonconservative forces
nodes.summary: Conservative forces (gravity, spring) are path-independent; nonconservative forces (friction) dissipate energy.

nodes.label: KE–PE interconversion (pendulum, falling)
nodes.summary: In conservative systems energy continuously trades between kinetic and potential, as in a pendulum or roller coaster.

nodes.label: Conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics)
nodes.summary: Energy is neither created nor destroyed; it only transforms among forms (ΔU = Q − W).

nodes.label: Energy loss to friction (Wnc = ΔE)
nodes.summary: Nonconservative work equals the change in mechanical energy, e.g. friction converts it to heat.

nodes.label: Total mechanical energy (E = KE + PE)
nodes.summary: Total mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy.

nodes.label: Mechanical efficiency
nodes.summary: Efficiency compares useful output energy to total input energy.

nodes.label: Efficiency (Wout/Win)
nodes.summary: Efficiency is the ratio of useful work or energy output to total energy input, always less than 100%.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy
nodes.summary: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion.

nodes.label: Kinetic energy (KE = ½mv²)
nodes.summary: Kinetic energy KE = ½mv² is the energy a mass has due to its motion.

nodes.label: Work–energy theorem (Wnet = ΔKE)
nodes.summary: The net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy.

nodes.label: Potential energy
nodes.summary: Potential energy is stored energy of position or configuration.

nodes.label: Elastic PE (PE = ½kx²)
nodes.summary: A stretched/compressed spring stores elastic potential energy PE = ½kx².

nodes.label: Force as the negative gradient of PE
nodes.summary: A conservative force points toward lower potential energy (F = −dU/dx), the basis of PE-curve analysis.

nodes.label: Gravitational PE (PE = mgh)
nodes.summary: Gravitational potential energy PE = mgh depends on mass, gravity, and height above a reference.

nodes.label: Reference point dependence of PE
nodes.summary: Potential energy is defined relative to a chosen reference level, so only differences are physically meaningful.

nodes.label: Power
nodes.summary: Power is the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred.

nodes.label: Power (P = W/t)
nodes.summary: Power P = W/t is the work done per unit time, measured in watts (J/s).

nodes.label: Power as force × velocity (P = Fv)
nodes.summary: Instantaneous power equals force times velocity along the direction of motion (P = Fv cosθ).

nodes.label: Watt and horsepower (units of power)
nodes.summary: Power is measured in watts (1 W = 1 J/s); horsepower is an alternative unit (1 hp ≈ 746 W).

nodes.label: Simple machines
nodes.summary: Simple machines trade force for distance to provide mechanical advantage.

nodes.label: Inclined plane (force–distance trade-off)
nodes.summary: An inclined plane reduces the force needed to raise a load by increasing the distance over which it acts.

nodes.label: Pulleys and mechanical advantage
nodes.summary: A pulley system can redirect force and multiply it by the number of supporting rope segments.

nodes.label: Types of simple machines
nodes.summary: Simple machines include the lever, inclined plane, pulley, wedge, screw, and wheel-and-axle.

nodes.label: Work conservation in ideal machines
nodes.summary: An ideal machine conserves work: gains in force are paid for by equal increases in distance (no free energy).

nodes.label: Work
nodes.summary: Work is force acting through a displacement, a scalar energy transfer.

nodes.label: Work as area under F–x graph
nodes.summary: For variable force, work equals the area under the force–displacement curve.

nodes.label: Work (W = Fd cosθ)
nodes.summary: Work W = Fd cosθ is done when a force has a component along the displacement.

nodes.label: Path independence of conservative-force work
nodes.summary: Work by a conservative force (e.g. gravity) depends only on endpoints, not the path taken.

nodes.label: Work by/on a gas (W = PΔV)
nodes.summary: In a constant-pressure process a gas does work W = PΔV against its surroundings.

nodes.label: Positive, negative, and zero work
nodes.summary: Work is positive when force aids motion, negative when it opposes, and zero when perpendicular to displacement.

nodes.label: Joule (unit of work/energy)
nodes.summary: Work and energy are measured in joules, where 1 J = 1 N·m.

nodes.label: Blood pH & Acid-Base Buffering
nodes.summary: The bicarbonate buffer system links CO2 transport to blood pH regulation via the respiratory and renal systems.

nodes.label: Bicarbonate Buffer System
nodes.summary: The CO2/HCO3- equilibrium is the primary buffer maintaining blood pH near 7.4.

nodes.label: Normal blood pH
nodes.summary: Arterial blood is tightly buffered to pH ~7.35-7.45; deviations cause acidosis or alkalosis.

nodes.label: CO2/bicarbonate equilibrium
nodes.summary: CO2 + H2O <-> H2CO3 <-> H+ + HCO3- ties dissolved CO2 directly to blood [H+] and pH.

nodes.label: Henderson-Hasselbalch (blood)
nodes.summary: Blood pH = 6.1 + log([HCO3-]/(0.03*PCO2)) links pH to bicarbonate and CO2 partial pressure.

nodes.label: Le Chatelier shifts of the buffer
nodes.summary: Adding CO2 or H+ drives the equilibrium to raise [H+] (lower pH); removing CO2 by breathing drives it back, illustrating Le Chatelier's principle.

nodes.label: Respiratory pH Control
nodes.summary: Ventilation rate adjusts CO2 levels to rapidly compensate for pH disturbances.

nodes.label: Respiratory acidosis/alkalosis
nodes.summary: Respiratory acidosis results from CO2 retention (low pH) and alkalosis from excess CO2 loss (high pH).

nodes.label: Chemoreceptor control of ventilation
nodes.summary: Central and peripheral chemoreceptors sense rising PCO2/H+ (and low PO2) and increase ventilation to restore blood pH and gases.

nodes.label: Ventilation regulates CO2 and pH
nodes.summary: Hyperventilation lowers CO2 (raising pH) and hypoventilation raises CO2 (lowering pH), giving fast respiratory compensation.

nodes.label: Circulatory System Hemodynamics
nodes.summary: The cardiovascular system applies fluid mechanics to pump and distribute blood through vessels.

nodes.label: Capillary Fluid Exchange
nodes.summary: Fluid moves across capillary walls by the balance of hydrostatic and osmotic (oncotic) pressures.

nodes.label: Bulk flow (filtration vs reabsorption)
nodes.summary: Net outward filtration at the arteriolar end and net inward reabsorption at the venular end constitute capillary bulk flow.

nodes.label: Edema
nodes.summary: Edema is interstitial fluid accumulation from elevated hydrostatic pressure or reduced plasma oncotic pressure.

nodes.label: Capillary hydrostatic pressure
nodes.summary: Blood hydrostatic pressure pushes fluid out of the capillary, dominating at the arteriolar end.

nodes.label: Lymphatic return of filtered fluid
nodes.summary: The small net excess of filtered fluid and leaked protein is returned to the bloodstream by the lymphatic system.

nodes.label: Oncotic (colloid osmotic) pressure
nodes.summary: Plasma proteins (mainly albumin) create osmotic pressure that draws fluid back into the capillary, dominating at the venular end.

nodes.label: Starling forces
nodes.summary: Net capillary filtration is governed by the balance of hydrostatic pressure (pushing out) and oncotic pressure (pulling in).

nodes.label: Heart as a Pump
nodes.summary: The heart generates the pressure gradient that drives blood through the systemic and pulmonary circuits.

nodes.label: Blood pressure (systolic/diastolic)
nodes.summary: Arterial blood pressure (~120/80 mmHg) is the systolic peak over diastolic trough of pressure in large arteries.

nodes.label: Blood pressure measurement (Korotkoff/turbulence)
nodes.summary: A sphygmomanometer reads systolic/diastolic pressure from turbulent Korotkoff sounds as a cuff occludes and releases the artery.

nodes.label: Cardiac output (CO = HR x SV)
nodes.summary: Cardiac output is the volume of blood pumped per minute, CO = heart rate x stroke volume, analogous to flow rate Q.

nodes.label: Four-chamber structure
nodes.summary: The heart has two atria (receiving) and two ventricles (pumping) separating oxygenated and deoxygenated blood.

nodes.label: Mean arterial & pulse pressure
nodes.summary: Pulse pressure is systolic minus diastolic; mean arterial pressure approx diastolic + one-third pulse pressure, the effective perfusion pressure.

nodes.label: Systole and diastole
nodes.summary: Systole is ventricular contraction (ejection) and diastole is relaxation (filling), generating pulsatile pressure.

nodes.label: Pulmonary vs systemic circuits
nodes.summary: The right heart drives the low-pressure pulmonary circuit (lungs) and the left heart the high-pressure systemic circuit (body), arranged in series.

nodes.label: Blood Vessels & Flow
nodes.summary: Vessel geometry governs blood velocity, pressure, and resistance throughout the circuit.

nodes.label: Arteries, capillaries, veins
nodes.summary: Arteries carry blood from the heart at high pressure, capillaries exchange materials, and veins return blood at low pressure.

nodes.label: Blood viscosity (hematocrit)
nodes.summary: Blood viscosity exceeds water and rises with hematocrit; changes alter resistance and flow per Poiseuille's eta term.

nodes.label: Vessel compliance and elasticity
nodes.summary: Elastic arteries stretch in systole and recoil in diastole (Windkessel effect), smoothing pulsatile flow.

nodes.label: Pressure drop across the circuit
nodes.summary: Blood pressure falls steadily from arteries to veins, with the largest drop across the high-resistance arterioles.

nodes.label: Vascular resistance and radius
nodes.summary: Per Poiseuille, vasoconstriction/dilation of arterioles strongly regulates resistance and blood flow via the r^4 term.

nodes.label: Total peripheral resistance & MAP = CO x TPR
nodes.summary: Mean arterial pressure equals cardiac output times total peripheral resistance, the circulatory analog of deltaP = Q*R.

nodes.label: Velocity vs total cross-sectional area
nodes.summary: By continuity, blood velocity is slowest in capillaries because their enormous total cross-sectional area is greatest.

nodes.label: Venous return (gravity, valves, muscle pump)
nodes.summary: Low-pressure venous return relies on one-way valves, skeletal-muscle pumping, and hydrostatic effects (gravity raises pressure in dependent veins).

nodes.label: Fluid Dynamics
nodes.summary: Fluid dynamics describes moving fluids via continuity, Bernoulli's energy conservation, and flow regimes.

nodes.label: Bernoulli's Equation
nodes.summary: Bernoulli's equation is conservation of energy per unit volume along a streamline in an ideal fluid.

nodes.label: Applications (lift, pitot, aneurysm)
nodes.summary: Bernoulli/continuity explain pitot tubes and why arterial widening (aneurysm) lowers flow speed and raises pressure; for an airfoil, faster flow over the wing accompanies lower pressure, but lift is not fully explained by Bernoulli alone (it arises mainly from downward deflection of air, Newton's third law).

nodes.label: Bernoulli assumptions / limits
nodes.summary: Bernoulli's equation requires an ideal fluid: incompressible, non-viscous, steady, laminar flow along a single streamline.

nodes.label: Bernoulli effect (faster flow, lower pressure)
nodes.summary: At constant height, an increase in fluid speed corresponds to a decrease in static pressure, and vice versa.

nodes.label: Static vs dynamic pressure
nodes.summary: Static pressure (P) is the fluid's thermodynamic pressure while dynamic pressure (1/2 rho*v^2) is the kinetic-energy contribution.

nodes.label: Bernoulli as energy conservation
nodes.summary: Bernoulli's equation is conservation of mechanical energy per unit volume for an ideal, incompressible, non-viscous fluid in steady flow.

nodes.label: Bernoulli's equation
nodes.summary: P + (1/2)rho*v^2 + rho*g*h = constant along a streamline, summing static pressure, dynamic pressure, and gravitational terms.

nodes.label: Pitot tube / stagnation pressure
nodes.summary: A pitot tube measures flow speed from the difference between stagnation (total) and static pressure via Bernoulli.

nodes.label: Torricelli's theorem (efflux speed)
nodes.summary: Fluid draining from a hole at depth h exits at v = sqrt(2gh), the free-fall speed, a direct Bernoulli application.

nodes.label: Venturi effect
nodes.summary: In a constricted tube, fluid speeds up and its pressure drops, the basis of the Venturi meter and atomizers.

nodes.label: Continuity Equation
nodes.summary: Conservation of mass requires that volumetric flow rate is constant along a streamline for an incompressible fluid.

nodes.label: Continuity equation (A1*v1 = A2*v2)
nodes.summary: For an incompressible fluid, A1*v1 = A2*v2, so flow speed increases where cross-sectional area decreases.

nodes.label: Incompressibility assumption
nodes.summary: Continuity in the form A1*v1 = A2*v2 assumes constant density (incompressible flow), valid for liquids and low-speed gas flow.

nodes.label: Conservation of mass basis
nodes.summary: The continuity equation expresses conservation of mass: what flows in must flow out for an incompressible fluid.

nodes.label: Types of Flow
nodes.summary: Flow may be laminar or turbulent, steady or unsteady, and is characterized by the Reynolds number.

nodes.label: Critical velocity (onset of turbulence)
nodes.summary: Above a critical velocity (Re roughly 2000-4000 in a pipe) laminar flow transitions to turbulent; vessel narrowing or anemia can trigger it physiologically.

nodes.label: Volumetric flow rate (Q = A*v)
nodes.summary: Volumetric flow rate Q = A*v (m^3/s) is the volume of fluid passing a cross-section per unit time.

nodes.label: Laminar flow
nodes.summary: Laminar flow is smooth, orderly flow in parallel layers with no mixing, occurring at low velocity/Reynolds number.

nodes.label: Reynolds number
nodes.summary: The Reynolds number Re = rho*v*D/eta is a dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces predicting laminar (low) vs turbulent (high) flow.

nodes.label: Steady (streamline) vs unsteady flow
nodes.summary: In steady flow the velocity at any fixed point does not change with time, the regime assumed by continuity and Bernoulli.

nodes.label: Streamlines
nodes.summary: Streamlines trace the path of fluid particles in steady flow and never cross; their spacing indicates speed.

nodes.label: Turbulent flow
nodes.summary: Turbulent flow is chaotic, eddying flow with mixing that occurs above a critical (high) Reynolds number.

nodes.label: Viscous (Real) Flow
nodes.summary: Real fluids lose pressure to viscous resistance, quantified by Poiseuille's law in tubes.

nodes.label: Poiseuille's law
nodes.summary: For laminar viscous flow in a tube, Q = (pi*r^4*deltaP)/(8*eta*L), so flow scales with the fourth power of radius.

nodes.label: Fourth-power radius dependence
nodes.summary: Because flow scales with r^4, small changes in vessel radius cause large changes in flow and resistance (vasoconstriction/dilation).

nodes.label: Flow resistance
nodes.summary: Resistance R = 8*eta*L/(pi*r^4) relates pressure drop and flow (deltaP = Q*R), analogous to electrical resistance.

nodes.label: Resistance in series and parallel
nodes.summary: Vascular resistances add in series and combine reciprocally in parallel, mirroring resistors and explaining low capillary-bed resistance.

nodes.label: Viscous drag / pressure drop in pipes
nodes.summary: Real viscous flow continuously dissipates energy as a pressure drop along a tube, unlike the ideal Bernoulli case.

nodes.label: Fluid Properties & Definitions
nodes.summary: A fluid is a substance (liquid or gas) that flows and conforms to its container, characterized by density, viscosity, and surface tension.

nodes.label: Basic Fluid Quantities
nodes.summary: Density, specific gravity, and pressure are the foundational scalar quantities describing a fluid.

nodes.label: Atmospheric pressure
nodes.summary: Atmospheric pressure (~101.3 kPa at sea level) is the weight of the air column pressing on a surface.

nodes.label: Barometer
nodes.summary: A barometer measures atmospheric pressure by the height of a mercury column it supports (760 mmHg at 1 atm), with vacuum above.

nodes.label: Density (rho = m/V)
nodes.summary: Density is mass per unit volume, rho = m/V, with SI units kg/m^3 and water ~1000 kg/m^3.

nodes.label: Definition of a fluid
nodes.summary: A fluid is any substance (liquid or gas) that cannot resist a shear stress and therefore flows continuously under it.

nodes.label: Gauge vs absolute pressure
nodes.summary: Absolute pressure is total pressure (P0 + rho*g*h) while gauge pressure is pressure relative to atmospheric (P_abs - P_atm).

nodes.label: Open-tube manometer
nodes.summary: A U-tube manometer measures gauge pressure from the height difference of fluid columns, with gas pressure read as rho*g*delta-h relative to atmosphere.

nodes.label: Pressure (P = F/A)
nodes.summary: Pressure is the normal force per unit area, P = F/A, a scalar with SI unit pascal (Pa = N/m^2).

nodes.label: Pressure units (Pa, atm, mmHg, torr)
nodes.summary: 1 atm = 101.3 kPa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr = 14.7 psi, with 1 mmHg approximately 1 torr.

nodes.label: Specific gravity
nodes.summary: Specific gravity is the dimensionless ratio of a substance's density to that of water (1000 kg/m^3 at 4 C).

nodes.label: Weight density (specific weight)
nodes.summary: Weight density is weight per unit volume (rho*g), the quantity that actually appears in hydrostatic and buoyancy expressions.

nodes.label: Viscosity & Surface Tension
nodes.summary: Viscosity quantifies a fluid's resistance to flow, and surface tension arises from cohesive forces at a liquid surface.

nodes.label: Capillary action
nodes.summary: Capillary action is the rise or fall of a liquid in a thin tube driven by the balance of adhesion, cohesion, and surface tension.

nodes.label: Cohesion vs adhesion
nodes.summary: Cohesion is attraction between like molecules while adhesion is attraction to a different surface; their balance sets meniscus shape and capillarity.

nodes.label: Ideal (inviscid) fluid
nodes.summary: An ideal fluid is incompressible and has zero viscosity, the simplifying assumption behind Bernoulli's equation and continuity.

nodes.label: Meniscus formation
nodes.summary: A concave meniscus forms when adhesion exceeds cohesion (water in glass) and a convex meniscus when cohesion dominates (mercury).

nodes.label: Surface tension (gamma)
nodes.summary: Surface tension is the energy or force per unit length at a liquid surface caused by unbalanced cohesive forces on surface molecules.

nodes.label: Viscosity (eta)
nodes.summary: Viscosity is a fluid's internal resistance to shear/flow, measured in Pa*s (SI) or poise.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence of viscosity
nodes.summary: Liquid viscosity decreases with temperature while gas viscosity increases with temperature, due to opposite molecular mechanisms.

nodes.label: Fluid Statics (Hydrostatics)
nodes.summary: Hydrostatics describes pressure, buoyancy, and force transmission in fluids at rest.

nodes.label: Buoyancy & Archimedes' Principle
nodes.summary: A submerged or floating object experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the weight of displaced fluid.

nodes.label: Apparent weight in fluid
nodes.summary: A submerged object's apparent weight equals its true weight minus the buoyant force (W_apparent = W - Fb).

nodes.label: Archimedes' principle
nodes.summary: The buoyant force on an object equals the weight of the fluid it displaces, Fb = rho_fluid * V_displaced * g.

nodes.label: Buoyant force equation
nodes.summary: Fb = rho_fluid * V_submerged * g, arising from the greater hydrostatic pressure on the bottom than top of an object.

nodes.label: Floating, sinking, and neutral buoyancy
nodes.summary: An object floats if its average density is less than the fluid's, sinks if greater, and is neutrally buoyant if equal.

nodes.label: Fraction submerged
nodes.summary: A floating object submerges a fraction equal to the ratio of its density to the fluid's density (rho_obj/rho_fluid).

nodes.label: Buoyancy from the pressure gradient
nodes.summary: The buoyant force originates from greater hydrostatic pressure on the lower surface than the upper surface of a submerged object.

nodes.label: Hydrostatic Pressure
nodes.summary: Pressure in a static fluid increases linearly with depth and is transmitted in all directions.

nodes.label: Connected vessels / hydrostatic equilibrium
nodes.summary: A connected static fluid reaches the same height in open columns and equal pressure at equal depth regardless of column shape or width.

nodes.label: Pressure depends only on depth
nodes.summary: In a static fluid, pressure depends only on vertical depth and fluid density, not on container shape (hydrostatic paradox).

nodes.label: Hydrostatic pressure equation (P = P0 + rho*g*h)
nodes.summary: Absolute pressure at depth h is P = P0 + rho*g*h, where rho*g*h is the gauge (hydrostatic) contribution.

nodes.label: Pressure acts in all directions (isotropy)
nodes.summary: At any point in a static fluid, pressure acts equally in all directions and perpendicular to any surface.

nodes.label: Pascal's Principle
nodes.summary: Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every part of the fluid.

nodes.label: Hydraulic lift / machines
nodes.summary: A hydraulic system multiplies force via F1/A1 = F2/A2 while conserving work (volume), so the larger piston moves less distance.

nodes.label: Hydraulic mechanical advantage & work conservation
nodes.summary: Force is multiplied by the area ratio A2/A1 but distance shrinks by the same factor (A1*d1 = A2*d2), so work in equals work out.

nodes.label: Pascal's principle statement
nodes.summary: A pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally to every point and the container walls.

nodes.label: Gas Exchange & Diffusion
nodes.summary: Gases move between alveoli, blood, and tissues by passive diffusion governed by Fick's law and partial pressure gradients.

nodes.label: Alveolar Gas Exchange
nodes.summary: O2 and CO2 are exchanged between alveolar air and pulmonary capillary blood at the lungs.

nodes.label: Alveolar structure for exchange
nodes.summary: Alveoli provide a vast, thin, moist, highly vascularized surface optimizing gas exchange efficiency.

nodes.label: CO2 unloading from blood
nodes.summary: CO2 diffuses from high-PCO2 blood (~46 mmHg) into lower-PCO2 alveolar air (~40 mmHg) for exhalation.

nodes.label: Law of Laplace (alveoli)
nodes.summary: For a sphere, pressure P = 2*gamma/r, so smaller alveoli have higher collapse pressure unless surfactant lowers surface tension.

nodes.label: O2 loading into blood
nodes.summary: O2 diffuses from high alveolar PO2 (~100 mmHg) into low-PO2 deoxygenated capillary blood (~40 mmHg).

nodes.label: Pulmonary surfactant
nodes.summary: Surfactant lowers alveolar surface tension, preventing collapse (atelectasis) and reducing the work of breathing.

nodes.label: Diffusion of Gases
nodes.summary: Gas exchange occurs by passive diffusion described quantitatively by Fick's law of diffusion.

nodes.label: Impaired diffusion (thickened membrane)
nodes.summary: Fibrosis, edema, or emphysema thicken the membrane or reduce surface area, slowing gas exchange per Fick's law.

nodes.label: Fick's law of diffusion
nodes.summary: Diffusion rate is proportional to membrane area and partial-pressure difference and inversely proportional to membrane thickness.

nodes.label: Factors affecting diffusion rate
nodes.summary: Diffusion is faster with greater surface area, larger partial-pressure gradient, higher solubility, and thinner membranes.

nodes.label: Passive diffusion down gradient
nodes.summary: Gases cross the respiratory membrane by passive diffusion, moving from high to low partial pressure with no energy cost.

nodes.label: Respiratory (alveolar-capillary) membrane
nodes.summary: The thin alveolar-capillary membrane maximizes surface area and minimizes thickness for efficient gas diffusion.

nodes.label: Specialized Exchange Mechanisms
nodes.summary: Some animals enhance gas exchange by countercurrent arrangements that maintain a gradient along the entire exchange surface.

nodes.label: Concurrent vs countercurrent efficiency
nodes.summary: Concurrent flow equilibrates partway and limits extraction, whereas countercurrent flow sustains the gradient for greater net transfer.

nodes.label: Countercurrent gas exchange (fish gills)
nodes.summary: Blood and water flow in opposite directions across fish gills, maintaining a favorable PO2 gradient along the whole length for near-complete extraction.

nodes.label: Tissue Gas Exchange
nodes.summary: At the tissues, O2 leaves blood and CO2 enters, driven by reversed partial-pressure gradients.

nodes.label: CO2 pickup from tissues
nodes.summary: CO2 produced by metabolism diffuses from high-PCO2 tissue into blood for transport to the lungs.

nodes.label: O2 delivery to tissues
nodes.summary: O2 diffuses from oxygenated blood (~100 mmHg) into low-PO2 metabolizing tissue (~40 mmHg or lower).

nodes.label: Gases & Gas Laws
nodes.summary: Gas behavior is described by the ideal gas law and partial pressures, foundational to gas movement and exchange.

nodes.label: Ideal Gas Behavior
nodes.summary: The ideal gas law relates pressure, volume, temperature, and moles of a gas.

nodes.label: Boyle's law
nodes.summary: At constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional (P1V1 = P2V2), explaining lung inflation.

nodes.label: Boyle's law in breathing mechanics
nodes.summary: Diaphragm contraction enlarges thoracic volume, lowering intrapulmonary pressure below atmospheric so air flows in (negative-pressure breathing).

nodes.label: Charles's law
nodes.summary: At constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to absolute temperature (V1/T1 = V2/T2).

nodes.label: Ideal gas law (PV = nRT)
nodes.summary: PV = nRT relates pressure, volume, moles, and absolute temperature with R = 8.314 J/(mol*K).

nodes.label: Partial Pressures
nodes.summary: In a gas mixture, each gas contributes a partial pressure proportional to its mole fraction.

nodes.label: Atmospheric vs alveolar partial pressures
nodes.summary: Inspired air PO2 (~160 mmHg) falls to alveolar PO2 (~100 mmHg) due to humidification and CO2 addition.

nodes.label: Dalton's law of partial pressures
nodes.summary: The total pressure of a gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of its components (P_total = sum P_i).

nodes.label: Partial pressure from mole fraction
nodes.summary: A gas's partial pressure equals its mole fraction times the total pressure (P_i = X_i * P_total).

nodes.label: Partial pressure gradient as driver
nodes.summary: Gases diffuse down their individual partial pressure (not total pressure) gradients between alveoli and blood.

nodes.label: Water vapor pressure of humidified air
nodes.summary: Inspired air is humidified to a water vapor partial pressure of ~47 mmHg, which lowers the partial pressures of the other gases.

nodes.label: Gas Solubility
nodes.summary: The amount of gas dissolved in a liquid depends on its partial pressure and solubility (Henry's law).

nodes.label: CO2 more soluble than O2
nodes.summary: CO2 is roughly 20x more soluble in plasma than O2, so a small PCO2 gradient suffices for adequate CO2 exchange.

nodes.label: Decompression sickness
nodes.summary: Rapid pressure drop lowers gas solubility per Henry's law, so dissolved N2 forms bubbles (the bends).

nodes.label: Henry's law
nodes.summary: The concentration of dissolved gas is proportional to its partial pressure above the liquid (C = k*P).

nodes.label: Factors affecting solubility
nodes.summary: Gas solubility increases with higher partial pressure and lower temperature, relevant to O2/CO2 transport and decompression.

nodes.label: Gas Transport in Blood
nodes.summary: Oxygen and carbon dioxide are carried in blood by hemoglobin and as bicarbonate, with cooperative and pH-dependent binding.

nodes.label: Carbon Dioxide Transport
nodes.summary: CO2 is transported mainly as bicarbonate, with smaller amounts bound to hemoglobin and dissolved in plasma.

nodes.label: Bicarbonate transport
nodes.summary: Most CO2 (~70%) travels as bicarbonate (HCO3-) formed in red blood cells via carbonic anhydrase.

nodes.label: Carbaminohemoglobin
nodes.summary: About 20-23% of CO2 binds to hemoglobin's globin amino groups as carbaminohemoglobin (not at the heme).

nodes.label: Carbonic anhydrase reaction
nodes.summary: Carbonic anhydrase catalyzes CO2 + H2O <-> H2CO3 <-> H+ + HCO3-, speeding bicarbonate formation in red blood cells.

nodes.label: Chloride shift
nodes.summary: As HCO3- exits the red blood cell, Cl- enters to maintain electrical neutrality (the chloride/Hamburger shift).

nodes.label: Dissolved CO2 in plasma
nodes.summary: About 7-10% of CO2 is carried physically dissolved in plasma per Henry's law.

nodes.label: Haldane effect
nodes.summary: Deoxygenated hemoglobin binds CO2 and H+ more readily, so O2 unloading at tissues enhances CO2 uptake.

nodes.label: Oxygen Transport
nodes.summary: Most oxygen is carried bound to hemoglobin, with a small fraction dissolved in plasma.

nodes.label: Right/left shifts of the curve
nodes.summary: A right shift lowers O2 affinity (more unloading); a left shift raises affinity (more loading).

nodes.label: Bohr effect
nodes.summary: Increased CO2, H+ (low pH), temperature, and 2,3-BPG shift the curve right, promoting O2 unloading at active tissues.

nodes.label: 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (2,3-BPG)
nodes.summary: 2,3-BPG binds deoxyhemoglobin, lowering O2 affinity (right shift) and increasing during high-altitude adaptation.

nodes.label: Carbon monoxide poisoning
nodes.summary: CO binds hemoglobin ~200x more tightly than O2, blocking O2 sites and left-shifting the curve so remaining O2 is poorly unloaded.

nodes.label: Cooperative O2 binding
nodes.summary: O2 binding to hemoglobin is cooperative, so each bound O2 increases affinity for the next, producing a sigmoidal curve.

nodes.label: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve
nodes.summary: The sigmoidal curve plots hemoglobin saturation vs PO2, with a steep midrange enabling efficient tissue O2 delivery.

nodes.label: Dissolved O2 in plasma
nodes.summary: A small fraction (~2%) of oxygen is physically dissolved in plasma per Henry's law and sets the blood PO2.

nodes.label: Fetal hemoglobin
nodes.summary: Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) has higher O2 affinity (left-shifted curve) than adult HbA, drawing O2 across the placenta.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin as O2 carrier
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin is a tetrameric protein that reversibly binds up to four O2 molecules, carrying ~98% of blood oxygen.

nodes.label: Myoglobin
nodes.summary: Myoglobin is a monomeric muscle O2-storage protein with a hyperbolic, high-affinity, non-cooperative binding curve.

nodes.label: O2 content & carrying capacity
nodes.summary: Blood O2 content depends on hemoglobin concentration and percent saturation far more than on dissolved O2; anemia lowers capacity despite normal PO2.

nodes.label: P50 (half-saturation PO2)
nodes.summary: P50 is the PO2 at 50% hemoglobin saturation (~26 mmHg); a higher P50 means lower affinity (right shift), lower P50 higher affinity.

nodes.label: Batteries and Practical Cells
nodes.summary: Real-world cells store and deliver energy through redox chemistry in various designs.

nodes.label: Corrosion
nodes.summary: Corrosion is an unwanted electrochemical oxidation of metals.

nodes.label: Cathodic protection
nodes.summary: Cathodic protection uses a more easily oxidized sacrificial anode to prevent corrosion of the protected metal.

nodes.label: Corrosion as electrochemistry
nodes.summary: Corrosion (e.g. rusting of iron) is the spontaneous electrochemical oxidation of a metal.

nodes.label: Battery Types
nodes.summary: Batteries are classified by whether their reactions can be electrically reversed.

nodes.label: Fuel cells
nodes.summary: A fuel cell continuously generates electricity from a fuel such as hydrogen oxidized at the anode.

nodes.label: Primary (non-rechargeable) cells
nodes.summary: Primary cells like the alkaline battery use an irreversible reaction and cannot be recharged.

nodes.label: Recharging reverses the cell to electrolytic mode
nodes.summary: Recharging a secondary cell drives the reaction backward, making it temporarily operate as an electrolytic cell.

nodes.label: Secondary (rechargeable) cells
nodes.summary: Secondary cells like lead-acid and lithium-ion are recharged by reversing the cell reaction electrolytically.

nodes.label: Capacitance and Dielectrics
nodes.summary: Capacitors store charge and electrical energy in an electric field between conductors.

nodes.label: Capacitor Basics
nodes.summary: A capacitor stores charge proportional to the applied voltage.

nodes.label: Capacitance (C = Q/V)
nodes.summary: Capacitance C = Q/V is the charge stored per unit voltage, measured in farads.

nodes.label: The farad
nodes.summary: One farad is one coulomb of stored charge per volt of applied potential difference.

nodes.label: Uniform field between plates (E = V/d)
nodes.summary: The electric field between parallel plates is uniform with magnitude E = V/d.

nodes.label: Parallel-plate capacitor (C = epsilon0 A/d)
nodes.summary: For a parallel-plate capacitor C = epsilon0 A/d, increasing with plate area and decreasing with gap.

nodes.label: Capacitors in Combination
nodes.summary: Capacitors combine oppositely to resistors in series and parallel.

nodes.label: Capacitors in parallel
nodes.summary: Parallel capacitances add directly: C_eq = C1 + C2 + ... and each shares the same voltage.

nodes.label: Capacitors in series
nodes.summary: Series capacitances combine reciprocally: 1/C_eq = 1/C1 + 1/C2 + ... and each holds the same charge.

nodes.label: Dielectrics
nodes.summary: Inserting a dielectric increases capacitance by reducing the internal field.

nodes.label: Dielectric breakdown
nodes.summary: Beyond a critical field strength a dielectric ionizes and conducts, causing dielectric breakdown.

nodes.label: Dielectric effect: battery-connected vs isolated
nodes.summary: Inserting a dielectric holds V fixed and raises Q if the battery stays connected, but holds Q fixed and lowers V if the capacitor is isolated.

nodes.label: Dielectric constant (C = kappa C0)
nodes.summary: A dielectric of constant kappa multiplies capacitance, C = kappa C0, by polarizing and weakening the field.

nodes.label: Dielectric polarization
nodes.summary: An applied field polarizes a dielectric, creating an opposing field that lowers the net field and net voltage.

nodes.label: Energy Stored in Capacitors
nodes.summary: A charged capacitor stores potential energy in its electric field.

nodes.label: Energy density of the electric field
nodes.summary: Energy stored in a capacitor resides in the electric field, with energy density u = 1/2 epsilon0 E^2.

nodes.label: Capacitor energy (U = 1/2 CV^2)
nodes.summary: Energy stored in a capacitor is U = 1/2 CV^2 = 1/2 QV = Q^2/(2C).

nodes.label: Cell Potential and Thermodynamics
nodes.summary: Cell potential quantifies the driving force of a redox reaction and connects to free energy and equilibrium.

nodes.label: The Nernst Equation
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation gives cell potential under nonstandard concentrations.

nodes.label: Concentration effects on potential
nodes.summary: Changing reactant or product concentrations shifts cell potential via the reaction quotient Q.

nodes.label: Nernst equation
nodes.summary: The Nernst equation E = E0 - (RT/nF) ln Q corrects cell potential for nonstandard concentrations.

nodes.label: E = 0 at equilibrium
nodes.summary: A cell reaches equilibrium (a dead battery) when Q = K and the cell potential equals zero.

nodes.label: Standard Reduction Potentials
nodes.summary: Standard reduction potentials rank species by tendency to be reduced under standard conditions.

nodes.label: Activity series and predicting reactions
nodes.summary: The activity series ranks metals by ease of oxidation, predicting which metal displaces another from solution.

nodes.label: Standard cell potential (E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode)
nodes.summary: Standard cell potential E_cell = E_cathode - E_anode using both as reduction potentials.

nodes.label: Cell potential is intensive
nodes.summary: Cell potential does not change when half-reaction coefficients are multiplied, since it is an intensive property.

nodes.label: Standard reduction potential
nodes.summary: A standard reduction potential measures a half-reaction's tendency to gain electrons relative to the SHE.

nodes.label: Standard hydrogen electrode (SHE)
nodes.summary: The standard hydrogen electrode is the reference assigned a potential of exactly 0 V.

nodes.label: Sign of E_cell and spontaneity
nodes.summary: A positive E_cell indicates a spontaneous (galvanic) reaction and a negative E_cell a nonspontaneous (electrolytic) one.

nodes.label: Standard conditions for electrochemistry
nodes.summary: Standard electrochemical conditions are 1 M solute concentrations, 1 atm gas pressures, and usually 25 C.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic Relationships
nodes.summary: Cell potential is connected to Gibbs free energy and the equilibrium constant.

nodes.label: Cell potential and K (delta G = -RT ln K)
nodes.summary: Standard cell potential relates to the equilibrium constant via delta G = -RT ln K = -nFE.

nodes.label: Free energy and cell potential (delta G = -nFE)
nodes.summary: Gibbs free energy relates to cell potential by delta G = -nFE_cell.

nodes.label: Electric Charge and Current
nodes.summary: Charge is the fundamental quantity carried by particles, and current is its flow rate through a conductor.

nodes.label: Electric Charge
nodes.summary: Charge is a conserved, quantized property of matter measured in coulombs.

nodes.label: Conservation of charge
nodes.summary: Total electric charge in an isolated system is conserved and never created or destroyed.

nodes.label: The coulomb
nodes.summary: One coulomb is the SI unit of charge, equal to the charge of about 6.24 x 10^18 elementary charges.

nodes.label: Elementary charge
nodes.summary: The elementary charge e = 1.6 x 10^-19 C is the magnitude of charge on a proton or electron.

nodes.label: Charge quantization
nodes.summary: Any observable charge is an integer multiple of the elementary charge e (Q = ne).

nodes.label: Two types of charge and like/unlike interaction
nodes.summary: Charge is positive or negative; like charges repel and unlike charges attract.

nodes.label: Electric Current
nodes.summary: Current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a cross-section of conductor.

nodes.label: The ampere
nodes.summary: One ampere equals one coulomb of charge flowing per second.

nodes.label: Charge carriers
nodes.summary: Charge carriers are mobile charges (electrons in metals, ions in solutions) responsible for current.

nodes.label: Conductors, insulators, and semiconductors
nodes.summary: Conductors have abundant mobile charges, insulators have almost none, and semiconductors are intermediate and tunable.

nodes.label: Conventional current direction
nodes.summary: Conventional current flows in the direction of positive charge movement, opposite to electron flow.

nodes.label: Direct current vs alternating current
nodes.summary: Direct current flows steadily in one direction while alternating current periodically reverses direction.

nodes.label: Definition of current (I = Q/t)
nodes.summary: Current I = Q/t is the charge passing a point per unit time, measured in amperes (C/s).

nodes.label: Drift velocity
nodes.summary: Drift velocity is the slow average net velocity of charge carriers superimposed on their random thermal motion (I = nqvA).

nodes.label: DC Circuits and Circuit Analysis
nodes.summary: Circuit analysis uses series/parallel rules and conservation laws to find currents and voltages.

nodes.label: Circuit Elements and Diagrams
nodes.summary: Circuits are built from sources and passive elements represented by standard symbols.

nodes.label: Ammeters and voltmeters
nodes.summary: An ideal ammeter (zero resistance) is placed in series and an ideal voltmeter (infinite resistance) in parallel.

nodes.label: Battery / voltage source
nodes.summary: A battery is a source of emf that drives current through a circuit.

nodes.label: Capacitor as a circuit element
nodes.summary: A capacitor stores charge and blocks steady DC current once fully charged, acting as an open circuit at steady state.

nodes.label: Open circuits and short circuits
nodes.summary: An open circuit breaks the path so no current flows, while a short circuit provides a near-zero-resistance bypass with large current.

nodes.label: Resistor
nodes.summary: A resistor is a circuit element that dissipates energy and limits current via its resistance.

nodes.label: Wires and switches
nodes.summary: Ideal wires have negligible resistance and a switch opens or closes a conducting path.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's Laws
nodes.summary: Kirchhoff's laws apply conservation of charge and energy to analyze any circuit.

nodes.label: Current divider
nodes.summary: Parallel resistors split current in inverse proportion to their resistances.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's junction (current) rule
nodes.summary: The sum of currents into a node equals the sum out, expressing conservation of charge.

nodes.label: Kirchhoff's loop (voltage) rule
nodes.summary: The sum of voltage changes around any closed loop is zero, expressing conservation of energy.

nodes.label: Voltage divider
nodes.summary: Series resistors split the source voltage in proportion to their resistances.

nodes.label: Series and Parallel Combinations
nodes.summary: Resistors and other elements combine differently depending on series or parallel arrangement.

nodes.label: Equivalent resistance reduction
nodes.summary: Complex networks are simplified by stepwise reduction of series and parallel sub-networks into one equivalent resistance.

nodes.label: Parallel equivalent is smaller than any branch
nodes.summary: Adding resistors in parallel lowers total resistance below that of the smallest branch and raises total current.

nodes.label: Resistors in parallel
nodes.summary: Parallel resistances combine reciprocally: 1/R_eq = 1/R1 + 1/R2 + ... and share the same voltage.

nodes.label: Parallel voltage is constant
nodes.summary: In a parallel circuit each branch experiences the same voltage across it.

nodes.label: Series current is constant
nodes.summary: In a series circuit the current is identical through every element.

nodes.label: Resistors in series
nodes.summary: Series resistances add directly: R_eq = R1 + R2 + ... and the same current flows through each.

nodes.label: Electrochemistry Fundamentals
nodes.summary: Electrochemistry links redox reactions to electrical energy through oxidation and reduction at electrodes.

nodes.label: Electrodes and Cell Components
nodes.summary: Every electrochemical cell has an anode, a cathode, an electrolyte, and an external circuit.

nodes.label: Anode (site of oxidation)
nodes.summary: The anode is the electrode where oxidation occurs in any electrochemical cell.

nodes.label: Cathode (site of reduction)
nodes.summary: The cathode is the electrode where reduction occurs in any electrochemical cell.

nodes.label: Electrolyte
nodes.summary: The electrolyte is an ion-conducting medium that completes the internal circuit between electrodes.

nodes.label: Electron flow direction
nodes.summary: Electrons always travel from anode to cathode through the external wire in any cell.

nodes.label: Inert vs active electrodes
nodes.summary: Active electrodes participate in the cell reaction while inert electrodes (e.g. Pt, graphite) only provide a surface for electron transfer.

nodes.label: Ion flow within the cell
nodes.summary: In the electrolyte cations migrate toward the cathode and anions toward the anode to maintain neutrality.

nodes.label: Redox Foundations
nodes.summary: Electrochemical cells are driven by coupled oxidation and reduction half-reactions.

nodes.label: Oxidizing and reducing agents
nodes.summary: The oxidizing agent is reduced and the reducing agent is oxidized in a redox reaction.

nodes.label: Balancing redox reactions
nodes.summary: Redox equations are balanced by half-reactions, equalizing atoms, charge, and electrons transferred.

nodes.label: Disproportionation reactions
nodes.summary: In disproportionation a single species is simultaneously oxidized and reduced.

nodes.label: Half-reactions
nodes.summary: A redox reaction splits into an oxidation half-reaction and a reduction half-reaction.

nodes.label: Oxidation numbers
nodes.summary: Oxidation numbers track electron ownership and identify which species is oxidized or reduced.

nodes.label: Oxidation and reduction
nodes.summary: Oxidation is loss of electrons and reduction is gain of electrons (OIL RIG).

nodes.label: Electrolytic Cells
nodes.summary: Electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous redox reactions.

nodes.label: Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis
nodes.summary: Faraday's laws relate the amount of substance produced to the charge passed.

nodes.label: Electroplating and refining
nodes.summary: Electrolysis is applied in electroplating, metal refining, and electrolytic production of elements.

nodes.label: Faraday's constant
nodes.summary: Faraday's constant F = 96485 C/mol is the charge carried by one mole of electrons.

nodes.label: Faraday's law (mass from charge)
nodes.summary: The moles of substance deposited equal charge passed divided by (n x F), linking current and time to mass.

nodes.label: Electrolysis and Operation
nodes.summary: An external power source forces a nonspontaneous reaction in an electrolytic cell.

nodes.label: Competing electrode reactions in aqueous electrolysis
nodes.summary: In aqueous solution water can be oxidized or reduced in competition with dissolved ions, set by reduction potentials and overpotential.

nodes.label: Electrolysis
nodes.summary: Electrolysis uses current to decompose a compound, such as splitting water or molten salts.

nodes.label: Nonspontaneous operation
nodes.summary: An electrolytic cell drives a nonspontaneous reaction (delta G > 0, E_cell < 0) using external energy.

nodes.label: Electrode polarity in electrolytic cells
nodes.summary: In an electrolytic cell the anode is positive and the cathode is negative, reversed from galvanic cells.

nodes.label: Galvanic (Voltaic) Cells
nodes.summary: Galvanic cells convert spontaneous redox reactions into electrical energy.

nodes.label: Cell Structure and Operation
nodes.summary: A galvanic cell uses two half-cells connected by a salt bridge and an external wire.

nodes.label: Cell (line) notation
nodes.summary: Cell notation writes anode | anode solution || cathode solution | cathode, with || the salt bridge.

nodes.label: Concentration cell
nodes.summary: A concentration cell generates voltage from a concentration difference of the same species at two electrodes.

nodes.label: Daniell cell example
nodes.summary: The Daniell cell uses a Zn anode and Cu cathode, with Zn oxidized and Cu2+ reduced.

nodes.label: Electrode mass changes during operation
nodes.summary: The anode loses mass as it is oxidized while the cathode gains mass as metal ions are reduced onto it.

nodes.label: Electrode polarity in galvanic cells
nodes.summary: In a galvanic cell the anode is negative and the cathode is positive.

nodes.label: Salt bridge
nodes.summary: The salt bridge supplies ions to maintain electrical neutrality and completes the circuit between half-cells.

nodes.label: Spontaneous operation
nodes.summary: A galvanic cell runs a spontaneous redox reaction (delta G < 0) to generate current.

nodes.label: Magnetism and Magnetic Forces
nodes.summary: Moving charges and currents produce and experience magnetic forces and fields.

nodes.label: Magnetic Fields
nodes.summary: Magnetic fields arise from moving charges and have direction and magnitude in teslas.

nodes.label: Magnetic dipoles and field lines
nodes.summary: Magnets are dipoles with north and south poles; field lines run from north to south outside and form closed loops with no magnetic monopoles.

nodes.label: Magnetic materials (dia-, para-, ferromagnetic)
nodes.summary: Materials are diamagnetic, paramagnetic, or ferromagnetic depending on how they respond to applied fields.

nodes.label: Source of magnetic fields
nodes.summary: Magnetic fields are produced by moving electric charges and electric currents.

nodes.label: Magnetic field units (tesla, gauss)
nodes.summary: Magnetic field strength B is measured in teslas (SI) or gauss, with 1 T = 10^4 G.

nodes.label: Force on Moving Charges
nodes.summary: A magnetic field exerts a velocity-dependent force on moving charges.

nodes.label: Circular motion in a magnetic field
nodes.summary: A charge moving perpendicular to a uniform B field undergoes uniform circular motion with r = mv/(qB).

nodes.label: Magnetic force on a charge (F = qvB sin theta)
nodes.summary: A charge moving in a magnetic field feels force F = qvB sin theta, maximal when v is perpendicular to B.

nodes.label: Magnetic force does no work
nodes.summary: Because it is perpendicular to velocity, the magnetic force does no work and changes only direction.

nodes.label: Right-hand rule
nodes.summary: The right-hand rule gives the direction of the magnetic force on a positive charge from v cross B.

nodes.label: Force on Current-Carrying Wires
nodes.summary: A current in a magnetic field experiences a force, the basis of motors.

nodes.label: Magnetic field of a long straight wire
nodes.summary: A long straight current produces a circular magnetic field B = mu0 I/(2 pi r) around it.

nodes.label: Force on a current-carrying wire (F = BIL sin theta)
nodes.summary: A current-carrying wire in a field feels force F = BIL sin theta.

nodes.label: Force between parallel currents
nodes.summary: Parallel currents in the same direction attract and antiparallel currents repel.

nodes.label: Magnetic field of a solenoid
nodes.summary: A solenoid carrying current produces a nearly uniform field inside, the basis of electromagnets, B = mu0 n I.

nodes.label: Resistance and Ohm's Law
nodes.summary: Ohm's law relates voltage, current, and resistance, the property opposing charge flow.

nodes.label: Resistance
nodes.summary: Resistance quantifies how strongly a component opposes current flow.

nodes.label: Conductance and conductivity
nodes.summary: Conductance is the reciprocal of resistance and conductivity is the reciprocal of resistivity.

nodes.label: The ohm
nodes.summary: Resistance is measured in ohms, where one ohm is one volt per ampere.

nodes.label: Ohmic vs non-ohmic materials
nodes.summary: Ohmic materials have constant resistance (linear V-I), while non-ohmic devices like diodes do not.

nodes.label: Ohm's law (V = IR)
nodes.summary: Ohm's law states V = IR, the voltage across an ohmic resistor is proportional to the current.

nodes.label: Resistivity and the geometry formula (R = rho L / A)
nodes.summary: Resistance R = rho L/A increases with length and resistivity and decreases with cross-sectional area.

nodes.label: Resistivity as a material property
nodes.summary: Resistivity rho is an intrinsic material property; conductors have low rho and insulators have high rho.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence of resistance
nodes.summary: Resistivity of metallic conductors rises with temperature while that of semiconductors falls.

nodes.label: Voltage / Potential Difference
nodes.summary: Voltage is the work per unit charge needed to move charge between two points.

nodes.label: Voltage as potential difference
nodes.summary: Voltage V = W/q is the electric potential energy difference per unit charge between two points, in volts.

nodes.label: Electromotive force (emf)
nodes.summary: Emf is the energy per unit charge supplied by a source such as a battery, driving current around a circuit.

nodes.label: Grounding and reference potential
nodes.summary: Ground is an arbitrary reference point assigned zero potential against which voltages are measured.

nodes.label: Terminal voltage and internal resistance
nodes.summary: Terminal voltage V = emf - Ir is less than emf because of voltage dropped across the battery's internal resistance r.

nodes.label: The volt
nodes.summary: One volt is one joule of energy per coulomb of charge.

nodes.label: Electric Power and Energy
nodes.summary: Electric power is the rate at which a circuit element converts electrical energy.

nodes.label: Electrical Energy
nodes.summary: Electrical energy is power integrated over time.

nodes.label: Electrical energy (E = Pt)
nodes.summary: Electrical energy consumed is E = Pt = IVt, with the kilowatt-hour as a common practical unit.

nodes.label: RMS voltage and current
nodes.summary: For AC, the rms value equals peak/sqrt(2) and gives the same average power as an equivalent DC value.

nodes.label: The watt
nodes.summary: One watt equals one joule per second of energy transfer.

nodes.label: Power Equations
nodes.summary: Power dissipated or delivered can be computed from voltage, current, and resistance.

nodes.label: Joule heating (P = I^2 R)
nodes.summary: Power dissipated as heat in a resistor is P = I^2 R, the basis of Joule heating.

nodes.label: Power formula (P = IV)
nodes.summary: Electric power P = IV is the rate of energy transfer by a circuit element, in watts.

nodes.label: Maximum power transfer
nodes.summary: A source delivers maximum power to a load when the load resistance equals the source's internal resistance.

nodes.label: Power form P = V^2 / R
nodes.summary: Power in a resistor can also be written P = V^2/R using Ohm's law substitution.

nodes.label: RC Circuits
nodes.summary: RC circuits exhibit exponential charging and discharging governed by the time constant.

nodes.label: Charging and Discharging
nodes.summary: Charge on a capacitor changes exponentially when connected through a resistor.

nodes.label: Exponential charging
nodes.summary: During charging the capacitor voltage rises as V = V0(1 - e^(-t/RC)).

nodes.label: Exponential discharging
nodes.summary: During discharging the capacitor voltage decays as V = V0 e^(-t/RC).

nodes.label: Capacitor at the instant of switching
nodes.summary: At t = 0 an uncharged capacitor behaves like a wire (max current), so its initial response mimics a short circuit.

nodes.label: Capacitor in steady state
nodes.summary: At steady state a fully charged capacitor carries no current and acts as an open circuit.

nodes.label: Time constant (tau = RC)
nodes.summary: The RC time constant tau = RC sets the timescale over which a capacitor charges or discharges.

nodes.label: Light & electromagnetic radiation
nodes.summary: Light is electromagnetic radiation, a transverse wave of oscillating electric and magnetic fields that also behaves as photons.

nodes.label: Color perception & mixing
nodes.summary: Color arises from wavelength composition; additive light mixing and subtractive pigment mixing follow different rules.

nodes.label: Additive color mixing (RGB)
nodes.summary: Adding red, green, and blue light produces white; combining additive primaries yields the secondary colors of light.

nodes.label: Complementary colors
nodes.summary: An object absorbing one wavelength appears its complementary color (e.g., absorbing red looks green).

nodes.label: Subtractive color mixing (pigments)
nodes.summary: Pigments and filters remove wavelengths; mixing cyan, magenta, and yellow pigments subtracts toward black.

nodes.label: Electromagnetic nature of light
nodes.summary: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave that travels through vacuum at speed c.

nodes.label: Travels through vacuum
nodes.summary: Unlike sound, light requires no medium and propagates through vacuum.

nodes.label: Light slows in a medium
nodes.summary: In a medium light travels at v = c/n; its frequency is unchanged but its wavelength shortens.

nodes.label: Speed of light c = 3×10⁸ m/s
nodes.summary: Light travels at c ≈ 3.00×10⁸ m/s in vacuum and obeys c = fλ.

nodes.label: Transverse EM wave
nodes.summary: Light consists of mutually perpendicular oscillating electric and magnetic fields, both transverse to the propagation direction.

nodes.label: Polarization
nodes.summary: Polarization describes the orientation of the electric-field oscillation of a transverse light wave.

nodes.label: Brewster's angle tanθ_B = n₂/n₁
nodes.summary: At Brewster's angle the reflected ray is fully linearly polarized and is perpendicular to the refracted ray.

nodes.label: Polarization by reflection
nodes.summary: Light reflected at an interface is partially polarized parallel to the surface, fully so at Brewster's angle.

nodes.label: Polarization concept
nodes.summary: Only transverse waves can be polarized; polarization restricts the E-field oscillation to a single plane.

nodes.label: Linear vs circular polarization
nodes.summary: Linearly polarized light oscillates in one plane; circularly polarized light has a rotating E-field of constant magnitude.

nodes.label: Malus's law I = I₀cos²θ
nodes.summary: Light passing a polarizer at angle θ to its axis has transmitted intensity I = I₀cos²θ.

nodes.label: Polarizer halves unpolarized light
nodes.summary: A single polarizer transmits half (I₀/2) the intensity of incident unpolarized light.

nodes.label: Only transverse waves polarize
nodes.summary: Polarizability is direct evidence light is transverse; longitudinal waves like sound cannot be polarized.

nodes.label: Unpolarized light
nodes.summary: Unpolarized light (e.g., from the sun) has E-field oscillations in random orientations.

nodes.label: Electromagnetic spectrum
nodes.summary: The EM spectrum orders radiation by frequency/wavelength from radio waves to gamma rays.

nodes.label: Energy increases with frequency
nodes.summary: Photon energy increases with frequency (and decreasing wavelength) across the spectrum.

nodes.label: Spectrum order (radio→gamma)
nodes.summary: In order of increasing frequency: radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-ray, gamma.

nodes.label: Visible range (~400–700 nm)
nodes.summary: Visible light spans about 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red); violet is highest energy, red lowest.

nodes.label: Geometrical optics
nodes.summary: Geometrical optics treats light as rays to describe reflection, refraction, mirrors, and lenses.

nodes.label: Dispersion
nodes.summary: Dispersion is the wavelength dependence of refractive index that separates white light into colors.

nodes.label: Dispersion concept
nodes.summary: Because n varies with wavelength, shorter wavelengths (violet) bend more than longer ones (red) in a prism.

nodes.label: Prisms & rainbows
nodes.summary: A prism or water droplet disperses white light into a spectrum, producing a rainbow.

nodes.label: Optical instruments & the eye
nodes.summary: The eye and corrective lenses are practical applications of refraction and image formation.

nodes.label: Accommodation & near/far point
nodes.summary: The lens changes shape (accommodation) to focus objects between the near point (~25 cm) and far point (infinity).

nodes.label: Eye forms real inverted image
nodes.summary: The cornea and lens focus light to form a real, inverted, reduced image on the retina.

nodes.label: Myopia & hyperopia correction
nodes.summary: Nearsightedness is corrected with diverging lenses, farsightedness with converging lenses.

nodes.label: Ray model & image fundamentals
nodes.summary: Geometric optics models light as straight rays and classifies images as real or virtual, upright or inverted.

nodes.label: Ray approximation
nodes.summary: When wavelength is small compared to objects, light is modeled as straight-line rays that reflect and refract at surfaces.

nodes.label: Real vs virtual images
nodes.summary: Real images form where rays actually converge and can be projected; virtual images form where rays only appear to diverge from.

nodes.label: Focal point, principal axis & focal length
nodes.summary: The focal point is where parallel rays converge; focal length f is its distance from the optic along the principal axis (the symmetry axis).

nodes.label: Optical instruments
nodes.summary: Lens combinations build magnifiers, microscopes, and telescopes.

nodes.label: Simple magnifier
nodes.summary: A single converging lens with the object inside its focal length produces an enlarged, upright, virtual image.

nodes.label: Microscope & telescope (two-lens)
nodes.summary: Microscopes and telescopes use an objective plus eyepiece; total magnification is the product of the two lenses' magnifications.

nodes.label: Lenses
nodes.summary: Converging and diverging lenses refract light to form real or virtual images.

nodes.label: Spherical & chromatic aberration
nodes.summary: Spherical aberration blurs from edge focusing; chromatic aberration is color fringing from dispersion.

nodes.label: Converging vs diverging lenses
nodes.summary: Convex (converging) lenses have positive f; concave (diverging) lenses have negative f.

nodes.label: Lens image behavior vs object distance
nodes.summary: A converging lens gives real/inverted images beyond f and a virtual/upright magnified image inside f; diverging lenses always give virtual, upright, reduced images.

nodes.label: Lensmaker's equation
nodes.summary: The lensmaker's equation relates focal length to surface radii and refractive index: 1/f = (n−1)(1/R₁ − 1/R₂).

nodes.label: Multi-lens / combination systems
nodes.summary: In a lens train the image of one lens becomes the object of the next; total magnification multiplies.

nodes.label: Lens power P = 1/f (diopters)
nodes.summary: Lens power in diopters equals 1/f (in meters); powers of stacked thin lenses add.

nodes.label: Sign conventions (lenses)
nodes.summary: For lenses, positive d_i means a real image on the opposite side; negative d_i a virtual image on the object side.

nodes.label: Thin-lens equation
nodes.summary: Thin lenses obey 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i with the same form as the mirror equation.

nodes.label: Mirrors
nodes.summary: Plane and spherical mirrors form images governed by the mirror equation and ray tracing.

nodes.label: Concave vs convex mirrors
nodes.summary: Concave (converging) mirrors have positive focal length; convex (diverging) mirrors have negative focal length.

nodes.label: f = R/2 for spherical mirrors
nodes.summary: A spherical mirror's focal length equals half its radius of curvature, f = R/2.

nodes.label: Image behavior vs object distance
nodes.summary: A concave mirror gives real/inverted images beyond f and a virtual/upright magnified image inside f; convex mirrors always give virtual, upright, reduced images.

nodes.label: Magnification m = −i/o
nodes.summary: Magnification m = −d_i/d_o = h_i/h_o; |m|>1 enlarges and negative m means inverted.

nodes.label: Mirror/lens equation 1/f = 1/o + 1/i
nodes.summary: The thin mirror/lens equation 1/f = 1/d_o + 1/d_i relates focal length to object and image distances.

nodes.label: Plane mirror images
nodes.summary: A plane mirror forms an upright, virtual, same-size image as far behind the mirror as the object is in front.

nodes.label: Ray tracing for mirrors
nodes.summary: Principal rays (parallel→focus, focus→parallel, center) locate the image graphically.

nodes.label: Sign conventions (mirrors)
nodes.summary: Real images (in front of mirror) have positive d_i; virtual images (behind) have negative d_i.

nodes.label: Reflection
nodes.summary: Reflection is the bouncing of light off a surface obeying the law of reflection.

nodes.label: Law of reflection θᵢ = θᵣ
nodes.summary: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal.

nodes.label: 180° phase shift on reflection
nodes.summary: Light reflecting off a higher-index medium undergoes a 180° (½λ) phase shift; reflecting off lower index does not.

nodes.label: Specular vs diffuse reflection
nodes.summary: Smooth surfaces produce specular (mirror) reflection; rough surfaces scatter light diffusely.

nodes.label: Refraction & Snell's law
nodes.summary: Refraction is the bending of light as it changes speed crossing between media of different index.

nodes.label: Apparent depth
nodes.summary: Refraction makes submerged objects appear shallower than their true depth.

nodes.label: Bending direction rule
nodes.summary: Going to higher n light bends toward the normal; going to lower n it bends away from the normal.

nodes.label: Critical angle sinθ_c = n₂/n₁
nodes.summary: The critical angle satisfies sinθ_c = n₂/n₁ (n₁ > n₂) and is the onset of total internal reflection.

nodes.label: Fiber optics & TIR application
nodes.summary: Optical fibers guide light by repeated total internal reflection along the core.

nodes.label: Index of refraction n = c/v
nodes.summary: The refractive index n = c/v ≥ 1 quantifies how much a medium slows light.

nodes.label: Snell's law n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂
nodes.summary: At an interface n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂; light bends toward the normal entering a denser medium.

nodes.label: Total internal reflection
nodes.summary: Beyond the critical angle, light going from high to low index is wholly reflected back into the dense medium.

nodes.label: Quantum & molecular light interactions
nodes.summary: At the quantum scale light is absorbed and emitted as photons via electronic transitions.

nodes.label: Beer–Lambert law A = εbc
nodes.summary: Absorbance A = εbc is proportional to molar absorptivity, path length, and concentration (spectrophotometry).

nodes.label: Electronic transitions & quantized levels
nodes.summary: Atoms/molecules absorb a photon only when its energy matches the gap between quantized energy levels.

nodes.label: Fluorescence & phosphorescence
nodes.summary: Absorbed light can be re-emitted at longer wavelength (lower energy) as fluorescence or, delayed, phosphorescence.

nodes.label: Photoelectric effect
nodes.summary: Photons above the work-function threshold frequency eject electrons; KE_max = hf − Φ.

nodes.label: Photon energy E = hf = hc/λ
nodes.summary: A photon carries quantized energy E = hf = hc/λ, with h Planck's constant.

nodes.label: Photon momentum p = E/c = h/λ
nodes.summary: Although massless, a photon carries momentum p = E/c = h/λ, the basis of radiation pressure.

nodes.label: Wave–particle duality
nodes.summary: Light exhibits both wave (interference/diffraction) and particle (photon) behavior.

nodes.label: Sound
nodes.summary: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical pressure wave that propagates through a material medium.

nodes.label: Attenuation & damping
nodes.summary: Sound loses energy to the medium through absorption and scattering as it propagates.

nodes.label: Absorption / attenuation
nodes.summary: Sound intensity decreases beyond the inverse-square effect as the medium absorbs and converts acoustic energy to heat.

nodes.label: Damped oscillation of source
nodes.summary: A real vibrating source loses amplitude over time as energy dissipates, damping the emitted sound.

nodes.label: Beats & interference of sound
nodes.summary: Superposition of sound waves produces beats and constructive/destructive interference patterns.

nodes.label: Beat frequency f_beat = |f₁ − f₂|
nodes.summary: Two slightly different frequencies superpose to produce loudness throbs at the beat frequency |f₁ − f₂|.

nodes.label: Superposition of sound
nodes.summary: Overlapping sound waves add by linear superposition, producing constructive or destructive interference.

nodes.label: Hearing & ultrasound applications
nodes.summary: The ear transduces sound and ultrasound is exploited for imaging and therapy.

nodes.label: Ear as sound transducer
nodes.summary: The ear converts pressure waves into neural signals via the eardrum, ossicles, and cochlear hair cells.

nodes.label: Ultrasound echolocation/imaging
nodes.summary: Ultrasound imaging times reflected echoes from tissue interfaces to map internal structures.

nodes.label: Sound at boundaries: reflection, echo & impedance
nodes.summary: Sound reflects and transmits at interfaces according to the acoustic impedance mismatch between media.

nodes.label: Acoustic impedance & mismatch
nodes.summary: Acoustic impedance Z = ρv; the larger the impedance mismatch at an interface, the more sound is reflected (basis of ultrasound contrast and matching gel).

nodes.label: Reflection of sound & echoes
nodes.summary: Sound reflects off boundaries to produce echoes and reverberation; echolocation times the round trip to estimate distance.

nodes.label: Doppler effect (sound)
nodes.summary: Relative motion between a sound source and observer shifts the perceived frequency.

nodes.label: Doppler effect concept
nodes.summary: Approaching source/observer raises perceived frequency (blueshift); receding lowers it (redshift).

nodes.label: Doppler formula f' = f(v±v_o)/(v∓v_s)
nodes.summary: f' = f(v ± v_o)/(v ∓ v_s); signs chosen so motion toward the other party raises f'.

nodes.label: Moving source vs moving observer
nodes.summary: A moving source changes the emitted wavelength while a moving observer changes the encounter rate; the two cases use different terms of the Doppler formula.

nodes.label: Shock waves & sonic boom
nodes.summary: When a source moves at or above the speed of sound it outruns its wavefronts, creating a shock wave and sonic boom.

nodes.label: Doppler sign convention
nodes.summary: Choose top sign for motion that decreases separation (raises pitch) and bottom sign for motion that increases it.

nodes.label: Doppler ultrasound (medical)
nodes.summary: Doppler shifts of reflected ultrasound measure blood flow velocity in clinical imaging.

nodes.label: Intensity & loudness
nodes.summary: Sound intensity is power per unit area and is perceived logarithmically as loudness.

nodes.label: I ∝ amplitude²
nodes.summary: Sound intensity is proportional to the square of the pressure (and displacement) amplitude.

nodes.label: Decibel scale β = 10 log(I/I₀)
nodes.summary: Sound level in decibels β = 10 log(I/I₀) with reference I₀ = 1×10⁻¹² W/m² (threshold of hearing).

nodes.label: Decibel arithmetic (×10 = +10 dB)
nodes.summary: Each factor-of-10 increase in intensity adds 10 dB; doubling intensity adds about 3 dB.

nodes.label: Intensity I = P/A
nodes.summary: Intensity is power transmitted per unit area (W/m²) perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

nodes.label: Inverse-square law
nodes.summary: For a point source radiating uniformly, intensity falls off as 1/r² (I = P/4πr²).

nodes.label: Loudness vs intensity (perception)
nodes.summary: Loudness is the subjective perception of intensity and depends on both intensity and frequency, scaling logarithmically.

nodes.label: Threshold of hearing & pain
nodes.summary: Human hearing spans roughly 0 dB (threshold ~10⁻¹² W/m²) to ~120 dB (threshold of pain).

nodes.label: Nature & production of sound
nodes.summary: Sound is produced by vibrating sources that create alternating compressions and rarefactions in a medium.

nodes.label: Compressions and rarefactions
nodes.summary: A compression is a region of high pressure/density and a rarefaction a region of low pressure/density in a sound wave.

nodes.label: Sound as a longitudinal wave
nodes.summary: In sound, particle displacement is parallel to the direction of energy propagation, forming compressions and rarefactions.

nodes.label: Pressure wave vs displacement wave
nodes.summary: The pressure variation in a sound wave is 90° out of phase with the particle displacement.

nodes.label: Requires a material medium
nodes.summary: Sound cannot travel through a vacuum because it needs particles of a medium to transmit the pressure disturbance.

nodes.label: Pitch, frequency & ranges
nodes.summary: Pitch is the perceptual correlate of frequency across infrasonic, audible, and ultrasonic ranges.

nodes.label: Audible range 20 Hz–20 kHz
nodes.summary: The normal human audible range spans about 20 Hz to 20,000 Hz.

nodes.label: Infrasound and ultrasound
nodes.summary: Infrasound is below 20 Hz and ultrasound above 20 kHz, the latter used in medical imaging and sonar.

nodes.label: Pitch ↔ frequency
nodes.summary: Higher frequency sound is perceived as higher pitch; pitch is the subjective experience of frequency.

nodes.label: Standing waves & resonance in sound
nodes.summary: Resonant cavities (pipes, strings) sustain standing waves at discrete harmonic frequencies.

nodes.label: Closed (open-closed) pipe
nodes.summary: A pipe closed at one end has a node at the closed end and antinode at the open end, giving f_n = nv/4L for odd n only.

nodes.label: Fundamental & overtones
nodes.summary: The fundamental (n=1) is the lowest resonant frequency; higher harmonics are overtones.

nodes.label: Nodes & antinodes
nodes.summary: Nodes are points of zero displacement and antinodes points of maximum displacement; their fixed positions define each harmonic.

nodes.label: Open-open pipe
nodes.summary: A pipe open at both ends has antinodes at each end and f_n = nv/2L for all integers n.

nodes.label: Resonance & forced oscillation
nodes.summary: A system driven at its natural frequency absorbs maximum energy and oscillates with large amplitude (resonance).

nodes.label: Standing waves & harmonics
nodes.summary: Reflection in a bounded medium produces standing waves with fixed nodes and antinodes at resonant frequencies.

nodes.label: String fixed at both ends
nodes.summary: A string fixed at both ends supports f_n = nv/2L (n = 1,2,3,…) with nodes at both ends.

nodes.label: Timbre / quality
nodes.summary: Timbre is the perceived sound quality determined by the relative amplitudes of the harmonics present.

nodes.label: Speed of sound
nodes.summary: The speed of sound depends on the elastic and inertial properties of the medium, not on the source.

nodes.label: Speed of sound v = √(B/ρ)
nodes.summary: In a fluid, sound speed v = √(B/ρ), where B is the bulk modulus and ρ the density.

nodes.label: v_solid > v_liquid > v_gas
nodes.summary: Sound travels fastest in solids and slowest in gases because stiffness (rigidity) dominates over the density effect.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence in gas
nodes.summary: In an ideal gas the speed of sound increases with temperature (v ∝ √T), about 343 m/s in air at room temperature.

nodes.label: v = fλ for sound
nodes.summary: Sound obeys v = fλ; when sound crosses media its speed and wavelength change but its frequency stays constant.

nodes.label: Wave optics & light–matter interactions
nodes.summary: Wave optics covers interference and diffraction; light–matter interaction covers absorption, emission, and scattering at the molecular and quantum level.

nodes.label: Absorption, transmission & color
nodes.summary: Matter selectively absorbs, transmits, and reflects wavelengths, which determines observed color.

nodes.label: Absorption & emission spectra
nodes.summary: Atoms absorb and emit light only at characteristic wavelengths matching their energy-level gaps.

nodes.label: Selective absorption & color
