nodes.summary: An object's color is the wavelengths it reflects/transmits, not those it absorbs.

nodes.label: Transparency, translucency, opacity
nodes.summary: Transparent media transmit light, translucent scatter it, and opaque absorb or reflect it fully.

nodes.label: Diffraction
nodes.summary: Diffraction is the bending and spreading of light around obstacles and through apertures.

nodes.label: Diffraction concept
nodes.summary: Light spreads when passing an aperture or edge comparable in size to its wavelength.

nodes.label: Diffraction grating
nodes.summary: A grating with many slits gives sharp maxima at d sinθ = mλ, useful for spectroscopy.

nodes.label: Resolution & Rayleigh criterion
nodes.summary: Diffraction limits resolving power; two sources are just resolvable when the central max of one falls on the first min of the other (θ ≈ 1.22λ/D).

nodes.label: Single-slit minima a sinθ = mλ
nodes.summary: Single-slit dark fringes occur at a sinθ = mλ (m = 1,2,…), giving a wide central maximum.

nodes.label: X-ray diffraction (Bragg)
nodes.summary: X-rays diffract off crystal planes per Bragg's law nλ = 2d sinθ, revealing atomic structure.

nodes.label: Interference
nodes.summary: Coherent light waves superpose to give constructive and destructive interference fringes.

nodes.label: Path-difference conditions
nodes.summary: Constructive interference occurs at path difference = mλ; destructive at (m + ½)λ.

nodes.label: Young's double slit d sinθ = mλ
nodes.summary: Two-slit bright fringes satisfy d sinθ = mλ, demonstrating light's wave nature.

nodes.label: Superposition & coherence
nodes.summary: Sustained interference requires coherent (fixed phase relationship) waves that superpose linearly.

nodes.label: Thin-film interference
nodes.summary: Reflections off the two surfaces of a thin film interfere, with conditions set by film thickness, index, and phase shifts.

nodes.label: Antireflective & anti-glare coatings
nodes.summary: Thin films are engineered (e.g., quarter-wavelength coatings) to cancel reflection or produce iridescent colors in soap films and oil slicks.

nodes.label: Scattering
nodes.summary: Light is redirected by particles; Rayleigh scattering's wavelength dependence explains the sky's color.

nodes.label: Rayleigh scattering (1/λ⁴)
nodes.summary: Scattering by small particles scales as 1/λ⁴, so blue scatters far more than red.

nodes.label: Blue sky & red sunset
nodes.summary: Preferential blue scattering makes the sky blue and, by removing blue, the setting sun red.

nodes.label: Atomic Structure & Composition
nodes.summary: Atoms consist of a dense positive nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons.

nodes.label: Ions
nodes.summary: Ions are atoms with unequal numbers of protons and electrons, carrying net charge.

nodes.label: Anion
nodes.summary: An anion is a negatively charged ion formed by gaining one or more electrons.

nodes.label: Cation
nodes.summary: A cation is a positively charged ion formed by losing one or more electrons.

nodes.label: Determining Net Charge
nodes.summary: Net charge equals the number of protons minus the number of electrons.

nodes.label: Atomic Notation & Definitions
nodes.summary: Atomic number, mass number, and isotopic notation define an atom's composition.

nodes.label: Atomic Number (Z)
nodes.summary: The atomic number Z equals the number of protons and uniquely defines the element.

nodes.label: Element Symbol
nodes.summary: Each element is denoted by a one- or two-letter symbol corresponding to a fixed atomic number.

nodes.label: Isotopic Notation
nodes.summary: An isotope is written as A superscript-Z subscript X, encoding mass number, atomic number, and element symbol.

nodes.label: Mass Number (A)
nodes.summary: The mass number A equals the total number of protons plus neutrons (A = Z + N).

nodes.label: Neutron Number (N = A − Z)
nodes.summary: The number of neutrons equals the mass number minus the atomic number.

nodes.label: The Nucleus
nodes.summary: The nucleus is the dense, positively charged core containing protons and neutrons (nucleons).

nodes.label: Nuclear Density & Size
nodes.summary: The nucleus is extremely small and dense relative to the atom, holding nearly all the mass in a tiny volume.

nodes.label: Nucleons
nodes.summary: Nucleons are the protons and neutrons bound together in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Rutherford Gold-Foil Model
nodes.summary: Rutherford's gold-foil experiment showed atoms have a small, dense, positively charged nucleus with mostly empty space.

nodes.label: Strong Nuclear Force
nodes.summary: The strong nuclear force binds nucleons over very short ranges, overcoming proton-proton electrostatic repulsion.

nodes.label: Subatomic Particles
nodes.summary: Protons, neutrons, and electrons are the three fundamental constituents of the atom.

nodes.label: Elementary Charge
nodes.summary: The elementary charge e equals 1.6 x 10^-19 C, the magnitude of charge on a proton or electron.

nodes.label: Electron
nodes.summary: The electron carries a -1 elementary charge and has a mass ~1/1836 that of a proton.

nodes.label: Neutral Atom (Z protons = Z electrons)
nodes.summary: A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, giving zero net charge.

nodes.label: Neutron
nodes.summary: The neutron is electrically neutral, has a mass of ~1 amu, and resides in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Proton
nodes.summary: The proton carries a +1 elementary charge, has a mass of ~1 amu, and resides in the nucleus.

nodes.label: Relative Particle Masses
nodes.summary: Nearly all atomic mass is in the nucleus because protons and neutrons are ~1836x heavier than electrons.

nodes.label: Bohr Model & Atomic Spectra
nodes.summary: The Bohr model quantizes electron orbits and explains discrete atomic emission and absorption spectra.

nodes.label: Photon-Matter Interactions
nodes.summary: The photoelectric effect and related phenomena reveal light's quantized, particle-like behavior.

nodes.label: Fluorescence & Phosphorescence
nodes.summary: Absorbed light can be re-emitted at longer wavelength as fluorescence (fast) or phosphorescence (delayed).

nodes.label: Photoelectric Effect
nodes.summary: Light above a threshold frequency ejects electrons from a metal, demonstrating the particle nature of light.

nodes.label: Work Function & Threshold Frequency
nodes.summary: The work function is the minimum photon energy needed to eject an electron; excess energy becomes kinetic energy.

nodes.label: Quantized Energy Levels
nodes.summary: Electrons occupy discrete, quantized energy levels around the nucleus.

nodes.label: Quantized Angular Momentum
nodes.summary: Bohr required orbital angular momentum to be an integer multiple of h/2pi, fixing the allowed orbits.

nodes.label: Bohr's Postulate
nodes.summary: Bohr proposed electrons travel in fixed circular orbits of quantized angular momentum and energy.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Energy Levels
nodes.summary: Hydrogen energy levels follow E = -13.6 eV / n^2, becoming less negative at higher n.

nodes.label: Ground vs. Excited States
nodes.summary: The ground state is the lowest-energy configuration; excited states have electrons in higher levels.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy of Hydrogen
nodes.summary: Removing hydrogen's electron from the ground state (n=1 to infinity) requires 13.6 eV.

nodes.label: Atomic Spectra
nodes.summary: Discrete spectral lines arise from quantized electron transitions, fingerprinting each element.

nodes.label: Absorption Spectrum
nodes.summary: An absorption spectrum shows dark lines where electrons absorb specific photon energies.

nodes.label: Spectra as Element Fingerprints
nodes.summary: Each element emits a unique set of spectral lines, allowing spectroscopic identification.

nodes.label: Emission Spectrum
nodes.summary: An emission spectrum shows bright lines from photons released as electrons drop to lower levels.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Spectral Series
nodes.summary: Lyman (UV), Balmer (visible), and Paschen (IR) series correspond to transitions ending at n=1, 2, and 3.

nodes.label: Discrete Line Spectra
nodes.summary: Atomic spectra are discrete lines, not continuous, because energy levels are quantized.

nodes.label: Rydberg Formula
nodes.summary: The Rydberg formula gives hydrogen spectral-line wavelengths from the two principal quantum numbers.

nodes.label: Electronic Transitions
nodes.summary: Electrons absorb or emit photons whose energy equals the gap between two levels.

nodes.label: Photon Absorption
nodes.summary: An electron jumps to a higher level by absorbing a photon of exactly the energy-gap value.

nodes.label: Transition Energy (ΔE = hf)
nodes.summary: The photon energy of a transition equals the difference between the two level energies, E = hf.

nodes.label: Photon Emission
nodes.summary: An electron falling to a lower level emits a photon equal to the energy difference.

nodes.label: The Photon (Quantized Light)
nodes.summary: Light is quantized into photons, discrete packets of energy that carry electromagnetic radiation.

nodes.label: Planck's Relation (E = hf)
nodes.summary: Photon energy is proportional to frequency through Planck's constant, E = hf = hc/lambda.

nodes.label: Electron Configuration
nodes.summary: Electron configuration assigns electrons to orbitals by energy following established filling rules.

nodes.label: Configuration Anomalies
nodes.summary: Chromium and copper deviate from Aufbau order for added stability.

nodes.label: Chromium & Copper Exceptions
nodes.summary: Cr and Cu promote an s electron to d for the stability of half-filled and fully filled d subshells.

nodes.label: Half-Filled & Full-Subshell Stability
nodes.summary: Half-filled and completely filled subshells have extra stability driving configuration anomalies.

nodes.label: Filling Rules
nodes.summary: The Aufbau principle, Pauli exclusion, and Hund's rule govern orbital filling order.

nodes.label: Aufbau Principle
nodes.summary: Electrons fill the lowest-energy orbitals first before occupying higher ones.

nodes.label: Diagonal (Madelung) Filling Order
nodes.summary: The diagonal rule predicts filling order 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 4s 3d 4p and so on.

nodes.label: Hund's Rule
nodes.summary: Electrons singly occupy degenerate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing.

nodes.label: (n + l) Energy Ordering
nodes.summary: Orbital energy increases with n + l, and ties are broken by lower n filling first.

nodes.label: Pauli Exclusion Principle
nodes.summary: No two electrons in an atom can share all four quantum numbers, so an orbital holds at most two opposite-spin electrons.

nodes.label: Configurations of Ions
nodes.summary: Ions form by removing or adding electrons, with cations losing highest-n electrons first.

nodes.label: Anion Configurations
nodes.summary: Anions gain electrons into the next available orbital, often reaching a noble-gas configuration.

nodes.label: Cation Configurations
nodes.summary: Cations lose electrons from the highest principal level first, so transition metals lose s before d.

nodes.label: Isoelectronic Species
nodes.summary: Isoelectronic species have identical electron configurations despite differing nuclear charges.

nodes.label: Paramagnetism & Diamagnetism
nodes.summary: Unpaired electrons make a species paramagnetic; all-paired electrons make it diamagnetic.

nodes.label: Diamagnetism
nodes.summary: A diamagnetic species has all paired electrons and is weakly repelled by a magnetic field.

nodes.label: Paramagnetism
nodes.summary: A paramagnetic species has unpaired electrons and is attracted to a magnetic field.

nodes.label: Configuration Notation
nodes.summary: Configurations are written in spdf or noble-gas-core shorthand, with orbital diagrams showing spins.

nodes.label: Noble-Gas Shorthand
nodes.summary: Configurations abbreviate inner electrons with the preceding noble gas in brackets.

nodes.label: Orbital (Box) Diagrams
nodes.summary: Orbital diagrams use boxes and arrows to depict electron occupancy and spin.

nodes.label: spdf Notation
nodes.summary: Configurations are written as subshell symbols with superscripts for electron count, e.g. 1s2 2s2 2p6.

nodes.label: Valence vs. Core Electrons
nodes.summary: Valence electrons in the outermost shell govern chemistry; core electrons are inner and inert.

nodes.label: Predicting Common Ion Charges
nodes.summary: Main-group ion charges follow from reaching the nearest noble-gas configuration (e.g. Na+, Mg2+, O2-).

nodes.label: Core Electrons
nodes.summary: Core electrons are inner-shell electrons that do not participate in bonding.

nodes.label: Group Number Predicts Valence
nodes.summary: For main-group elements, the group number equals the number of valence electrons.

nodes.label: Octet Rule & Stable Configurations
nodes.summary: Atoms gain, lose, or share electrons to reach a stable full-octet (noble-gas) valence configuration.

nodes.label: Valence Electrons
nodes.summary: Valence electrons occupy the highest principal energy level and determine bonding behavior.

nodes.label: Isotopes & Atomic Mass
nodes.summary: Isotopes are atoms of an element with differing neutron counts; atomic weight is their weighted average.

nodes.label: Atomic Weight
nodes.summary: Atomic weight is the abundance-weighted average mass of an element's naturally occurring isotopes.

nodes.label: Natural Abundance
nodes.summary: Natural abundance is the relative fraction in which each isotope occurs in nature.

nodes.label: Back-Calculating Abundance
nodes.summary: Given two isotope masses and the average atomic weight, the fractional abundances can be solved algebraically.

nodes.label: Mass Spectrometry of Isotopes
nodes.summary: A mass spectrum resolves isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio, with peak heights giving relative abundances.

nodes.label: Non-Integer Atomic Masses
nodes.summary: Periodic-table masses are non-integers because they average multiple isotopes by abundance.

nodes.label: Weighted-Average Calculation
nodes.summary: Atomic weight is computed as the sum of each isotope's mass times its fractional natural abundance.

nodes.label: Definition of Isotopes
nodes.summary: Isotopes share atomic number but differ in mass number due to differing neutron numbers.

nodes.label: Identical Chemical Behavior
nodes.summary: Isotopes of an element have nearly identical chemical behavior because they have the same electron configuration.

nodes.label: Hydrogen Isotopes
nodes.summary: Hydrogen's isotopes are protium (no neutrons), deuterium (one neutron), and tritium (two neutrons).

nodes.label: Isotopes Concept
nodes.summary: Isotopes of an element have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.

nodes.label: Atomic Mass Units
nodes.summary: Atomic mass is measured in unified atomic mass units defined relative to carbon-12.

nodes.label: Atomic Mass Unit (amu)
nodes.summary: One amu is defined as exactly 1/12 the mass of a carbon-12 atom.

nodes.label: Isotopic Mass
nodes.summary: The isotopic mass is the actual mass of a specific isotope, close to but not exactly equal to its mass number.

nodes.label: Mole & Avogadro's Number
nodes.summary: The mole links atomic-scale mass to macroscopic gram quantities via Avogadro's number.

nodes.label: Avogadro's Number
nodes.summary: One mole contains 6.022 x 10^23 particles, Avogadro's number.

nodes.label: Molar Mass
nodes.summary: The molar mass in grams per mole is numerically equal to the atomic or molecular weight in amu.

nodes.label: Nuclear Decay & Reactions
nodes.summary: Unstable nuclei undergo radioactive decay or fission/fusion, transforming into other nuclides.

nodes.label: Balancing Nuclear Equations
nodes.summary: Nuclear equations balance conserved mass number and atomic number on both sides.

nodes.label: Conservation of A and Z
nodes.summary: In a nuclear reaction the total mass number and total charge (atomic number) are conserved.

nodes.label: Identifying Decay Products
nodes.summary: The daughter nuclide is found by conserving A and Z across the decay equation.

nodes.label: Nuclear Transmutation
nodes.summary: Transmutation changes one element into another, either by natural decay or by particle bombardment.

nodes.label: Radioactive Decay Modes
nodes.summary: Unstable nuclei emit alpha, beta, gamma, or positron radiation to gain stability.

nodes.label: Alpha Decay
nodes.summary: Alpha decay emits a helium-4 nucleus, decreasing the mass number by 4 and atomic number by 2.

nodes.label: Alpha Particle Identity
nodes.summary: An alpha particle is a helium-4 nucleus, two protons and two neutrons with a +2 charge.

nodes.label: Beta-Minus Decay
nodes.summary: Beta-minus decay converts a neutron to a proton, emitting an electron and increasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: Beta Particle Identity
nodes.summary: A beta-minus particle is a high-speed electron emitted from the nucleus, with mass number 0 and charge -1.

nodes.label: Beta-Plus (Positron) Decay
nodes.summary: Positron emission converts a proton to a neutron, emitting a positron and decreasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: Decay Series & Daughter Chains
nodes.summary: An unstable nuclide may decay through a sequence of steps until reaching a stable daughter isotope.

nodes.label: Electron Capture
nodes.summary: Electron capture absorbs an inner electron, converting a proton to a neutron and decreasing Z by 1.

nodes.label: Gamma Decay
nodes.summary: Gamma decay releases a high-energy photon from an excited nucleus without changing A or Z.

nodes.label: Ionizing Radiation & Biological Effect
nodes.summary: Ionizing radiation ejects electrons from atoms; alpha is most ionizing per distance, gamma least but most penetrating.

nodes.label: Penetrating Power of Radiation
nodes.summary: Alpha is least penetrating, beta intermediate, and gamma most penetrating.

nodes.label: The Positron
nodes.summary: A positron is the antimatter counterpart of an electron with +1 charge and equal mass.

nodes.label: Fission & Fusion
nodes.summary: Fission splits heavy nuclei and fusion combines light nuclei, both releasing nuclear energy.

nodes.label: Energy from Mass Loss
nodes.summary: Both fission and fusion release energy because the products have a higher binding energy per nucleon.

nodes.label: Nuclear Fission
nodes.summary: Fission splits a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei, releasing energy and neutrons.

nodes.label: Nuclear Fusion
nodes.summary: Fusion combines light nuclei into a heavier nucleus, releasing large amounts of energy.

nodes.label: Decay Kinetics & Half-Life
nodes.summary: Radioactive decay is a first-order process characterized by a constant half-life.

nodes.label: Activity & Decay Rate
nodes.summary: Activity is the number of decays per unit time, equal to lambda times the number of nuclei present.

nodes.label: Decay Constant
nodes.summary: The decay constant relates to half-life by t-half = ln(2)/lambda.

nodes.label: First-Order Decay
nodes.summary: Radioactive decay follows first-order kinetics, with rate proportional to the number of nuclei present.

nodes.label: Half-Life
nodes.summary: The half-life is the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay.

nodes.label: Exponential Decay Equation
nodes.summary: Remaining amount equals the initial amount times (1/2) raised to the number of elapsed half-lives.

nodes.label: Radiometric Dating
nodes.summary: Comparing remaining isotope fraction to half-life dates samples, as in carbon-14 dating.

nodes.label: Nuclear Stability
nodes.summary: Nuclear stability depends on the neutron-to-proton ratio and total nucleon count.

nodes.label: Band of Stability
nodes.summary: Nuclei above the band undergo beta-minus decay and those below undergo positron emission or electron capture.

nodes.label: Nuclear Binding Energy
nodes.summary: Binding energy is the energy needed to disassemble a nucleus into its separate nucleons.

nodes.label: Binding Energy per Nucleon
nodes.summary: Binding energy per nucleon peaks near iron-56, the most stable nuclide.

nodes.label: Mass-Energy Equivalence (E = mc^2)
nodes.summary: The mass defect is converted to binding energy via Einstein's relation E = mc^2.

nodes.label: Heavy-Nucleus Instability
nodes.summary: Nuclei with Z greater than 83 are inherently unstable and tend to decay, often by alpha emission.

nodes.label: Mass Defect
nodes.summary: The mass defect is the difference between a nucleus's mass and the summed masses of its nucleons.

nodes.label: Neutron-to-Proton Ratio
nodes.summary: Stable nuclei lie within a band of stability defined by an appropriate neutron-to-proton ratio.

nodes.label: Periodic Table & Periodic Trends
nodes.summary: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number, and periodic trends follow electron structure.

nodes.label: Atomic & Ionic Radius
nodes.summary: Atomic size decreases across a period and increases down a group; ions differ predictably from atoms.

nodes.label: Anion Radius
nodes.summary: Anions are larger than their parent atoms due to added electron-electron repulsion.

nodes.label: Atomic Radius Trend
nodes.summary: Atomic radius decreases left-to-right across a period and increases top-to-bottom down a group.

nodes.label: Cation Radius
nodes.summary: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms due to fewer electrons and stronger per-electron pull.

nodes.label: Isoelectronic Radius Trend
nodes.summary: Among isoelectronic species, greater nuclear charge gives a smaller radius.

nodes.label: Element Classifications
nodes.summary: Elements are grouped as metals, nonmetals, metalloids, and named families.

nodes.label: Alkali Metals
nodes.summary: Group 1 alkali metals have one valence electron and are highly reactive, forming +1 cations.

nodes.label: Alkaline Earth Metals
nodes.summary: Group 2 alkaline earth metals have two valence electrons and form +2 cations.

nodes.label: Chalcogens & Pnictogens
nodes.summary: Group 16 chalcogens (6 valence e-) and group 15 pnictogens (5 valence e-) are reactive nonmetal families.

nodes.label: Halogens
nodes.summary: Group 17 halogens have seven valence electrons and are highly reactive, forming -1 anions.

nodes.label: Lanthanides & Actinides
nodes.summary: The inner-transition lanthanides and actinides fill f subshells and sit below the main table.

nodes.label: Metalloids
nodes.summary: Metalloids border the metal/nonmetal staircase and have intermediate properties (semiconductors).

nodes.label: Metals
nodes.summary: Metals are typically lustrous, conductive, malleable, and tend to lose electrons forming cations.

nodes.label: Noble Gases
nodes.summary: Group 18 noble gases have full valence shells and are largely chemically inert.

nodes.label: Nonmetals
nodes.summary: Nonmetals are poor conductors that tend to gain electrons forming anions.

nodes.label: Transition Metals
nodes.summary: Transition metals fill d subshells and commonly show variable oxidation states and colored complexes.

nodes.label: Effective Nuclear Charge & Shielding
nodes.summary: Effective nuclear charge is the net positive pull on valence electrons after core shielding.

nodes.label: Electron Shielding
nodes.summary: Inner electrons shield outer electrons, reducing the effective nuclear charge they experience.

nodes.label: Effective Nuclear Charge (Z_eff)
nodes.summary: Z-eff is the net nuclear charge felt by a valence electron, approximately Z minus core electrons.

nodes.label: Z_eff Periodic Trend
nodes.summary: Z-eff increases across a period and changes little down a group, driving most periodic trends.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity
nodes.summary: Electron affinity is the energy change when an atom gains an electron, generally exothermic.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity Definition
nodes.summary: Electron affinity is the energy released when a gaseous atom gains an electron.

nodes.label: Electron Affinity Trend
nodes.summary: Electron affinity generally becomes more exothermic across a period (excluding noble gases).

nodes.label: Electronegativity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity measures an atom's pull on shared bonding electrons, peaking at fluorine.

nodes.label: Electronegativity Definition
nodes.summary: Electronegativity is the tendency of a bonded atom to attract shared electrons.

nodes.label: Pauling Scale
nodes.summary: The Pauling scale quantifies electronegativity, assigning fluorine the highest value (~4.0).

nodes.label: Electronegativity Trend
nodes.summary: Electronegativity increases across a period and decreases down a group, highest for fluorine.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy
nodes.summary: Ionization energy is the energy to remove an electron and rises with effective nuclear charge.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy Definition
nodes.summary: Ionization energy is the energy required to remove the most loosely held electron from a gaseous atom.

nodes.label: IE Trend Irregularities
nodes.summary: Small dips occur after group 2 (s-to-p) and group 15 (pairing), exceptions to the smooth trend.

nodes.label: Successive Ionization Energies
nodes.summary: Each successive ionization energy is larger, with a large jump after removing all valence electrons.

nodes.label: Ionization Energy Trend
nodes.summary: Ionization energy increases across a period and decreases down a group.

nodes.label: Metallic Character & Reactivity
nodes.summary: Metallic character and reactivity trends follow ionization energy and electronegativity patterns.

nodes.label: Acidic vs. Basic Oxides
nodes.summary: Metal oxides tend to be basic and nonmetal oxides acidic, tracking metallic character.

nodes.label: Reactivity Trends
nodes.summary: Metal reactivity rises down a group; nonmetal reactivity rises up a group toward fluorine.

nodes.label: Metallic Character Trend
nodes.summary: Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period.

nodes.label: Organization of the Table
nodes.summary: Elements are arranged in periods and groups reflecting recurring electron configurations.

nodes.label: s, p, d, f Blocks
nodes.summary: The table divides into s, p, d, and f blocks based on the subshell being filled.

nodes.label: Periodic Law
nodes.summary: Element properties are periodic functions of atomic number.

nodes.label: Periods and Groups
nodes.summary: Rows are periods sharing the highest n; columns are groups sharing valence configuration.

nodes.label: Representative vs. Transition Elements
nodes.summary: Representative (main-group) elements fill s and p subshells; transition elements fill d subshells.

nodes.label: Oxidation States from Periodicity
nodes.summary: Common oxidation states follow group position and valence electron count.

nodes.label: Group-Predicted Oxidation States
nodes.summary: Main-group elements show characteristic oxidation states tied to gaining or losing valence electrons to an octet.

nodes.label: Variable Oxidation States
nodes.summary: Transition metals adopt multiple oxidation states because s and d electrons have similar energies.

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanical Model
nodes.summary: The quantum model describes electrons by probability orbitals defined by four quantum numbers.

nodes.label: Orbital Shapes
nodes.summary: s, p, d, and f orbitals have characteristic spatial shapes.

nodes.label: d and f Orbitals
nodes.summary: d orbitals hold up to 10 and f orbitals up to 14 electrons with more complex shapes.

nodes.label: Degenerate Orbitals
nodes.summary: Orbitals within the same subshell are degenerate, having equal energy in an isolated atom.

nodes.label: Nodes in Orbitals
nodes.summary: A node is a region of zero electron probability; the number of nodes increases with n and l.

nodes.label: p Orbital
nodes.summary: p orbitals are dumbbell-shaped, three per subshell, holding up to 6 electrons.

nodes.label: s Orbital
nodes.summary: The s orbital is spherical and holds up to 2 electrons.

nodes.label: The Four Quantum Numbers
nodes.summary: Each electron is uniquely described by n, l, m-l, and m-s.

nodes.label: Allowed Quantum-Number Sets
nodes.summary: Valid sets require l < n and -l <= m-l <= l, so impossible combinations can be ruled out.

nodes.label: Azimuthal Quantum Number (l)
nodes.summary: The azimuthal number l (0 to n-1) defines the subshell and orbital shape (s, p, d, f).

nodes.label: Magnetic Quantum Number (m_l)
nodes.summary: The magnetic number m-l ranges from -l to +l and specifies an orbital's spatial orientation.

nodes.label: Orbitals & Electrons per Subshell
nodes.summary: Each subshell holds 2l+1 orbitals, and each orbital holds 2 electrons (max 2, 6, 10, 14 for s,p,d,f).

nodes.label: Principal Quantum Number (n)
nodes.summary: The principal quantum number n gives the shell and primary energy and size; n = 1, 2, 3, ...

nodes.label: Shell Electron Capacity (2n^2)
nodes.summary: A shell of principal number n holds a maximum of 2n^2 electrons.

nodes.label: Spin Quantum Number (m_s)
nodes.summary: The spin number m-s is +1/2 or -1/2, describing an electron's intrinsic spin orientation.

nodes.label: Wave-Particle Duality
nodes.summary: Electrons exhibit both particle and wave behavior, described probabilistically.

nodes.label: de Broglie Wavelength
nodes.summary: Matter has a wavelength lambda = h/(mv), significant for small fast particles like electrons.

nodes.label: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
nodes.summary: One cannot simultaneously know an electron's exact position and momentum with arbitrary precision.

nodes.label: Orbital as Probability Cloud
nodes.summary: An orbital is a region of space where an electron is most probably found, not a fixed orbit.

nodes.label: Colligative properties
nodes.summary: Colligative properties depend on the number of dissolved particles, not their identity.

nodes.label: Boiling-point elevation and freezing-point depression
nodes.summary: Solutes raise the boiling point and lower the freezing point of a solvent.

nodes.label: Boiling-point elevation
nodes.summary: Boiling-point elevation is given by ΔTb = i·Kb·m, raising the boiling point with solute concentration.

nodes.label: Kb and Kf constants
nodes.summary: Kb (ebullioscopic) and Kf (cryoscopic) constants are solvent-specific proportionality constants for boiling and freezing changes.

nodes.label: Freezing-point depression
nodes.summary: Freezing-point depression is given by ΔTf = i·Kf·m, lowering the freezing point with solute concentration.

nodes.label: Phase-diagram effect of solutes
nodes.summary: Adding solute shifts the solvent's phase boundaries, widening the liquid range by lowering the freezing curve and raising the boiling curve.

nodes.label: Colligative concept
nodes.summary: Solute particles alter solvent properties in proportion to their concentration.

nodes.label: Definition of colligative property
nodes.summary: A colligative property depends only on the number of solute particles in solution, not their chemical nature.

nodes.label: Van't Hoff factor (i)
nodes.summary: The van't Hoff factor i is the number of particles a solute yields in solution and scales all colligative effects.

nodes.label: Ion pairing and real van't Hoff factor
nodes.summary: The measured van't Hoff factor is often less than the ideal value because of ion pairing in concentrated solution.

nodes.label: Osmotic pressure and osmosis
nodes.summary: Solute concentration differences across a semipermeable membrane drive water flow and generate osmotic pressure.

nodes.label: Effects on cells (lysis/crenation)
nodes.summary: Cells swell or lyse in hypotonic solutions and shrink (crenate or plasmolyze) in hypertonic solutions.

nodes.label: Osmolarity
nodes.summary: Osmolarity is the total concentration of solute particles per liter, accounting for dissociation.

nodes.label: Osmosis
nodes.summary: Osmosis is the net diffusion of water across a semipermeable membrane from low to high solute concentration.

nodes.label: Osmotic pressure (Π = iMRT)
nodes.summary: Osmotic pressure, the pressure needed to stop osmosis, is Π = iMRT (the van't Hoff equation).

nodes.label: Semipermeable membrane
nodes.summary: A semipermeable membrane lets solvent (water) pass but blocks solute, the prerequisite for osmosis.

nodes.label: Tonicity (hypo-, iso-, hypertonic)
nodes.summary: Tonicity describes a solution as hypotonic, isotonic, or hypertonic relative to a cell, predicting water movement.

nodes.label: Vapor pressure and its lowering
nodes.summary: Nonvolatile solutes lower a solvent's vapor pressure as described by Raoult's law.

nodes.label: Ideal vs non-ideal solutions
nodes.summary: Ideal solutions obey Raoult's law exactly; real solutions show positive or negative deviations based on relative solute-solvent attraction.

nodes.label: Raoult's law
nodes.summary: Raoult's law states the vapor pressure of a solvent equals its mole fraction times the pure-solvent vapor pressure.

nodes.label: Vapor pressure
nodes.summary: Vapor pressure is the pressure of vapor in equilibrium with its liquid at a given temperature.

nodes.label: Vapor-pressure lowering
nodes.summary: A nonvolatile solute lowers the solvent's vapor pressure in proportion to the solute mole fraction.

nodes.label: Concentration units
nodes.summary: Concentration expresses the amount of solute per amount of solution or solvent in several interconvertible units.

nodes.label: Amount-based concentration
nodes.summary: Molarity and molality relate moles of solute to solution volume or solvent mass.

nodes.label: Equivalents and equivalent weight
nodes.summary: An equivalent is the amount of a species that supplies one mole of reacting units (H⁺, OH⁻, or electrons), used to define normality.

nodes.label: Molality (m)
nodes.summary: Molality is moles of solute per kilogram of solvent and is temperature-independent.

nodes.label: Molarity (M)
nodes.summary: Molarity is moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L).

nodes.label: Mole fraction
nodes.summary: Mole fraction is the moles of one component divided by the total moles of all components.

nodes.label: Normality (N)
nodes.summary: Normality is equivalents of solute per liter of solution, useful for acid-base and redox stoichiometry.

nodes.label: Dilution and conversions
nodes.summary: Concentrations are altered by dilution and converted among units using density and molar mass.

nodes.label: Dilution equation (M₁V₁ = M₂V₂)
nodes.summary: Dilution conserves moles of solute, so M₁V₁ = M₂V₂ relates initial and final concentration and volume.

nodes.label: Converting between concentration units
nodes.summary: Molarity, molality, mole fraction, and percent can be interconverted using density and molar mass.

nodes.label: Ratio and percent concentration
nodes.summary: Percent and parts-per units express concentration as fractions of mass or volume.

nodes.label: Mass percent
nodes.summary: Mass percent is the mass of solute divided by the total mass of solution times 100%.

nodes.label: Parts per million and per billion
nodes.summary: Parts per million (ppm) and per billion (ppb) express very dilute concentrations as mass or mole ratios.

nodes.label: Volume percent
nodes.summary: Volume percent is the volume of solute divided by the total volume of solution times 100%.

nodes.label: Anomalous physical properties of water
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonding gives water unusually high melting/boiling points, heat capacity, and a less-dense solid.

nodes.label: Cohesion, adhesion, and surface tension
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonding makes water stick to itself and to surfaces, producing high surface tension and capillarity.

nodes.label: Adhesion
nodes.summary: Adhesion is the attraction of water to other polar or charged surfaces.

nodes.label: Capillary action
nodes.summary: Capillary action is the rise of water in narrow tubes from the combination of adhesion to walls and cohesion within the column.

nodes.label: Cohesion
nodes.summary: Cohesion is the attraction of water molecules to one another via hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Meniscus formation
nodes.summary: Water forms a concave meniscus in glass because adhesion to the walls exceeds cohesion.

nodes.label: Surface tension
nodes.summary: Surface tension arises because surface molecules are pulled inward by cohesion, minimizing surface area.

nodes.label: Surfactants lower surface tension
nodes.summary: Surfactants (amphipathic molecules like soaps) accumulate at the surface and disrupt cohesion, lowering surface tension.

nodes.label: Density anomalies
nodes.summary: Solid water is less dense than liquid water, and liquid water is densest near 4°C.

nodes.label: Biological significance of floating ice
nodes.summary: Floating ice insulates lakes and allows aquatic life to survive beneath a frozen surface.

nodes.label: Ice is less dense than liquid
nodes.summary: Ice floats because its open, hexagonal hydrogen-bonded lattice holds molecules farther apart than in liquid water.

nodes.label: Maximum density at 4°C
nodes.summary: Liquid water reaches maximum density at about 4°C, below which it expands as the ice-like lattice begins to form.

nodes.label: Thermal properties
nodes.summary: Water's hydrogen-bond network requires large energy input to heat, melt, or vaporize.

nodes.label: Evaporative cooling
nodes.summary: Evaporation removes the most energetic molecules, so sweating and transpiration cool organisms by exploiting water's high heat of vaporization.

nodes.label: Heat of fusion
nodes.summary: Melting ice requires a substantial heat of fusion (≈6.0 kJ/mol) to disrupt the ordered hydrogen-bonded lattice.

nodes.label: High heat of vaporization
nodes.summary: Water's large heat of vaporization (≈40.7 kJ/mol) reflects the energy needed to break hydrogen bonds during boiling, enabling evaporative cooling.

nodes.label: High boiling and melting points
nodes.summary: Water has anomalously high melting (0°C) and boiling (100°C) points for its molar mass because of extensive hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: High specific heat capacity
nodes.summary: Water has a high specific heat (≈4.18 J/g·°C) because much energy goes into breaking hydrogen bonds rather than raising temperature.

nodes.label: Thermal buffering of organisms
nodes.summary: High heat capacity lets water resist temperature change, buffering cells and environments against thermal swings.

nodes.label: Solutions and solubility
nodes.summary: Solubility describes how much solute dissolves and is governed by saturation, temperature, pressure, and equilibrium.

nodes.label: Factors affecting solubility
nodes.summary: Temperature and pressure shift how much solute dissolves, especially for gases.

nodes.label: Henry's law (pressure and gas solubility)
nodes.summary: Henry's law states the solubility of a gas is proportional to its partial pressure above the liquid (C = kP).

nodes.label: pH effect on solubility
nodes.summary: Salts of weak acids or bases (e.g. hydroxides, carbonates) become more soluble in acidic solution as protonation removes a product ion.

nodes.label: Temperature effect on gas solubility
nodes.summary: Gas solubility in water decreases as temperature increases.

nodes.label: Temperature effect on solid solubility
nodes.summary: Solubility of most solids in water increases with temperature, though some salts decrease.

nodes.label: Solubility product (Ksp)
nodes.summary: The solubility-product constant quantifies the equilibrium of a slightly soluble salt with its ions.

nodes.label: Common-ion effect
nodes.summary: Adding a common ion shifts dissolution equilibrium toward the solid, decreasing a salt's solubility.

nodes.label: Complex-ion formation increases solubility
nodes.summary: Formation of a soluble complex ion removes free metal ion from solution, shifting dissolution equilibrium and increasing the salt's solubility.

nodes.label: Ksp definition
nodes.summary: Ksp is the equilibrium constant for dissolution of a slightly soluble ionic solid into its aqueous ions.

nodes.label: Ion product (Q) and precipitation prediction
nodes.summary: Comparing the ion product Q to Ksp predicts precipitation: Q > Ksp precipitates, Q < Ksp dissolves, Q = Ksp is saturated.

nodes.label: Molar solubility from Ksp
nodes.summary: Molar solubility is the moles of salt that dissolve per liter and can be calculated from Ksp.

nodes.label: Saturation
nodes.summary: Solutions are unsaturated, saturated, or supersaturated relative to the solubility limit.

nodes.label: Dynamic dissolution-precipitation equilibrium
nodes.summary: At saturation, the rate of dissolution equals the rate of precipitation in a dynamic equilibrium with undissolved solute.

nodes.label: Saturated solution
nodes.summary: A saturated solution holds the maximum solute at equilibrium with undissolved solute.

nodes.label: Solubility
nodes.summary: Solubility is the maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a specified temperature.

nodes.label: Supersaturated solution
nodes.summary: A supersaturated solution temporarily holds more solute than its equilibrium solubility and precipitates upon disturbance.

nodes.label: Unsaturated solution
nodes.summary: An unsaturated solution contains less solute than the maximum it can dissolve and can dissolve more.

nodes.label: Solubility rules and precipitation
nodes.summary: Empirical solubility rules predict whether ionic compounds dissolve or precipitate in water.

nodes.label: Electrolytes and solution conductivity
nodes.summary: Solutions conduct electricity in proportion to their free-ion concentration, so strong electrolytes conduct well and nonelectrolytes do not.

nodes.label: Strong, weak, and nonelectrolytes
nodes.summary: Strong electrolytes dissociate completely, weak electrolytes partially, and nonelectrolytes not at all when dissolved.

nodes.label: Net ionic and spectator ions
nodes.summary: A net ionic equation shows only the ions that react to form a precipitate, omitting spectator ions that remain dissolved.

nodes.label: Precipitation reactions
nodes.summary: Mixing solutions can form an insoluble precipitate when product ions exceed their solubility.

nodes.label: Solubility rules for ionic compounds
nodes.summary: Solubility rules state, e.g., that Group 1, ammonium, nitrate, and most acetate salts are soluble, while many sulfides, carbonates, and hydroxides are not.

nodes.label: Solution terminology
nodes.summary: Solutions are defined by solute, solvent, and degree of saturation.

nodes.label: Aqueous solution
nodes.summary: An aqueous solution is one in which water is the solvent.

nodes.label: Colloids and suspensions vs solutions
nodes.summary: Colloids disperse intermediate-size particles that scatter light (Tyndall effect) and suspensions settle out, unlike true homogeneous solutions.

nodes.label: Miscibility
nodes.summary: Miscible liquids dissolve in each other in all proportions, while immiscible liquids form separate phases.

nodes.label: Solute and solvent
nodes.summary: The solute is the dissolved substance present in smaller amount and the solvent is the dissolving medium present in larger amount.

nodes.label: Solution (homogeneous mixture)
nodes.summary: A solution is a homogeneous mixture of solute uniformly dispersed in solvent.

nodes.label: Water as a solvent
nodes.summary: Water's polarity makes it the universal solvent for ionic and polar substances while excluding nonpolar ones.

nodes.label: Dissolution and solvation
nodes.summary: Water dissolves solutes by surrounding ions and polar molecules with oriented water shells.

nodes.label: Dissociation of ionic compounds
nodes.summary: Ionic compounds dissociate into their constituent ions when dissolved in water.

nodes.label: H-bonding with polar solutes
nodes.summary: Polar molecular solutes (alcohols, sugars, ammonia) dissolve by forming hydrogen bonds with water rather than by dissociating into ions.

nodes.label: High dielectric constant
nodes.summary: Water's high dielectric constant screens electrostatic attraction between dissolved ions, stabilizing them in solution and promoting dissociation.

nodes.label: Hydration shell
nodes.summary: A hydration shell is the oriented layer of water molecules surrounding a dissolved ion, with oxygen toward cations and hydrogen toward anions.

nodes.label: Ion-dipole interactions
nodes.summary: Ions dissolve through ion-dipole interactions between the charged ion and the polar water dipoles.

nodes.label: Solvation (hydration)
nodes.summary: Solvation, called hydration when the solvent is water, is the surrounding of solute particles by solvent molecules.

nodes.label: Universal solvent
nodes.summary: Water dissolves a wide range of ionic and polar solutes, earning the description 'universal solvent'.

nodes.label: Energetics of dissolution
nodes.summary: Dissolution depends on the balance of lattice/solute-solute, solvent-solvent, and solute-solvent energies plus entropy.

nodes.label: Enthalpy of solution
nodes.summary: The enthalpy of solution is the net energy of breaking solute-solute and solvent-solvent attractions and forming solute-solvent attractions.

nodes.label: Entropy of dissolution
nodes.summary: Dissolution usually increases entropy as ordered solute spreads through the solvent, favoring solubility.

nodes.label: Hydration (solvation) energy
nodes.summary: Hydration energy is the energy released when gaseous ions are surrounded by water; it offsets the lattice energy during dissolution.

nodes.label: Lattice energy and dissolution
nodes.summary: The lattice energy holding an ionic solid together must be overcome by hydration energy for the salt to dissolve.

nodes.label: Spontaneity of dissolving (ΔG)
nodes.summary: Whether a solute dissolves depends on the sign of ΔG = ΔH − TΔS for the dissolution process.

nodes.label: The hydrophobic effect
nodes.summary: Nonpolar solutes aggregate in water because clustering increases the entropy of the surrounding water.

nodes.label: Clathrate (ordered water) cages
nodes.summary: Water forms ordered clathrate-like cages around nonpolar solutes, an entropically unfavorable ordering that drives the hydrophobic effect.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic effect (entropy-driven)
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect drives nonpolar molecules together to minimize ordered water cages, increasing water's entropy.

nodes.label: Membrane and micelle assembly
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect drives amphipathic molecules to form micelles and lipid bilayers in water.

nodes.label: Role in protein folding
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect buries nonpolar side chains in a protein's interior, stabilizing its folded structure.

nodes.label: Like dissolves like
nodes.summary: Polar and ionic solutes dissolve in polar water, while nonpolar solutes do not.

nodes.label: Amphipathic molecules
nodes.summary: Amphipathic molecules contain both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions, e.g. phospholipids and detergents.

nodes.label: Hydrophilic substances
nodes.summary: Hydrophilic ('water-loving') polar or charged substances interact favorably with water and dissolve readily.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic substances
nodes.summary: Hydrophobic ('water-fearing') nonpolar substances do not dissolve appreciably in water.

nodes.label: Like-dissolves-like principle
nodes.summary: Solutes dissolve best in solvents of similar polarity, so polar/ionic solutes favor water.

nodes.label: Self-ionization and acid-base nature of water
nodes.summary: Water autoionizes and acts as both acid and base, defining the pH scale and neutrality.

nodes.label: Amphoteric nature of water
nodes.summary: Water can donate or accept a proton, acting as either acid or base.

nodes.label: Water is amphoteric
nodes.summary: Water is amphoteric, acting as a Brønsted acid (donating H⁺) or base (accepting H⁺) depending on its partner.

nodes.label: Water's conjugate pairs
nodes.summary: Water's conjugate acid is hydronium (H₃O⁺) and its conjugate base is hydroxide (OH⁻).

nodes.label: Autoionization of water
nodes.summary: Water self-ionizes into hydronium and hydroxide, defining the ion-product constant Kw.

nodes.label: Ion-product constant Kw
nodes.summary: The ion-product of water is Kw = [H₃O⁺][OH⁻] = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴ at 25°C.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence of Kw
nodes.summary: Kw increases with temperature because autoionization is endothermic, shifting neutral pH below 7 when warm.

nodes.label: Neutral water concentrations
nodes.summary: In neutral water at 25°C, [H₃O⁺] = [OH⁻] = 1.0×10⁻⁷ M.

nodes.label: Autoionization reaction
nodes.summary: Two water molecules autoionize: 2H₂O ⇌ H₃O⁺ + OH⁻.

nodes.label: Hydronium and proton hydration
nodes.summary: Protons in water exist as hydrated hydronium ions, not bare H⁺.

nodes.label: Hydronium ion (H₃O⁺)
nodes.summary: A free proton in water binds a water molecule to form the hydronium ion, H₃O⁺.

nodes.label: Grotthuss proton hopping
nodes.summary: Protons move unusually fast in water by hopping along hydrogen bonds (the Grotthuss mechanism) rather than by bulk diffusion.

nodes.label: pH and pOH scale
nodes.summary: The pH and pOH scales quantify hydronium and hydroxide concentrations on a logarithmic scale.

nodes.label: Acidic, neutral, and basic ranges
nodes.summary: At 25°C a solution is acidic below pH 7, neutral at pH 7, and basic above pH 7.

nodes.label: Logarithmic nature of pH
nodes.summary: Each unit change in pH represents a tenfold change in hydronium ion concentration.

nodes.label: pH below 0 and above 14
nodes.summary: Concentrated strong acids or bases can give pH values below 0 or above 14 because the scale is not bounded to 0–14.

nodes.label: Definition of pH
nodes.summary: pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydronium ion concentration, pH = −log[H₃O⁺].

nodes.label: pH + pOH = 14
nodes.summary: At 25°C, pH + pOH = 14, following from Kw = 1.0×10⁻¹⁴.

nodes.label: Definition of pOH
nodes.summary: pOH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydroxide concentration, pOH = −log[OH⁻].

nodes.label: Structure and properties of water
nodes.summary: Water's bent polar geometry and hydrogen bonding give rise to its anomalous physical properties.

nodes.label: Hydrogen bonding in water
nodes.summary: Water molecules form extensive hydrogen-bond networks that govern most of its unique behavior.

nodes.label: Hydrogen bond definition
nodes.summary: A hydrogen bond is an attraction between a hydrogen bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on a nearby electronegative atom.

nodes.label: H-bond donors and acceptors
nodes.summary: Each water molecule can donate two hydrogen bonds (its two H atoms) and accept two (its two lone pairs).

nodes.label: Anomalous trend vs group-16 hydrides
nodes.summary: Water's boiling point is far higher than the trend of H₂S, H₂Se, and H₂Te would predict, evidence that hydrogen bonding (not mass) dominates.

nodes.label: Tetrahedral H-bond network
nodes.summary: Each water can form up to four hydrogen bonds, creating a dynamic three-dimensional network in liquid and a fixed lattice in ice.

nodes.label: H-bond strength vs covalent
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds are far weaker than covalent O-H bonds but stronger than typical dipole-dipole or London forces.

nodes.label: Intermolecular forces in water
nodes.summary: Water is dominated by hydrogen bonding but also experiences dipole-dipole and London dispersion forces.

nodes.label: Dipole-dipole interactions
nodes.summary: Polar water molecules attract one another through alignment of their permanent dipoles.

nodes.label: Hydrogen bonding as dominant IMF
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonding is the strongest and dominant intermolecular force in water and explains its anomalous properties.

nodes.label: Relative ordering of IMFs
nodes.summary: In strength, ion-dipole > hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion, governing relative interaction energies.

nodes.label: London dispersion forces
nodes.summary: Even polar molecules like water have London dispersion forces from instantaneous induced dipoles.

nodes.label: Molecular structure of water
nodes.summary: Water is a bent, polar molecule of two O-H bonds with two lone pairs on oxygen.

nodes.label: Bent (angular) geometry
nodes.summary: Water adopts a bent geometry with an H-O-H bond angle of about 104.5° due to two bonding pairs and two lone pairs.

nodes.label: O-H bond polarity
nodes.summary: Each O-H bond is polar because oxygen is much more electronegative than hydrogen, pulling electron density toward oxygen.

nodes.label: Net dipole moment
nodes.summary: The bent shape makes the bond dipoles add to a large net molecular dipole, with oxygen partially negative and hydrogens partially positive.

nodes.label: Lone-pair repulsion and angle compression
nodes.summary: Greater lone-pair–bonding-pair repulsion compresses the H-O-H angle below the ideal tetrahedral 109.5° to about 104.5°.

nodes.label: Partial charges
nodes.summary: Oxygen carries a partial negative charge (δ-) and each hydrogen a partial positive charge (δ+).

nodes.label: sp³ hybridized oxygen
nodes.summary: Oxygen in water is sp³ hybridized, with two bonding orbitals and two lone-pair orbitals in a tetrahedral electron arrangement.

nodes.label: The covalent bond
nodes.summary: A covalent bond is the sharing of one or more electron pairs between two atoms to attain stable electron configurations.

nodes.label: Formation & nature of the covalent bond
nodes.summary: Covalent bonds form when atomic orbitals overlap and shared electrons lower the system's potential energy.

nodes.label: Bond formation is exothermic
nodes.summary: Forming a bond releases energy (exothermic) while breaking a bond requires energy input (endothermic).

nodes.label: Covalent vs. ionic vs. metallic bonding
nodes.summary: The three primary chemical bonds are covalent (shared electrons), ionic (transferred electrons / electrostatic attraction), and metallic (delocalized electron sea), spanning an electronegativity-driven continuum.

nodes.label: Duet rule for hydrogen
nodes.summary: Hydrogen is stable with two valence electrons, filling its single 1s orbital rather than an octet.

nodes.label: Electron sharing
nodes.summary: In a covalent bond two atoms share a pair of valence electrons rather than transferring them.

nodes.label: Octet rule
nodes.summary: Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to achieve eight valence electrons (a noble-gas configuration).

nodes.label: Bond potential-energy curve
nodes.summary: As two atoms approach, potential energy drops to a minimum at the equilibrium bond length, then rises sharply due to nuclear repulsion.

nodes.label: Valence electrons
nodes.summary: Valence electrons are the outermost-shell electrons that participate in bonding and are counted from the group number for main-group elements.

nodes.label: Bond length, energy & strength
nodes.summary: Bond length and bond dissociation energy quantify the geometry and strength of a covalent bond.

nodes.label: Average vs. specific bond energy
nodes.summary: Tabulated bond energies are averages across many molecules; a given bond's actual energy depends on its specific molecular environment.

nodes.label: Bond dissociation energy
nodes.summary: Bond energy is the energy required to homolytically break one mole of a bond in the gas phase.

nodes.label: Bond length
nodes.summary: Bond length is the equilibrium internuclear distance that minimizes the bond's potential energy.

nodes.label: Estimating reaction enthalpy from bond energies
nodes.summary: ΔH ≈ (sum of bonds broken) − (sum of bonds formed), using average bond dissociation energies.

nodes.label: Bond order vs. length & strength
nodes.summary: As bond order increases, bond length decreases and bond strength (energy) increases.

nodes.label: Atomic radius vs. bond length
nodes.summary: Larger atomic radii give longer, generally weaker single bonds between the bonded atoms.

nodes.label: Bond order & multiplicity
nodes.summary: Bond order counts the number of shared electron pairs between two atoms (single, double, triple).

nodes.label: Bond order
nodes.summary: Bond order is the number of bonding electron pairs shared between two atoms; higher bond order means stronger, shorter bonds.

nodes.label: Coordinate (dative) covalent bond
nodes.summary: A coordinate covalent bond is one in which both shared electrons originate from the same atom.

nodes.label: Double bond
nodes.summary: A double bond shares two electron pairs (one sigma + one pi bond) between two atoms.

nodes.label: Fractional bond order from resonance
nodes.summary: Delocalized (resonance) species have fractional average bond orders, e.g., each C–O in carbonate is bond order ~1.33.

nodes.label: Single bond
nodes.summary: A single bond shares one electron pair (one sigma bond) between two atoms.

nodes.label: Triple bond
nodes.summary: A triple bond shares three electron pairs (one sigma + two pi bonds) between two atoms.

nodes.label: Molecular polarity & dipole moment
nodes.summary: Molecular polarity is the vector sum of bond dipoles and depends on both bond polarity and molecular geometry.

nodes.label: Dipole moment
nodes.summary: A dipole moment quantifies charge separation as the product of partial charge magnitude and distance.

nodes.label: Bond dipoles
nodes.summary: Each polar bond has a bond dipole vector pointing toward the more electronegative atom.

nodes.label: Dipole moment (μ = q·d)
nodes.summary: Dipole moment μ = q·d is a vector with magnitude equal to charge times separation, measured in debyes.

nodes.label: Lone-pair contribution to dipole
nodes.summary: Lone pairs contribute their own dipole vector, often reinforcing the net molecular dipole (e.g., in NH₃ and H₂O).

nodes.label: Net dipole as vector sum
nodes.summary: The molecular dipole moment is the vector sum of all individual bond dipoles.

nodes.label: Polarizability
nodes.summary: Polarizability is the ease with which an electron cloud is distorted to form an induced dipole.

nodes.label: Polarizability defined
nodes.summary: Polarizability measures how easily an atom or molecule's electron cloud is deformed by an external field.

nodes.label: Polarizability increases with size
nodes.summary: Larger atoms/molecules with more loosely held electrons are more polarizable, increasing dispersion forces.

nodes.label: Geometry, symmetry & polarity
nodes.summary: Symmetric geometries can cancel bond dipoles, making molecules nonpolar despite polar bonds.

nodes.label: Asymmetry & lone pairs create polarity
nodes.summary: Asymmetric geometry or lone pairs (e.g., H₂O, NH₃) prevent dipole cancellation, giving a net molecular dipole.

nodes.label: Predicting polar vs. nonpolar molecules
nodes.summary: A molecule is polar if it has polar bonds that do not cancel by symmetry; otherwise it is nonpolar.

nodes.label: Symmetric molecules are nonpolar
nodes.summary: In symmetric molecules (e.g., CO₂, CCl₄, BF₃) bond dipoles cancel, giving zero net dipole.

nodes.label: Electronegativity & bond polarity
nodes.summary: Differences in electronegativity make bonds polar, distributing shared electrons unevenly between atoms.

nodes.label: Bond character (ionic–covalent continuum)
nodes.summary: The electronegativity difference (ΔEN) determines whether a bond is nonpolar covalent, polar covalent, or ionic.

nodes.label: Ionic bond character
nodes.summary: A large ΔEN (roughly > 1.7–2.0) results in essentially complete electron transfer and predominantly ionic bonding.

nodes.label: Nonpolar covalent bond
nodes.summary: When ΔEN is very small (roughly < 0.5) electrons are shared nearly equally and the bond is nonpolar covalent.

nodes.label: Partial ionic character
nodes.summary: Most bonds have partial ionic character; the ionic–covalent distinction is a continuum, not a sharp boundary.

nodes.label: Polar covalent bond
nodes.summary: An intermediate ΔEN (roughly 0.5–1.7) produces unequal sharing and a polar covalent bond with partial charges.

nodes.label: Electronegativity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity is an atom's ability to attract bonding electrons toward itself within a bond.

nodes.label: Definition of electronegativity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity measures the tendency of a bonded atom to attract the shared electron pair.

nodes.label: Most electronegative elements
nodes.summary: Fluorine, oxygen, nitrogen, and chlorine are the most electronegative biologically relevant elements (F > O > N ≈ Cl).

nodes.label: Pauling scale
nodes.summary: The Pauling scale assigns dimensionless electronegativity values with fluorine the most electronegative (~4.0).

nodes.label: Periodic trend in electronegativity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity increases across a period (left to right) and decreases down a group.

nodes.label: Electronegativity vs. electron affinity
nodes.summary: Electronegativity describes electron-attracting power of a bonded atom, whereas electron affinity is the energy change when an isolated gaseous atom gains an electron.

nodes.label: Partial charges & dipole arrow
nodes.summary: Polar bonds carry partial positive (δ+) and partial negative (δ−) charges represented by a dipole arrow.

nodes.label: δ+ / δ− notation
nodes.summary: The more electronegative atom carries the partial negative charge (δ−) and the less electronegative atom δ+.

nodes.label: Bond dipole arrow convention
nodes.summary: A bond dipole is drawn as an arrow pointing from δ+ toward δ− (toward the more electronegative atom).

nodes.label: Partial charge vs. formal charge
nodes.summary: Partial charges arise from electronegativity (real charge separation), whereas formal charges are a bookkeeping assignment assuming equal sharing.

nodes.label: Hybridization & molecular orbitals
nodes.summary: Atomic orbitals mix into hybrid orbitals that form sigma and pi bonds consistent with molecular geometry.

nodes.label: Molecular orbital theory
nodes.summary: Atomic orbitals combine into bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals delocalized over the molecule.

nodes.label: MO bond order
nodes.summary: MO bond order = ½(bonding electrons − antibonding electrons); a value of zero predicts no stable bond.

nodes.label: Bonding & antibonding orbitals
nodes.summary: Constructive combination gives a lower-energy bonding MO; destructive combination gives a higher-energy antibonding MO.

nodes.label: Conjugation & delocalized pi systems
nodes.summary: Alternating single/double bonds create conjugated, delocalized pi systems that stabilize the molecule.

nodes.label: HOMO & LUMO
nodes.summary: The HOMO (highest occupied) and LUMO (lowest unoccupied) molecular orbitals are the frontier orbitals governing reactivity and light absorption.

nodes.label: LCAO & MO formation
nodes.summary: Molecular orbitals form by the linear combination of atomic orbitals; N atomic orbitals yield N molecular orbitals.

nodes.label: Paramagnetism from unpaired electrons
nodes.summary: MO theory explains paramagnetism: molecules with unpaired electrons in MOs (e.g., O₂) are attracted to a magnetic field, while diamagnetic species are not.

nodes.label: Hybridization schemes
nodes.summary: The steric number determines the sp, sp², sp³, sp³d, or sp³d² hybridization of the central atom.

nodes.label: Assigning hybridization from structure
nodes.summary: Count the steric number (bonded atoms + lone pairs) on an atom to assign its hybridization: 2→sp, 3→sp², 4→sp³.

nodes.label: s character vs. bond angle/length
nodes.summary: Greater s character (sp > sp² > sp³) gives larger bond angles, shorter bonds, and higher electronegativity.

nodes.label: sp hybridization
nodes.summary: sp hybridization (steric number 2) gives linear geometry, 180° angles, and 50% s character.

nodes.label: sp² hybridization
nodes.summary: sp² hybridization (steric number 3) gives trigonal planar geometry and 120° angles.

nodes.label: sp³ hybridization
nodes.summary: sp³ hybridization (steric number 4) gives tetrahedral geometry and 109.5° angles.

nodes.label: sp³d and sp³d² hybridization
nodes.summary: sp³d (steric 5, trigonal bipyramidal) and sp³d² (steric 6, octahedral) involve d-orbital participation.

nodes.label: Unhybridized p orbitals form pi bonds
nodes.summary: Leftover unhybridized p orbitals (one for sp², two for sp) form the pi bonds of double and triple bonds.

nodes.label: Sigma & pi bonds
nodes.summary: Sigma bonds form by head-on overlap and pi bonds by side-on overlap of unhybridized p orbitals.

nodes.label: Sigma/pi composition of bonds
nodes.summary: Single bonds are one σ; double bonds are one σ + one π; triple bonds are one σ + two π.

nodes.label: Counting σ and π bonds in a molecule
nodes.summary: Every bond (single, double, or triple) contains exactly one σ bond; each additional bond in a double/triple adds one π bond.

nodes.label: Pi (π) bond
nodes.summary: A pi bond results from side-by-side overlap of parallel p orbitals with electron density above and below the bond axis.

nodes.label: Sigma vs. pi bond strength
nodes.summary: A single σ bond is generally stronger than a single π bond because head-on overlap is more effective than side-on overlap.

nodes.label: Restricted rotation about pi bonds
nodes.summary: Sigma bonds allow free rotation, but pi bonds restrict rotation, fixing double-bond geometry (cis/trans).

nodes.label: Sigma (σ) bond
nodes.summary: A sigma bond results from head-on (axial) orbital overlap with electron density along the internuclear axis.

nodes.label: Valence bond theory
nodes.summary: Valence bond theory describes bonds as overlap of atomic (or hybrid) orbitals containing paired electrons.

nodes.label: Orbital conservation in hybridization
nodes.summary: The number of hybrid orbitals formed equals the number of atomic orbitals mixed (and the steric number).

nodes.label: Concept of hybridization
nodes.summary: Hybridization mixes atomic s and p (and sometimes d) orbitals into equivalent hybrid orbitals oriented for bonding.

nodes.label: Orbital overlap
nodes.summary: A covalent bond forms where two atomic orbitals overlap and concentrate electron density between the nuclei.

nodes.label: Intermolecular forces
nodes.summary: Intermolecular forces are attractions between molecules that govern physical properties such as boiling point and solubility.

nodes.label: Dipole–dipole forces
nodes.summary: Dipole–dipole forces are attractions between the permanent dipoles of polar molecules.

nodes.label: Dipole–dipole attraction
nodes.summary: Polar molecules orient so the δ+ of one aligns with the δ− of another, creating a net attraction.

nodes.label: Dipole–induced dipole force
nodes.summary: A permanent dipole can induce a temporary dipole in a nearby nonpolar molecule, producing a weak attraction.

nodes.label: Hydrogen bonding
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonding is an especially strong dipole interaction when H bonds to N, O, or F.

nodes.label: H-bond donor & acceptor
nodes.summary: The donor supplies the polarized H atom; the acceptor supplies the electronegative atom's lone pair.

nodes.label: Boiling-point anomaly of NH₃, H₂O, HF
nodes.summary: NH₃, H₂O, and HF have anomalously high boiling points versus their group hydrides because of hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Intramolecular hydrogen bonding
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds can form within a single molecule (e.g., in ortho-nitrophenol), lowering its boiling point relative to intermolecular H-bonders.

nodes.label: Hydrogen-bond requirement (N, O, F)
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonding requires H covalently bonded to N, O, or F interacting with a lone pair on N, O, or F.

nodes.label: Strength of hydrogen bonds
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds are the strongest IMF (~5–30 kJ/mol) but still far weaker than covalent bonds.

nodes.label: Hydrogen bonding in water
nodes.summary: Water's extensive hydrogen bonding gives it an anomalously high boiling point, high heat capacity, and ice less dense than liquid.

nodes.label: Ion–dipole forces
nodes.summary: Ion–dipole forces are attractions between an ion and the oppositely charged end of a polar molecule.

nodes.label: Ion–dipole attraction
nodes.summary: An ion attracts the partial charge of a polar molecule, e.g., Na⁺ surrounded by water's δ− oxygens.

nodes.label: Hydration / solvation shells
nodes.summary: Ion–dipole attractions form hydration (solvation) shells of solvent molecules around dissolved ions.

nodes.label: Ion–induced dipole force
nodes.summary: An ion can polarize a nearby nonpolar molecule, inducing a temporary dipole and a weak attraction (e.g., O₂ dissolving in saline).

nodes.label: London dispersion forces
nodes.summary: London dispersion forces arise from instantaneous induced dipoles and exist between all molecules.

nodes.label: Effect of branching on dispersion
nodes.summary: Branching reduces surface contact area, weakening dispersion forces and lowering boiling point relative to straight-chain isomers.

nodes.label: Instantaneous & induced dipoles
nodes.summary: Momentary electron-distribution fluctuations create instantaneous dipoles that induce dipoles in neighbors.

nodes.label: Strength scales with size/polarizability
nodes.summary: Dispersion forces strengthen with greater molar mass, surface area, and polarizability (more electrons).

nodes.label: London forces are universal
nodes.summary: London dispersion forces exist between all atoms and molecules and are the only IMF in nonpolar species.

nodes.label: Nature & relative strength of IMFs
nodes.summary: Intermolecular forces are much weaker than covalent bonds but determine condensed-phase behavior.

nodes.label: Electrostatic origin of IMFs
nodes.summary: All intermolecular forces are fundamentally electrostatic attractions between regions of partial or full opposite charge.

nodes.label: Intermolecular vs. intramolecular forces
nodes.summary: Intramolecular bonds hold atoms within a molecule; intermolecular forces act between separate molecules and are weaker.

nodes.label: Relative strength ordering
nodes.summary: General IMF strength order is ion–dipole > hydrogen bonding > dipole–dipole > London dispersion (for comparable mass).

nodes.label: Van der Waals forces
nodes.summary: Van der Waals forces collectively refer to dipole–dipole, dipole–induced dipole, and London dispersion attractions.

nodes.label: Lewis acids & bases
nodes.summary: Lewis acids accept and Lewis bases donate electron pairs, defining acid–base behavior by electron-pair transfer.

nodes.label: Lewis definitions
nodes.summary: The Lewis model classifies acids and bases by electron-pair acceptance and donation.

nodes.label: Lewis adduct & coordinate bond
nodes.summary: A Lewis acid–base reaction forms an adduct joined by a coordinate covalent bond from the base's lone pair.

nodes.label: Lewis acid
nodes.summary: A Lewis acid is an electron-pair acceptor, typically an electron-deficient species or cation.

nodes.label: Lewis base
nodes.summary: A Lewis base is an electron-pair donor, typically a species with an available lone pair.

nodes.label: Relation to other acid–base models
nodes.summary: The Lewis model generalizes and overlaps with the Brønsted–Lowry electron/proton perspectives.

nodes.label: Common Lewis acids/bases
nodes.summary: BF₃, AlCl₃, H⁺, and metal cations are typical Lewis acids; NH₃, H₂O, and halide ions are typical Lewis bases.

nodes.label: Lewis vs. Brønsted–Lowry
nodes.summary: Every Brønsted base is a Lewis base, but the Lewis model also covers electron-pair acceptors that lack protons.

nodes.label: Lewis structures & formal charge
nodes.summary: Lewis structures depict bonding and lone-pair electrons, and formal charge identifies the best resonance contributor.

nodes.label: Drawing Lewis structures
nodes.summary: A Lewis structure is built by distributing all valence electrons as bonds and lone pairs to satisfy the octet rule.

nodes.label: Bonding pairs
nodes.summary: Bonding pairs are shared electron pairs forming the bonds drawn as lines between atoms.

nodes.label: Choosing the central atom
nodes.summary: The least electronegative atom (excluding hydrogen) is usually placed in the center of a Lewis structure.

nodes.label: Count total valence electrons
nodes.summary: Sum valence electrons from all atoms, adding for anion charge and subtracting for cation charge.

nodes.label: Lewis (electron-dot) symbols
nodes.summary: A Lewis symbol shows an element with dots representing its valence electrons around the atomic symbol.

nodes.label: Lone (nonbonding) pairs
nodes.summary: Lone pairs are valence electron pairs localized on a single atom and not shared in a bond.

nodes.label: Completing octets with multiple bonds
nodes.summary: If a central atom lacks an octet after single bonds and lone pairs, convert lone pairs into double or triple bonds.

nodes.label: Exceptions to the octet rule
nodes.summary: Some atoms have incomplete octets, expanded octets, or odd electron counts that violate the octet rule.

nodes.label: Expanded octets
nodes.summary: Period 3+ elements (e.g., P, S, Cl, Xe) can hold more than eight electrons using available d orbitals.

nodes.label: Incomplete octets
nodes.summary: Be (4 e−) and B (6 e−) are stable with fewer than eight valence electrons (electron deficient).

nodes.label: Odd-electron species (radicals)
nodes.summary: Molecules with an odd number of electrons (e.g., NO, NO₂) cannot satisfy all octets and contain an unpaired electron.

nodes.label: Formal charge
nodes.summary: Formal charge tracks electron ownership in a Lewis structure to identify the most plausible arrangement.

nodes.label: Choosing the best Lewis structure
nodes.summary: The best structure minimizes formal charges and places any negative formal charge on the most electronegative atom.

nodes.label: Formal charge formula
nodes.summary: Formal charge = (valence electrons) − (nonbonding electrons) − ½(bonding electrons).

nodes.label: Formal charges sum to overall charge
nodes.summary: The sum of all formal charges in a species equals its net molecular or ionic charge.

nodes.label: Resonance
nodes.summary: Resonance describes delocalized electrons using several equivalent Lewis structures whose average is the true structure.

nodes.label: Resonance structures
nodes.summary: Resonance structures differ only in the placement of electrons (not atoms) and depict electron delocalization.

nodes.label: Delocalization stabilization
nodes.summary: Greater electron delocalization across more resonance structures lowers energy and increases stability.

nodes.label: Equalized bonds from resonance
nodes.summary: Resonance averages bond character so delocalized bonds are equal in length and order (e.g., the three identical C–O bonds of carbonate).

nodes.label: Resonance hybrid
nodes.summary: The actual molecule is a weighted average (hybrid) of its resonance contributors, not any single structure.

nodes.label: Major vs. minor contributors
nodes.summary: Contributors with full octets and minimal/most-favorable formal charges contribute most to the hybrid.

nodes.label: Ionic, metallic & network bonding
nodes.summary: Beyond discrete covalent molecules, ionic, metallic, and network-covalent bonding give extended solids with characteristic properties.

nodes.label: Ionic bonding
nodes.summary: Ionic bonds are electrostatic attractions between cations and anions formed by electron transfer between a metal and nonmetal.

nodes.label: Ionic bond as electron transfer
nodes.summary: An ionic bond forms when a metal transfers electrons to a nonmetal, producing oppositely charged ions held by electrostatic attraction.

nodes.label: Lattice energy & Coulomb's law
nodes.summary: Lattice energy (∝ q₁q₂/r) increases with larger ionic charges and smaller ionic radii, governing ionic-solid stability and melting point.

nodes.label: Properties of ionic solids
nodes.summary: Ionic solids have high melting points, are hard but brittle, and conduct electricity only when molten or dissolved (mobile ions).

nodes.label: Metallic bonding
nodes.summary: Metallic bonding is the attraction between metal cations and a delocalized 'sea' of valence electrons.

nodes.label: Electron-sea model
nodes.summary: In a metal, valence electrons are delocalized over a lattice of cations, forming a mobile electron sea.

nodes.label: Metallic properties (conductivity, malleability)
nodes.summary: Delocalized electrons make metals electrically/thermally conductive, malleable, ductile, and lustrous.

nodes.label: Network covalent solids
nodes.summary: Network covalent solids are giant lattices held together entirely by covalent bonds.

nodes.label: Network covalent solids defined
nodes.summary: In network covalent solids (e.g., diamond, SiO₂, graphite) atoms are linked by a continuous network of covalent bonds rather than discrete molecules.

nodes.label: Properties of network solids
nodes.summary: Network covalent solids have very high melting points and great hardness because melting requires breaking covalent bonds, not IMFs.

nodes.label: Classifying solids by bonding
nodes.summary: Solids are classified as ionic, metallic, network covalent, or molecular, and their melting points reflect the strength of the forces broken on melting.

nodes.label: IMFs & physical properties
nodes.summary: Intermolecular forces determine boiling/melting points, vapor pressure, viscosity, surface tension, and solubility.

nodes.label: Cohesion, adhesion & capillarity
nodes.summary: Cohesive and adhesive forces, both intermolecular, drive capillary action and meniscus shape.

nodes.label: Capillary action & meniscus
nodes.summary: Capillary rise occurs when adhesion to the walls exceeds cohesion, producing a concave meniscus.

nodes.label: Cohesion vs. adhesion
nodes.summary: Cohesion is attraction between like molecules; adhesion is attraction between a liquid and a different surface.

nodes.label: Boiling & melting points
nodes.summary: Stronger intermolecular forces raise the energy needed to separate molecules, increasing boiling and melting points.

nodes.label: IMF strength vs. boiling point
nodes.summary: Higher boiling points correspond to stronger intermolecular forces among comparable molecules.

nodes.label: Heat of vaporization & IMFs
nodes.summary: Greater intermolecular attractions raise the enthalpy of vaporization required to convert liquid to gas.

nodes.label: IMFs & melting point
nodes.summary: Melting point rises with stronger intermolecular forces and more ordered, efficiently packed solids.

nodes.label: Ranking compounds by IMFs
nodes.summary: To compare boiling/melting points, identify the dominant IMF (H-bond > dipole > dispersion) and, within a class, weigh size/polarizability.

nodes.label: Solubility & 'like dissolves like'
nodes.summary: Substances dissolve best in solvents with similar polarity and compatible intermolecular forces.

nodes.label: Amphipathic molecules
nodes.summary: Amphipathic molecules (e.g., soaps, phospholipids) have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions and form micelles/bilayers in water.

nodes.label: Hydrophilic vs. hydrophobic
nodes.summary: Polar/charged groups are hydrophilic (water-soluble); nonpolar groups are hydrophobic (water-insoluble).

nodes.label: Like dissolves like
nodes.summary: Polar (and ionic) solutes dissolve in polar solvents, and nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents.

nodes.label: Miscibility
nodes.summary: Two liquids are miscible when their intermolecular forces are similar enough to mix in all proportions.

nodes.label: Vapor pressure & volatility
nodes.summary: Weaker intermolecular forces let more molecules escape to the gas phase, raising vapor pressure and volatility.

nodes.label: Surface tension
nodes.summary: Surface tension arises from unbalanced cohesive forces at a liquid's surface and increases with IMF strength.

nodes.label: Vapor pressure vs. IMFs
nodes.summary: Liquids with weaker IMFs have higher vapor pressure and are more volatile (lower boiling point).
