nodes.label: Viscosity
nodes.summary: Stronger intermolecular forces (and larger molecules) increase viscosity, a liquid's resistance to flow.

nodes.label: VSEPR & molecular geometry
nodes.summary: VSEPR theory predicts molecular shape by minimizing repulsion between electron domains around a central atom.

nodes.label: VSEPR shapes & bond angles
nodes.summary: Each combination of bonding and lone-pair domains gives a characteristic geometry and ideal bond angle.

nodes.label: Axial vs. equatorial positions
nodes.summary: In trigonal bipyramidal geometry, lone pairs occupy the less crowded equatorial positions to minimize repulsion.

nodes.label: Bent from trigonal planar (1 lone pair)
nodes.summary: Three domains with one lone pair give a bent shape with angles slightly less than 120° (e.g., SO₂, O₃).

nodes.label: Bent from tetrahedral (2 lone pairs)
nodes.summary: Four domains with two lone pairs give a bent shape with ~104.5° angles (e.g., H₂O).

nodes.label: Linear (2 domains, 180°)
nodes.summary: Two electron domains give a linear geometry with 180° bond angles (e.g., CO₂, BeCl₂).

nodes.label: Octahedral (6 domains, 90°)
nodes.summary: Six bonding domains give an octahedral geometry with 90° bond angles (e.g., SF₆).

nodes.label: Seesaw, T-shaped, square planar/pyramidal
nodes.summary: Lone pairs on 5- and 6-domain centers yield seesaw, T-shaped, square pyramidal, and square planar geometries.

nodes.label: Tetrahedral (4 domains, 109.5°)
nodes.summary: Four bonding domains give a tetrahedral geometry with 109.5° bond angles (e.g., CH₄).

nodes.label: Trigonal bipyramidal (5 domains)
nodes.summary: Five bonding domains give a trigonal bipyramidal geometry with 90° and 120° angles (e.g., PCl₅).

nodes.label: Trigonal planar (3 domains, 120°)
nodes.summary: Three bonding domains give a trigonal planar shape with 120° angles (e.g., BF₃).

nodes.label: Trigonal pyramidal (1 lone pair)
nodes.summary: Four domains with one lone pair give a trigonal pyramidal shape with ~107° angles (e.g., NH₃).

nodes.label: VSEPR principles
nodes.summary: Electron domains arrange to be as far apart as possible to minimize electron-pair repulsion.

nodes.label: Electron domains (steric number)
nodes.summary: The steric number equals the count of bonded atoms plus lone pairs on the central atom; multiple bonds count as one domain.

nodes.label: Electron geometry vs. molecular geometry
nodes.summary: Electron geometry counts all domains; molecular geometry describes only the arrangement of bonded atoms.

nodes.label: Lone pairs compress bond angles
nodes.summary: Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, compressing the angles between bonding pairs.

nodes.label: Multiple bonds count as one domain
nodes.summary: A double or triple bond counts as a single electron domain for VSEPR geometry, the same as a single bond.

nodes.label: Lone-pair repulsion ordering
nodes.summary: Repulsion strength is lone pair–lone pair > lone pair–bonding pair > bonding pair–bonding pair.

nodes.label: Chromatography
nodes.summary: Chromatography separates components by their differential partitioning between a stationary and a mobile phase.

nodes.label: Affinity chromatography
nodes.summary: Affinity chromatography isolates a target using a specific binding partner immobilized on the column.

nodes.label: Elution with free ligand or competitive agent
nodes.summary: The target is released by washing with free ligand or changing conditions to break the specific interaction.

nodes.label: His-tag / Ni-NTA affinity purification
nodes.summary: A polyhistidine tag binds a nickel column and elutes with imidazole, a common recombinant-protein purification.

nodes.label: Immobilized ligand binds target specifically
nodes.summary: A ligand (antibody, substrate, receptor) bound to the resin selectively retains only its binding partner.

nodes.label: Column chromatography
nodes.summary: Column chromatography passes mobile phase through a packed column to separate larger sample amounts.

nodes.label: Nonpolar compounds elute first (normal phase)
nodes.summary: On a polar silica column, less polar compounds elute first and polar compounds are retained longer.

nodes.label: Fractions collected over time
nodes.summary: Eluent is collected in sequential fractions that separate the resolved components.

nodes.label: Gradient elution
nodes.summary: Gradually increasing mobile-phase polarity (gradient) elutes increasingly retained, more polar components in sequence.

nodes.label: Gravity and flash column chromatography
nodes.summary: Mobile phase flows down a silica-packed column under gravity or applied pressure (flash) to separate components.

nodes.label: Gas chromatography (GC)
nodes.summary: Gas chromatography separates volatile compounds carried by an inert gas through a column.

nodes.label: GC-MS detector coupling
nodes.summary: Coupling GC to a mass spectrometer (GC-MS) both separates and identifies components by mass spectrum.

nodes.label: Inert gas mobile phase
nodes.summary: An inert carrier gas (helium or nitrogen) transports vaporized analytes through the heated column.

nodes.label: Retention time
nodes.summary: Retention time is the characteristic time for a component to traverse the column and reach the detector.

nodes.label: Used for volatile compounds
nodes.summary: GC requires analytes that are volatile and thermally stable so they can be vaporized into the gas mobile phase.

nodes.label: General chromatographic principles
nodes.summary: Components migrate at different rates based on relative affinity for the stationary versus mobile phase.

nodes.label: Separation by differential affinity
nodes.summary: Components with greater affinity for the stationary phase move more slowly than those favoring the mobile phase.

nodes.label: Elution order from polarity
nodes.summary: In normal-phase chromatography, less polar compounds elute first because they spend more time in the mobile phase.

nodes.label: Normal-phase vs reverse-phase
nodes.summary: Normal phase uses a polar stationary phase (polar compounds retained); reverse phase uses a nonpolar stationary phase (nonpolar compounds retained).

nodes.label: Resolution between components
nodes.summary: Resolution measures how completely two components are separated; better separation gives sharper, more widely spaced bands or peaks.

nodes.label: Reverse-phase elution order
nodes.summary: On a nonpolar (reverse-phase) stationary phase, polar compounds elute first and nonpolar compounds are retained longer.

nodes.label: Stationary vs mobile phase
nodes.summary: All chromatography uses a fixed stationary phase and a moving mobile phase that carries the sample through it.

nodes.label: High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)
nodes.summary: HPLC uses high pressure to push liquid mobile phase through a tightly packed column for high-resolution separation.

nodes.label: High pressure improves resolution
nodes.summary: HPLC forces solvent through a fine-particle column under high pressure, giving fast, high-resolution separations.

nodes.label: Ion-exchange chromatography
nodes.summary: Ion-exchange chromatography separates charged molecules by their attraction to a charged stationary phase.

nodes.label: Cation vs anion exchanger
nodes.summary: A cation-exchange resin carries fixed negative groups (binds positive analytes); an anion-exchange resin carries fixed positive groups (binds negative analytes).

nodes.label: Charged resin binds opposite charges
nodes.summary: A charged column resin retains oppositely charged analytes: cation exchangers bind cations, anion exchangers bind anions.

nodes.label: Elution by salt gradient or pH change
nodes.summary: Bound molecules are released by increasing salt concentration or changing pH to alter their charge.

nodes.label: Paper chromatography
nodes.summary: Paper chromatography separates compounds on cellulose paper as solvent ascends by capillary action.

nodes.label: Cellulose/bound water stationary phase
nodes.summary: The stationary phase is polar (cellulose with adsorbed water), so polar compounds are retained and migrate less, analogous to normal-phase TLC.

nodes.label: Size-exclusion (gel-filtration) chromatography
nodes.summary: Size-exclusion chromatography separates molecules by size using a porous bead matrix.

nodes.label: Porous bead stationary phase
nodes.summary: The stationary phase is a gel of porous beads whose pore size sets the size cutoff for separation.

nodes.label: Large molecules elute first
nodes.summary: Large molecules cannot enter the beads' pores and pass quickly, while small molecules are delayed by entering pores.

nodes.label: Thin-layer chromatography (TLC)
nodes.summary: TLC separates compounds on a thin silica/alumina plate as solvent rises by capillary action.

nodes.label: Co-spotting with known standards
nodes.summary: Spotting an unknown alongside a reference; matching Rf (and a single spot when co-spotted) supports identity.

nodes.label: Polar compounds have lower Rf (normal phase)
nodes.summary: In normal-phase TLC, more polar compounds bind the silica and travel less, giving a smaller Rf.

nodes.label: Retention factor Rf
nodes.summary: Rf = distance traveled by spot ÷ distance traveled by solvent front, a characteristic value under fixed conditions.

nodes.label: TLC plate and developing chamber
nodes.summary: A spotted plate is placed in a sealed chamber so the mobile-phase solvent ascends by capillary action.

nodes.label: Silica/alumina stationary phase is polar
nodes.summary: The polar silica or alumina stationary phase retains polar compounds via hydrogen bonding and dipole interactions.

nodes.label: Increasing mobile-phase polarity raises Rf
nodes.summary: A more polar solvent elutes polar compounds faster, increasing their Rf in normal-phase TLC.

nodes.label: Visualization of TLC spots
nodes.summary: Spots are visualized with UV light, iodine vapor, or chemical stains since many compounds are colorless.

nodes.label: Recrystallization and sublimation
nodes.summary: Crystallization-based methods purify solids using differential solubility and direct solid-vapor transitions.

nodes.label: Recrystallization
nodes.summary: Recrystallization purifies a solid by dissolving it hot and slowly cooling to form pure crystals.

nodes.label: Decolorizing with activated charcoal
nodes.summary: Activated carbon adsorbs colored/highly polar impurities from the hot solution and is removed by hot filtration.

nodes.label: Hot filtration removes insoluble impurities
nodes.summary: Filtering the hot saturated solution removes insoluble impurities before cooling, while keeping the product dissolved.

nodes.label: Impurities excluded from lattice
nodes.summary: As crystals grow, the regular lattice rejects differently shaped/sized impurity molecules.

nodes.label: Mixed-solvent recrystallization
nodes.summary: When no single solvent works, a soluble solvent is paired with a miscible antisolvent to tune solubility for crystal formation.

nodes.label: Seeding/scratching induces nucleation
nodes.summary: Adding a seed crystal or scratching the flask provides nucleation sites that initiate crystallization from a supersaturated solution.

nodes.label: Slow cooling yields pure crystals
nodes.summary: Slow cooling forms an ordered crystal lattice that excludes impurities, which remain dissolved in the cold solvent.

nodes.label: Solubility increases with temperature
nodes.summary: The compound is dissolved in minimal hot solvent in which its solubility rises sharply with temperature.

nodes.label: Ideal recrystallization solvent
nodes.summary: A good solvent dissolves the compound when hot but not when cold and keeps impurities soluble at all temperatures.

nodes.label: Sublimation
nodes.summary: Sublimation purifies a solid that converts directly to vapor and re-deposits without melting.

nodes.label: Direct solid-to-vapor transition
nodes.summary: A sublimable solid passes directly to the gas phase and re-condenses on a cold surface, leaving nonvolatile impurities.

nodes.label: Distillation
nodes.summary: Distillation separates liquids by differences in boiling point through vaporization and selective condensation.

nodes.label: Azeotropes
nodes.summary: An azeotrope is a mixture that distills at constant composition and cannot be further separated by ordinary distillation.

nodes.label: Constant-boiling mixture
nodes.summary: At the azeotrope, vapor and liquid have identical compositions, so distillation cannot enrich either component further.

nodes.label: Minimum- vs maximum-boiling azeotropes
nodes.summary: Positive deviation from Raoult's law gives a minimum-boiling azeotrope; negative deviation gives a maximum-boiling azeotrope.

nodes.label: Ethanol-water azeotrope (95% EtOH)
nodes.summary: Ethanol-water forms a ~95.6% ethanol azeotrope that ordinary distillation cannot exceed.

nodes.label: Fractional distillation
nodes.summary: A fractionating column enables separation of liquids with close boiling points.

nodes.label: Used for close boiling points
nodes.summary: Fractional distillation separates liquids whose boiling points differ by less than ~25 °C.

nodes.label: Fractionating column provides many vaporization-condensation cycles
nodes.summary: The packed column creates repeated vaporization-condensation equilibria (theoretical plates) that progressively enrich the vapor.

nodes.label: Industrial fractionation of crude oil
nodes.summary: Crude oil is separated into fractions (gas, gasoline, kerosene, diesel) by boiling-point range in a fractionating tower.

nodes.label: Theoretical plates
nodes.summary: Each effective vaporization-condensation cycle is a theoretical plate; more plates give better separation.

nodes.label: Principles of distillation
nodes.summary: A liquid is heated to its boiling point, vaporized, and condensed back to a purer liquid.

nodes.label: Boiling chips prevent bumping
nodes.summary: Boiling chips (or stir bars) provide nucleation sites for smooth boiling and prevent superheating and violent bumping.

nodes.label: Raoult's law for ideal mixtures
nodes.summary: Each component's partial vapor pressure equals its mole fraction times its pure vapor pressure (P_A = X_A·P°_A).

nodes.label: Vapor is enriched in the volatile component
nodes.summary: The vapor above a boiling mixture is richer in the lower-boiling (more volatile) component.

nodes.label: Boiling point set by vapor pressure
nodes.summary: A liquid boils when its vapor pressure equals the external (atmospheric) pressure.

nodes.label: Rotary evaporation
nodes.summary: A rotovap removes volatile solvent from a solution under reduced pressure with a rotating heated flask.

nodes.label: Gentle solvent removal under vacuum
nodes.summary: Reduced pressure and a warm rotating flask evaporate solvent quickly at low temperature, concentrating a dissolved product.

nodes.label: Simple distillation
nodes.summary: Simple distillation separates liquids whose boiling points differ widely (roughly > 25 °C).

nodes.label: Simple distillation apparatus
nodes.summary: The setup uses a heated flask, thermometer at the side-arm, condenser, and collection (receiving) flask.

nodes.label: Used for large boiling-point differences
nodes.summary: Simple distillation is effective only when components' boiling points differ by roughly 25 °C or more.

nodes.label: Condenser cools vapor to liquid
nodes.summary: A water-cooled condenser removes heat so vapor condenses back to liquid distillate flowing into the receiving flask.

nodes.label: Thermometer at the still-head
nodes.summary: The thermometer bulb is positioned at the side-arm opening to read the temperature of the vapor being collected.

nodes.label: Steam distillation
nodes.summary: Steam distillation co-distills water-immiscible volatile compounds below 100 °C, used for temperature-sensitive natural products.

nodes.label: Isolating essential oils / natural products
nodes.summary: Steam distillation isolates fragrant, heat-sensitive, water-immiscible compounds (e.g. essential oils) that would decompose at their normal boiling point.

nodes.label: Immiscible vapor pressures add (Dalton)
nodes.summary: For two immiscible liquids each exerts its full vapor pressure independently, so the mixture boils when the sum equals atmospheric pressure—below either pure boiling point.

nodes.label: Vacuum distillation
nodes.summary: Reduced pressure lowers boiling points, allowing distillation of high-boiling or heat-sensitive compounds.

nodes.label: Used for high-boiling/heat-sensitive compounds
nodes.summary: Vacuum distillation is chosen when a compound boils above ~150 °C or decomposes near its normal boiling point.

nodes.label: Lowering pressure lowers boiling point
nodes.summary: Reducing external pressure lets a liquid boil at a lower temperature, avoiding thermal decomposition.

nodes.label: Electrophoresis
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis separates charged macromolecules in a gel under an electric field by size and charge.

nodes.label: 2D gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: 2D electrophoresis combines IEF then SDS-PAGE to resolve complex protein mixtures by pI and size.

nodes.label: IEF first dimension, SDS-PAGE second
nodes.summary: Proteins are separated by pI in the first dimension, then by molecular weight in the perpendicular second dimension.

nodes.label: Agarose gel for nucleic acids
nodes.summary: DNA/RNA fragments separate by size on an agarose gel since the phosphate backbone gives uniform negative charge.

nodes.label: Molecular-weight ladder/markers
nodes.summary: A reference ladder of known-size fragments run alongside the sample lets unknown band sizes be estimated.

nodes.label: Smaller fragments travel farther
nodes.summary: Shorter DNA fragments move through the gel pores faster and migrate farther from the wells.

nodes.label: Uniform negative charge of nucleic acids
nodes.summary: The phosphate backbone gives DNA a constant charge-to-mass ratio, so separation depends only on size.

nodes.label: Isoelectric focusing (IEF)
nodes.summary: Isoelectric focusing separates proteins by their isoelectric point along a pH gradient.

nodes.label: Net charge zero at pI
nodes.summary: At its pI a protein carries no net charge, so the electric field no longer drives its migration.

nodes.label: Proteins migrate to their pI
nodes.summary: In a pH gradient each protein migrates until it reaches the pH equal to its isoelectric point, where its net charge is zero and it stops.

nodes.label: Native PAGE
nodes.summary: Native PAGE separates proteins in their folded state by size, shape, and native charge together.

nodes.label: Proteins remain folded
nodes.summary: Without SDS, proteins keep their native conformation and charge, so migration reflects size, shape, and charge.

nodes.label: Principles of gel electrophoresis
nodes.summary: An electric field drives charged molecules through a sieving gel matrix toward an oppositely charged electrode.

nodes.label: Running buffer maintains pH and conducts current
nodes.summary: The buffer holds a constant pH (fixing analyte charge) and carries the current between electrodes during the run.

nodes.label: Mobility depends on charge-to-mass ratio
nodes.summary: Electrophoretic mobility increases with charge and decreases with size (frictional drag).

nodes.label: Migration toward opposite electrode
nodes.summary: Negatively charged molecules migrate toward the positive anode and positively charged molecules toward the negative cathode.

nodes.label: Gel sieves by size
nodes.summary: The gel matrix retards larger molecules, so for equal charge density smaller molecules migrate farther.

nodes.label: SDS-PAGE for proteins
nodes.summary: SDS-PAGE separates denatured proteins by size by masking their intrinsic charge with SDS.

nodes.label: Reducing agent breaks disulfide bonds
nodes.summary: A reducing agent (e.g. β-mercaptoethanol or DTT) cleaves disulfide bonds to fully linearize subunits.

nodes.label: SDS denatures and coats proteins
nodes.summary: The detergent SDS denatures proteins and coats them with negative charge proportional to length, masking native charge.

nodes.label: Separation by size alone
nodes.summary: Because SDS gives a uniform charge-to-mass ratio, proteins separate purely by molecular weight (smaller = farther).

nodes.label: Protein staining (Coomassie/silver)
nodes.summary: Separated protein bands are visualized by Coomassie blue or more sensitive silver staining after the run.

nodes.label: Extraction
nodes.summary: Extraction separates compounds based on differential solubility between two immiscible liquid phases.

nodes.label: Acid-base extraction
nodes.summary: Adjusting pH converts compounds between neutral (organic-soluble) and ionic (water-soluble) forms to separate them.

nodes.label: Basic wash extracts carboxylic acids/phenols
nodes.summary: Washing with aqueous base (e.g. NaHCO3 or NaOH) deprotonates carboxylic acids/phenols to water-soluble salts.

nodes.label: Acidic wash extracts amines
nodes.summary: Washing with aqueous acid (e.g. HCl) protonates an amine to its ammonium salt, pulling it into the aqueous layer.

nodes.label: Back-extraction by re-neutralization
nodes.summary: Re-adjusting pH reconverts a salt to the neutral compound so it can be extracted back into fresh organic solvent.

nodes.label: NaHCO3 separates carboxylic acids from phenols
nodes.summary: Weak base sodium bicarbonate deprotonates strong carboxylic acids but not weaker phenols, allowing their separation.

nodes.label: Charged species partition into water
nodes.summary: A charged (ionized) compound preferentially dissolves in the aqueous layer.

nodes.label: Neutral species partition into organic
nodes.summary: A neutral (uncharged) organic compound preferentially dissolves in the organic layer.

nodes.label: pKa vs pH determines ionization
nodes.summary: A group is mostly ionized when the pH is on the charged side of its pKa (Henderson-Hasselbalch), setting which layer it enters.

nodes.label: pH controls protonation state
nodes.summary: Acids and bases are deprotonated/protonated by adjusting pH, switching their solubility between phases.

nodes.label: Drying agents
nodes.summary: Anhydrous salts remove residual water from an organic layer after extraction.

nodes.label: Free-flowing solid signals dryness
nodes.summary: Enough drying agent has been added when the solid swirls freely rather than clumping; it is then removed by filtration before solvent evaporation.

nodes.label: Anhydrous salts remove trace water
nodes.summary: Drying agents such as MgSO4 or Na2SO4 absorb dissolved water from an organic solution.

nodes.label: Liquid-liquid extraction
nodes.summary: A solute is partitioned between an aqueous and an immiscible organic solvent in a separatory funnel.

nodes.label: Common organic extraction solvents
nodes.summary: Diethyl ether and ethyl acetate are low-density solvents; dichloromethane and chloroform are dense, halogenated solvents.

nodes.label: Emulsion formation
nodes.summary: Vigorous shaking can form an emulsion (suspended droplets) that prevents clean layer separation.

nodes.label: Immiscible solvent pair
nodes.summary: Extraction requires two solvents that do not mix, typically water and an organic solvent.

nodes.label: Identifying organic vs aqueous layer by density
nodes.summary: The denser solvent forms the bottom layer; ether floats on water while chloroform/DCM sinks below it.

nodes.label: Multiple small extractions outperform one large
nodes.summary: Several extractions with small solvent portions recover more solute than a single extraction with the same total volume.

nodes.label: Solute partitioning during extraction
nodes.summary: A solute moves into the layer for which it has greater affinity, governed by the partition coefficient.

nodes.label: Salting out
nodes.summary: Adding salt (e.g. NaCl) to the aqueous layer lowers organic-solute solubility in water, driving it into the organic layer and breaking emulsions.

nodes.label: Separatory funnel technique
nodes.summary: Two immiscible layers are shaken, vented, allowed to separate, and the desired layer is drained off.

nodes.label: Venting to release pressure
nodes.summary: Volatile solvents and gas-producing reactions build pressure that must be vented during extraction.

nodes.label: Washing vs extracting
nodes.summary: Extraction removes the desired product into a layer; washing removes unwanted impurities into the discarded layer.

nodes.label: Solid-liquid extraction
nodes.summary: A soluble component is dissolved out of a solid mixture by a chosen solvent.

nodes.label: Selective dissolution from a solid
nodes.summary: A solvent dissolves the desired component while leaving insoluble solids behind (e.g. brewing coffee).

nodes.label: Continuous (Soxhlet) extraction
nodes.summary: A Soxhlet apparatus repeatedly recycles fresh distilled solvent through a solid sample for exhaustive extraction with little solvent.

nodes.label: Filtration and centrifugation
nodes.summary: These methods separate components based on physical phase boundaries, particle size, or density.

nodes.label: Centrifugation
nodes.summary: Centrifugation separates components by density using strong centrifugal force.

nodes.label: Separation by density and mass
nodes.summary: Spinning sediments denser/larger particles to the bottom (pellet) while less dense material stays in the supernatant.

nodes.label: Density-gradient centrifugation
nodes.summary: Components band at positions matching their buoyant density within a preformed density gradient (e.g. sucrose, CsCl).

nodes.label: Differential centrifugation
nodes.summary: Successive spins at increasing speed pellet progressively smaller/lighter organelles, fractionating a cell lysate.

nodes.label: Pellet vs supernatant
nodes.summary: The sedimented solid is the pellet and the overlying liquid is the supernatant.

nodes.label: Ultracentrifugation and the Svedberg unit
nodes.summary: Very high-speed ultracentrifugation separates macromolecules by sedimentation coefficient (S, Svedberg), which depends on size, shape, and density.

nodes.label: Dialysis
nodes.summary: Dialysis separates small molecules from large ones across a semipermeable membrane by diffusion.

nodes.label: Semipermeable membrane size cutoff
nodes.summary: Small solutes diffuse across the membrane down their concentration gradient while large molecules are retained.

nodes.label: Filtration
nodes.summary: Filtration separates a solid from a liquid by passing the mixture through a porous barrier.

nodes.label: Gravity filtration
nodes.summary: Gravity filtration through filter paper is used when the desired product is the liquid (filtrate).

nodes.label: Residue vs filtrate
nodes.summary: The solid retained on the filter is the residue; the liquid that passes through is the filtrate.

nodes.label: Vacuum (suction) filtration
nodes.summary: Vacuum filtration through a Büchner funnel speeds collection and is used when the desired product is the solid.

nodes.label: General separation principles
nodes.summary: Separations exploit differences in physical/chemical properties such as polarity, size, charge, volatility, and solubility to isolate components of a mixture.

nodes.label: Property-based separation
nodes.summary: Each technique separates compounds by a specific distinguishing property.

nodes.label: Charge differences
nodes.summary: Electrophoresis and ion-exchange chromatography separate species according to net charge.

nodes.label: Density differences
nodes.summary: Centrifugation and layer identification in extraction rely on differences in density/buoyancy.

nodes.label: Partitioning between phases
nodes.summary: A solute distributes between two phases according to its relative affinity, the foundation of extraction and chromatography.

nodes.label: Polarity differences
nodes.summary: Many separations (extraction, chromatography) rely on differences in molecular polarity governing affinity for phases.

nodes.label: Molecular size/weight differences
nodes.summary: Size-based methods (size-exclusion chromatography, SDS-PAGE, dialysis) separate by molecular dimensions or mass.

nodes.label: Solubility differences
nodes.summary: Recrystallization, extraction, and precipitation exploit differential solubility in chosen solvents.

nodes.label: Volatility/boiling point differences
nodes.summary: Distillation separates components by differences in vapor pressure and boiling point.

nodes.label: Distribution (partition) coefficient
nodes.summary: The partition coefficient quantifies how a solute distributes between two immiscible solvents at equilibrium.

nodes.label: Calculating amount extracted from K
nodes.summary: Given K and phase volumes, the fraction of solute remaining after one or more extractions can be calculated from the equilibrium distribution.

nodes.label: Partition coefficient K = [solute]organic/[solute]aqueous
nodes.summary: K equals the equilibrium concentration ratio of solute in the two phases and is temperature-dependent.

nodes.label: Like-dissolves-like rule
nodes.summary: Nonpolar solutes favor nonpolar (organic) solvents while polar/ionic solutes favor polar (aqueous) solvents.

nodes.label: Assessing purity
nodes.summary: Purity is judged by criteria such as a sharp narrow melting range, a single spot, or a single peak.

nodes.label: Narrow melting range as purity test
nodes.summary: A pure crystalline solid melts over a narrow temperature range, while impurities broaden and lower the melting range.

nodes.label: Mixed melting point identification
nodes.summary: Mixing an unknown with a known authentic sample depresses and broadens the melting point unless they are identical, confirming identity.

nodes.label: Percent recovery / yield
nodes.summary: Percent recovery = (mass recovered ÷ mass started) × 100; purification trades yield for purity, so recovery is below 100%.

nodes.label: Single chromatographic spot/peak
nodes.summary: A single TLC spot or single chromatographic peak across conditions is evidence of purity.

nodes.label: Amino acids
nodes.summary: Amino acids are the monomeric building blocks of proteins, each with an amino group, carboxyl group, and a distinguishing side chain on a central alpha carbon.

nodes.label: Acid-base behavior
nodes.summary: Amino acids are polyprotic and their charge state depends on pH relative to their pKa values.

nodes.label: Histidine physiological buffering
nodes.summary: Histidine's imidazole pKa near 6 makes it a key buffer and catalytic residue at physiological pH.

nodes.label: Isoelectric point (pI)
nodes.summary: The pI is the pH at which an amino acid carries no net charge, calculated by averaging the two pKa values flanking the neutral species.

nodes.label: Amino pKa (~9-10)
nodes.summary: The alpha-amino group has a pKa around 9-10 and is protonated (positively charged) below that pH.

nodes.label: Carboxyl pKa (~2)
nodes.summary: The alpha-carboxyl group has a pKa around 2 and is deprotonated above that pH.

nodes.label: Ionizable side-chain pKa values
nodes.summary: Asp/Glu (~4), His (~6), Cys (~8), Tyr (~10), Lys (~10.5), and Arg (~12) carry ionizable side chains with characteristic pKa values.

nodes.label: Amino acid titration curves
nodes.summary: Titration of an amino acid shows buffering plateaus near each pKa and equivalence points between them.

nodes.label: Zwitterion
nodes.summary: At intermediate pH an amino acid exists as a zwitterion with a protonated amino group and deprotonated carboxyl group, net charge zero.

nodes.label: Amino acid and peptide analysis
nodes.summary: Amino acids and peptides are separated by charge and size and sequenced by chemical degradation.

nodes.label: Ion-exchange chromatography
nodes.summary: Ion-exchange and other chromatography separate amino acids by charge and polarity for analysis.

nodes.label: Edman degradation / sequencing
nodes.summary: Edman degradation removes and identifies N-terminal residues one at a time to determine peptide sequence.

nodes.label: Electrophoretic separation by charge
nodes.summary: At a chosen pH, amino acids and peptides migrate in an electric field according to net charge relative to their pI.

nodes.label: Peptide bond hydrolysis
nodes.summary: Acid or enzymatic hydrolysis cleaves peptide bonds (adding water) to break a protein into its component amino acids.

nodes.label: Classification of side chains
nodes.summary: The 20 standard amino acids are grouped by side-chain polarity, charge, and chemistry.

nodes.label: Acidic side chains
nodes.summary: Aspartate and glutamate have carboxylate side chains that are negatively charged at physiological pH.

nodes.label: Amphoteric nature
nodes.summary: Amino acids are amphoteric: the carboxyl can donate a proton (acid) and the amino group can accept one (base), so they act as both.

nodes.label: Aromatic side chains
nodes.summary: Phenylalanine, tyrosine, and tryptophan have aromatic rings and absorb UV light around 280 nm.

nodes.label: Basic side chains
nodes.summary: Lysine, arginine, and histidine have basic side chains that can be positively charged at physiological pH.

nodes.label: Essential vs nonessential
nodes.summary: Essential amino acids cannot be synthesized by humans and must be obtained from the diet.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic vs hydrophilic R groups
nodes.summary: Nonpolar/aromatic side chains are hydrophobic and bury inside; polar and charged side chains are hydrophilic and prefer the aqueous surface.

nodes.label: Nonpolar (hydrophobic) side chains
nodes.summary: Nonpolar amino acids (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Met, Trp) bury in the protein interior to avoid water.

nodes.label: Polar uncharged side chains
nodes.summary: Polar uncharged amino acids (Ser, Thr, Asn, Gln, Tyr, Cys) form hydrogen bonds and often sit on the protein surface.

nodes.label: Proline (cyclic imino acid)
nodes.summary: Proline's side chain loops back to the backbone nitrogen, forming a rigid ring that disrupts secondary structure.

nodes.label: Sulfur-containing amino acids
nodes.summary: Cysteine and methionine contain sulfur; cysteine's thiol can form disulfide bonds.

nodes.label: UV absorbance at 280 nm
nodes.summary: Aromatic tryptophan and tyrosine absorb at ~280 nm, the basis for spectrophotometric protein quantification.

nodes.label: Amino acid reactions
nodes.summary: Amino acids undergo condensation, oxidation, and characteristic detection reactions.

nodes.label: Disulfide bond formation
nodes.summary: Two cysteine thiols are oxidized to form a covalent disulfide bond (cystine) that stabilizes tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Ninhydrin reaction
nodes.summary: Ninhydrin reacts with free alpha-amino groups to give a purple (Ruhemann's) color, used to visualize amino acids on chromatograms.

nodes.label: Peptide (amide) bond formation
nodes.summary: A peptide bond forms by condensation between the carboxyl of one amino acid and the amino group of another, releasing water.

nodes.label: Strecker and Gabriel syntheses
nodes.summary: The Strecker synthesis (aldehyde + ammonia + cyanide) and Gabriel synthesis are classic routes to amino acids.

nodes.label: General amino acid structure
nodes.summary: A standard amino acid has a central alpha carbon bonded to an amino group, a carboxyl group, a hydrogen, and an R side chain.

nodes.label: D/L vs R/S notation
nodes.summary: L-amino acids are S-configured at the alpha carbon except for cysteine, which is R because sulfur changes priority.

nodes.label: Alpha carbon center
nodes.summary: The alpha carbon is the central carbon bearing the amino group, carboxyl group, hydrogen, and R group.

nodes.label: Chirality of amino acids
nodes.summary: All amino acids except glycine are chiral because the alpha carbon has four different substituents.

nodes.label: Four substituent groups
nodes.summary: Every standard amino acid has an amino group, a carboxyl group, an H, and a variable R group bonded to the alpha carbon.

nodes.label: Glycine is achiral
nodes.summary: Glycine is the only standard amino acid that is achiral because its R group is a hydrogen.

nodes.label: L-amino acids in proteins
nodes.summary: Eukaryotic proteins are built almost exclusively from L-amino acids (S configuration except cysteine).

nodes.label: Carbohydrates
nodes.summary: Carbohydrates are polyhydroxy aldehydes or ketones that serve as energy stores and structural materials.

nodes.label: Di- and polysaccharides
nodes.summary: Glycosidic bonds link monosaccharides into disaccharides and polysaccharides.

nodes.label: Amino sugars (N-acetylglucosamine)
nodes.summary: Amino sugars replace a hydroxyl with an amino/acetamido group and build polymers like chitin and peptidoglycan.

nodes.label: Cellulose
nodes.summary: Cellulose is a structural polymer of β1,4-linked glucose that humans cannot digest.

nodes.label: Chitin
nodes.summary: Chitin is a structural polysaccharide of β1,4-linked N-acetylglucosamine in arthropod exoskeletons and fungal walls.

nodes.label: Maltose, lactose, sucrose
nodes.summary: Maltose (Glc-α1,4-Glc), lactose (Gal-β1,4-Glc), and sucrose (Glc-α1,2-β-Fru) are the key disaccharides; sucrose is nonreducing.

nodes.label: Glycoproteins and proteoglycans
nodes.summary: Carbohydrates attach to proteins (glycoproteins, proteoglycans) and lipids, functioning in cell recognition and the extracellular matrix.

nodes.label: Glycogen
nodes.summary: Glycogen is the animal energy-storage polysaccharide, highly branched with α1,4 and frequent α1,6 linkages.

nodes.label: Glycosidic linkages
nodes.summary: Glycosidic bonds join sugars via the anomeric carbon, specified by the carbons linked and alpha/beta configuration.

nodes.label: Hydrolysis of glycosidic bonds
nodes.summary: Glycosidic bonds are cleaved by acid or specific glycosidase enzymes to release monosaccharides.

nodes.label: Starch (amylose/amylopectin)
nodes.summary: Starch stores energy in plants as amylose (α1,4-linked) and branched amylopectin (α1,4 with α1,6 branches).

nodes.label: Cyclic forms & anomers
nodes.summary: In solution sugars cyclize into rings creating new stereochemistry at the anomeric carbon.

nodes.label: Alpha and beta anomers
nodes.summary: In the alpha anomer the anomeric OH is trans (axial) to the CH2OH reference; in beta it is cis (equatorial).

nodes.label: Anomeric carbon
nodes.summary: The anomeric carbon is the former carbonyl carbon that becomes a new stereocenter upon cyclization.

nodes.label: Chair conformation of pyranoses
nodes.summary: Pyranoses adopt chair conformations; beta-D-glucose places all bulky groups equatorial, the most stable arrangement.

nodes.label: Haworth projections
nodes.summary: Haworth projections depict the cyclic sugar ring with substituents above or below the plane.

nodes.label: Mutarotation
nodes.summary: Mutarotation is the interconversion of alpha and beta anomers through the open-chain form, changing optical rotation.

nodes.label: Pyranose and furanose rings
nodes.summary: Six-membered sugar rings are pyranoses and five-membered rings are furanoses.

nodes.label: Hemiacetal/hemiketal cyclization
nodes.summary: An intramolecular reaction of the carbonyl with a hydroxyl forms a cyclic hemiacetal (aldose) or hemiketal (ketose).

nodes.label: Monosaccharides
nodes.summary: Monosaccharides are single sugar units classified by carbon number and carbonyl type.

nodes.label: Aldose vs ketose
nodes.summary: An aldose has a terminal aldehyde while a ketose has an internal ketone carbonyl.

nodes.label: Anomers vs epimers vs enantiomers
nodes.summary: Enantiomers are mirror images, epimers differ at one non-anomeric carbon, and anomers differ specifically at the anomeric carbon.

nodes.label: Glucose, fructose, galactose, ribose
nodes.summary: Glucose, fructose, and galactose are common hexoses; ribose and deoxyribose are biologically key pentoses.

nodes.label: D and L sugars
nodes.summary: A sugar is D or L based on the configuration of the highest-numbered chiral carbon in the Fischer projection.

nodes.label: Fischer projections of sugars
nodes.summary: Fischer projections draw the open-chain sugar vertically with the carbonyl near the top, used to assign D/L and identify epimers.

nodes.label: Triose, pentose, hexose
nodes.summary: Monosaccharides are named by carbon count: triose (3), pentose (5, e.g., ribose), hexose (6, e.g., glucose).

nodes.label: Stereoisomers and epimers
nodes.summary: Sugars with n chiral centers have 2^n stereoisomers; epimers differ at exactly one chiral carbon.

nodes.label: Monosaccharide reactions
nodes.summary: Sugars undergo oxidation, reduction, esterification, and glycoside formation.

nodes.label: Esterification (phosphorylation)
nodes.summary: Sugar hydroxyls can be esterified, e.g., phosphorylation of glucose to glucose-6-phosphate.

nodes.label: Glycoside formation
nodes.summary: Reaction of the anomeric OH with an alcohol forms a glycosidic bond (acetal), locking the anomeric configuration.

nodes.label: Oxidation to acids
nodes.summary: Oxidation of the aldehyde gives an aldonic acid; oxidation of the terminal CH2OH or both ends gives uronic/aldaric acids.

nodes.label: Reducing sugars
nodes.summary: A reducing sugar has a free anomeric carbon (hemiacetal) that can open and reduce Tollens' or Benedict's reagent.

nodes.label: Reduction to alditols
nodes.summary: Reduction of a sugar's carbonyl yields a sugar alcohol (alditol), such as glucose to sorbitol.

nodes.label: Tollens' and Benedict's tests
nodes.summary: Tollens' (silver mirror) and Benedict's (brick-red Cu2O) tests detect reducing sugars by oxidation of the aldehyde.

nodes.label: Enzymes & catalysis
nodes.summary: Enzymes are biological catalysts, mostly proteins, that accelerate reactions by lowering activation energy.

nodes.label: Catalytic principles
nodes.summary: Enzymes speed reactions without being consumed or altering the equilibrium position.

nodes.label: Active site
nodes.summary: The active site is the pocket where substrate binds and catalysis occurs, formed by specific residues.

nodes.label: Induced-fit model
nodes.summary: The induced-fit model holds that substrate binding induces a conformational change that optimizes the active site.

nodes.label: Local active-site environment
nodes.summary: Active sites create microenvironments (excluded water, altered pKa, proximity/orientation) that promote catalysis.

nodes.label: Lock-and-key model
nodes.summary: The lock-and-key model proposes a rigid active site precisely complementary to the substrate shape.

nodes.label: Lowering activation energy
nodes.summary: Enzymes increase reaction rate by lowering the activation energy via stabilization of the transition state.

nodes.label: Equilibrium and ΔG unchanged
nodes.summary: Enzymes do not change the equilibrium constant or overall free-energy change; they speed both forward and reverse rates equally.

nodes.label: Ribozymes (RNA catalysts)
nodes.summary: Not all enzymes are proteins: ribozymes are catalytic RNA molecules, such as the peptidyl transferase of the ribosome.

nodes.label: Substrate specificity
nodes.summary: Enzymes are highly specific, recognizing particular substrates through complementary active-site geometry and chemistry.

nodes.label: Transition-state stabilization
nodes.summary: Enzymes bind and stabilize the high-energy transition state more than substrate, which is the molecular basis for lowering activation energy.

nodes.label: Enzyme classification
nodes.summary: Enzymes are grouped into six functional classes by the type of reaction catalyzed.

nodes.label: Hydrolases
nodes.summary: Hydrolases cleave bonds by adding water (e.g., proteases, lipases, phosphatases).

nodes.label: Isomerases
nodes.summary: Isomerases rearrange atoms within a molecule to form isomers.

nodes.label: Ligases
nodes.summary: Ligases join two molecules together using energy from ATP hydrolysis (e.g., DNA ligase).

nodes.label: Lyases
nodes.summary: Lyases break bonds without hydrolysis or oxidation, often forming double bonds or rings (e.g., decarboxylases).

nodes.label: Oxidoreductases
nodes.summary: Oxidoreductases catalyze redox reactions, transferring electrons between molecules (e.g., dehydrogenases).

nodes.label: Transferases
nodes.summary: Transferases move a functional group from one molecule to another (e.g., kinases transfer phosphate).

nodes.label: Cofactors and coenzymes
nodes.summary: Many enzymes require non-protein helpers for activity.

nodes.label: Apoenzyme vs holoenzyme
nodes.summary: An apoenzyme lacks its cofactor and is inactive; the complete, active form with cofactor is the holoenzyme.

nodes.label: Coenzymes (organic)
nodes.summary: Coenzymes are small organic molecules, often vitamin-derived, that assist catalysis (e.g., NAD+, FAD, CoA).

nodes.label: Cofactors (metal ions)
nodes.summary: Cofactors are inorganic ions (e.g., Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+) required for catalytic activity.

nodes.label: Prosthetic groups
nodes.summary: A prosthetic group is a tightly (often covalently) bound cofactor that stays associated with the enzyme.

nodes.label: Enzyme inhibition
nodes.summary: Inhibitors decrease enzyme activity by reversible or irreversible mechanisms.

nodes.label: Competitive inhibition
nodes.summary: Competitive inhibitors bind the active site, raising apparent Km while leaving Vmax unchanged.

nodes.label: Irreversible inhibition
nodes.summary: Irreversible inhibitors form covalent or very tight bonds that permanently inactivate the enzyme.

nodes.label: Mixed inhibition
nodes.summary: Mixed inhibitors bind E and ES with different affinities, altering both Km and Vmax.

nodes.label: Noncompetitive inhibition
nodes.summary: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind an allosteric site of E or ES equally, lowering Vmax with Km unchanged.

nodes.label: Uncompetitive inhibition
nodes.summary: Uncompetitive inhibitors bind only the ES complex, lowering both Vmax and Km.

nodes.label: Enzyme kinetics
nodes.summary: Michaelis-Menten kinetics describes how reaction velocity depends on substrate concentration.

nodes.label: Cooperative (sigmoidal) kinetics
nodes.summary: Allosteric multi-subunit enzymes show sigmoidal velocity curves instead of hyperbolic Michaelis-Menten behavior.

nodes.label: kcat and catalytic efficiency
nodes.summary: kcat (turnover number) is the number of substrate molecules converted per enzyme per second; kcat/Km measures catalytic efficiency.

nodes.label: Km (Michaelis constant)
nodes.summary: Km is the substrate concentration at half-Vmax and inversely reflects substrate affinity.

nodes.label: Lineweaver-Burk plot
nodes.summary: The double-reciprocal plot linearizes Michaelis-Menten data, giving 1/Vmax as the y-intercept and -1/Km as the x-intercept.

nodes.label: Michaelis-Menten equation
nodes.summary: v = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]) relates initial velocity to substrate concentration for a hyperbolic kinetic curve.

nodes.label: Vmax
nodes.summary: Vmax is the maximum velocity reached when the enzyme is saturated with substrate.

nodes.label: Enzyme regulation
nodes.summary: Enzyme activity is controlled by allostery, covalent modification, zymogens, and environment.

nodes.label: Allosteric regulation
nodes.summary: Allosteric effectors bind sites distinct from the active site to activate or inhibit the enzyme.

nodes.label: Covalent modification
nodes.summary: Reversible phosphorylation, methylation, or other modifications switch enzyme activity on or off.

nodes.label: Feedback inhibition
nodes.summary: A pathway's end product inhibits an upstream enzyme to prevent overproduction.

nodes.label: pH and temperature optima
nodes.summary: Each enzyme has optimal pH and temperature; extremes denature it and reduce activity.

nodes.label: Zymogens (proenzymes)
nodes.summary: Zymogens are inactive precursors activated by irreversible proteolytic cleavage (e.g., trypsinogen to trypsin).

nodes.label: Lipids
nodes.summary: Lipids are hydrophobic biomolecules that store energy, form membranes, and act as signals.

nodes.label: Lipid classification
nodes.summary: Lipids are grouped by structure and role into storage, structural, and signaling classes.

nodes.label: Defining feature (hydrophobicity)
nodes.summary: Lipids are defined by hydrophobicity/insolubility in water rather than a shared structural backbone.

nodes.label: Storage vs structural vs signaling
nodes.summary: Triacylglycerols store energy, phospholipids and sterols build membranes, and steroids/eicosanoids signal.

nodes.label: Fatty acids
nodes.summary: Fatty acids are long hydrocarbon chains with a terminal carboxylic acid.

nodes.label: Amphipathic character
nodes.summary: Fatty acids are amphipathic, with a hydrophilic carboxyl head and a hydrophobic tail, forming micelles.

nodes.label: Cis vs trans double bonds
nodes.summary: Naturally occurring cis double bonds kink the chain and disrupt packing; trans fats pack tightly like saturated fats.

nodes.label: Chain length & melting point
nodes.summary: Melting point rises with chain length and saturation due to stronger van der Waals packing.

nodes.label: Micelle formation
nodes.summary: In water, single-tailed amphipaths cluster into spherical micelles with tails inward and polar heads outward.

nodes.label: Saturated vs unsaturated
nodes.summary: Saturated fatty acids have no C=C double bonds; unsaturated ones contain one or more, lowering melting point.

nodes.label: Soaps and emulsification
nodes.summary: Fatty acid salts (soaps) emulsify nonpolar grease by surrounding it in micelles with polar heads facing water.

nodes.label: Fatty acid structure
nodes.summary: A fatty acid is a carboxylic acid with a long, mostly saturated or unsaturated hydrocarbon tail.

nodes.label: Membrane lipids
nodes.summary: Phospholipids, glycolipids, and sterols build cell membranes.

nodes.label: Lipid bilayer / amphipathicity
nodes.summary: Amphipathic phospholipids self-assemble into bilayers with hydrophobic tails inward and polar heads facing water.

nodes.label: Cholesterol in membranes
nodes.summary: Cholesterol modulates membrane fluidity, buffering it against temperature changes.

nodes.label: Membrane fluidity factors
nodes.summary: Fluidity increases with shorter, more unsaturated (cis) tails and is modulated by cholesterol and temperature.

nodes.label: Glycolipids
nodes.summary: Glycolipids carry carbohydrate head groups on the membrane exterior and participate in cell recognition.

nodes.label: Phosphoglycerides
nodes.summary: Phosphoglycerides (e.g., phosphatidylcholine) are glycerol-based phospholipids that dominate membranes.

nodes.label: Phospholipids
nodes.summary: Phospholipids have a glycerol or sphingosine backbone, two fatty-acid tails, and a polar phosphate head group.

nodes.label: Sphingolipids
nodes.summary: Sphingolipids are built on a sphingosine backbone and include sphingomyelin and glycosphingolipids.

nodes.label: Signaling & vitamin lipids
nodes.summary: Some lipids act as hormones, second messengers, or fat-soluble vitamins.

nodes.label: Fat-soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K)
nodes.summary: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are lipid-soluble and stored in body fat.

nodes.label: Prostaglandins / eicosanoids
nodes.summary: Eicosanoids such as prostaglandins are arachidonic-acid-derived local signaling lipids in inflammation.

nodes.label: Lipid second messengers
nodes.summary: Membrane-derived lipids like IP3 and DAG act as intracellular second messengers.

nodes.label: Steroids & terpenes
nodes.summary: Steroids and terpenes are isoprenoid-derived lipids with diverse signaling roles.

nodes.label: Cholesterol as precursor
nodes.summary: Cholesterol is the precursor for steroid hormones, bile acids, and vitamin D.

nodes.label: Steroid hormones
nodes.summary: Steroid hormones (e.g., cortisol, estrogen, testosterone) are lipid-soluble signals derived from cholesterol.

nodes.label: Steroid ring system
nodes.summary: Steroids share a fused four-ring (three six-membered, one five-membered) cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene core.

nodes.label: Terpenes and terpenoids
nodes.summary: Terpenes are built from five-carbon isoprene units and include fat-soluble vitamins and many natural products.

nodes.label: Triacylglycerols & waxes
nodes.summary: Storage lipids are esters of fatty acids with glycerol or long-chain alcohols.

nodes.label: High energy density
nodes.summary: Triacylglycerols store more energy per gram than carbohydrates because their carbons are highly reduced.

nodes.label: Saponification
nodes.summary: Base-catalyzed hydrolysis of triacylglycerols (saponification) yields glycerol and fatty acid salts (soap).

nodes.label: Triacylglycerol structure
nodes.summary: A triacylglycerol is glycerol esterified to three fatty acids, the principal energy-storage lipid.

nodes.label: Waxes
nodes.summary: Waxes are esters of long-chain fatty acids with long-chain alcohols, serving as protective coatings.

nodes.label: Nucleotides & nucleic acids
nodes.summary: Nucleotides are the monomers of DNA and RNA and serve as energy and signaling molecules.

nodes.label: Nucleotide components
nodes.summary: A nucleotide consists of a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar, and one or more phosphate groups.

nodes.label: Thymine (DNA) vs uracil (RNA)
nodes.summary: DNA contains thymine while RNA substitutes uracil, which lacks thymine's methyl group.

nodes.label: N-glycosidic bond (base to sugar)
nodes.summary: The base attaches to C1' of the pentose through an N-glycosidic bond to form a nucleoside.

nodes.label: Nucleoside vs nucleotide
nodes.summary: A nucleoside is base plus sugar; adding a phosphate makes it a nucleotide.

nodes.label: Purines (A, G)
nodes.summary: Purines (adenine, guanine) are double-ring nitrogenous bases.

nodes.label: Pyrimidines (C, T, U)
nodes.summary: Pyrimidines (cytosine, thymine, uracil) are single-ring nitrogenous bases.

nodes.label: Ribose vs deoxyribose
nodes.summary: RNA uses ribose with a 2'-OH while DNA uses deoxyribose lacking that hydroxyl, making DNA more stable.

nodes.label: Base tautomers and pairing
nodes.summary: Each base predominates in one tautomeric form (keto/amino) that enables correct Watson-Crick pairing; rare tautomers cause mispairs.

nodes.label: Base, sugar, phosphate
nodes.summary: A nucleotide is a nitrogenous base plus a pentose sugar plus one to three phosphate groups.

nodes.label: Nucleotide functions
nodes.summary: Nucleotides function beyond nucleic acids as energy carriers and cofactors.

nodes.label: ATP as energy currency
nodes.summary: ATP stores energy in high-energy phosphoanhydride bonds whose hydrolysis powers cellular work.

nodes.label: Cyclic AMP (second messenger)
nodes.summary: Cyclic AMP is a nucleotide-derived second messenger in hormone signaling.

nodes.label: Nucleotide-containing coenzymes
nodes.summary: NAD+, FAD, and coenzyme A incorporate nucleotides and carry electrons or acyl groups in metabolism.

nodes.label: GTP and other NTPs
nodes.summary: GTP and other nucleotide triphosphates drive signaling, translation, and biosynthesis.

nodes.label: Nucleic acid structure
nodes.summary: Nucleotides polymerize into directional strands that base-pair into the DNA double helix.

nodes.label: Antiparallel strands
nodes.summary: The two strands of the DNA double helix run in opposite 5'-to-3' directions (antiparallel).

nodes.label: Watson-Crick base pairing
nodes.summary: A pairs with T (two H-bonds) and G pairs with C (three H-bonds) via complementary hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Chargaff's rules
nodes.summary: In double-stranded DNA the amount of A equals T and the amount of G equals C.

nodes.label: DNA denaturation / Tm
nodes.summary: Heat or pH separates the strands; higher GC content raises the melting temperature because of extra H-bonds.

nodes.label: 5' to 3' directionality
nodes.summary: Each nucleic acid strand has a free 5'-phosphate and 3'-OH end, giving it directionality.

nodes.label: Double helix & stabilization
nodes.summary: DNA forms a right-handed double helix stabilized by base stacking and hydrogen bonding.

nodes.label: Phosphodiester backbone
nodes.summary: Nucleotides link via phosphodiester bonds between the 3'-OH of one sugar and the 5'-phosphate of the next.

nodes.label: Protein structure
nodes.summary: Proteins are polymers of amino acids whose function is dictated by a hierarchy of structural levels.

nodes.label: Folding, denaturation & conjugation
nodes.summary: Folding can be reversed by denaturation, and many proteins carry non-amino-acid components.

nodes.label: Chaperones
nodes.summary: Molecular chaperones assist proper folding and prevent aggregation of nascent polypeptides.

nodes.label: Conjugated proteins / prosthetic groups
nodes.summary: Conjugated proteins contain non-peptide prosthetic groups such as heme (hemoproteins), lipids, carbohydrates, or metals.

nodes.label: Denaturation
nodes.summary: Denaturation disrupts higher-order structure (not the peptide backbone) via heat, pH, detergents, or chaotropes, abolishing function.

nodes.label: Disulfide reduction in denaturation
nodes.summary: Reducing agents like beta-mercaptoethanol or DTT cleave disulfide bonds, helping fully denature and unfold proteins.

nodes.label: Solvation layer / entropy
nodes.summary: Burying hydrophobic residues releases ordered water, increasing entropy and driving folding.

nodes.label: Nonenzymatic protein function
nodes.summary: Beyond catalysis, proteins serve structural, transport, motor, binding, and defense roles.

nodes.label: Binding and transport proteins
nodes.summary: Proteins such as hemoglobin and albumin bind and transport ligands like oxygen, ions, and lipids.

nodes.label: Antibodies (immune function)
nodes.summary: Immunoglobulins bind antigens with high specificity, mediating immune recognition and defense.

nodes.label: Motor proteins
nodes.summary: Motor proteins (myosin, kinesin, dynein) convert ATP energy into mechanical movement along filaments.

nodes.label: Receptor and signaling proteins
nodes.summary: Membrane and intracellular receptor proteins bind signals to trigger cellular responses.

nodes.label: Structural proteins
nodes.summary: Collagen, keratin, elastin, and actin/tubulin provide mechanical support and shape to cells and tissues.

nodes.label: Peptide bond properties
nodes.summary: The peptide bond has partial double-bond character that constrains backbone geometry.

nodes.label: Sequence directionality (N to C)
nodes.summary: Peptide sequences are written and synthesized from the N-terminus to the C-terminus.

nodes.label: N-terminus and C-terminus
nodes.summary: A polypeptide has a free amino group (N-terminus) at one end and a free carboxyl group (C-terminus) at the other.

nodes.label: Phi and psi dihedral angles
nodes.summary: Backbone flexibility comes from rotation about the phi (N-Cα) and psi (Cα-C) bonds, constrained by sterics.

nodes.label: Peptide bond resonance / planarity
nodes.summary: Resonance delocalization gives the peptide bond partial double-bond character, making the amide unit planar and rigid.

nodes.label: Trans conformation preference
nodes.summary: Peptide bonds favor the trans configuration to minimize steric clash between adjacent side chains.

nodes.label: Primary structure
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the linear amino acid sequence held by covalent peptide bonds.

nodes.label: Sequence determines fold
nodes.summary: The primary sequence contains all the information needed to specify a protein's three-dimensional fold.

nodes.label: Amino acid sequence
nodes.summary: Primary structure is the specific order of amino acid residues encoded by the gene.

nodes.label: Quaternary structure
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure is the assembly of multiple polypeptide subunits into a functional complex.

nodes.label: Cooperativity / allostery
nodes.summary: Binding at one subunit changes affinity at others, producing the sigmoidal cooperative binding curve of hemoglobin.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin as quaternary model
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin is a tetramer (two alpha and two beta subunits) whose subunit cooperation enables oxygen binding.

nodes.label: Subunit assembly
nodes.summary: Quaternary structure arises when two or more folded polypeptide chains associate noncovalently or via disulfides.

nodes.label: Secondary structure
nodes.summary: Secondary structure is local backbone folding stabilized by hydrogen bonds.

nodes.label: Alpha helix
nodes.summary: The alpha helix is a right-handed coil stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds between residues i and i+4.

nodes.label: Beta pleated sheet
nodes.summary: Beta sheets form from extended strands hydrogen-bonded side by side, in parallel or antiparallel orientation.

nodes.label: Backbone hydrogen bonding
nodes.summary: Secondary structure is stabilized by hydrogen bonds between backbone carbonyl oxygens and amide hydrogens.

nodes.label: Proline and glycine effects
nodes.summary: Proline breaks helices and sheets (rigid ring, no amide H) while glycine adds flexibility that can disrupt regular structure.

nodes.label: Beta turns and loops
nodes.summary: Beta turns reverse the chain direction, often using glycine and proline, connecting elements of secondary structure.

nodes.label: Protein separation & quantification
nodes.summary: Proteins are isolated and measured by size, charge, and absorbance methods.

nodes.label: Protein chromatography
nodes.summary: Size-exclusion, ion-exchange, and affinity chromatography purify proteins by size, charge, and specific binding.

nodes.label: Isoelectric focusing (separation by pI)
nodes.summary: In a pH gradient, proteins migrate until they reach the pH equal to their pI, where net charge is zero.

nodes.label: Protein quantification (UV / Bradford)
nodes.summary: Protein concentration is measured by 280 nm UV absorbance (aromatic residues) or colorimetric Bradford/Lowry assays.

nodes.label: SDS-PAGE (separation by size)
nodes.summary: SDS-PAGE denatures proteins with uniform negative charge so gel electrophoresis separates them by molecular weight.

nodes.label: Fibrous vs globular proteins
nodes.summary: Overall protein shape divides into elongated structural fibers and compact functional globules.

nodes.label: Fibrous proteins
nodes.summary: Fibrous proteins (collagen, keratin, elastin) are elongated, insoluble, and play structural roles.

nodes.label: Globular proteins
nodes.summary: Globular proteins are compact and water-soluble, functioning as enzymes, transporters, and antibodies.

nodes.label: Tertiary structure
nodes.summary: Tertiary structure is the overall 3D fold of a single polypeptide stabilized by side-chain interactions.

nodes.label: Disulfide bonds in folding
nodes.summary: Covalent disulfide bonds between cysteines lock tertiary structure, especially in extracellular proteins.

nodes.label: Domains and motifs
nodes.summary: Domains are independently folding functional units and motifs are recurring small structural patterns.

nodes.label: Side-chain hydrogen bonds
nodes.summary: Hydrogen bonds between polar side chains help stabilize the folded conformation.

nodes.label: Hydrophobic core packing
nodes.summary: The hydrophobic effect drives nonpolar side chains into the protein interior, the dominant force in folding.

nodes.label: Salt bridges (ionic bonds)
nodes.summary: Electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged side chains forms salt bridges that stabilize tertiary structure.

nodes.label: Van der Waals / London forces
nodes.summary: Weak van der Waals interactions among tightly packed atoms add cumulative stability to the fold.

nodes.label: Calorimetry and heat capacity
nodes.summary: Calorimetry measures heat flow using temperature change, mass, and heat capacity.

nodes.label: Heat capacity and specific heat
nodes.summary: Heat capacity relates absorbed heat to the resulting temperature change of a substance.

nodes.label: Cp vs Cv
nodes.summary: Constant-pressure heat capacity exceeds constant-volume heat capacity because expansion work also absorbs energy.

nodes.label: Heat capacity (C = q/ΔT)
nodes.summary: Heat capacity C is the heat needed to raise an entire object by one degree (q = CΔT); it is extensive, unlike intensive specific heat.

nodes.label: Molar heat capacity
nodes.summary: Molar heat capacity is the heat needed to raise one mole of a substance by one degree.

nodes.label: q = mcΔT
nodes.summary: The heat absorbed equals mass times specific heat times temperature change (q = mcΔT).

nodes.label: Specific heat capacity (c)
nodes.summary: Specific heat is the heat needed to raise one gram of a substance by one degree (water = 4.184 J/g·°C).

nodes.label: Calorimeter types
nodes.summary: Coffee-cup and bomb calorimeters measure heat at constant pressure and constant volume respectively.

nodes.label: Constant-volume (bomb) calorimetry
nodes.summary: A bomb calorimeter measures heat at constant volume, so q measured equals ΔU of the process.

nodes.label: Calorimeter constant
nodes.summary: A real calorimeter absorbs some heat itself; its heat capacity (calorimeter constant) must be included so q_rxn = −(q_water + q_calorimeter).

nodes.label: Constant-pressure (coffee-cup) calorimetry
nodes.summary: A coffee-cup calorimeter measures heat at constant pressure, so q measured equals ΔH of the process.

nodes.label: Heat balance (q_lost = -q_gained)
nodes.summary: In an isolated calorimeter the heat released by one component equals the heat absorbed by the rest.

nodes.label: Energy changes in chemical reactions / system & surroundings
nodes.summary: Thermodynamics studies energy exchange between a system and its surroundings during physical and chemical change.

nodes.label: State functions and path functions
nodes.summary: State functions depend only on the current state, while path functions depend on the route taken.

nodes.label: Path functions (q and w)
nodes.summary: Heat (q) and work (w) are path functions whose values depend on the specific process route.

nodes.label: Reversible vs irreversible processes
nodes.summary: A reversible process proceeds through equilibrium states infinitely slowly and yields maximum work; real (irreversible) processes are spontaneous and do less work.

nodes.label: State functions
nodes.summary: State functions (U, H, S, G, T, P, V) depend only on initial and final states, not the path between them.

nodes.label: System, surroundings, and boundaries
nodes.summary: The universe is partitioned into the system under study and everything else, the surroundings.

nodes.label: System vs surroundings
nodes.summary: The system is the portion of the universe being studied; the surroundings are everything else with which it exchanges energy and matter.

nodes.label: Endothermic vs exothermic processes
nodes.summary: Endothermic processes absorb heat from the surroundings (+q to system); exothermic processes release heat (-q from system).

nodes.label: Heat vs temperature
nodes.summary: Heat is energy transferred due to a temperature difference; temperature measures the average molecular kinetic energy and is not itself energy.

nodes.label: Thermal equilibrium and direction of heat flow
nodes.summary: Heat flows spontaneously from hot to cold until both reach the same temperature (thermal equilibrium).

nodes.label: Open, closed, and isolated systems
nodes.summary: Open systems exchange matter and energy, closed exchange only energy, and isolated exchange neither.

nodes.label: Zeroth law of thermodynamics
nodes.summary: If two systems are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, they are in thermal equilibrium with each other, defining temperature.

nodes.label: Enthalpy and thermochemistry
nodes.summary: Enthalpy tracks heat flow at constant pressure and is the basis of thermochemical calculations.

nodes.label: Definition of enthalpy
nodes.summary: Enthalpy combines internal energy with the product of pressure and volume.

nodes.label: ΔH = q_p (heat at constant pressure)
nodes.summary: At constant pressure the enthalpy change equals the heat absorbed or released by the system.

nodes.label: Relation of ΔH to ΔU (ΔH = ΔU + Δ(PV))
nodes.summary: ΔH = ΔU + Δ(PV); for ideal-gas reactions ΔH = ΔU + Δn_gas·RT, so ΔH and ΔU differ only when moles of gas change.

nodes.label: Enthalpy H = U + PV
nodes.summary: Enthalpy is defined as H = U + PV and is a state function whose change equals heat at constant pressure.

nodes.label: Sign of ΔH for endo/exothermic
nodes.summary: ΔH is positive for endothermic reactions and negative for exothermic reactions.

nodes.label: Standard enthalpies
nodes.summary: Standard-state enthalpies of formation tabulate the heat to form compounds from elements.

nodes.label: Elements in standard state have ΔH°f = 0
nodes.summary: By definition the standard enthalpy of formation of an element in its most stable form is zero.

nodes.label: Standard enthalpy of formation (ΔH°f)
nodes.summary: ΔH°f is the enthalpy change to form one mole of a compound from its elements in their standard states.

nodes.label: ΔH°rxn from formation enthalpies
nodes.summary: ΔH°rxn = Σ ΔH°f(products) - Σ ΔH°f(reactants), weighted by stoichiometric coefficients.

nodes.label: Standard state and standard conditions
nodes.summary: The thermodynamic standard state is 1 atm (or 1 bar), 1 M for solutions, and the most stable form of each substance (often quoted at 25°C).

nodes.label: Hess's law and bond enthalpies
nodes.summary: Enthalpy changes are additive over reaction steps and estimable from bond energies.

nodes.label: Bond dissociation enthalpy
nodes.summary: Bond enthalpy is the energy required to break one mole of a bond in the gas phase; breaking bonds is endothermic, forming bonds exothermic.

nodes.label: ΔH from bond enthalpies
nodes.summary: ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed), using average bond enthalpies.

nodes.label: Hess's law
nodes.summary: Because enthalpy is a state function, ΔH for an overall reaction equals the sum of the ΔH values of its constituent steps.

nodes.label: Manipulating thermochemical equations
nodes.summary: Reversing a reaction flips the sign of ΔH and multiplying coefficients scales ΔH proportionally.

nodes.label: Named enthalpy changes
nodes.summary: Specific named enthalpy terms describe combustion, fusion, vaporization, and solution.

nodes.label: Enthalpy of combustion
nodes.summary: Standard enthalpy of combustion is the heat released when one mole of a substance burns completely in oxygen.

nodes.label: Lattice energy and hydration energy
nodes.summary: ΔH_solution is the sum of the endothermic lattice energy (breaking the ionic lattice) and the exothermic hydration/solvation energy of the ions.

nodes.label: Enthalpy of neutralization
nodes.summary: Enthalpy of neutralization is the heat released when an acid and base react to form one mole of water (≈ −57 kJ/mol for strong acid + strong base).

nodes.label: Enthalpies of fusion and vaporization
nodes.summary: Enthalpy of fusion and vaporization are the heats absorbed during melting and boiling at constant temperature.

nodes.label: Enthalpy of solution
nodes.summary: Enthalpy of solution is the heat absorbed or released when a solute dissolves, balancing lattice and solvation energies.

nodes.label: Entropy and the second law
nodes.summary: Entropy measures disorder/dispersal of energy, and the universe's total entropy always increases for spontaneous change.

nodes.label: Entropy concept
nodes.summary: Entropy quantifies the number of microstates and the dispersal of energy and matter.

nodes.label: Boltzmann relation S = k ln W
nodes.summary: Entropy relates to the number of microstates W by S = k_B ln W.

nodes.label: Entropy (S) as disorder/microstates
nodes.summary: Entropy is a state function measuring the dispersal of energy and matter, related to the number of accessible microstates.

nodes.label: ΔS = q_rev/T
nodes.summary: For a reversible process the entropy change equals the heat transferred divided by absolute temperature.

nodes.label: Units of entropy (J/mol·K)
nodes.summary: Entropy carries units of energy per temperature (J/K, or J/mol·K for molar entropy), unlike enthalpy in kJ/mol — a common unit trap in ΔG = ΔH − TΔS.

nodes.label: Second and third laws
nodes.summary: The second law sets the direction of spontaneous change and the third law fixes the zero of entropy.

nodes.label: Second law of thermodynamics
nodes.summary: For any spontaneous process the total entropy of the universe (system + surroundings) increases.

nodes.label: Spontaneity (thermodynamic favorability)
nodes.summary: A spontaneous process occurs without continuous external input; spontaneity refers to thermodynamic favorability, NOT speed.

nodes.label: Entropy change of the surroundings (ΔS_surr = −ΔH_sys/T)
nodes.summary: Heat released by an exothermic system raises the surroundings' entropy; ΔS_surr ≈ −ΔH_sys/T, larger in magnitude at low temperature.

nodes.label: Third law of thermodynamics
nodes.summary: The entropy of a perfect crystalline substance at absolute zero is zero, providing an absolute entropy scale.

nodes.label: ΔS_universe = ΔS_sys + ΔS_surr
nodes.summary: Spontaneity requires ΔS_universe > 0, where ΔS_surr ≈ -ΔH_sys/T.

nodes.label: Predicting entropy changes
nodes.summary: Phase, temperature, moles of gas, and mixing determine the sign of an entropy change.

nodes.label: ΔS°rxn from standard entropies
nodes.summary: ΔS°rxn = Σ S°(products) - Σ S°(reactants), using absolute standard molar entropies.

nodes.label: More moles of gas → higher entropy
nodes.summary: Reactions that increase the number of moles of gas tend to increase entropy.

nodes.label: Entropy of mixing
nodes.summary: Mixing gases or forming solutions increases entropy because particles can occupy more microstates (positional disorder).

nodes.label: Entropy increases gas > liquid > solid
nodes.summary: Entropy generally increases in the order solid < liquid < gas as disorder increases.

nodes.label: Entropy and temperature/volume increase
nodes.summary: Increasing temperature, volume, or dissolving a solid into solution generally increases entropy.

nodes.label: First law of thermodynamics (energy conservation)
nodes.summary: Energy is conserved: the internal energy change of a system equals heat added minus work done by the system.

nodes.label: Internal energy
nodes.summary: Internal energy U is the total kinetic and potential energy of all particles in a system.

nodes.label: Energy conservation / first law statement
nodes.summary: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; the internal energy of an isolated system is constant and energy only transfers between system and surroundings.

nodes.label: Chemistry vs physics work-sign conventions
nodes.summary: The physics convention writes ΔU = q − w (w = work done BY the system); the chemistry convention writes ΔU = q + w (w = work done ON the system). Watch which is implied.

nodes.label: Internal energy (U) defined
nodes.summary: Internal energy is the sum of the molecular kinetic and potential energies of a system and is a state function.

nodes.label: First-law equation ΔU = q + w
nodes.summary: The change in internal energy equals heat added to the system plus work done on the system (ΔU = q + w, chemistry sign convention).

nodes.label: Sign conventions for q and w
nodes.summary: q is positive when heat flows into the system; w is positive when work is done on the system (chemistry convention).

nodes.label: Thermodynamic processes
nodes.summary: Idealized processes hold one variable constant: isothermal, adiabatic, isobaric, or isochoric.

nodes.label: Adiabatic process
nodes.summary: An adiabatic process exchanges no heat with the surroundings (q = 0), so ΔU = w.

nodes.label: Isobaric process
nodes.summary: An isobaric process occurs at constant pressure, where heat absorbed equals the enthalpy change (q_p = ΔH).

nodes.label: Isochoric (constant-volume) process
nodes.summary: An isochoric process occurs at constant volume; no PV work is done so all heat changes internal energy (q_v = ΔU).

nodes.label: Isothermal process
nodes.summary: An isothermal process occurs at constant temperature, so for an ideal gas ΔU = 0 and q = -w.

nodes.label: Pressure-volume work
nodes.summary: Expansion and compression work is done as a gas changes volume against external pressure.

nodes.label: Expansion vs compression work sign
nodes.summary: Expansion (ΔV > 0) does negative work on the system (energy leaves); compression (ΔV < 0) does positive work on the system.

nodes.label: Constant-volume process (ΔU = q_v)
nodes.summary: At constant volume no PV work is done, so the heat absorbed equals the internal energy change (q_v = ΔU).

nodes.label: PV work (w = -PΔV)
nodes.summary: Work done on a system at constant external pressure is w = -P_ext·ΔV; expansion does negative work on the system.

nodes.label: Gibbs free energy and spontaneity
nodes.summary: Gibbs free energy combines enthalpy and entropy to predict spontaneity at constant temperature and pressure.

nodes.label: Reaction coupling
nodes.summary: A favorable reaction can drive an unfavorable one when the two share an intermediate.

nodes.label: Coupling exergonic to endergonic reactions
nodes.summary: An endergonic reaction can be driven by coupling it to a more strongly exergonic reaction (e.g., ATP hydrolysis) so the net ΔG is negative.

nodes.label: Gibbs free energy definition
nodes.summary: Gibbs free energy is the maximum non-PV work obtainable at constant T and P.

nodes.label: ΔG = ΔH - TΔS
nodes.summary: At constant temperature and pressure the Gibbs free energy change is ΔG = ΔH - TΔS.

nodes.label: ΔG and ΔS_universe
nodes.summary: ΔG = −TΔS_universe at constant T and P, so ΔG < 0 (spontaneous) is just a restatement of the second law in system-only variables.

nodes.label: ΔG as maximum non-PV work
nodes.summary: The magnitude of ΔG equals the maximum useful (non-expansion) work a process can perform.

nodes.label: Sign of ΔG determines spontaneity
nodes.summary: ΔG < 0 is spontaneous (exergonic), ΔG > 0 is nonspontaneous (endergonic), and ΔG = 0 is equilibrium.

nodes.label: Standard free energy and equilibrium
nodes.summary: Standard free energy relates to the equilibrium constant and the reaction quotient.

nodes.label: ΔG vs ΔG° distinction
nodes.summary: ΔG° is fixed for standard conditions and sets K; ΔG is the actual driving force at the current Q and reaches zero at equilibrium even when ΔG° ≠ 0.

nodes.label: ΔG°rxn from free energies of formation
nodes.summary: ΔG°rxn = Σ ΔG°f(products) - Σ ΔG°f(reactants); ΔG°f of an element in standard state is zero.

nodes.label: ΔG° = -RT ln K
nodes.summary: Standard free energy change relates to the equilibrium constant by ΔG° = -RT ln K.

nodes.label: Relation of K to spontaneity
nodes.summary: K > 1 corresponds to ΔG° < 0 (products favored); K < 1 corresponds to ΔG° > 0 (reactants favored).

nodes.label: ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q
nodes.summary: Under nonstandard conditions ΔG = ΔG° + RT ln Q, where Q is the reaction quotient.

nodes.label: Q vs K predicts reaction direction
nodes.summary: Q < K (ΔG < 0) drives the reaction forward; Q > K (ΔG > 0) drives it in reverse; Q = K (ΔG = 0) is equilibrium.

nodes.label: Temperature dependence of spontaneity
nodes.summary: The signs of ΔH and ΔS and the temperature jointly determine whether a reaction is spontaneous.

nodes.label: Crossover temperature (T = ΔH/ΔS)
nodes.summary: Setting ΔG = 0 gives the temperature T = ΔH/ΔS at which spontaneity switches.

nodes.label: Enthalpy-driven vs entropy-driven reactions
nodes.summary: When ΔH dominates ΔG the reaction is enthalpy-driven; when the −TΔS term dominates it is entropy-driven, an emphasis that shifts with temperature.
