nodes.label: Sign chart of ΔH, ΔS, and T
nodes.summary: ΔH-/ΔS+ is always spontaneous, ΔH+/ΔS- never, ΔH-/ΔS- only at low T, and ΔH+/ΔS+ only at high T.

nodes.label: Reaction rates and rate laws
nodes.summary: Chemical kinetics describes how fast reactions proceed and how rate depends on concentration.

nodes.label: Specific reaction orders
nodes.summary: Zero-, first-, and second-order reactions have characteristic integrated forms and half-lives.

nodes.label: First-order reactions
nodes.summary: For first order, rate ∝ [A]; ln[A] = ln[A]₀ - kt gives a linear ln[A]-vs-t plot and constant half-life.

nodes.label: First-order kinetics in decay/elimination
nodes.summary: Radioactive decay and many drug-elimination processes follow first-order kinetics with a concentration-independent half-life.

nodes.label: Graphical determination of order
nodes.summary: Plotting [A], ln[A], or 1/[A] vs time gives a straight line for zero-, first-, and second-order reactions respectively.

nodes.label: Half-life and order
nodes.summary: First-order half-life t½ = 0.693/k is concentration-independent, unlike zero- and second-order half-lives.

nodes.label: Half-life formulas by order
nodes.summary: t½ = [A]₀/2k (zero, shortens over time), 0.693/k (first, constant), 1/(k[A]₀) (second, lengthens over time).

nodes.label: Integrated rate laws
nodes.summary: Integrated rate laws relate concentration to time: [A]=[A]₀−kt (zero), ln[A]=ln[A]₀−kt (first), 1/[A]=1/[A]₀+kt (second).

nodes.label: Second-order reactions
nodes.summary: For second order, rate ∝ [A]²; 1/[A] = 1/[A]₀ + kt gives a linear 1/[A]-vs-t plot.

nodes.label: Zero-order reactions
nodes.summary: For zero order, rate is constant and independent of concentration; [A] = [A]₀ - kt with a linear [A]-vs-t plot.

nodes.label: Reaction rate
nodes.summary: Rate is the change in concentration of a species per unit time, normalized by stoichiometry.

nodes.label: Rate laws and reaction order
nodes.summary: The rate law expresses rate as a function of concentrations raised to experimental orders.

nodes.label: Differential rate law (rate = k[A]^m[B]^n)
nodes.summary: The rate law rate = k[A]^m[B]^n relates rate to reactant concentrations through the rate constant and orders.

nodes.label: Orders are determined experimentally
nodes.summary: Reaction orders cannot be read from the balanced equation; they must be determined from experimental data.

nodes.label: Method of initial rates
nodes.summary: Comparing how initial rates change when one reactant concentration is varied reveals each reactant's order.

nodes.label: Units of k depend on overall order
nodes.summary: k has units M^(1−n)·s^−1 for overall order n: M/s (zero), 1/s (first), 1/(M·s) (second) — a frequent MCAT calculation/check.

nodes.label: Reaction order
nodes.summary: The order in a reactant is its exponent in the rate law; the overall order is the sum of all exponents.

nodes.label: Rate constant (k) and its units
nodes.summary: The rate constant k is temperature-dependent and concentration-independent, with units that depend on the overall order.

nodes.label: Rate vs rate constant distinction
nodes.summary: Rate changes as concentrations change during a reaction; the rate constant k is fixed at a given temperature and only changes with T or a catalyst.

nodes.label: Average vs instantaneous rate
nodes.summary: Average rate is measured over a time interval; instantaneous rate is the slope of the concentration-time curve at a point.

nodes.label: Reaction rate definition
nodes.summary: Reaction rate is the change in concentration of a reactant or product per unit time.

nodes.label: Initial rate
nodes.summary: The initial rate is the instantaneous rate at t = 0, before products accumulate or reverse reaction interferes; it is used to determine rate laws.

nodes.label: Rate normalized by stoichiometry
nodes.summary: For aA + bB → cC, rate = -(1/a)d[A]/dt = -(1/b)d[B]/dt = (1/c)d[C]/dt.

nodes.label: Collision theory and activation energy
nodes.summary: Reactions require effective molecular collisions that surmount an activation-energy barrier.

nodes.label: Activation energy and transition state
nodes.summary: The energy barrier and the high-energy transition state govern reaction rate.

nodes.label: Activation energy (Ea)
nodes.summary: Activation energy is the minimum energy required for reactants to reach the transition state and react.

nodes.label: Reaction energy (coordinate) diagram
nodes.summary: An energy diagram plots potential energy vs reaction coordinate, showing Ea (forward and reverse) and ΔH.

nodes.label: Intermediates as energy-diagram valleys
nodes.summary: In a multistep energy diagram each peak is a transition state and each valley between peaks is a reaction intermediate; the highest peak is the RDS barrier.

nodes.label: Forward vs reverse Ea and ΔH
nodes.summary: ΔH equals the forward activation energy minus the reverse activation energy.

nodes.label: Transition state / activated complex
nodes.summary: The transition state is the highest-energy, unstable arrangement at the peak of the energy barrier, with partially formed and broken bonds.

nodes.label: Arrhenius equation
nodes.summary: The Arrhenius equation quantifies the temperature and Ea dependence of the rate constant.

nodes.label: Ea governs temperature sensitivity of rate
nodes.summary: A higher Ea makes the rate constant more sensitive to temperature; lowering Ea (e.g., by a catalyst) raises k at a given T.

nodes.label: Arrhenius equation k = Ae^(-Ea/RT)
nodes.summary: The rate constant follows k = A·e^(-Ea/RT), with A the frequency factor and the exponential the fraction of effective collisions.

nodes.label: Frequency (pre-exponential) factor A
nodes.summary: The pre-exponential factor A reflects collision frequency and the fraction of collisions with proper orientation.

nodes.label: Linear Arrhenius form
nodes.summary: ln k = ln A - Ea/(RT); a plot of ln k vs 1/T is linear with slope -Ea/R.

nodes.label: Collision theory
nodes.summary: Reaction rate depends on collision frequency, orientation, and energy.

nodes.label: Concentration, pressure, and surface area effects
nodes.summary: Higher concentration or partial pressure of gases, and greater surface area of a solid, increase collision frequency and therefore rate.

nodes.label: Factors affecting reaction rate
nodes.summary: Rate increases with concentration, temperature, surface area, and the presence of a catalyst.

nodes.label: Effective collisions (energy + orientation)
nodes.summary: A productive reaction requires colliding molecules to have sufficient energy and correct orientation.

nodes.label: Steric (orientation) factor
nodes.summary: Only collisions with proper molecular orientation react; the steric factor accounts for the fraction of geometrically productive collisions.

nodes.label: Temperature and rate
nodes.summary: Raising temperature increases both collision frequency and the fraction of molecules exceeding Ea, sharply increasing rate.

nodes.label: Reaction mechanisms and catalysis
nodes.summary: Multistep mechanisms, rate-determining steps, and catalysts explain observed kinetics.

nodes.label: Catalysis
nodes.summary: Catalysts speed reactions by lowering activation energy without being consumed.

nodes.label: Catalyst lowers Ea, not consumed
nodes.summary: A catalyst provides an alternative pathway with lower activation energy and is regenerated, increasing rate without being consumed.

nodes.label: Catalyst on an energy diagram
nodes.summary: A catalyst lowers the peak(s) of the energy diagram (lower Ea, sometimes new intermediates) but leaves reactant and product energies — and therefore ΔH/ΔG — unchanged.

nodes.label: Enzymes as biological catalysts
nodes.summary: Enzymes are protein catalysts that lower Ea of biochemical reactions, often by stabilizing the transition state.

nodes.label: Homogeneous vs heterogeneous catalysis
nodes.summary: Homogeneous catalysts share the phase of the reactants; heterogeneous catalysts (e.g., surfaces) are in a different phase.

nodes.label: Catalysts do not change ΔG, ΔH, or K
nodes.summary: A catalyst speeds forward and reverse rates equally, so it does not alter ΔH, ΔG, equilibrium position, or K.

nodes.label: Elementary steps and molecularity
nodes.summary: Mechanisms are sequences of elementary steps characterized by molecularity.

nodes.label: Intermediate vs catalyst in a mechanism
nodes.summary: An intermediate is produced in an early step then consumed later; a catalyst is consumed early and regenerated later. Neither appears in the overall balanced equation.

nodes.label: Molecularity (uni-, bi-, termolecular)
nodes.summary: Molecularity counts the species in an elementary step; termolecular steps are rare because three-body collisions are unlikely.

nodes.label: Molecularity vs reaction order
nodes.summary: Molecularity is a theoretical count for one elementary step (gives its rate law); overall reaction order is experimental and may not match the balanced equation.

nodes.label: Rate law of an elementary step
nodes.summary: For an elementary step (only), the rate law can be written directly from its stoichiometric coefficients.

nodes.label: Elementary reactions and intermediates
nodes.summary: An elementary step occurs in a single collision event; intermediates are produced then consumed and do not appear in the overall equation.

nodes.label: Rate-determining step
nodes.summary: The slowest step controls the overall reaction rate and the observed rate law.

nodes.label: Rate-determining (slowest) step
nodes.summary: The slowest elementary step is the rate-determining step and governs the overall rate law.

nodes.label: Mechanism must match overall rate law
nodes.summary: A proposed mechanism is valid only if its steps sum to the overall reaction and reproduce the experimental rate law.

nodes.label: Fast pre-equilibrium approximation
nodes.summary: When a fast step precedes the RDS, intermediate concentrations are expressed via the pre-equilibrium to eliminate them from the rate law.

nodes.label: Rate law from a slow first step
nodes.summary: When the first step is rate-determining, the overall rate law is simply the rate law of that elementary step.

nodes.label: Steady-state approximation
nodes.summary: The steady-state approximation sets the net rate of change of a reactive intermediate to ≈ 0, allowing its concentration to be solved and removed from the rate law.

nodes.label: Thermodynamic vs kinetic control
nodes.summary: Thermodynamics predicts whether a reaction can occur; kinetics determines how fast and which product forms.

nodes.label: Spontaneity vs rate
nodes.summary: A spontaneous reaction is not necessarily fast, and a fast reaction is not necessarily favorable.

nodes.label: Thermodynamics and kinetics are independent
nodes.summary: ΔG governs whether a reaction is spontaneous, while Ea governs how fast it occurs; the two are independent.

nodes.label: Kinetic vs thermodynamic product
nodes.summary: The kinetic product forms fastest (lowest Ea) while the thermodynamic product is most stable (lowest G); conditions select which dominates.

nodes.label: Metastable states
nodes.summary: A thermodynamically unstable species can persist indefinitely if a large activation barrier blocks its conversion (kinetic stability).

nodes.label: Temperature selects kinetic vs thermodynamic product
nodes.summary: Low temperature and short times favor the kinetic product; high temperature and reversibility favor the more stable thermodynamic product.

nodes.label: Foundations of social inequality
nodes.summary: Social inequality is the unequal distribution of resources, power, and opportunity among groups, organized through stratification, status, and theoretical lenses.

nodes.label: Core definitions
nodes.summary: Key terms—inequality, stratification, status, power, and prestige—define how societies rank groups and distribute scarce resources.

nodes.label: Life chances
nodes.summary: Life chances are the opportunities each individual has to improve quality of life, which are shaped by their position in the stratification system.

nodes.label: Power
nodes.summary: Power is the ability to achieve one's goals or impose one's will on others, even against resistance.

nodes.label: Prestige
nodes.summary: Prestige is the respect and admiration that a status or position carries within a society.

nodes.label: Privilege
nodes.summary: Privilege is an unearned advantage or benefit conferred on members of a dominant group by virtue of their social position.

nodes.label: Social inequality defined
nodes.summary: Social inequality is the unequal distribution of resources, opportunities, and rewards among individuals and groups in a society.

nodes.label: Equality vs. equity
nodes.summary: Equality gives everyone identical resources, whereas equity allocates resources according to need to produce fair outcomes.

nodes.label: Social stratification
nodes.summary: Social stratification is the hierarchical arrangement of large social groups based on their control over basic resources.

nodes.label: Wealth, power, and prestige as stratification dimensions
nodes.summary: Societies stratify people along three intertwined dimensions—economic wealth, political power, and social prestige.

nodes.label: Status and social position
nodes.summary: A status is a recognized social position; statuses can be ascribed or achieved and combine into a person's status set, sometimes producing status inconsistency.

nodes.label: Achieved status
nodes.summary: An achieved status is a social position earned through one's own effort, choices, or merit, such as occupation or education.

nodes.label: Ascribed status
nodes.summary: An ascribed status is a social position assigned at birth or involuntarily acquired, such as race, sex, or family caste.

nodes.label: Master status
nodes.summary: A master status is a status that dominates all others and shapes a person's overall social identity.

nodes.label: Status defined
nodes.summary: A status is a position a person occupies within a social structure, carrying associated rights and obligations.

nodes.label: Status inconsistency
nodes.summary: Status inconsistency occurs when an individual's statuses rank unevenly across dimensions of stratification (e.g., high education but low income).

nodes.label: Status set
nodes.summary: A status set is the full collection of statuses a person holds at a given time.

nodes.label: Status symbols
nodes.summary: Status symbols are material or behavioral signs (cars, clothing, titles) used to display one's social position.

nodes.label: Systems of stratification
nodes.summary: Societies stratify through closed systems like caste and slavery or open systems like class, differing in the permeability of boundaries between strata.

nodes.label: Caste system
nodes.summary: A caste system is a closed stratification system in which status is ascribed at birth and movement between strata is essentially impossible.

nodes.label: Class system
nodes.summary: A class system is an open stratification system based largely on economic position, allowing some movement between strata.

nodes.label: Estate (feudal) system
nodes.summary: An estate system stratifies society by legally defined groups (nobility, clergy, commoners) tied to land ownership and birth.

nodes.label: Meritocracy
nodes.summary: A meritocracy is an idealized stratification system in which social position is allocated purely on the basis of ability and effort.

nodes.label: Open vs. closed systems
nodes.summary: Open systems permit movement between strata based on achievement, whereas closed systems fix position by ascription and allow little mobility.

nodes.label: Slavery
nodes.summary: Slavery is a closed system in which some people are legally owned as the property of others.

nodes.label: Social closure
nodes.summary: Social closure is the process by which a group restricts access to resources and opportunities to its own members, excluding outsiders.

nodes.label: Theoretical perspectives on inequality
nodes.summary: Functionalist, conflict, and symbolic-interactionist theories offer competing explanations for why stratification exists and persists.

nodes.label: Bourgeoisie vs. proletariat
nodes.summary: Marx divided capitalist society into the bourgeoisie, who own the means of production, and the proletariat, who sell their labor.

nodes.label: Conflict theory (Marx) of inequality
nodes.summary: Conflict theory views stratification as the product of class conflict in which the dominant class exploits and controls subordinate classes.

nodes.label: Davis-Moore thesis (functionalist)
nodes.summary: The Davis-Moore thesis holds that stratification is functional because it ensures the most important and demanding roles are filled by the most qualified people through unequal rewards.

nodes.label: Exploitation and surplus value
nodes.summary: Marx argued capitalists extract surplus value by paying workers less than the value their labor produces, the basis of exploitation.

nodes.label: Fundamental cause theory
nodes.summary: Fundamental cause theory holds that SES remains a root cause of health inequality because the advantaged deploy flexible resources to protect health as risks change.

nodes.label: Means of production
nodes.summary: The means of production are the resources—land, factories, capital—used to produce goods; ownership of them defines class in Marxist theory.

nodes.label: Symbolic interactionist view of inequality
nodes.summary: Symbolic interactionism examines how stratification is reproduced through everyday interaction, conspicuous consumption, and status symbols.

nodes.label: Weber's three dimensions of stratification
nodes.summary: Weber argued stratification has three dimensions—class (wealth), status (prestige), and party (power)—rather than economics alone.

nodes.label: Health and healthcare disparities
nodes.summary: Social inequality produces systematic differences in health status and healthcare access across SES, race, and gender, mediated by the social determinants of health.

nodes.label: Healthcare access and disparities
nodes.summary: Disparities arise from unequal access to care, differential insurance coverage, and group-specific outcome gaps.

nodes.label: Gender health disparities
nodes.summary: Health outcomes and treatment differ by gender due to biological, behavioral, and social factors.

nodes.label: Healthcare access barriers
nodes.summary: Barriers such as cost, insurance status, geography, and provider availability produce unequal access to healthcare.

nodes.label: Healthcare deserts / provider shortage
nodes.summary: Healthcare deserts are areas with too few providers or facilities, limiting access for rural and low-income populations.

nodes.label: Insurance coverage disparity
nodes.summary: Differences in health insurance coverage across class and race groups strongly affect access to and use of medical care.

nodes.label: Life expectancy and mortality gaps
nodes.summary: Disadvantaged groups show shorter life expectancy and higher morbidity and mortality than advantaged groups.

nodes.label: Racial and ethnic health disparities
nodes.summary: Racial and ethnic minorities often experience worse health outcomes and access due to structural and interpersonal discrimination.

nodes.label: SES and health outcomes
nodes.summary: Lower socioeconomic status is associated with higher rates of chronic disease, disability, and mortality.

nodes.label: Social determinants of health
nodes.summary: Health outcomes are shaped by social and economic conditions—income, education, environment, and access—captured in the social-gradient concept.

nodes.label: Health disparity defined
nodes.summary: A health disparity is a preventable difference in disease, injury, or health outcomes experienced by a disadvantaged group.

nodes.label: Health equity vs. equality
nodes.summary: Health equity means everyone has a fair opportunity to be healthy, addressing need rather than treating everyone identically.

nodes.label: Social gradient in health
nodes.summary: The social gradient in health is the stepwise pattern in which higher socioeconomic position predicts better health at every level.

nodes.label: Social determinants of health defined
nodes.summary: Social determinants of health are the conditions in which people are born, grow, live, work, and age that shape health outcomes.

nodes.label: Weathering and allostatic load
nodes.summary: Weathering describes how chronic stress from disadvantage accelerates health decline via cumulative physiological wear (allostatic load).

nodes.label: Intersecting bases of inequality
nodes.summary: Class-based inequality intersects with race, gender, and age, compounding disadvantage through overlapping systems of stratification.

nodes.label: Bases and intersection of inequality
nodes.summary: Inequality is organized along multiple axes—class, race, gender, age—that combine to shape outcomes.

nodes.label: Age stratification
nodes.summary: Age stratification ranks people and allocates resources and roles according to age, producing inequalities across the life course.

nodes.label: Class, race, and gender stratification
nodes.summary: Stratification operates simultaneously along class, race, and gender lines, each producing distinct inequalities.

nodes.label: Disability and inequality
nodes.summary: People with disabilities face stratification through barriers to access, employment, and full participation in social life.

nodes.label: Gender inequality (glass ceiling, second shift)
nodes.summary: Gender inequality includes the glass ceiling limiting women's advancement, occupational segregation, the wage gap, and the second shift of unpaid domestic labor.

nodes.label: Intersectionality
nodes.summary: Intersectionality is the idea that overlapping social identities (race, class, gender) combine to create unique experiences of advantage and disadvantage.

nodes.label: Social mobility
nodes.summary: Social mobility is movement within or between strata, characterized by direction, frame of reference, and the openness of the stratification system.

nodes.label: Reproduction of class position
nodes.summary: Inheritance of wealth, cultural capital, and limited mobility tend to reproduce class position across generations.

nodes.label: Cumulative advantage (Matthew effect)
nodes.summary: Cumulative advantage, or the Matthew effect, describes how early advantages compound over time so the advantaged pull further ahead.

nodes.label: Educational tracking
nodes.summary: Tracking sorts students into ability or curricular groups that can reinforce class differences and constrain mobility.

nodes.label: Meritocracy and mobility limits
nodes.summary: Although class systems permit mobility, structural barriers limit how fully outcomes reflect pure merit.

nodes.label: Social reproduction
nodes.summary: Social reproduction is the transmission of social inequality from one generation to the next through institutions like family and education.

nodes.label: Types of mobility
nodes.summary: Mobility is classified by direction (upward/downward), reference point (intra- vs. intergenerational), and structural cause.

nodes.label: Exchange (individual) mobility
nodes.summary: Exchange mobility is individual movement between strata that leaves the overall distribution unchanged as people trade positions.

nodes.label: Horizontal mobility
nodes.summary: Horizontal mobility is a change in position or occupation that does not alter one's overall social class.

nodes.label: Intergenerational mobility
nodes.summary: Intergenerational mobility is a change in social position from one generation to the next (parents to children).

nodes.label: Intragenerational mobility
nodes.summary: Intragenerational mobility is a change in social position occurring within a single person's lifetime.

nodes.label: Structural mobility
nodes.summary: Structural mobility is movement of many people caused by changes in society or the economy rather than individual effort.

nodes.label: Upward vs. downward mobility
nodes.summary: Upward mobility is a rise in social position while downward mobility is a fall to a lower position.

nodes.label: Vertical mobility
nodes.summary: Vertical mobility is movement up or down the social hierarchy that changes a person's class position.

nodes.label: Poverty
nodes.summary: Poverty is deprivation of resources defined absolutely or relatively, measured by poverty lines, and concentrated through social and structural processes.

nodes.label: Causes and consequences of poverty
nodes.summary: Poverty is perpetuated by cyclical processes, blame attributions, and social exclusion that compound disadvantage.

nodes.label: Blaming the victim vs. structural causes
nodes.summary: Explanations of poverty differ between blaming individuals (the victim) and identifying structural, societal causes.

nodes.label: Culture of poverty
nodes.summary: The culture-of-poverty thesis attributes persistent poverty to values and behaviors said to develop among the poor, a controversial and often criticized view.

nodes.label: Cycle of poverty
nodes.summary: The cycle of poverty is a self-perpetuating set of conditions that trap families in poverty across generations.

nodes.label: Social exclusion / marginalization
nodes.summary: Social exclusion is the process by which individuals or groups are systematically blocked from rights, opportunities, and resources.

nodes.label: Social welfare and safety net
nodes.summary: Social welfare programs and the safety net are government and institutional supports intended to reduce poverty and buffer hardship.

nodes.label: Defining and measuring poverty
nodes.summary: Poverty can be absolute or relative and is operationalized with poverty thresholds and rates.

nodes.label: Absolute poverty
nodes.summary: Absolute poverty is a lack of the resources needed for basic survival, such as food, shelter, and clean water.

nodes.label: Feminization of poverty
nodes.summary: The feminization of poverty is the trend in which women, especially female-headed households, make up a disproportionate share of the poor.

nodes.label: Poverty line / threshold
nodes.summary: The poverty line is an income threshold below which a person or family is officially classified as poor.

nodes.label: Relative deprivation
nodes.summary: Relative deprivation is the perception of being worse off compared with a reference group, regardless of absolute living standard.

nodes.label: Relative poverty
nodes.summary: Relative poverty is a standard of living below that of the surrounding society, defined relative to its average.

nodes.label: Situational vs. generational poverty
nodes.summary: Situational poverty is temporary deprivation triggered by an event, whereas generational poverty persists across two or more generations.

nodes.label: Social class and socioeconomic status
nodes.summary: Social class reflects economic and social standing measured by income, wealth, education, occupation, and power, and shapes culture, mobility, and prestige.

nodes.label: Class structure
nodes.summary: Modern class structures are commonly described by upper, middle, working, and lower strata, with the middle class often subdivided.

nodes.label: Class consciousness
nodes.summary: Class consciousness is awareness of one's own social class and of shared class interests, a concept central to Marxist theory.

nodes.label: False consciousness
nodes.summary: False consciousness is a Marxist concept describing a subordinate class's misperception of its true position and interests within the stratification system.

nodes.label: Lower class / underclass
nodes.summary: The lower class or underclass has the least access to income, wealth, and opportunity and often faces persistent poverty.

nodes.label: Middle class
nodes.summary: The middle class comprises professionals and white-collar workers with moderate income and education, often split into upper- and lower-middle tiers.

nodes.label: Upper class
nodes.summary: The upper class holds the greatest concentration of wealth, power, and prestige, often through inherited assets.

nodes.label: Working class
nodes.summary: The working class consists largely of manual and blue-collar laborers with limited job security and lower income.

nodes.label: The working poor
nodes.summary: The working poor are people who are employed but whose earnings still fall below or near the poverty line.

nodes.label: Cultural and social capital
nodes.summary: Beyond money, people hold cultural and social capital—knowledge, tastes, and networks—that confer advantage and reproduce class position.

nodes.label: Anomie and strain
nodes.summary: Anomie is a state of normlessness arising when cultural goals (e.g., wealth) outstrip the legitimate means available to achieve them.

nodes.label: Conspicuous consumption
nodes.summary: Conspicuous consumption is the purchase of goods and services to publicly display wealth and signal social status.

nodes.label: Cultural capital
nodes.summary: Cultural capital is the non-financial social assets—education, tastes, skills, and credentials—that promote social mobility and signal class.

nodes.label: Economic capital
nodes.summary: Economic capital is the material and financial resources—income, wealth, property—that an individual or group controls.

nodes.label: Opportunity hoarding
nodes.summary: Opportunity hoarding is the use of social and cultural capital by advantaged groups to monopolize valuable opportunities for their members.

nodes.label: Social capital
nodes.summary: Social capital is the value derived from one's social networks, relationships, and group memberships.

nodes.label: Socioeconomic status (SES)
nodes.summary: SES summarizes a person's economic and social position using income, wealth, education, occupation, and power as component measures.

nodes.label: SES defined
nodes.summary: Socioeconomic status is a combined measure of an individual's or group's economic and social position relative to others.

nodes.label: Education as an SES component
nodes.summary: Educational attainment is a key SES indicator strongly correlated with income, occupation, and health.

nodes.label: Gini coefficient
nodes.summary: The Gini coefficient is a 0-to-1 statistical measure of inequality, where 0 is perfect equality and 1 is maximal concentration.

nodes.label: Income inequality
nodes.summary: Income inequality is the uneven distribution of income across a population, frequently widening between top and bottom earners.

nodes.label: Income vs. wealth
nodes.summary: Income is money received over time (wages, salary), whereas wealth is the total value of assets owned minus debts.

nodes.label: Occupational prestige
nodes.summary: Occupations carry differing levels of prestige that contribute to a person's overall social standing.

nodes.label: Power as an SES component
nodes.summary: Control over resources and the ability to influence others is a dimension of socioeconomic standing beyond money alone.

nodes.label: Wealth inequality
nodes.summary: Wealth inequality—the uneven distribution of accumulated assets—is typically more extreme and persistent than income inequality.

nodes.label: Spatial inequality
nodes.summary: Spatial inequality is the unequal distribution of resources and services across physical space—residential, environmental, and global.

nodes.label: Environmental justice
nodes.summary: The burdens of environmental hazards and the benefits of environmental goods are distributed unequally across groups and places.

nodes.label: Digital divide
nodes.summary: The digital divide is the gap between groups with and without access to information technology and the internet, reproducing inequality.

nodes.label: Environmental justice
nodes.summary: Environmental justice concerns the fair distribution of environmental benefits and burdens across social groups.

nodes.label: Environmental racism
nodes.summary: Environmental racism is the disproportionate exposure of racial minority and low-income communities to environmental hazards.

nodes.label: Food deserts
nodes.summary: A food desert is an area with limited access to affordable, nutritious food, contributing to diet-related health disparities.

nodes.label: Food insecurity
nodes.summary: Food insecurity is the unreliable access to sufficient, affordable, nutritious food, a marker of deprivation linked to spatial inequality.

nodes.label: Global inequality
nodes.summary: Inequality also exists between nations, often described through core-periphery and world-systems frameworks.

nodes.label: Global stratification
nodes.summary: Global stratification is the unequal distribution of wealth, power, and resources among the nations of the world.

nodes.label: Patterns of inequality (global vs. domestic)
nodes.summary: Patterns of social inequality appear both within societies (domestic) and between nations (global), shaped by similar processes of stratification.

nodes.label: World-systems / core-periphery
nodes.summary: World-systems theory divides nations into wealthy core, poor periphery, and intermediate semi-periphery linked by exploitative economic ties.

nodes.label: Residential segregation and neighborhoods
nodes.summary: Where people live shapes their access to resources through segregation, neighborhood effects, and patterns of urban and suburban development.

nodes.label: Concentrated poverty / ghettoization
nodes.summary: Concentrated poverty is the geographic clustering of poor households, compounding disadvantage through eroded services and isolation.

nodes.label: Gentrification
nodes.summary: Gentrification is the influx of wealthier residents into a lower-income area, raising costs and often displacing original residents.

nodes.label: Neighborhood effects
nodes.summary: Neighborhood effects are the influences a community's resources and conditions exert on the life outcomes of its residents.

nodes.label: Neighborhood safety and exposure to violence
nodes.summary: Unequal exposure to crime and violence across neighborhoods is a key channel through which residential segregation harms health and life chances.

nodes.label: Redlining
nodes.summary: Redlining is the historical practice of denying loans, insurance, or services to residents of areas deemed risky, often along racial lines, entrenching segregation.

nodes.label: Residential segregation
nodes.summary: Residential segregation is the physical separation of groups, often by race or class, into different neighborhoods.

nodes.label: Suburbanization and urban sprawl
nodes.summary: Suburbanization and sprawl shift population and resources outward from urban cores, often concentrating disadvantage in inner cities.

nodes.label: Urbanization
nodes.summary: Urbanization is the increasing concentration of population in cities, reshaping access to resources and services.

nodes.label: White flight
nodes.summary: White flight is the large-scale movement of white residents out of racially diversifying urban areas to suburbs, deepening segregation.

nodes.label: Chemical Senses (Taste & Smell)
nodes.summary: Gustation and olfaction use chemoreceptors to detect dissolved or airborne chemicals.

nodes.label: Flavor (Taste-Smell Integration)
nodes.summary: Flavor is the multimodal percept produced by combining gustation, olfaction (often retronasal), and texture/temperature into a unified experience.

nodes.label: Smell (Olfaction)
nodes.summary: Detection of airborne chemical molecules.

nodes.label: Olfaction's Link to Memory & Emotion
nodes.summary: Olfaction's direct limbic connections give smell a strong, immediate link to memory and emotion.

nodes.label: Olfactory Bulb & Direct Cortical Route
nodes.summary: Olfactory signals project via the olfactory bulb directly to the cortex and limbic system, bypassing the thalamus.

nodes.label: Olfactory Receptor Neurons
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal epithelium bind odorant molecules and transduce them into neural signals.

nodes.label: Pheromones
nodes.summary: Pheromones are chemical signals released by one organism that can influence the behavior or physiology of another.

nodes.label: Taste (Gustation)
nodes.summary: Detection of chemicals dissolved in saliva.

nodes.label: Five Basic Tastes
nodes.summary: The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami (savory).

nodes.label: Gustatory Pathway
nodes.summary: Taste signals travel via cranial nerves to the brainstem and thalamus, reaching the gustatory cortex (insula/frontal operculum).

nodes.label: Taster Variation (Supertasters)
nodes.summary: Individuals vary in taste sensitivity; supertasters have high papillae/receptor density and heightened sensitivity to bitter compounds (e.g., PTC/PROP).

nodes.label: Taste Buds & Receptor Cells
nodes.summary: Taste buds on the tongue's papillae contain gustatory receptor cells that transduce dissolved chemicals.

nodes.label: Foundations of Sensation & Perception
nodes.summary: Sensation is the detection of stimuli by sensory receptors, while perception is the brain's interpretation and organization of that sensory information.

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation
nodes.summary: Diminished sensitivity to constant, unchanging stimuli.

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is the decrease in sensitivity (receptor response) to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time.

nodes.label: Sensory Adaptation vs. Habituation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is a receptor-level decline in responding, whereas habituation is a central/learned decrease in behavioral response; the two are distinct mechanisms.

nodes.label: Tonic vs. Phasic Receptors
nodes.summary: Tonic receptors adapt slowly and fire continuously to sustained stimuli, whereas phasic receptors adapt quickly and respond mainly to changes.

nodes.label: Core Distinctions
nodes.summary: The basic vocabulary distinguishing how stimuli are detected versus interpreted.

nodes.label: Bottom-Up Processing
nodes.summary: Bottom-up (data-driven) processing builds perception starting from raw sensory input and assembling it into a whole.

nodes.label: Perception
nodes.summary: Perception is the brain's processing, organization, and interpretation of sensory information into a meaningful experience.

nodes.label: Sensation
nodes.summary: Sensation is the process by which sensory receptors detect physical stimulus energy from the environment.

nodes.label: Top-Down Processing
nodes.summary: Top-down (theory-driven) processing uses prior knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret sensory input.

nodes.label: Sensory Pathways & Coding
nodes.summary: The general route sensory information travels from receptor to cortex.

nodes.label: Afferent (Sensory) Neurons
nodes.summary: Afferent neurons carry sensory information from receptors toward the central nervous system.

nodes.label: First-, Second-, Third-Order Neurons
nodes.summary: Most sensory pathways relay through first-order (receptor to spinal cord/brainstem), second-order (to thalamus), and third-order (to cortex) neurons.

nodes.label: Cortical Projection Areas
nodes.summary: Each sense projects to a dedicated primary cortex: visual (occipital), auditory (temporal), somatosensory (parietal), gustatory (insula/frontal), olfactory (olfactory cortex).

nodes.label: Thalamus as Sensory Relay
nodes.summary: The thalamus relays nearly all sensory information (except smell) to the appropriate primary sensory cortex.

nodes.label: Sensory Transduction
nodes.summary: How physical stimulus energy is converted into neural signals.

nodes.label: Transduction
nodes.summary: Transduction is the conversion of physical or chemical stimulus energy into an electrochemical neural signal (action potentials).

nodes.label: Labeled Lines & Specific Nerve Energies
nodes.summary: The doctrine of specific nerve energies (labeled-line coding) holds that the modality perceived depends on which dedicated pathway is activated, not on the stimulus itself.

nodes.label: Receptive Field
nodes.summary: A receptive field is the region of sensory space (e.g., area of skin or retina) in which a stimulus alters a given sensory neuron's firing; smaller fields yield finer acuity.

nodes.label: Receptor (Generator) Potential
nodes.summary: A receptor potential is a graded change in membrane potential that, if sufficient, triggers an action potential in a sensory neuron.

nodes.label: Sensory Receptor Types
nodes.summary: Receptors are classified by stimulus: photoreceptors (light), mechanoreceptors (pressure/movement), chemoreceptors (chemicals), thermoreceptors (temperature), and nociceptors (pain).

nodes.label: Sensory Coding
nodes.summary: Stimulus intensity is coded by action-potential frequency and number of activated receptors, while stimulus type is coded by which receptors/pathways fire (labeled lines).

nodes.label: Hearing (Audition)
nodes.summary: The auditory system transduces sound-wave vibrations into neural signals encoding pitch, loudness, and location.

nodes.label: Inner Ear & Transduction
nodes.summary: Where mechanical vibration becomes a neural signal.

nodes.label: Basilar Membrane
nodes.summary: The basilar membrane vibrates within the cochlea, with different regions resonating to different frequencies (tonotopic organization).

nodes.label: Cochlea
nodes.summary: The cochlea is the fluid-filled, coiled inner-ear structure that houses the organ of hearing.

nodes.label: Hair Cell Stereocilia Bending
nodes.summary: Bending of hair-cell stereocilia opens ion channels, depolarizing the cell and triggering auditory nerve signals.

nodes.label: Organ of Corti & Hair Cells
nodes.summary: The organ of Corti sits on the basilar membrane and contains hair cells that transduce vibrations into neural signals.

nodes.label: Round Window
nodes.summary: The round window is a flexible membrane that bulges to relieve fluid pressure as the oval window drives the incompressible cochlear fluid.

nodes.label: Tectorial Membrane
nodes.summary: The tectorial membrane overlies the hair cells; relative motion bends their stereocilia during sound transduction.

nodes.label: Outer & Middle Ear
nodes.summary: Structures that collect and amplify sound.

nodes.label: Ossicles (Malleus, Incus, Stapes)
nodes.summary: The three ossicles—malleus, incus, and stapes—amplify and transmit eardrum vibrations to the oval window.

nodes.label: Oval Window
nodes.summary: The stapes presses on the oval window, transmitting vibrations into the fluid-filled cochlea.

nodes.label: Pinna & Auditory Canal
nodes.summary: The pinna (auricle) gathers sound and funnels it through the auditory canal toward the eardrum.

nodes.label: Tympanic Membrane (Eardrum)
nodes.summary: The tympanic membrane vibrates in response to sound waves, transferring vibrations to the ossicles.

nodes.label: Auditory Pathway & Pitch Theories
nodes.summary: How auditory signals reach cortex and how pitch is encoded.

nodes.label: Auditory Nerve to Cortex
nodes.summary: The vestibulocochlear nerve carries auditory signals via the brainstem and medial geniculate nucleus to the auditory cortex in the temporal lobe.

nodes.label: Frequency (Temporal) Theory
nodes.summary: Frequency theory holds that pitch is encoded by the rate of neural firing matching the sound's frequency, best for low frequencies.

nodes.label: Conductive vs. Sensorineural Hearing Loss
nodes.summary: Conductive loss involves outer/middle-ear transmission failure, while sensorineural loss involves cochlear hair-cell or auditory-nerve damage.

nodes.label: Place Theory
nodes.summary: Place theory holds that pitch is determined by which location on the basilar membrane vibrates most, best for high frequencies.

nodes.label: Sound Localization
nodes.summary: Sound is localized using interaural time and intensity differences between the two ears.

nodes.label: Tonotopic Organization
nodes.summary: Tonotopy is the orderly frequency-to-place mapping preserved from the basilar membrane through to the auditory cortex.

nodes.label: Volley Principle
nodes.summary: The volley principle extends frequency theory: groups of neurons fire in alternating volleys to encode frequencies above a single neuron's firing limit.

nodes.label: Sound Wave Properties
nodes.summary: The physical characteristics of sound that map to auditory experience.

nodes.label: Amplitude & Loudness
nodes.summary: Sound-wave amplitude (measured in decibels) determines perceived loudness.

nodes.label: Frequency & Pitch
nodes.summary: Sound-wave frequency (Hz) determines perceived pitch; humans hear roughly 20–20,000 Hz.

nodes.label: Timbre
nodes.summary: Timbre is the perceived quality of a sound arising from its mixture of frequencies, distinguishing sources of the same pitch.

nodes.label: Perceptual Organization & Constancies
nodes.summary: The brain organizes raw sensory input into coherent, stable percepts of form, depth, motion, and constancy.

nodes.label: Perceptual Constancy
nodes.summary: Perceiving objects as stable despite changing sensory input.

nodes.label: Perceptual Constancy (Overview)
nodes.summary: Perceptual constancy is perceiving objects as unchanging in size, shape, color, and lightness despite changes in retinal images.

nodes.label: Shape, Color & Lightness Constancy
nodes.summary: Shape, color, and lightness constancies preserve perceived form and appearance despite changes in viewing angle or illumination.

nodes.label: Size Constancy
nodes.summary: Size constancy is perceiving an object as the same size regardless of changes in its retinal image with distance.

nodes.label: Depth Perception
nodes.summary: Cues that yield three-dimensional perception from two-dimensional retinal input.

nodes.label: Binocular Cues
nodes.summary: Binocular depth cues use both eyes: retinal disparity (differing images) and convergence (inward eye rotation for near objects).

nodes.label: Convergence
nodes.summary: Convergence is the inward turning of the eyes for nearby objects, providing a depth cue.

nodes.label: Monocular Cues
nodes.summary: Monocular cues (one eye) include relative size, interposition, linear perspective, texture gradient, relative height, and motion parallax.

nodes.label: Retinal (Binocular) Disparity
nodes.summary: Retinal disparity is the difference between the two eyes' images, which the brain uses to compute depth.

nodes.label: Extrasensory Perception (ESP) & Parapsychology
nodes.summary: ESP is the claimed perception of stimuli without sensory input (telepathy, clairvoyance, precognition); parapsychology studies it but it lacks reproducible scientific support.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles
nodes.summary: Rules by which the brain groups elements into wholes.

nodes.label: Closure
nodes.summary: Closure is the Gestalt principle that we fill in gaps to perceive a complete, whole object.

nodes.label: Common Fate
nodes.summary: Common fate is the Gestalt principle that elements moving together in the same direction are perceived as a single group.

nodes.label: Continuity (Good Continuation)
nodes.summary: Continuity is the Gestalt principle that we perceive smooth, continuous patterns rather than abrupt breaks.

nodes.label: Figure-Ground
nodes.summary: Figure-ground organization is the perceptual separation of an object (figure) from its background (ground).

nodes.label: Law of Prägnanz
nodes.summary: The law of Prägnanz holds that we perceive ambiguous stimuli in the simplest, most stable form possible.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles (Overview)
nodes.summary: Gestalt principles describe how the brain organizes parts into a whole, where the whole is perceived as more than the sum of its parts.

nodes.label: Proximity
nodes.summary: Proximity is the Gestalt principle that elements close together are perceived as a group.

nodes.label: Similarity
nodes.summary: Similarity is the Gestalt principle that similar elements are grouped together.

nodes.label: Symmetry
nodes.summary: Symmetry (prägnanz/good form) is the Gestalt tendency to perceive figures as simple, orderly, and symmetrical.

nodes.label: Top-Down Influences on Perception
nodes.summary: How expectations and context shape what we perceive.

nodes.label: Context Effects
nodes.summary: Context effects describe how the surrounding environment influences perception of a stimulus.

nodes.label: Culture, Emotion & Motivation Effects
nodes.summary: Cultural background, emotional state, and motivation bias perception, illustrating that top-down factors extend beyond simple expectation.

nodes.label: Perceptual Set
nodes.summary: A perceptual set is a mental predisposition to perceive one thing and not another, shaped by experience and expectation.

nodes.label: Schemata & Prior Experience
nodes.summary: Schemata are mental frameworks from experience that guide top-down interpretation of sensory input.

nodes.label: Motion Perception & Illusions
nodes.summary: Perception of movement and systematic perceptual errors.

nodes.label: Motion Perception
nodes.summary: Motion perception detects movement from changes across the retina and is supported by dedicated motion-sensitive pathways.

nodes.label: Perceptual Illusions
nodes.summary: Perceptual illusions (e.g., Müller-Lyer, moon illusion, Ponzo) reveal the brain's reliance on cues and assumptions that can misfire.

nodes.label: Phi Phenomenon & Apparent Motion
nodes.summary: The phi phenomenon is illusory (apparent) motion perceived when stationary lights flash in sequence, as in motion pictures.

nodes.label: Psychophysics
nodes.summary: Psychophysics studies the relationship between the physical magnitude of stimuli and the psychological sensations they produce.

nodes.label: Psychophysical Laws
nodes.summary: Quantitative relations between stimulus magnitude and perceived intensity.

nodes.label: Fechner's Law
nodes.summary: Fechner's law states perceived sensation magnitude grows logarithmically with stimulus intensity (S = k log I).

nodes.label: Stevens' Power Law
nodes.summary: Stevens' power law states perceived magnitude is a power function of stimulus intensity, fitting some sensations better than Fechner's log relation.

nodes.label: Weber's Law
nodes.summary: Weber's law states the JND is a constant proportion (Weber fraction, ΔI/I = k) of the original stimulus intensity, not a fixed amount.

nodes.label: Signal Detection Theory
nodes.summary: How detection decisions reflect both sensitivity and non-sensory factors like expectation and motivation.

nodes.label: Noise
nodes.summary: Noise is irrelevant internal or external activity against which a true signal must be detected.

nodes.label: Hits, Misses, False Alarms, Correct Rejections
nodes.summary: SDT outcomes are hits, misses, false alarms, and correct rejections, arranged by whether a signal was present and whether the observer responded.

nodes.label: Signal Detection Theory (SDT)
nodes.summary: Signal detection theory explains how internal (psychological/contextual) and external factors influence whether a stimulus is detected against background noise.

nodes.label: Response Bias (Criterion)
nodes.summary: Response bias is the observer's decision criterion, shifted by motivation/expectation, separate from true sensory sensitivity (d').

nodes.label: Sensitivity (d')
nodes.summary: Sensitivity (d-prime) quantifies how well an observer can discriminate signal from noise, independent of their response criterion/bias.

nodes.label: Thresholds
nodes.summary: The minimum stimulus levels needed for detection or discrimination.

nodes.label: Absolute Threshold
nodes.summary: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

nodes.label: Difference Threshold (JND)
nodes.summary: The difference threshold, or just-noticeable difference (JND), is the minimum change in stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

nodes.label: Subliminal Stimuli
nodes.summary: Subliminal stimuli fall below the absolute threshold of conscious perception but may still be detected and weakly influence processing.

nodes.label: Threshold of Conscious Perception
nodes.summary: The threshold of conscious perception is the intensity above which a stimulus reaches awareness; below it stimuli may still be processed unconsciously.

nodes.label: Somatosensation
nodes.summary: Somatosensation encompasses touch, pressure, temperature, pain, and body position from skin and internal receptors.

nodes.label: Somatosensory Cortex
nodes.summary: Cortical mapping of body sensation.

nodes.label: Sensory Homunculus
nodes.summary: The somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus, parietal lobe) maps the body, with area proportional to sensitivity rather than size (homunculus).

nodes.label: Proprioception & Kinesthesia
nodes.summary: Sensing body position and movement.

nodes.label: Proprioception
nodes.summary: Proprioception is the sense of the relative position and movement of body parts, via receptors in muscles, tendons, and joints.

nodes.label: Kinesthesia
nodes.summary: Kinesthesia is the sense of body movement and limb motion, closely tied to proprioception.

nodes.label: Muscle Spindles & Golgi Tendon Organs
nodes.summary: Muscle spindles detect muscle stretch and Golgi tendon organs detect muscle tension, providing proprioceptive feedback.

nodes.label: Temperature & Pain
nodes.summary: Thermoreceptors and nociceptors detecting temperature and tissue damage.

nodes.label: Free Nerve Endings
nodes.summary: Free nerve endings are unencapsulated afferent terminals in the skin that serve as the primary receptors for temperature and pain.

nodes.label: Gate Control Theory of Pain
nodes.summary: Gate control theory proposes that a spinal 'gate' can block or allow pain signals, modulated by non-painful input and top-down brain signals.

nodes.label: Nociceptors
nodes.summary: Nociceptors are free nerve endings that detect noxious stimuli (mechanical, thermal, chemical) and signal pain.

nodes.label: Referred & Phantom Pain
nodes.summary: Referred pain is perceived at a site distinct from its source due to shared pathways, while phantom limb pain is felt in a missing limb, illustrating central pain processing.

nodes.label: Thermoreceptors
nodes.summary: Thermoreceptors detect warmth and cold, responding most to changes in skin temperature.

nodes.label: Touch & Pressure Receptors
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors in the skin that detect physical contact.

nodes.label: Cutaneous Mechanoreceptors
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors such as Merkel cells, Meissner's, Pacinian, and Ruffini corpuscles detect touch, pressure, vibration, and stretch.

nodes.label: Pacinian Corpuscles
nodes.summary: Pacinian corpuscles are deep, rapidly-adapting receptors that detect deep pressure and high-frequency vibration.

nodes.label: Two-Point Discrimination
nodes.summary: Two-point discrimination measures tactile acuity, which is finest where receptor density and cortical representation are greatest (e.g., fingertips, lips).

nodes.label: Vestibular Sense (Equilibrioception)
nodes.summary: The vestibular system in the inner ear detects head position, balance, and acceleration.

nodes.label: Balance & Proprioceptive Integration
nodes.summary: How vestibular input supports equilibrium.

nodes.label: Equilibrium & Balance
nodes.summary: The vestibular system integrates with vision and proprioception to maintain balance and a stable sense of body orientation.

nodes.label: Motion Sickness & Sensory Conflict
nodes.summary: Motion sickness arises from sensory-conflict (mismatch) between vestibular and visual cues about self-motion.

nodes.label: Vestibulo-Ocular Reflex
nodes.summary: The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze by moving the eyes opposite to head movement.

nodes.label: Vestibular Structures
nodes.summary: Inner-ear organs that sense rotation and linear movement.

nodes.label: Vestibular Hair Cells
nodes.summary: Hair cells in vestibular organs transduce fluid (endolymph) movement into neural signals about head motion.

nodes.label: Otolithic Organs (Utricle & Saccule)
nodes.summary: The utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and head position relative to gravity via otoliths.

nodes.label: Semicircular Canals
nodes.summary: The three semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration of the head in three planes.

nodes.label: Vision
nodes.summary: The visual system transduces light into neural signals processed for color, form, motion, and depth.

nodes.label: Color Vision Theories
nodes.summary: Complementary theories explaining how color is perceived.

nodes.label: Afterimages
nodes.summary: Negative afterimages occur when opponent-process channels fatigue, producing the complementary color after staring at a stimulus.

nodes.label: Color Blindness
nodes.summary: Color blindness, often X-linked, results from missing or defective cone types, most commonly red-green deficiency.

nodes.label: Opponent-Process Theory
nodes.summary: Opponent-process theory holds that color is encoded by opposing pairs (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white), explaining afterimages.

nodes.label: Trichromatic (Young-Helmholtz) Theory
nodes.summary: The trichromatic theory holds that color vision arises from combined activity of three cone types tuned to red, green, and blue.

nodes.label: Eye Anatomy & Light Path
nodes.summary: The structures that focus light onto the retina.

nodes.label: Aqueous Humor
nodes.summary: The aqueous humor is the watery fluid in the anterior chamber that nourishes the cornea and lens and maintains intraocular pressure.

nodes.label: Choroid
nodes.summary: The choroid is the vascular middle layer that supplies blood to the retina and absorbs stray light.

nodes.label: Cornea
nodes.summary: The cornea is the transparent outer layer that performs most of the eye's light refraction.

nodes.label: Iris & Pupil
nodes.summary: The iris is the pigmented muscle that controls pupil size, regulating the amount of light entering the eye.

nodes.label: Lens & Accommodation
nodes.summary: The lens changes shape via the ciliary muscles (accommodation) to focus images at varying distances onto the retina.

nodes.label: Presbyopia & Near/Far Point
nodes.summary: Presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation as the lens stiffens, raising the near point (closest distance of clear focus).

nodes.label: Refractive Errors (Myopia, Hyperopia, Astigmatism)
nodes.summary: Myopia (nearsightedness) focuses images in front of the retina, hyperopia (farsightedness) behind it, and astigmatism reflects an irregularly curved cornea/lens.

nodes.label: Sclera
nodes.summary: The sclera is the tough white outer layer that protects and maintains the shape of the eyeball.

nodes.label: Visual Acuity
nodes.summary: Visual acuity is the sharpness or resolving power of vision, highest at the fovea and dependent on proper refraction onto the retina.

nodes.label: Vitreous Humor
nodes.summary: The vitreous humor is the gel filling the posterior eye that maintains its shape and transmits light to the retina.

nodes.label: Visual Pathways
nodes.summary: How visual signals travel from retina to cortex.

nodes.label: Feature Detectors
nodes.summary: Feature detectors are cortical neurons (e.g., Hubel and Wiesel's) that respond to specific stimulus features like edges, orientation, or movement.

nodes.label: Lateral Geniculate Nucleus
nodes.summary: The lateral geniculate nucleus of the thalamus relays visual information to the primary visual cortex.

nodes.label: Optic Nerve & Optic Chiasm
nodes.summary: Ganglion axons form the optic nerve; at the optic chiasm, nasal-retina fibers cross so each hemisphere processes the contralateral visual field.

nodes.label: Parallel Processing
nodes.summary: Parallel processing simultaneously analyzes separate features of a scene (color, motion, form, depth) along distinct pathways.

nodes.label: Primary Visual Cortex (V1)
nodes.summary: The primary visual cortex in the occipital lobe receives and begins cortical processing of visual input.

nodes.label: Ventral (What) & Dorsal (Where) Streams
nodes.summary: The ventral 'what' stream (to temporal lobe) processes object identity, while the dorsal 'where/how' stream (to parietal lobe) processes spatial location and motion.

nodes.label: Phototransduction
nodes.summary: The molecular conversion of light into a neural signal.

nodes.label: Dark & Light Adaptation
nodes.summary: Dark adaptation regenerates photopigment for dim-light sensitivity, while light adaptation rapidly reduces sensitivity in bright light.

nodes.label: Photoreceptor Hyperpolarization
nodes.summary: Light causes photoreceptors to hyperpolarize and reduce glutamate release, an unusual response to a stimulus.

nodes.label: Photopigments (Rhodopsin)
nodes.summary: Photopigments such as rhodopsin contain retinal bound to opsin; light isomerizes retinal, initiating phototransduction.

nodes.label: Retina & Photoreceptors
nodes.summary: The neural layer that transduces light into signals.

nodes.label: Bipolar & Ganglion Cells
nodes.summary: Bipolar cells relay photoreceptor signals to ganglion cells, whose axons form the optic nerve.

nodes.label: Cones
nodes.summary: Cones are photoreceptors for color and fine-detail (photopic) vision, concentrated in the fovea and active in bright light.

nodes.label: Duplexity (Duplicity) Theory
nodes.summary: Duplexity theory holds that the retina contains two separate systems—rods for low-light/achromatic vision and cones for bright-light/color vision.

nodes.label: Fovea
nodes.summary: The fovea is the cone-dense central retinal pit that provides the sharpest visual acuity.

nodes.label: Horizontal & Amacrine Cells
nodes.summary: Horizontal and amacrine cells provide lateral connections that enable lateral inhibition and edge enhancement in the retina.

nodes.label: Optic Disc (Blind Spot)
nodes.summary: The optic disc is where the optic nerve exits the retina; it lacks photoreceptors, creating the blind spot.

nodes.label: Retinal Cell Layers
nodes.summary: Light passes through ganglion and bipolar cell layers to reach photoreceptors at the back, with signals relayed back forward to ganglion cells.

nodes.label: Rods
nodes.summary: Rods are highly light-sensitive photoreceptors for dim-light (scotopic) and peripheral vision, but do not detect color.

nodes.label: Three Cone Types (S, M, L)
nodes.summary: Three cone types respond maximally to short (blue), medium (green), and long (red) wavelengths, underlying trichromatic color vision.

nodes.label: Attention
nodes.summary: Attention selectively allocates limited perceptual and cognitive resources to some stimuli over others.

nodes.label: Divided attention and multitasking
nodes.summary: Performing tasks simultaneously is limited by capacity and depends on automaticity and task similarity.

nodes.label: Automatic vs. controlled processing
nodes.summary: Automatic processing requires little attention or effort, whereas controlled processing demands focused conscious attention.

nodes.label: Divided attention
nodes.summary: Divided attention is the allocation of attention across multiple tasks or stimuli at once.

nodes.label: Multitasking and task-switching cost
nodes.summary: Attempting concurrent demanding tasks incurs performance and reaction-time costs from limited capacity and task switching.

nodes.label: Stroop effect
nodes.summary: The Stroop effect is the slowed naming of ink color when it conflicts with the printed color word, demonstrating automatic reading.

nodes.label: Sustained attention (vigilance)
nodes.summary: Sustained attention is the ability to maintain focus on a task over time; performance declines (the vigilance decrement) during prolonged monitoring.

nodes.label: Filter and resource models
nodes.summary: Bottleneck and resource theories explain how and when unattended information is filtered out.

nodes.label: Broadbent's early-selection filter
nodes.summary: Broadbent's filter model proposes selection occurs early, blocking unattended input before meaning is processed.

nodes.label: Late-selection models
nodes.summary: Late-selection models propose all input is processed for meaning before selection determines what reaches awareness.

nodes.label: Resource (capacity) model
nodes.summary: Resource models treat attention as a limited pool of capacity allocated across competing tasks.

nodes.label: Treisman's attenuation model
nodes.summary: Treisman's model proposes unattended input is attenuated rather than fully blocked, allowing salient words through.

nodes.label: Selective attention
nodes.summary: Selective attention filters the flood of sensory input to focus on relevant stimuli.

nodes.label: Change blindness
nodes.summary: Change blindness is the failure to detect changes in a visual scene when attention is not directed to them.

nodes.label: Cocktail party effect
nodes.summary: The cocktail party effect is the ability to focus on one voice amid background noise while still detecting salient cues like one's name.

nodes.label: Selective attention
nodes.summary: Selective attention is the focusing of conscious awareness on a particular stimulus while ignoring others.

nodes.label: Dichotic listening and shadowing
nodes.summary: In dichotic listening, different messages are played to each ear and the participant shadows one, revealing how little of the unattended channel is processed.

nodes.label: Inattentional blindness
nodes.summary: Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected stimulus when attention is engaged elsewhere.

nodes.label: Spotlight model of attention
nodes.summary: The spotlight model treats visual attention as a movable focus that enhances processing of whatever falls within it.

nodes.label: Hearing (audition)
nodes.summary: The auditory system converts sound pressure waves into neural signals encoding pitch, loudness, and location.

nodes.label: Auditory pathway and localization
nodes.summary: Auditory signals ascend to the cortex while interaural cues enable localization of sound sources.

nodes.label: Auditory cortex
nodes.summary: The primary auditory cortex in the temporal lobe processes and interprets sound.

nodes.label: Auditory (vestibulocochlear) nerve
nodes.summary: The vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) carries auditory signals from the cochlea toward the brainstem.

nodes.label: Conductive vs. sensorineural hearing loss
nodes.summary: Conductive loss results from impaired sound transmission through the outer/middle ear; sensorineural loss results from cochlear hair-cell or auditory-nerve damage.

nodes.label: Sound localization (interaural cues)
nodes.summary: Sound is localized using interaural time and intensity differences between the two ears.

nodes.label: Structure of the ear
nodes.summary: The outer, middle, and inner ear progressively collect, amplify, and transduce sound into neural signals.

nodes.label: Basilar membrane
nodes.summary: The basilar membrane vibrates along its length according to sound frequency, supporting tonotopic coding.

nodes.label: Cochlea
nodes.summary: The cochlea is the fluid-filled spiral structure of the inner ear that houses the organ of hearing.

nodes.label: Endolymph and perilymph
nodes.summary: Endolymph and perilymph are the cochlear fluids whose vibration deflects hair-cell stereocilia to drive transduction.

nodes.label: Eustachian tube
nodes.summary: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the pharynx and equalizes middle-ear pressure with the environment.

nodes.label: Organ of Corti and hair cells
nodes.summary: The organ of Corti sits on the basilar membrane and contains the hair cells that transduce vibration into neural signals.

nodes.label: Ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes)
nodes.summary: The three middle-ear bones amplify and transmit eardrum vibrations to the oval window of the cochlea.

nodes.label: Outer ear (pinna, auditory canal)
nodes.summary: The pinna and auditory canal collect and funnel sound waves toward the eardrum.

nodes.label: Oval window
nodes.summary: The oval window is the membrane where the stapes transmits vibration into the fluid of the cochlea.

nodes.label: Round window
nodes.summary: The round window is the membrane that bulges to relieve pressure as fluid waves travel through the cochlea.

nodes.label: Tympanic membrane (eardrum)
nodes.summary: The tympanic membrane vibrates in response to incoming sound waves, transmitting motion to the ossicles.

nodes.label: Pitch perception theories
nodes.summary: Place and temporal codes together explain how the auditory system represents the frequency of a sound.

nodes.label: Frequency (temporal) theory
nodes.summary: Frequency theory holds that pitch is coded by the firing rate of auditory neurons matching the sound's frequency, best for low frequencies.

nodes.label: Place theory
nodes.summary: Place theory holds that pitch is coded by the location along the basilar membrane that vibrates most, best for high frequencies.

nodes.label: Tonotopic organization
nodes.summary: Tonotopy is the orderly mapping of sound frequency onto position along the cochlea and auditory cortex.

nodes.label: Volley principle
nodes.summary: The volley principle holds that groups of neurons fire in alternating volleys to encode frequencies above a single neuron's firing limit.

nodes.label: Properties of sound
nodes.summary: Sound waves carry frequency, amplitude, and timbre information that map onto perceived pitch, loudness, and quality.

nodes.label: Amplitude and loudness
nodes.summary: Amplitude (intensity) of a sound wave determines its perceived loudness, measured in decibels.

nodes.label: Frequency and pitch
nodes.summary: Frequency (cycles per second, Hz) of a sound wave determines its perceived pitch.

nodes.label: Timbre
nodes.summary: Timbre is the perceived quality of a sound arising from its mix of harmonic frequencies.

nodes.label: Other senses
nodes.summary: Somatosensation, taste, smell, and the vestibular and kinesthetic senses provide information about the body and chemical environment.

nodes.label: Taste (gustation)
nodes.summary: Taste receptors on the tongue detect dissolved chemicals corresponding to the basic tastes.

nodes.label: Five basic tastes
nodes.summary: The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami.

nodes.label: Flavor (taste-smell integration)
nodes.summary: Flavor is a multimodal percept combining taste, retronasal smell, and texture; much of perceived flavor depends on olfaction.

nodes.label: Papillae
nodes.summary: Papillae are the bumps on the tongue surface that house taste buds.

nodes.label: Taster status and supertasters
nodes.summary: Individual differences in papillae density and bitter-receptor genes produce non-tasters, tasters, and supertasters with heightened sensitivity.

nodes.label: Taste buds and receptor cells
nodes.summary: Taste buds contain chemoreceptor cells that detect dissolved tastant molecules.

nodes.label: Kinesthetic sense (proprioception)
nodes.summary: Proprioception monitors the position and movement of body parts via receptors in muscles and joints.

nodes.label: Kinesthesis vs. vestibular sense
nodes.summary: Kinesthesis senses body-part position/movement from muscle and joint receptors, whereas the vestibular sense tracks whole-head position and acceleration.

nodes.label: Muscle spindles and Golgi tendon organs
nodes.summary: Muscle spindles detect muscle stretch and Golgi tendon organs detect muscle tension to inform proprioception.

nodes.label: Proprioception
nodes.summary: Proprioception is the sense of the relative position and movement of one's own body parts.

nodes.label: Smell (olfaction)
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptors detect airborne chemicals and project directly to the brain, bypassing the thalamus.

nodes.label: Olfaction–limbic memory link
nodes.summary: Direct olfactory projections to the amygdala and hippocampus give smells a strong, immediate link to emotion and memory.

nodes.label: Olfactory bulb and direct pathway
nodes.summary: Olfactory information projects via the olfactory bulb directly to the cortex and limbic system without a thalamic relay.

nodes.label: Olfactory receptor neurons
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal epithelium bind odorant molecules to transduce smell.

nodes.label: Pheromones
nodes.summary: Pheromones are chemical signals that may influence behavior and physiology of others of the same species.

nodes.label: Somatosensation (touch)
nodes.summary: The skin houses multiple receptor types that detect pressure, temperature, vibration, and pain.

nodes.label: Endorphins and pain modulation
nodes.summary: Endogenous opioids (endorphins) and descending pathways can close the pain gate, reducing perceived pain.

nodes.label: Four touch modalities
nodes.summary: Somatosensation comprises four submodalities: pressure, vibration, temperature, and pain.

nodes.label: Free nerve endings
nodes.summary: Free nerve endings are unencapsulated dendrites that detect pain and temperature (warmth and cold).

nodes.label: Gate theory of pain
nodes.summary: Gate control theory proposes a spinal 'gate' that can block or allow pain signals from reaching the brain.

nodes.label: Meissner corpuscles
nodes.summary: Meissner corpuscles are superficial, rapidly adapting receptors sensitive to light touch and fine detail.

nodes.label: Merkel discs
nodes.summary: Merkel discs are slowly adapting receptors that detect sustained pressure and fine texture.

nodes.label: Pacinian corpuscles
nodes.summary: Pacinian corpuscles are deep, rapidly adapting receptors that detect deep pressure and vibration.

nodes.label: Referred and phantom-limb pain
nodes.summary: Referred pain is felt at a site distant from its source; phantom-limb pain is perceived in a missing limb, illustrating central pain processing.

nodes.label: Ruffini endings
nodes.summary: Ruffini endings are slowly adapting receptors that detect skin stretch.

nodes.label: Two-point discrimination
nodes.summary: Two-point discrimination measures tactile acuity by the minimum separation at which two touches feel distinct, varying with receptor density.

nodes.label: Vestibular sense
nodes.summary: The vestibular system detects head position and acceleration to maintain balance and spatial orientation.

nodes.label: Balance and equilibrium
nodes.summary: Vestibular signals combine with vision and proprioception to maintain balance and equilibrium.

nodes.label: Otolith organs (utricle, saccule)
nodes.summary: The utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and the head's position relative to gravity.

nodes.label: Semicircular canals
nodes.summary: The three semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration of the head.

nodes.label: Vestibulo-ocular reflex
nodes.summary: The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze by driving eye movements opposite to head movement.

nodes.label: Perceptual organization
nodes.summary: The brain organizes sensory input into coherent, stable percepts of objects, depth, motion, and constancy.

nodes.label: Perceptual constancy
nodes.summary: The brain perceives objects as stable in size, shape, and color despite changing retinal input.

nodes.label: Color and brightness constancy
nodes.summary: Color and brightness constancy is perceiving consistent color and lightness despite changes in illumination.

nodes.label: Shape constancy
nodes.summary: Shape constancy is perceiving an object as the same shape despite changes in viewing angle.

nodes.label: Size constancy
nodes.summary: Size constancy is perceiving an object as the same size despite changes in its retinal image with distance.

nodes.label: Constructive perception and illusions
nodes.summary: Perception is an active construction shaped by expectations, context, and prior experience, sometimes producing errors.

nodes.label: Context effects
nodes.summary: Context effects occur when surrounding information influences the interpretation of an ambiguous stimulus.

nodes.label: Visual illusions
nodes.summary: Visual illusions are systematic perceptual errors that reveal the brain's interpretive assumptions.

nodes.label: Müller-Lyer illusion
nodes.summary: The Müller-Lyer illusion makes equal lines with inward vs. outward arrowheads appear unequal, attributed to misapplied size-constancy depth cues.

nodes.label: Perceptual set
nodes.summary: A perceptual set is a mental predisposition to perceive one thing rather than another based on expectations and experience.

nodes.label: Ponzo and moon illusions
nodes.summary: The Ponzo illusion and the moon illusion both arise when depth/distance cues distort perceived size of equal-retinal-image stimuli.

nodes.label: Schemas, culture, and experience
nodes.summary: Prior schemas, cultural background, motivation, and emotion bias top-down interpretation of ambiguous input.

nodes.label: Depth perception
nodes.summary: Binocular and monocular cues let the brain reconstruct three-dimensional depth from two-dimensional retinal images.

nodes.label: Binocular cues
nodes.summary: Binocular depth cues require both eyes and are strongest for nearby objects.

nodes.label: Convergence
nodes.summary: Convergence is the inward turning of the eyes for near objects, providing a depth cue from eye-muscle feedback.

nodes.label: Interposition (overlap)
nodes.summary: An object that partially blocks another is perceived as closer, providing a monocular depth cue.

nodes.label: Light, shading, and aerial perspective
nodes.summary: Shading conveys form while haze (aerial perspective) makes distant objects appear bluer and blurrier, giving depth.

nodes.label: Linear perspective
nodes.summary: Parallel lines appear to converge with distance, a monocular cue to depth.

nodes.label: Monocular (pictorial) cues
nodes.summary: Monocular cues such as relative size, interposition, linear perspective, and texture gradient provide depth with one eye.

nodes.label: Motion parallax
nodes.summary: Motion parallax is the depth cue in which near objects appear to move faster than distant ones as the viewer moves.

nodes.label: Relative height
nodes.summary: Objects higher in the visual field (toward the horizon) are perceived as more distant.

nodes.label: Relative size
nodes.summary: When objects are assumed equal in size, the one casting the smaller retinal image is perceived as farther away.

nodes.label: Retinal (binocular) disparity
nodes.summary: Retinal disparity is the slight difference between the two eyes' images that the brain uses to compute depth.

nodes.label: Texture gradient
nodes.summary: Texture appears denser and finer with distance, signaling depth from a single eye.

nodes.label: Gestalt principles
nodes.summary: Gestalt principles describe how the brain groups elements into organized wholes, where the whole exceeds the sum of its parts.

nodes.label: Closure
nodes.summary: The principle of closure states that we fill in gaps to perceive complete, whole objects.

nodes.label: Common fate (common motion)
nodes.summary: The principle of common fate states that elements moving together are perceived as a group.

nodes.label: Connectedness and common region
nodes.summary: Elements that are physically connected or enclosed within a common region are perceived as belonging together.

nodes.label: Continuity (good continuation)
nodes.summary: The principle of continuity states that elements arranged along a smooth line or curve are perceived as continuous.

nodes.label: Figure-ground
nodes.summary: Figure-ground organization is the perception of objects (figures) as distinct from their background.

nodes.label: Law of prägnanz
