nodes.label: Migration and urbanization
nodes.summary: Rural-to-urban migration is a major driver of urbanization and the growth of cities.

nodes.label: Voluntary versus forced migration
nodes.summary: Voluntary migration is chosen (e.g., for jobs), while forced migration is compelled by persecution, war, disaster, or slavery.

nodes.label: Mortality and death
nodes.summary: Mortality measures the rate of death in a population and is a major determinant of population size and age structure.

nodes.label: Age-specific death rate
nodes.summary: The age-specific death rate is the number of deaths per 1,000 people in a given age group, allowing comparison across populations with different age structures.

nodes.label: Crude death rate
nodes.summary: The crude death rate is the number of deaths per 1,000 people in a population per year.

nodes.label: Epidemiologic transition
nodes.summary: The epidemiologic transition is the shift from infectious-disease mortality toward chronic and degenerative disease as societies develop.

nodes.label: Infant mortality rate
nodes.summary: The infant mortality rate is the number of deaths of infants under one year per 1,000 live births, a sensitive indicator of population health.

nodes.label: Life expectancy
nodes.summary: Life expectancy is the average number of years a person can expect to live given current mortality rates.

nodes.label: Maximum life span
nodes.summary: Maximum life span is the greatest age a member of a species can reach, distinct from average life expectancy.

nodes.label: Maternal mortality rate
nodes.summary: The maternal mortality rate is the number of deaths from pregnancy-related causes per 100,000 live births, another sensitive indicator of health-system quality.

nodes.label: Morbidity
nodes.summary: Morbidity is the rate or burden of disease and illness in a population, distinct from mortality (death).

nodes.label: Population change equation
nodes.summary: The components of population change combine births, deaths, and migration to determine total growth.

nodes.label: Demographic balancing equation
nodes.summary: Population change equals (births - deaths) + (immigration - emigration), summing natural increase and net migration.

nodes.label: Doubling time (rule of 70)
nodes.summary: Doubling time is approximated by dividing 70 by the percent annual growth rate.

nodes.label: Rate of natural increase
nodes.summary: The rate of natural increase is the birth rate minus the death rate, excluding migration.

nodes.label: Population growth rate
nodes.summary: The population growth rate is the percentage change in population size over a period, combining natural increase and net migration.

nodes.label: Zero population growth
nodes.summary: Zero population growth occurs when births plus immigration equal deaths plus emigration, leaving population size unchanged.

nodes.label: Demographic structure of society
nodes.summary: Demographic structure describes how a population is composed along dimensions such as age, sex/gender, race/ethnicity, immigration status, and sexual orientation.

nodes.label: Age structure
nodes.summary: The age distribution of a population shapes its dependency burden, labor supply, and future demographic trajectory.

nodes.label: Age cohort
nodes.summary: An age cohort is a group of people of similar age who move through the life course and historical events together.

nodes.label: Population aging / graying
nodes.summary: Population aging is the rising share of older adults caused by falling fertility and increasing longevity.

nodes.label: Baby boom cohort effect
nodes.summary: A large birth cohort (baby boom) creates a bulge that strains institutions sequentially as it ages.

nodes.label: Age, period, and cohort effects
nodes.summary: Demographic change can be attributed to age effects (life stage), period effects (events at a time), or cohort effects (shared generational experience).

nodes.label: Constrictive pyramid
nodes.summary: A constrictive (narrow-based) pyramid reflects low birth rates and an aging population that may shrink.

nodes.label: Age dependency ratio
nodes.summary: The dependency ratio compares the dependent population (under 15 and over 64) to the working-age population (15-64).

nodes.label: Expansive pyramid
nodes.summary: An expansive (wide-based) pyramid reflects high birth rates and a young, rapidly growing population.

nodes.label: Generation
nodes.summary: A generation is a large birth cohort (e.g., Boomers, Gen X, Millennials, Gen Z) whose shared formative experiences shape distinctive attitudes and behaviors.

nodes.label: Median age of a population
nodes.summary: Median age divides a population into equal younger and older halves and rises as a population ages.

nodes.label: Population (age-sex) pyramid
nodes.summary: A population pyramid is a back-to-back bar graph showing the distribution of a population by age and sex.

nodes.label: Stationary pyramid
nodes.summary: A stationary pyramid has roughly equal numbers across age groups, indicating a stable population size.

nodes.label: Youth versus old-age dependency
nodes.summary: The youth dependency ratio counts those under 15, while the old-age (aged) dependency ratio counts those over 64; their balance shapes social-policy strain.

nodes.label: Foundational concepts of demography
nodes.summary: Demography is the statistical study of human populations and the structures and dynamics that shape them.

nodes.label: Census
nodes.summary: A census is a periodic, complete count of a population that provides the baseline data for demographic analysis.

nodes.label: Cohort
nodes.summary: A cohort is a group of people who share a common demographic characteristic, most often having been born in the same period (a birth cohort).

nodes.label: Demographic variables overview
nodes.summary: Core demographic variables include age, sex/gender, race/ethnicity, immigration status, and sexual orientation, each shaping a population's structure.

nodes.label: Demography
nodes.summary: Demography is the scientific study of the size, composition, distribution, and change of human populations.

nodes.label: Population
nodes.summary: A population is a group of people living within a defined geographic area at a given time.

nodes.label: Population distribution and density
nodes.summary: Population distribution is how people are spread across geographic space, while density is the number of people per unit area.

nodes.label: Vital statistics and registration
nodes.summary: Vital statistics are records of vital events (births, deaths, marriages, divorces) gathered through ongoing civil registration that supplement the census.

nodes.label: Sex and gender structure
nodes.summary: The distribution and social organization of a population by sex and gender shapes roles, inequality, and demographic processes.

nodes.label: Gender identity
nodes.summary: Gender identity is a person's internal sense of their own gender, which may differ from their assigned sex.

nodes.label: Gender inequality
nodes.summary: Gender inequality is the unequal distribution of power, resources, and opportunity across genders, e.g., wage gaps and the glass ceiling.

nodes.label: Gender roles
nodes.summary: Gender roles are the socially prescribed expectations for behavior, attitudes, and activities based on perceived gender.

nodes.label: Gender segregation
nodes.summary: Gender segregation is the separation of people into different roles, occupations, or spaces on the basis of gender.

nodes.label: Gender stratification
nodes.summary: Gender stratification is the systematic ranking of genders such that one (typically men) has greater access to wealth, power, and status.

nodes.label: Sex ratio
nodes.summary: The sex ratio is the number of males per 100 females in a population, varying by age and influenced by mortality and sex-selective practices.

nodes.label: Sex versus gender
nodes.summary: Sex refers to biological/anatomical characteristics, whereas gender refers to socially constructed roles, behaviors, and identities.

nodes.label: Transgender and nonbinary identities
nodes.summary: Transgender people have a gender identity differing from their assigned sex; nonbinary people identify outside the male/female binary, adding dimensions to gender structure.

nodes.label: Immigration status structure
nodes.summary: Immigration status differentiates populations by legal residency and shapes access to rights and resources.

nodes.label: Documented versus undocumented status
nodes.summary: Legal/documented immigrants hold authorized residency, while undocumented immigrants lack legal status, sharply limiting access to rights and resources.

nodes.label: Emigrant
nodes.summary: An emigrant is a person who leaves their country of origin to settle elsewhere.

nodes.label: Immigrant
nodes.summary: An immigrant is a person who moves into a country to take up permanent residence.

nodes.label: Internally displaced persons
nodes.summary: Internally displaced persons are forced from their homes but remain within their own country's borders, unlike refugees who cross borders.

nodes.label: Refugee versus asylum seeker
nodes.summary: A refugee flees persecution or danger and seeks protection in another country; an asylum seeker requests such protection upon arrival.

nodes.label: Race and ethnicity structure
nodes.summary: Race and ethnicity are socially constructed categories that organize populations and produce stratification.

nodes.label: Amalgamation
nodes.summary: Amalgamation (melting pot) is the blending of majority and minority groups into a new, combined culture.

nodes.label: Assimilation
nodes.summary: Assimilation is the process by which a minority group gradually adopts the culture of the dominant group.

nodes.label: Dominant (majority) group
nodes.summary: The dominant group holds the most power, privilege, and social status in a society.

nodes.label: Ethnicity
nodes.summary: Ethnicity is shared cultural heritage, including language, ancestry, religion, and traditions.

nodes.label: Hypodescent (one-drop rule)
nodes.summary: Hypodescent assigns multiracial individuals to the socially subordinate group, as in the historical 'one-drop rule', illustrating race's arbitrary social construction.

nodes.label: Minority group
nodes.summary: A minority group is a category that receives unequal treatment and has less power, regardless of its numerical size.

nodes.label: Multiculturalism / pluralism
nodes.summary: Multiculturalism (pluralism) is the coexistence of distinct cultural groups that retain their own identities within one society.

nodes.label: Race (social construction)
nodes.summary: Race is a socially constructed category based on perceived physical differences, with no firm biological basis.

nodes.label: Racialization
nodes.summary: Racialization is the process by which a group comes to be defined and treated as a distinct race.

nodes.label: Segregation (residential and social)
nodes.summary: Segregation is the physical and social separation of groups; de jure segregation is legally enforced while de facto segregation arises through practice and residential patterns.

nodes.label: Symbolic ethnicity
nodes.summary: Symbolic ethnicity is a voluntary, occasional identification with one's ethnic heritage that has little effect on daily life, common among assimilated groups.

nodes.label: Sexual orientation structure
nodes.summary: Sexual orientation is a demographic dimension describing patterns of attraction and identity within a population.

nodes.label: Orientation versus gender identity
nodes.summary: Sexual orientation (whom one is attracted to) is distinct from gender identity (one's internal sense of gender).

nodes.label: Sexual orientation
nodes.summary: Sexual orientation describes the pattern of a person's romantic or sexual attraction to others.

nodes.label: Population growth and dynamics
nodes.summary: Population dynamics describe theories and patterns of how populations grow, stabilize, or decline over time.

nodes.label: Demographic transition theory
nodes.summary: Demographic transition theory describes how societies move from high to low birth and death rates as they industrialize.

nodes.label: Demographic transition model (DTM)
nodes.summary: The demographic transition model maps the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates across four to five stages.

nodes.label: Second demographic transition
nodes.summary: The second demographic transition describes sustained sub-replacement fertility in advanced societies driven by changing values, delayed marriage, cohabitation, and individualism.

nodes.label: DTM Stage 1: pre-industrial
nodes.summary: Stage 1 has high birth rates and high death rates, yielding stable, low population growth.

nodes.label: DTM Stage 2: early industrial
nodes.summary: Stage 2 has high birth rates but falling death rates (better food and medicine), causing rapid growth.

nodes.label: DTM Stage 3: mature industrial
nodes.summary: Stage 3 has falling birth rates and low death rates, slowing population growth.

nodes.label: DTM Stage 4: post-industrial
nodes.summary: Stage 4 has low birth and low death rates, yielding a stable, large population.

nodes.label: DTM Stage 5: declining
nodes.summary: A proposed Stage 5 has birth rates below death rates, producing population decline.

nodes.label: Models of population growth
nodes.summary: Population growth follows exponential or logistic patterns depending on resource constraints.

nodes.label: Carrying capacity
nodes.summary: Carrying capacity is the maximum population size an environment can sustain given available resources.

nodes.label: Exponential growth
nodes.summary: Exponential growth is unconstrained increase at a constant percentage rate, producing a J-shaped curve.

nodes.label: Logistic growth and carrying capacity
nodes.summary: Logistic growth slows as a population approaches the carrying capacity its environment can sustain, producing an S-shaped curve.

nodes.label: Malthusian theory
nodes.summary: Malthus argued that population growth tends to outpace food supply, leading to checks on population.

nodes.label: Malthusian catastrophe
nodes.summary: A Malthusian catastrophe is the predicted forced return to subsistence when population outgrows food supply.

nodes.label: Malthusian theory
nodes.summary: Malthus held that population grows geometrically while food supply grows arithmetically, so growth is ultimately checked.

nodes.label: Neo-Malthusianism and critiques
nodes.summary: Neo-Malthusians revive concerns about overpopulation and resource limits, while critics note that technology and the demographic transition have so far averted catastrophe.

nodes.label: Positive (Malthusian) checks
nodes.summary: Positive checks are factors that raise the death rate, such as famine, disease, and war.

nodes.label: Preventive (Malthusian) checks
nodes.summary: Preventive checks are factors that lower the birth rate, such as delayed marriage and abstinence.

nodes.label: Decline and projection
nodes.summary: Populations can decline or be projected forward depending on fertility, mortality, and migration trends.

nodes.label: Overpopulation and sustainability
nodes.summary: Overpopulation is when a population exceeds the sustainable carrying capacity of its environment, straining resources and ecological limits.

nodes.label: Population decline / depopulation
nodes.summary: Population decline occurs when deaths plus emigration exceed births plus immigration over time.

nodes.label: Population momentum
nodes.summary: Population momentum is continued growth after fertility falls to replacement level because a large cohort is still of childbearing age.

nodes.label: Population projection
nodes.summary: A population projection estimates future population size and structure based on assumed fertility, mortality, and migration.

nodes.label: Social movements and demographic change
nodes.summary: Collective behavior, social movements, globalization, and urbanization reshape population distribution and composition.

nodes.label: Collective behavior
nodes.summary: Collective behavior is spontaneous, often short-lived group action outside of established norms.

nodes.label: Collective behavior
nodes.summary: Collective behavior is relatively spontaneous, unstructured group activity that emerges in response to a common situation.

nodes.label: Contagion versus emergent-norm theory
nodes.summary: Contagion theory says crowds induce irrational anonymity-driven behavior, while emergent-norm theory says crowds develop new shared norms that guide action.

nodes.label: Crowds, mobs, and riots
nodes.summary: Crowds are temporary gatherings; mobs are emotionally charged crowds intent on action; riots are violent, prolonged crowd actions.

nodes.label: Mass hysteria, fads, and fashions
nodes.summary: Mass hysteria is widespread irrational fear, while fads and fashions are temporary patterns of behavior adopted by many.

nodes.label: Masses and publics
nodes.summary: A mass is a large dispersed set of people responding to the same stimulus, and a public is a dispersed group debating a shared issue, both lacking physical proximity.

nodes.label: Globalization
nodes.summary: Globalization integrates economies and cultures across borders, reshaping migration and demographic patterns.

nodes.label: Civil unrest and terrorism
nodes.summary: Civil unrest and terrorism are disruptive responses to globalization-related strain that can drive migration and demographic shifts.

nodes.label: Cultural diffusion and homogenization
nodes.summary: Globalization spreads cultural practices across borders (diffusion), sometimes producing homogenization and tension with local identities.

nodes.label: Economic interdependence and trade
nodes.summary: Globalization links national economies through trade, capital flows, and multinational firms, creating interdependence that drives labor migration.

nodes.label: Globalization
nodes.summary: Globalization is the increasing interconnection and interdependence of societies, economies, and cultures worldwide.

nodes.label: Social movements
nodes.summary: Social movements are organized collective efforts to promote or resist social change.

nodes.label: Social movement life cycle
nodes.summary: Movements typically pass through stages: emergence, coalescence, bureaucratization (institutionalization), and decline (success, failure, co-optation, or repression).

nodes.label: Types of social movements
nodes.summary: Movements vary by scope and aim: alternative, redemptive, reformative, and revolutionary movements differ in how much and whom they seek to change.

nodes.label: New social movement theory
nodes.summary: New social movement theory emphasizes identity, culture, and quality-of-life goals (e.g., environmental, LGBTQ, peace movements) over class-based economic aims.

nodes.label: Proactive versus reactive movements
nodes.summary: Proactive movements promote social change, whereas reactive movements resist it.

nodes.label: Relative deprivation theory
nodes.summary: Relative deprivation theory holds that movements arise when people perceive a gap between what they have and what they feel entitled to.

nodes.label: Resource mobilization theory
nodes.summary: Resource mobilization theory holds that movements succeed when they can organize and deploy resources such as money, people, and media.

nodes.label: Social movement
nodes.summary: A social movement is a sustained, organized collective effort to bring about or resist social change.

nodes.label: Urbanization
nodes.summary: Urbanization is the growth of cities and the shift of population from rural to urban areas.

nodes.label: Megacities and world cities
nodes.summary: Megacities are urban areas exceeding ten million people, increasingly common in the developing world; world (global) cities are command centers of the global economy.

nodes.label: Suburbanization
nodes.summary: Suburbanization is the movement of population from city centers to surrounding suburban areas.

nodes.label: Urban decline and gentrification
nodes.summary: Urban decline is the deterioration of a city area, while gentrification is the influx of wealthier residents that displaces lower-income ones.

nodes.label: Urban sprawl
nodes.summary: Urban sprawl is the unplanned, low-density spreading of development outward from a city into surrounding land.

nodes.label: Urbanization
nodes.summary: Urbanization is the increasing concentration of a population in cities and urban areas.

nodes.label: White flight
nodes.summary: White flight is the large-scale departure of white residents from racially diversifying urban neighborhoods to suburbs, reinforcing residential segregation.
